An Bonn√°n Bu√≠ ( The Yellow Bittern ) is a classic poem in

Irish by the poet Cathal Bu√≠ Mac Giolla Ghunna (c. 1680-1756).

The poem is in the form of a lament for a bittern that died of thirst, but is
items.lyrics:
Oh the bonambuie that ne‚Äôer broke out

On a drinking bout he might as well have drunk;

For his bones lie thrown on a naked stone

Where he lived all alone like a hermit monk.

Oh the bonambuie I pity your lot,

Though they say that a sot like myself is cursed.

I was sober a while, but I will drink and be wise,

For I fear I may die in the end of thirst.

It‚Äôs not for the kind word that I would mourn,

The blackbird, the corncrake or the crane,

But the bonambuie that is shy and apart

And drinks from the marsh on the lone bog trail.

If I had known you were near your death

While my breath held out I‚Äôd have run to you,

‚ÄôTil the splash in the Lake of the Son of the Bird,

Your soul would have brought to life again.

And my true love told me to drink no more

Or my life would be over in a little while;

It‚Äôs the taste of the whiskey it gives me health and strength

And will lengthen my road by many‚Äôs a mile.

You can see how the bird of the long smooth neck,

He gets his death from the thirst at last.

So come, son of my soul, and fill up your glass,

For you‚Äôll get no more sup when this life has passed.

versions.lyrics:
O an bunan buidhe that ne‚Äôer broke out

Where he lived all alone like the hermit monk.

O an bunan buidhe, I pity your lot

Though they say that a sot like myself is cursed

I was sober a while but I will drink and be wise

For I fear I may die in the end of thirst.

It‚Äôs not for the common bird that I mourn

For the blackbird, the corncrake or the crane

But the bunan buidhe that is shy and apart

And drinks from the marsh where the lone bog drains

If I‚Äôd have known you were near your death

While my breath held out I‚Äôd have run to you

Til a splash in the Lake of the Son of the Bird

Your soul would have stirred back to life anew.

My darling told me to drink no more

Or my life would be over in a little while

But it‚Äôs the taste of the whisky that gives me health and strength

And will lengthen my road by many a mile.

You can see how the bird of the long, smooth neck

He might catch his death from the thirst at last

So come song of my soul and fill your glass

For you‚Äôll get no more sup when this life is past.

On a wintering island I constant times recall

A bittern who calls from a wineless place

And tells me that hither he cannot come

Til the summer is here and the sunny day

When he crosses the stream there and wings over the sea

Then the thought comes to me he may fail in his flight

Now the milk and the ale they are drunk every drop

And a dram it won‚Äôt stop our thirst this night.

It grieves me more than the sack of Troy

And Devil the harm or hurt you‚Äôve done

And you would shrink from wine for a brackish pool.

And it‚Äôs not for the common bird that I mourn

For the blackbird, the corncrake or the crane,

But for the yellow bittern who is shy and apart

And who drinks from the marsh in the long bog drain.

Had I but known you were near your death

While my breath held out I‚Äôd have run to you,

‚ÄėTil a splash from the lake of Lough MacNean

Your soul would have stirred to life in you.

And my darling told me to drink no more

Or my life would be over in a very short while;

But it‚Äôs the taste of the whiskey gives me life and strength

And will shorten the road by many‚Äôs the mile.

You can see how the bird of the long smooth neck

Could get its death from the thirst at last.

So come son of my soul and fill up your cup,

For you‚Äôll get‚Äônaer a sup when your life is passed.

items.title: Living by the Water
items.notes:
Living by the Water

[Anne Briggs]

Anne Briggs wrote

Living by the Water

for her 1971 Topic album,

Anne Briggs .

Like all tracks from this album it was reissued on her two compilations

Classic Anne Briggs

and

A Collection .

A.L. Lloyd wrote in the sleeve notes:

Another of Anne Briggs‚Äôs own compositions. If
items.lyrics:
I was living by the water

Late July moon‚Äôs early quarter

Summer mornings, early dawnings

Paid no heed to me, gave no warning

Of their endless way

Seatide flowing in the river

Is all the music I would ever

Have, a long, long day since I went away

By the sea curlews calling

Hear the summer stars falling

Fire burning in the sun

Lighting up their way

On the lonely sands of the western strands

It was there I made my way

On the mountain there my song I‚Äôll sing

When the wind plays in the raven‚Äôs wing

And I saw moorland horses

Dancing over the plains of the deadland marshes

Down to the sea voices from the empty moor

They call me past the stranger‚Äôs door

Because I keep no company I make no enemies

The tide is turning, there is no waiting

Day was long, the sun setting

Sand shifting in the wind

These times they have no end

On the lonely sands of the western strands

It was there I made my way

items.lyrics:
It fell upon Martinmas time

When snow lay on the borders,

There came a troop of soldiers here

To take up their winter quarters.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

With me right fol-de-li-dle ly-die li-dle ly-dee-o

With me right fol-de-li-dle ly-dee la-ry.

They rode up and they rode down

And they rode over the border.

There they met a fair pretty girl

And she was a farmer‚Äôs daughter.

They made her swear a solemn oath

With a salt tear in her eye, oh,

That she would call at their quarter gates

When no-one did her spy, oh.

So she goes to the barber shop,

To the barber shop went soon, oh.

She‚Äôs made him cut her fine yellow hair

As short as any dragoon, oh.

Then she goes to the tailor shop

And dresses in soldier‚Äôs clothes, oh.

A pair of pistols down by her side

And a nice little boy was she, oh.

When she came to the quarter gates,

It‚Äôs loud, loud she did call, oh,

‚ÄúThere comes a troop of soldiers here

And we must have lodgings all, oh!‚ÄĚ

The quartermaster he comes out,

He gives her half a crown, oh:

‚ÄúGo and find lodgings for yourself,

For here there is no room, oh.‚ÄĚ

But she drew nearer to the gates

And louder she did call, oh:

‚ÄúRoom, room, you gentlemen,

We must have lodgings all, oh!‚ÄĚ

The quartermaster he comes out,

He gives her eighteen pence, oh:

‚ÄúGo and find lodgings in the town

For tonight there comes a wench, oh.‚ÄĚ

She‚Äôs pulled the garters from her legs,

The ribbons from her hair, oh.

She‚Äôs tied them ‚Äôround the quarter gates

As a token she‚Äôd been there, oh.

She drew a whistle from her side,

And blew it loud and shrill, oh:

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre all very free with your eighteen pence,

But you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre not for a girl at all, oh.‚ÄĚ

And when they knew that it was her

They tried to overtake her.

She‚Äôs clapped her spurs to her horse‚Äôs side

And she‚Äôs galloped home a maiden.

But were I to have him or were I to not,

I will think of him once and then,

Yes, I‚Äôll think of him once now and then.

versions.title: Daisy Chapman sings I Once Had a Boy
versions.lyrics:
I once had a boy and a bonny, bonny boy,

And a boy that I once call√®d mine;

But noo he‚Äôs gane an left me tae coort some ither een,

And he‚Äôs left me to sing fare thee well, fare thee well,

And he‚Äôs left me to sing fare thee well.

As I was a-walking down by yon crystal river,

And down by the bonnie banks o‚Äô Clyde;

So whom did I spy but my ain bonny boy,

Lockit fast in another girlie‚Äôs arms, arms, arms,

Lockit fast in another girlie‚Äôs arms.

He gave me a wink o‚Äô his bonnie black ee,

And a wave o‚Äô his lily white hand;

But I proudly passed him by and I made him no reply,

For I hated to be slighted by a man, a man, a man,

For I hated to be slighted by a man.

For I‚Äôve got sweethearts plenty, I could count to one and twenty,

I‚Äôve a mind that would change like the wind;

For mony is the lang nicht he‚Äôs rowed me in his airms,

But I‚Äôm gey weel sure he‚Äôll never dee‚Äôt again, again, again,

But I‚Äôm gey weel sure he‚Äôll never dee‚Äôt again.

Here‚Äôs a health and a wealth tae ma ain bonny boy,

And I wish him safe o‚Äôer the lea;

For mony is the lang nicht he‚Äôs rowed me in his airms,

But I‚Äôm gey weel sure he‚Äôll never dee‚Äôt again, again, again,

But I‚Äôm gey weel sure he‚Äôll never dee‚Äôt again.

versions.title: Tim Radford sings My Bonny, Bonny Boy
versions.lyrics:
I lov-ed a boy and a bonny bonny boy,

I loved him I vow and protest,

I loved him so well, there‚Äôs no tongue can tell,

Till I built him a berth on my breast.

‚ÄôTwas up the wild forest and through the green groves

Like one that was troubled in mind,

I hallooed, I whooped and I played on my flute

But no bonny boy could I find.

I looked up high and I looked down low

The weather being wonderfully warm;

And who should I spy but my own bonny boy

Locked fast in another girl‚Äôs arms.

He took me upon his assembled knees

And looked me quite hard in the face,

He gave unto me one sweet smile and a kiss

But his heart‚Äôs in another girl‚Äôs breast.

Now my bonny, bonny boy is across the salt seas

And I hope he will safely return;

But if he loves another girl better than me

Let him take her, and why should I mourn?

Now the girl that enjoys my own bonny boy,

She is not to be blamed, I am sure,

For many‚Äôs the long night he hath robbed me of my rest

But he never shall do it no more.

versions.title: The Furrow Collective sing Many‚Äôs the Night‚Äôs Rest
versions.lyrics:
Many‚Äôs the night‚Äôs rest you‚Äôve robbed me of,

That you never shall never do it again,

No you never shall do it again.

She‚Äôs looked up high and she‚Äôs looked down low,

But no bonny boy could she find,

No bonny boy could she find.

So she hollered and she whooped and she played on her flute,

And the sun did shine wondrous warm,

The sun did shine wondrous warm.

At last there I spied my own bonny boy,

Wrapped up in some other girl‚Äôs arms,

Wrapped up in some other girl‚Äôs arms.

Many‚Äôs the night‚Äôs rest you‚Äôve robbed me of,

That you never shall never do it again,

You never shall do it again.

versions.title: Cathy Tattersfield sings My Bonny, Bonny Boy
versions.lyrics:
O once I was courted by a bonny, bonny boy,

Who would come and go on my request

I loved him so well and so very well,

That I could build him a nest in my breast. (√ó2)

So it‚Äôs up the green meadows and down the steep valley,

Like one not troubled in mind,

I holloaed and hooped and I played on my flute

But no bonny, bonny boy could I find. (√ó2)

I set myself down on the green mossy bank,

Where the sun it shone wonderful warm,

But who did I see but my own bonny he,

Wrapped up in some other girls arms. (√ó2)

She that hath got my bonny, bonny boy,

Let her make of him what she can,

My freedom to me is far sweeter that he,

For freedom is softer than chains. (√ó2)

items.title: Rosemary Lane / He Called for a Candle / Bell Bottom Trousers / Home Boys Home
items.notes:

/ Song Subject MAS979

items.lyrics:
I lived in service in Rosemary Lane,

I kept the good will of my master and dame.

Till a sailor came there one night for to lay,

And that was the beginning of my misery.

He called for a candle to light him to bed,

And likewise a silk handkerchief to tie up his head.

To tie up his head as he used for to do,

Says he, ‚ÄúPretty Polly, Won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you come to bed too?‚ÄĚ

This girl, being young and foolish, she thought it no harm

To jump into bed to keep herself warm.

But what was done next I‚Äôll never declare,

But I wish that short night had been seven long year.

It was early next morning the sailor arose

And into her lap he threw handfuls of gold,

Saying, ‚ÄúThis I will give, and more I will do

If you‚Äôll be my Polly wherever I go.

‚ÄúAnd when your baby is born, you put it to nurse,

And sit like a lady with gold in your purse.

With gold in your purse and milk in your breast.‚ÄĚ

Saying, ‚ÄúThat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs what you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve got by your sailor in the west.

‚ÄúAnd if it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a boy, he shall fight for his king,

And if it‚Äôs a girl, she shall wear the gold ring.

She shall wear the gold ring and her top knot shall blow.‚ÄĚ

Saying, ‚ÄúThat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs what you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve got by your sailor true blue.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Martin Carthy sings He Called for a Candle
versions.lyrics:
He called for a candle to light his way to bed,

And likewise a handkerchief to tie around his head,

To tie around his head like the sailors often do

And he says, ‚ÄúMe pretty fair maid, will you go too?‚ÄĚ

And Maggie was a young girl, she thought it was no harm,

She laid her down beside him for to keep his bosom warm.

In the middle of the night when the sailor he grew bold

He throwed into her apron five hundred pounds in gold.

And it was early the next morning when Maggie‚Äôs mother rose,

Saying, ‚ÄúDaughter, dearest daughter, you have thrown yourself away,

You have thrown yourself away and you‚Äôve birthed a baby,

And you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll pass for a young girl in a foreign country.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh mother, dearest mother, oh, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve done nothing wrong

For I‚Äôve gold in me pocket and silver in me purse.

And when me baby‚Äôs born we will pay the nurse a fee,

And I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll pass for a young girl in a foreign country.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAnd if it bes a girl child, she√Ę¬Ä¬ôll stay at home with me,

And if it bes a boy he will plough the dark blue sea.

He‚Äôll plough the dark blue sea like his daddy done before

And he√Ę¬Ä¬ôll skip about the deck like a young sailor boy.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAnd it is God bless the ocean that my love sails upon

And likewise the bonny ship that carries him along.

I‚Äôll write me love a letter and I‚Äôll seal it with a ring

And I never will be happy till me love returns again.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Jumbo Brightwell sings The Oak and the Ash
versions.lyrics:
Now, Jack he fell drowsy; he hung his poor head.

He asked for a candle for to light him to bed.

A maiden she carried it like maidens ought to do,

Then Jack he did propose that she should get in bed, too.

Chorus (after each verse):

So it‚Äôs home, dearest home, your/and home it shall be,

Though it‚Äôs far, far away from your own counterie,

Where the oak and the ash and the bonny willow tree,

They‚Äôre all a-growing green in the North Americee.

Now, she considered it proper and thought it no harm,

If she got into bed for to keep his back warm.

She huddled him; she cuddled him; she bid him draw near.

She only wished that night had been seven long year.

It was early next morning so bright and so bold

Into her lap he put handfuls of gold,

Saying, ‚ÄúTake it, my pretty one, for the harm that I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve done.

For I think that I have left you with a daughter or a son.

‚ÄúNow, if it prove a female you shall dance it on your knee.

And if it prove a male he shall sail the raging sea.

With his low-ankle shoes and his jacket so blue,

He will have to walk the quarter deck like daddy used to do.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Lucy Woodall sings Rosemary Lane
versions.lyrics:
I once was in service down Rosemary Lane.

I had a kind mistress and master the same.

One day, a young sailor came to our house to tea

And this was the commencement of my misery.

When supper was over he hung down his head

Then he asked for a candle to light him to bed.

I gave him a candle as a maiden should do

But he vowed and declared that I should go too.

Early next morning when the young sailor rose,

He threw in my apron two handful of gold.

‚ÄúOh take it, oh take it, for the wrong I have done,

I have left you a daughter or else a fine son.

‚Äúf it be a daughter, she shall wait upon me,

But if it‚Äôs a sonny, he shall cross the deep sea.

He shall wear a blue jacket and his cap lined with gold,

He shall cross the blue ocean like his young father bold.‚ÄĚ

Now all you young lasses take a warning from me.

Never trust a young sailor whoe‚Äôer he may be.

They kiss you, they court you, they swear they‚Äôll be true,

But the very next moment, they‚Äôll bid you adieu.

Like the flower in the garden when its beauty‚Äôs all gone,

So you see what I‚Äôve come to through loving that one.

No father, no mother, no friend in the world.

So me and my baby to the workhouse must go.

versions.title: Ted Chaplin sings The Oak & the Ash
versions.lyrics:
Johnny was a sailor, a sailor on the main,

He earned a good living and captured a good name.

He was lying in the harbour, three months there to stay,

It was there he fell in love with a Polly so gay.

Chorus (after each verse):

Singing home dearest home, over there let me be,

Far, far away from the old country.

Where the oak and the ash, the bonny elm tree,

They‚Äôre all a-growing greener in the North Amerikee.

She jumped into bed not at least alarmed,

Not thinking that the sailor boy would do her any harm.

He huddled her, he cuddled her the whole night through,

And she only wished the night had been seven times as long.

Now if it be a daughter we‚Äôll send her out to nurse,

Silver in her pocket and gold in her purse.

And if it be a boy he shall wear the jacket blue,

And sail the blooming ocean like his daddy used to do.

Now all young maidens, this warning take by me,

Never trust a sailor boy an inch above your knee.

For I trusted one, he‚Äôs been the ruin of me,

For he left me with a pair of twins to bounce upon my knee.

Now all young fellows, this warning take by me,

Never build your nest upon an old (bye?) tree.

For all the leaves they will glitter and the fruit it will decay,

And the beauty of a fair maid will soon pass away.

versions.title: Charlie Stringer sings The Serving Maid
versions.lyrics:
Once there was a servant girl lived down in Drury Lane,

The mistress was so kind to her, the master just the same.

One fine evening a sailor came to tea,

This was the beginning of her misery.

He asked for a candle to light him up to bed,

He asked for a bandage to wrap around his head.

‚ÄôCourse she was a poor and silly girl, and thought there was no harm:

She jumped into bed to keep that sailor warm.

Early next morning the sailor he arose,

Hands in his pocket, he pulled out some gold.

He said, ‚ÄúHere you are my darling, the damage I have done.

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve left you in charge of a daughter or a son.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIf it be a daughter, all smiles upon your knee.

If he be a son, a sailor he shall be,

With his bell-bottom trousers and a suit of navy blue,

He shall climb the riggings just like all sailor√Ę¬Ä¬ôs do.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Cyril Tawney sings Bell Bottom Trousers
versions.lyrics:
I was serving maid down in Drury Lane,

My master he was good to me, my mistress was the same.

When along came a sailor ashore on liberty,

And oh, to my woe, he took liberties with me.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Singing bell-bottom trousers, coat of Navy blue,

Let him climb the rigging like his dayy used to do.

It was at a ball I met him, he asked me for a dance.

I knew he was a sailor by the way he wore his pants.

When the ball was over he asked to see me home,

Then asked if he could stay the night as he was all alone.

He asked me for a candle to light his way to bed.

He asked me for a handkerchief to tie around his head.

Me a foolish maiden, not thinking it no harm,

I jumped into the bed with him to keep the sailor warm.

He really was no Sampson but that night he went to town.

He laid me on the bed there till my blue eyes turned to brown.

Early next morning the sailor he awoke,

And the crafty bastard handed me a crabby ten bob note.

Saying, ‚ÄúTake this my darling for the damage I have done,

For if it be a daughter or if it be a son.

If it be a daughter, jounce her on your knee,

But if it be a son, send the bastard off to sea.‚ÄĚ

Now come all you young maidens and listen to my plea,

Don‚Äôt ever let a matelot get an inch above your knee.

I trusted one once and he put out to sea,

And left me with a daughter to jounce upon my knee.

versions.title: The Haar sing Home Boys Home
versions.lyrics:
There once was an apprentice lived in Strawberry Lane,

Loved by her master and her mistress the same,

Until a young sailor lad came sailing o‚Äôer the sea

And that was the begining of her misery.

Chorus (after each verse):

And it‚Äôs home boys home, home I‚Äôd like to be,

Home for a while in my own country,

Where the oak and the ash and the bonny rowan tree

Are all growing greener in my own country.

Well the maid being [foolish?] since she thought it was no harm

To go into bed for to keep her back warm;

He hugged her and he kissed her and he called her his dear,

Said, ‚ÄúI wish my love this night it was as long as a year.‚ÄĚ

It was early the next morning as the sailor he arose

An into her apron he put handfuls of gold.

‚ÄúOh take this my dear for the mischief I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve done

For last night I may have left you with a daughter or a son.

‚ÄúAnd if you have a girl child then [dandle?] her on your knee,

And if you have a male child look for him after me

And when he‚Äôs a man you can dress him up in blue

And he√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go skipping up the rigging like his father used to.‚ÄĚ

So come all you lasses and a warning take by me

And never let a sailor lad an inch above your knee.

For once I met a sailor lad and he beguiled me

And he left me with two twins to dandle upon my knee.

items.title: She Moved/Moves Through the Fair / Our Wedding Day
items.notes:
[

; Henry H141

; Ballad Index K165

; DT MOVEFAIR

; Mudcat 869

; trad. adapted Padraic Colum / Herbert Hughes]

Gale Huntington:

Sam Henry‚Äôs Songs of the People

She Moved Through the Fair

was first collected in Donegal by poet Padraic Colum (1881-1972) and

musicologist Herbert Hughes (1882-1937).

A three verse version of
items.lyrics:
My young love said to me, ‚ÄúMy mother won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt mind

And my father won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt slight you for your lack of kind.‚ÄĚ

And she stepp‚Äôd away from me and this she did say,

‚ÄúIt will not be long, love, till our wedding day.‚ÄĚ

She stepp‚Äôd away from me and she went thro‚Äô the fair,

And fondly I watch‚Äôd her move here and move there,

And then she went homeward with one star awake,

As she swan in the evening moves over the lake.

Last night she came to me, she came softly in,

So softly she came that her feet made no din,

And she laid her hand on me and she did say,

‚ÄúIt will not be long, love, till our wedding day.‚ÄĚ

The people were saying no two were e‚Äôer wed

I dreamt it last night that my young love came in,

‚ÄúIt will not be long, love, till our wedding day.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Standing on the Shore
items.notes:
Standing on the Shore

[

; Mudcat 71138

; trad. arr. Terry Woods / Johnny Moynihan]

Sweeney√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Men‚ÄĒthen Johnny Moynihan and Terry Woods after having lost

Andy Irvine‚ÄĒrecorded Standing on the Shore in 1969 for their

second LP,

The Tracks of Sweeney .

Moynihan‚Äôs then girlfriend Anne Briggs recorded

Standing on the Shore

two years later in 1971 for her album

The Time Has Come .
items.lyrics:
Through the mist my ship has sailed,

Leaving me behind,

All the things left unsaid

That were in my mind.

When I stood on its deck

Strangely things I saw;

Strangely they have vanished now

Standing on the shore.

Memories of the seas I sailed,

Lands I gazed upon.

When I turned to draw the chart

All of them were gone.

When I stood on its deck

Strangely things I saw;

Strangely they have vanished now

Standing on the shore.

Sandy hills lying round,

Nothing standing clear;

Softly light is falling through

Time was never here.

Headlands fading out of sight,

Horizons disappear;

Hazy shores and silent seas,

Nothing do I hear.

Air and water, grass and sand,

Merging into one;

In the silence of all this

I am still alone.

The logbook closed, the ship has gone

As it has done before;

Where it sails no tongue can tell,

No one was on board.

items.title: Sullivan‚Äôs John
items.notes:
Sullivan‚Äôs John

; Mudcat 73572

items.lyrics:
Sullivan‚Äôs John, to the road you‚Äôve gone

Far away from your native home.

You‚Äôve gone with a tinker‚Äôs daughter

For along the road to roam.

Sullivan‚Äôs John, you won‚Äôt stick it long,

Your belly will soon get slack.

You‚Äôll be roaming the road with a mighty load

And a tooten box on your back.

I met Katie Coffey, she‚Äôd her neat baby

Tossed behind on her back strapped on.

She‚Äôd an old ash plant all in her hand

To drive her donkey along,

Enquiring at every farmer‚Äôs house

That along the road she passed.

O it‚Äôs where would she get an old pot to mend?

Or where would she swap an ass?

I heard of a fair in the County Clare

At a place call Spancil Hill

Where my brother James got a rap of the hames [harness],

Poor Paddy they tried to kill.

They loaded him up on an ass and cart

While Pat and Mary looked on.

O bad luck to the day that I went away

To join a tinker‚Äôs band.

(repeat first verse)

items.title: Summer‚Äôs In
items.notes:
Summer‚Äôs In

[

; Mudcat 120957

; Anne Briggs]

Anne Briggs recorded her song

Summer‚Äôs In

in 1973 for her album

Sing a Song for You .

On this track she was accompanied by Barry Dransfield and Ragged Robin.

Sadly the album remained unpublished until 1996 when it finally appeared on CD.

Anne Briggs noted:

This song is a celebration of a way of life I lived in the West of Ireland
items.lyrics:
Cuckoo‚Äôs call the evening to still,

The sky went down and the nightbird‚Äôs wing

Falls over, the last day of Spring.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

For Summer is in,

Everyone sing

Ah, ah, ah,

Let us begin!

The other day, it being warm

We walked beside the fresh green corn,

Gathered flowers in a garland to deck the day.

Friends and neighbours gather near,

Come drink with us a brew of beer?

And share in our fortune that the sun does bring.

The king and the queen one morning in May

Played chess in the valley to see who could stay,

The stream in its travels laughing away.

We‚Äôve sung in the markets, the pubs and the fair,

We made no money, but what do we care?

We‚Äôll sleep in a haystack and be on our way.

We‚Äôve gathered our feast along the seashore,

Cockles and mussels, enough for us all.

We‚Äôll eat in the sand dunes and then go back for more.

Scattered around us, where we lay,

Are the bones of million years away,

Bleached by the sunshine in the past few day.

Think of the ages blown in the sand,

Think where an ancient people did stand,

And think of your fortune to be child of this land.

items.title: The Recruited Collier / Jenny‚Äôs Complaint / Jimmy‚Äôs Enlisted
items.notes:

Robert Anderson:

Karl Dallas:

The Cruel Wars

A.L. Lloyd:
items.lyrics:
O what‚Äôs the matter with you, my lass, and where‚Äôs your dashing Jimmy?

The soldier boys have picked him up and sent him far, far from me.

Last pay day he went off to town and them red-coated fellows

Enticed him in and made him drunk‚ÄĒand he√Ę¬Ä¬ôd be better gone to the gallows.

The very sight of his cockade, it sets us all a-crying,

And me! I nearly fainted twice, I thought that I was dying.

My father would have paid the smart and he‚Äôd run for the golden guinea.

But the sergeant swore he‚Äôd kissed the book, so now they‚Äôve got young Jimmy.

When Jimmy talks about the wars it‚Äôs worse than death to hear him.

I must go out and hide my tears because I cannot bear him.

A brigadeer or grenadier he says they‚Äôre sure to make him,

But aye, he gibes and cracks his jokes and begs me not forsake him.

As I walked o‚Äôer the stubble field below it runs the seam,

I thought o‚Äô Jimmy hewing there but it was all a dream.

He hewed the very coals we burn and, when the fire I‚Äôs leeting.

To think the lumps was in his hands, it sets my heart a-beating.

[ For three long years he‚Äôs followed me, now I must live without him.

There‚Äôs nothing now that I can do but weep and think about him. ] 2

So break my heart and then it‚Äôs o‚Äôer, so break my heart, my dearie.

For I‚Äôll lie in the cold green ground for of single life I‚Äôm weary.

Peter Buchan included The Whirly Wha

in his 1832 anthology of risqu√© and convivial songs,
items.lyrics:
The very first wedding I was bridesmaid at

Was on a Saturday.

There was plenty of lively fun

And likewise sporting and play.

The bells they rung and us girls we sung

And to the church we went all.

And the bride‚Äôs off to bed with the silly old groom

To play at the whirly whorl.

Well, first she modestly turned her back

And then she turned her front,

And long she wished for kindliness

But kindness she got none.

So at last she‚Äôs grabbed him all in her arms

And shoved him against the wall,

versions.title: Eliza Carthy sings The Whirly Whorl
versions.lyrics:

items.lyrics:
(Philip Lumpkin) Order please for Underneath the Apron

Now it was little Sarah with her pail and broom,

She had to loose her apron strings to give her belly room.

When in came her master, and unto her did say,

‚ÄúO what is that you have got beneath your apron?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs nothing, Sir, it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs nothing sir, O nothing Sir‚ÄĚ, said she.

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs only a muslin gown my mother gave to me.

I had nowhere to put it, ‚Äôtwas too tight to be worn

So I tucked it snugly underneath me apron.‚ÄĚ

After some months, a baby boy was born.

Born without a father, without a home at all.

When in came her master, and unto her did say,

‚ÄúDid you get that from underneath your apron.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWas it by a tinker, was it by a clown,

Was it by a soldier-boy who fights for England√Ę¬Ä¬ôs crown?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNo sir, it was by a sailor, who ploughs the angry sea,

Who was it who popped it underneath me apron.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNow was it in the parlour, was it in the hall,

Was it in the larder, or in the house at all?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNo sir it was in the garden, up against the wall,

When Jack he popped it underneath me apron.‚ÄĚ

Now all you pretty girls, Take this tip from me,

Never trust a sailor-boy one inch above your knee,

If you do, the sailor he‚Äôll haul your colours down,

And he‚Äôll drop his anchor underneath your apron.

items.title: Tidewave
items.notes:
Tidewave

[

; Mudcat 75813

; Anne Briggs]

Anne Briggs sang her own song

Tidewave

on her 1971 album

The Time Has Come .

She noted:

A song for friends. I could never see the beginnings nor the end of this

song, though I know it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs there‚ÄĒit√Ę¬Ä¬ôs just a bit misty.

Base string on the bouzouki dropped to an octave drone.
items.lyrics:
Oh, you came to stay and the day was brighter,

The way much lighter.

You brought your joy, your music too,

We‚Äôd laugh and sing and play the Summer through.

And with time and faces your love for all things grew

But the day was growing colder, cold Winter reaching you.

And the words of a song were the only exception

To your lonely reflection.

You brought so much and your friends to me

With your friends you tried to share your own eternity.

And nobody knowing how you wanted things to be,

But the day was growing darker and you couldn‚Äôt really see.

And when the fortune-teller spoke, you held lightly in your hand

The mirror of your mind,

Which you thought to be true to when you‚Äôd see

The days ahead were nothing but transparency.

An image of the no-man‚Äôs land, the sun at the edge of the sea,

Tides came and departed, took you away from me.

Oh, you came to stay and the day was brighter

The way much lighter.

You brought your joy, your music too,

We‚Äôd laugh and sing and play the Summer through.

And with time and faces your love for all things grew

But the day was growing colder, cold Winter reaching you.

versions.title: Joe McCafferty sings John Barbour

versions.title: Bellowhead sing Amsterdam
versions.lyrics:
In the port of Amsterdam

In the port of Amsterdam

There‚Äôs a sailor who sleeps

In the port of Amsterdam

There‚Äôs a sailor who dies

But in the port of Amsterdam

There‚Äôs a sailor who‚Äôs born

In the port of Amsterdam

Betsy Baker

items.lyrics:
Old Covent Garden fare-thee-weil,

Adieu to old Long Acre,

I never knew what ‚Äôtwas to sigh.

Till I saw Betsy Baker.

At church I met her, dressed so neat,

One Sunday in hot weather;

With love I found my heart did beat,

As we sang psalms together.

When church was over out she walked

And I did overtake her;

Determined I would not be bulked,

I spoke to Betsy Baker.

She blushed and seemed too shy for talk.

But quickly I did make her;

Says I, ‚ÄúMy dear, will you take a walk?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI shan√Ę¬Ä¬ôt,‚ÄĚ says Betsy Baker.

At last she got acquainted with

A ramping, mad play actor;

He gammoned her to run away,

So I lost Betsy Baker.

My mother said ‚Äôtwould ease my mind

All quickly to forsake her;

But I think all day, and dream all night

About that Betsy Baker.

versions.title: Bellowhead sing Betsy Baker
versions.lyrics:
From noise and bustle far away

As I walked over each acre

I never knew what it was to sigh

Till I saw Betsy Baker

At church I met her dressed so neat

One Sunday in hot weather

With love I found my heart did beat

As we sang psalms together

So modestly she turned her head

The while her voice did quaver

I thought if ever I did wed

‚ÄôTwould be with Betsy Baker

When church was over out she went

But I did follow after

Determined I would not be baulked

I spoke to Betsy Baker

But all my entreaties she did slight

And I was forced to leave her

I got no sleep at all that night

For love had brought a fever

At last she got acquainted with

A rambling mad play actor

He gammoned her to run away

And I lost Betsy Baker

Though I strive another way

My heart will never forsake her

I dream all night and think all day

Of cruel Betsy Baker

items.title: Clock-o‚Äô-Clay
items.notes:
Clock-o‚Äô-Clay

[

; John Clare]

Jon Boden sang John Clare‚Äôs Clock-o‚Äô-Clay

in 2024 on his Hudson album with the Remnant Kings,

Parlour Ballads .

He noted:

A poem written by ‚Äúpeasant poet‚ÄĚ and fiddler John Clare in

around 1820. It describes the existence of a solitary ladybird hiding in a

cowslip and is full of the forensic descriptive detail and deep emotional

connection with nature that marked Clare out from his more well‚Äďheeled poetic

contemporaries. I wrote the melody in 2020 as part of a choral commission
items.lyrics:
In the cowslip pips I lie,

Hidden from the buzzing fly,

While green grass beneath me lies,

Pearled with dew like fishes‚Äô eyes,

Here I lie, a clock-o‚Äô-clay,

Waiting for the time o‚Äô day.

While the forest quakes surprise,

And the wild wind sobs and sighs,

My home rocks as like to fall,

On its pillar green and tall;

When the pattering rain drives by

Clock-o‚Äô-clay keeps warm and dry.

Day by day and night by night,

All the week I hide from sight;

In the cowslip pips I lie,

In the rain still warm and dry;

Day and night and night and day,

Red, black-spotted clock-o‚Äô-clay.

My home shakes in wind and showers,

Pale green pillar topped with flowers,

Bending at the wild wind‚Äôs breath,

Till I touch the grass beneath;

Here I live, lone clock-o‚Äô-clay,

Watching for the time of day.

items.lyrics:
Down in the lone green valley where the violets fade and bloom

That‚Äôs where my sweetheart Ella lies molded in her tomb.

She died not broken hearted nor by disease she fell

But in a moment she parted from the ones she loved so well.

One night the moon was shining, the stars were shining too

And slowly to her cottage her jealous lover drew.

‚ÄúCome, love, and let√Ę¬Ä¬ôs go wander out in these woods so gay.

While wandering we will ponder and plan our wedding day.‚ÄĚ

The way grew dark before them, said she, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm afraid to roam.

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll bid fare ye well forever, to parents, friends and home.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNow, Ella, you see I have you, you have no wings to fly.

You have no hands to guide you; Ella, you must die.‚ÄĚ

Down on her knees before him, she pleaded for her life

Into her fair small bosom he plunged a dagger knife.

‚ÄúYour parents must forgive me for the deed that I have done

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go to some far country and never more return.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYes, Willy, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll forgive you; I wish we never had met.

Yes, Willy, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll forgive you,‚ÄĚ and she closed her eyes in death.

One night the moon was shining, a-coming through the mound

The angels found her body a-lying upon the ground.

He took little Ellen‚Äôs body and placed it in the grave

Way down in yonder‚Äôs valley where the weeping willows wave.

versions.title: Jon Boden sings Cruel Knife
versions.lyrics:
Down by a weeping willow, where the white violets bloom,

There lies sweet lovely Nancy so silent in her tomb.

She died not broken-hearted nor sickness her befell,

But in one moment was parted from the life she loved so well.

One night the moon shone brightly and the gentle zephyrs blew

When to her bedroom window her lover then he drew.

He said, ‚ÄúCome let us wander, in those dark woods we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll stray,

And there we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sit and ponder all upon our wedding day.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThese woods are dark and dreadful, I am afraid to stay,

Of wandering I am weary, so I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll retrace my way.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThose woods, these gentle zephyrs your feet no more will roam,

So bid farewell forever to all the things that you have known.‚ÄĚ

Down on her knees before him then she pleaded for her life

When deep into her bosom he plunged the cruel knife.

‚ÄúOh William, cruel William,‚ÄĚ it was her dying breath;

Her heart‚Äôs blood stained the leaves below and her eyes were closed in death.

(repeat first verse)

items.title: Days Gone By
items.notes:
Days Gone By

items.lyrics:
Lying on the motorway this morning

It was over in a moment

Then I closed my eyes on the motorway

I dreamed about the days gone by

Lying on the motorway this evening

The gypsies came up close while I was sleeping

So quietly down the motorway

Pulling their possessions in the cover of the night

They too had seen the whirlwind

The shining black procession

Screaming past like Gods ascending through a tarmac sky

So they hid themselves by the motorway

And dreamed about the days gone by, brave boys

They dreamed about the days gone by

And what were they doing these people from the government?

Burning sacrificial gasoline to keep the dogs at bay?

There are shadows in the hedgerows

And strange lights from the slip roads

So we huddle on the motorway, brave boys

We huddle on the motorway

items.title: Innocent When You Dream
items.notes:
Innocent When You Dream

[Tom Waits]

John Spiers and Jon Boden recorded Tom Waits‚Äô song

Innocent When You Dream in 2005 for their Fellside CD

Songs .

They commented in their sleeve notes:

Written by Tom Waits for the musical-play

Frank‚Äôs Wild Years

although we prefer to think of it as a great Victorian parlour-ballad

that got mislaid.

This video shows them at Wallingford BunkFest 2005:

Jon Boden sang Innocent When You Dream
items.lyrics:
Sorry, I can't show the lyrics here as I don't have the copyright
owners' permission to publish them.
But please feel free and ask me to send you the song's lyrics.

items.title: It Was a Lover and His Lass
items.notes:
It Was a Lover and His Lass

[words William Shakespeare]

It Was a Lover and His Lass

is a song that the second page sang in Act 5, Scene 3 of

William Shakespeare‚Äôs play As You Like It .

The Aire Valley Singers sang It Was a Lover and His Lass

in 1979 on their Hill & Dale album

Out of the Aire .

Jon Boden sang it with an extended first line as

There Once Was a Lover and He Loved a Lass

to his one tune as the 3 April 2011 entry of his project

A Folk Song a Day .
items.lyrics:
It was a lover and his lass,

With a hey and a ho and a hey nonino,

That o‚Äôer the green corn-field did pass,

In spring-time, the only pretty ring-time,

When birds do sing, hey ding a ding, ding,

Sweet lovers love the spring.

Between the acres of the rye,

With a hey and a ho and a hey nonino,

These pretty country-folks would lie,

In spring-time, the only pretty ring-time,

When birds do sing, hey ding a ding, ding,

Sweet lovers love the spring.

This carol they began that hour,

With a hey and a ho and a hey nonino,

How that a life was but a flower,

In spring-time, the only pretty ring-time,

When birds do sing, hey ding a ding, ding,

Sweet lovers love the spring.

And therefore take the present time,

With a hey and a ho and a hey nonino,

For love is crowned with the prime

In spring-time, the only pretty ring-time,

When birds do sing, hey ding a ding, ding,

Sweet lovers love the spring.

items.title: Roll the Woodpile Down
items.notes:
Roll the Woodpile Down

[

The pumping or capstan shanty Roll the Woodpile Down

items.lyrics:
Way down south where the cocks do crow

‚ÄôWay down in Florida

The gals all dance to the old banjo

And we‚Äôll roll the woodpile down!

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Rollin‚Äô! Rollin‚Äô! Rollin‚Äô the whole world ‚Äôround

That brown girl o‚Äô mine‚Äôs down the Georgia Line

And we‚Äôll roll the woodpile down!

When I was a young man in me prime

I‚Äôd take them yaller gals two at a time.

We‚Äôll roll him high and we‚Äôll roll him low

We‚Äôll heave him up and away we‚Äôll go.

Oh rouse and bust ‚Äôer is the cry

A black man‚Äôs wage is never high.

O Curly goes on the old ran-tan

O Curly‚Äôs just a down-east man.

O one more heave and that will do

We‚Äôre the bullies for to kick ‚Äôer through.

versions.title: The Spectre Review
versions.lyrics:
From out of his grave the drummer,

When midnight‚Äôs chime hath tolled

Rises, and wanders nightly,

The drum within his hold.

With armbones white and fleshless

He moves the drumsticks two

Plays many a wild reveill√©

And many a weird tattoo.

And through the dark, loud calling,

The drum-taps beat and shake;

The dead forgotten soldiers

From out of their graves awake.

Those buried in the Northland,

Stark beneath ice and snow

And those whose bones are sweltering

Italia‚Äôs earth below.

Those whom the Nile stream covers

And the Arabian sand,‚ÄĒ

All from their graves are rising,

With weapons in their hands

Then from his grave the trumpeter

At midnight rises slow,

And ever at the midnight

The ghostly trumpets blow.

Next come, on prancing horses,

The brave dead cavalry.

The bloody shot-pierced squadrons

All weaponed diversely.

Skulls grin beneath the shadow

Each dinted helm affords;

Hands that are dry and fleshless

Brandish long, rusty swords.

And last, his grave forsaking

When chimes of midnight sound,

Comes the General, slowly riding,

With his phantom staff around.

Cocked and small the hat he weareth,

And his coat is grey and wide,

And he bears a small-sword hanging

In the sheath at his left side.

The moon with yellow glances

O‚Äôer the wide plain doth shine;

The General watches mutely,

the troops they form in line.

The ranks present and shoulder

Their arms right soldierly,

With regimental music

the army marches by.

The marshals and the generals

Gather around him near;

A word the leader whispers

Within his neighbour‚Äôs ear.

‚ÄúThe Word‚ÄĚ goes round the circle,

Resounds o‚Äôer all the plain:

‚ÄúLa France‚ÄĚ the ringing password,

The answer ‚ÄúSt H√É¬©l√É¬®ne!‚ÄĚ.

Thus at the hour of midnight,

In the Champs √Člys√©es,

The long-dead Caesar holdeth

his weird review, men say.

versions.title: Bellowhead sing Spectre Review
versions.lyrics:

Rises and wanders nightly, the drum within his hold

With arm bones white and fleshless he moves the drumsticks two

Plays many a wild reveille and many a weird tattoo

And through the dark loud calling, the drum-taps beat and shake

And the dead forgotten soldiers from out of their graves awake

Those buried in the northlands under the ice and snow

And those whose bones are swelt‚Äôring Italia‚Äôs earth below

And those who the Nile-stream cover, and the Arabian sands

All from their graves are rising with weapons in their hands

Then from his grave the trumpeter at midnight rises slow

And ever at the midnight the ghostly trumpets blow

Next come the prancing horses, the brave dead cavalry

The bloody shot-pierced squadrons all weaponed diversely

Skulls grin beneath the shadows each dinted helm affords

Arms white and fleshless brandish long and rusty swords

And last his grave forsaking when chimes of midnight sound

Comes the general riding with his phantom staff around

Small and cocked the hat he wears and his coat is grey and wide

And he bears a short sword hanging in the sheath at his left side

The moon with yellow glances over the wide plain shines

The general watches mutely; the troops they form in line

The ranks present and shoulder their arms right soldierly

And with regimental music the army marches by

The marshals and the general gather around him near

A word the general whispers into Caesar‚Äôs ear

The word goes round the circle, resounding over the plain

‚ÄúLa France!‚ÄĚ the ringing password, the answer ‚ÄúSt H√É¬©l√É¬®ne!‚ÄĚ

Thus at the hour of midnight in the Champs √Člys√©es

The long-dead Caesar holds his weird review, men say

items.title: Willie and Earl Richard‚Äôs Daughter / The Birth of Robin Hood
items.notes:
[

; Child 102

; Ballad Index C102

; DT ERICDAU2

, ERICDAU3

; trad.]

James Kinsley:

The Oxford Book of Ballads

Sigrid Rieuwerts:

The Ballad Repertoire of Anna Gordon, Mrs Brown of Falkland

John Spiers and Jon Boden sang

The Birth of Robin Hood
items.lyrics:
Oh Willie‚Äôs tall, and Willie‚Äôs strong

And he is born of high degree,

And he has gone to Earl Richard

To serve obediently.

Earl Richard had one daughter dear,

The fairest to be seen,

And Willie fell in love with her

All in the garden green.

Well, the summer‚Äôs night was warm and still

And brightly shone the moon,

When Willie‚Äôs met his sweetheart

In the garden, all alone.

‚ÄúOh narrow is my gown, Willie,

That wont be so wide,

And gone is all my fair colour

That wont to be my pride.

‚ÄúBut if my father should find out

What‚Äôs passed between us two,

Before that he would eat or drink

He would hang you over that wall.

‚ÄúBut come up to my bower, Willie,

Just as the sun goes down,

And catch me in your two strong arms

And let me not fall down.‚ÄĚ

So when the sun was setting low

He has gone up to her bower,

And by the pale light of the moon

Her window she looked over.

All in that robe of red scarlet

She jumped, fearless of harm.

And Willie was tall and Willie was strong,

He caught her in his arms.

When night was done, and day was come

And the light began to creep,

Well up and rose the Earl Richard

From out of his drowsy sleep.

‚ÄúWell I dreamed a dreadful dream last night,

God grant it come to good:

I dreamed I saw my daughter dear

Drowning in the flood.‚ÄĚ

So he‚Äôs called to him his servant men

By one, by two, by three,

‚ÄúOh what√Ę¬Ä¬ôs become of my daughter dear

That she√Ę¬Ä¬ôll not come to me?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh if that she√Ę¬Ä¬ôs been stolen away

Or taken from this hall,

Well I‚Äôll make a vow and I‚Äôll keep it true:

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll hang you one and all!‚ÄĚ

So they searched east and they searched west,

And they searched up and down.

They found her in the merry green wood

Nursing her bonny young son.

Well he‚Äôs taken the baby all in his arms

And kissed him tenderly,

Saying, ‚ÄúAlthough I would your father hang

Yet your mother is dear to me.‚ÄĚ

He kissed him once, he kissed him twice:

‚ÄúMy grandson I thee claim,

And Robin Hood in the merry green wood

That shall be your name.‚ÄĚ

There‚Äôs many that sing of green, green grass

And sing of golden corn,

And there‚Äôs many that sing of Robin Hood

Know not where he was born.

Well, it wasn‚Äôt in the lofty hall

Nor in the painted bower,

But it was in the merry green wood

All among the lily-flowers.

items.title: The Mistletoe Bough
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS182

; Ballad Index R802

; Bodleian

; GlosTrad

; Wiltshire

390

; Folkinfo 516

; Mudcat 19827

;

Thomas Haynes Bayly (1797-1839)]

Bob Copper:
items.lyrics:
The mistletoe hung in the castle hall,

The holly branch hung on the old oak wall

And the baron‚Äôs retainers were blithe and gay,

And keeping their Christmas holiday.

The Baron beheld with a father‚Äôs pride,

His beautiful child, young Lovell√Ę¬Ä¬ôs bride‚Ä¶

While she with her bright eyes seemed to be

The star of the goodly company

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

‚ÄúI am weary of dancing now,‚ÄĚ she cried,

‚ÄúPray tarry a moment, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll hide, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll hide

And Lovell, be sure the first to trace

The clue to my secret flirting place.‚ÄĚ

Away she ran, and her friends began

Each bower to search and each nook to scan

And young Lovell cried, ‚ÄúOh where dost thou hide?

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm lonesome without thee, own dear bride.‚ÄĚ

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

They sought her that night, they sought her next day

They sought her in vain when a week passed away

In the highest, the lowest, the lowliest spot

Young Lovell sought widely and found her not

And years flew by and the grief at last

Was told as a sorrowful tale long past

And when Lovell appeared the children cried,

‚ÄúSee the old man weeps for his fairy bride.‚ÄĚ

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

At length an old chest that had long lain hid

Was found in the castle, they raised the lid

And the skeleton form lay mouldering there

In the bridal wreath of the lady fair.

Oh sad was her fate, and the sportive jest

She hid from her lord in the old oak chest.

It closed with a spring and the bridal bloom

Lay withering there in a living tomb.

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

versions.title: Gordon Syrett sings The Mistletoe Bough
versions.lyrics:
The mistletoe hung in the castle hall,

The holly branch hung on the old oak wall.

The Baron‚Äôs retainers were blithe and gay,

Keeping their Christmas holiday.

The Baron beheld with a father‚Äôs pride,

His beautiful daughter, young Lovel‚Äôs bride.

While she with her bright eyes seemed to be,

The star of the goodly company.

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm tired of dancing now,‚ÄĚ she cried,

‚ÄúHere, tarry one moment, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll hide, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll hide,

And Lovel be sure thou art first to trace

A clue to my secret hiding place.‚ÄĚ

Away she ran and her friends all began,

Each nook to search and each nook to scan,

And young Lovel cried, ‚ÄúOh where dost thou hide?

For I am lonely without thee, my own dear bride.‚ÄĚ

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

They sought her that night and they sought her next day,

They sought her in vain till the weeks passed away,

And years flew by, and the tale at last,

Was told as a sorrowing tale of the past,

And when Lovel appeared the children cried,

‚ÄúSee the old man weeps for his own dear bride.‚ÄĚ

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

Oh! the mistletoe bough!

At length an oak chest that had long lain hid

Was found in the castle, they raised the lid,

And a skeleton form lay mouldering there,

In the bridled wreath of a lady fair.

Oh! sad was her fate, for in sportive jest

She hid from her Lord, in an old oak chest,

It had closed with a spring, and a dreadful doom,

For the bride she laid clasped in a living tomb.

items.title: The Rain It Rains
items.notes:
The Rain It Rains

[words William Shakespeare, music Jon Boden]

John Spiers and Jon Boden took William Shakespeare‚Äôs verses and sang them as

The Rain It Rains

in 2008 on their Navigator CD

Vagabond .

They noted:

Sung by Feste, the itinerant Jester in Twelfth Night ,

after the other characters have gone inside to enjoy their happy ending

leaving Feste outside the gate to finish the play off by himself.

This video shows them at Biddulph Town Hall in summer 2007:

Jon Boden also sang The Rain It Rains as the 20 July 2010 entry
items.lyrics:
When that I was a little wee boy

Sing hey ho the wind and rain

Life was a song and love a toy

The rain it rains most every day

But when that I came to man‚Äôs estate

Sing hey ho the wind and rain

Against thieves and knaves men shut their gates

The rain it rains most every day

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Hey ho the wind and rain

The rain it rains most every day

Ten pretty girls all in one row

Will toss their petticoats to and fro

Ten brave boys on the courtyard wall

The bigger they come the harder they fall

The wind blows high and the wind blows low

And turns a friend into a foe

A long time ago the world was begun

And since that day this song‚Äôs been sung

items.title: Widow‚Äôs Curse
items.notes:
Widow‚Äôs Curse

[

; Bodleian

; Mudcat 135918

;

trad. / Pete Flood arr. Pete Flood]

Bellowhead sang

Widow‚Äôs Curse

in 2008 on their Navigator CD

Matachin .

They noted:

The tune is Pete‚Äôs. The text is based on a broadside
items.lyrics:
In London dwelt a merchant man who left unto his son

A thousand pounds in land each year to spend when he was gone.

No sooner was his father dead and buried in his grave

Than this his wild and wanton son his mind to lewdness gave.

Within the seas of fleshly love his heart was drowned so deep,

At night he could not quietly without strange women sleep.

So he kept women secretly to feed his heart‚Äôs desire

And he dressed them all as gallant boys in pages‚Äô trim attire.

But then one day upon his round he spied a lovely dame

Who was a widow‚Äôs daughter dear of good report and fame.

Her beauty like the purple rose so glittered in his eye,

That ravished by the same he sought her secret company.

Then like a lustful lecher he found such convenient time

That he enforced her to drink till she was drunk with wine.

And being over-charged with wine, a maiden‚Äôs head is weak,

He ravished her and when that she could no reluctance make.

And mark I pray what then befell unto this modest dame

When she recovered her lost sense and found of her defame.

Her womb began to swell; in time the babe received life

Though she was neither widow nor maid nor yet a married wife.

Said she, ‚ÄúThe babe within my womb shall never yet be born

Not called a bastard by such wives who hold my fate in scorn.

‚ÄúFor I a strumpet in disgrace though one against my will

Before that I would shame my friends my own life‚Äôs blood I‚Äôll spill.

And as with wine I was deceived and made a vicious dame

So I will wash away with wine my scarlet spots of shame.‚ÄĚ

Then drinking down hot burning wine she yielded up her breath

By which the same the unborn babe was scalded unto death.

Upon her knees her mother fell; to heaven did cry and call,

‚ÄúIf ever widow√Ę¬Ä¬ôs curse,‚ÄĚ quoth she, ‚Äúon mortal man did fall,

Then say Amen to mine, oh Lord, that he may never thrive

Who was the cause of this sad fate but rot away alive.‚ÄĚ

His nails from out his fingers fell, his eyes from out his head,

His toes they rotted from his feet before that he was dead.

His tongue that had false sworn so oft to compass his desire

Within his mouth did swell and burn like coals of sparkling fire.

And thus in torment for his sins the wicked villain died

Whose hateful carcass after death could not in earth abide.

But in the maws of carrion crows the ravens made their tomb

And then in Hell he screamed and writhed in everlasting doom!

items.title: Blood, Ice and Ashes
items.notes:
Blood, Ice and Ashes

[Kim Edgar, Karine Polwart, Kenny Anderson]

Kim Edgar and Karine Polwart were fed up with Scottish murder ballads

where women only had a passive role as cause or victim of the murder.

They wanted a woman to get the chance to be the murderer and thus wrote

Blood, Ice and Ashes .

Karine Polwart sang Blood, Ice and Ashes

in 2010 on the Burns Unit album

Side Show

and in 2011 on her DVD

Here‚Äôs Where Tomorrow Starts .

Kim Edgar recorded Blood, Ice and Ashes
items.lyrics:
The night she went looking, there was blood, ice and ashes

A shivering ember that cut up the dark

She had flint in her fingers, eyes sparking with ire

And splinters of other cold nights in her heart

Just a little white rose, a glint in the snow, ice in the bone and she said:

I‚Äôll be right behind you, in the shards of the mirror, I‚Äôll be there, I‚Äôll be there

Just over your shoulder, you won‚Äôt see me coming, I‚Äôll be there, I‚Äôll be there

Well she sought him in shadows and in whispers and ditches

She could still taste the bolt of his breath on the wind

Through barbed wire and bracken and broken illusions

She could still taste the salt in the cracks of her skin

Just a little black rose, a thorn in the snow, ash in her veins and she said:

I‚Äôll be right behind you, in the shards of the mirror, I‚Äôll be there, I‚Äôll be there

I‚Äôll follow, I‚Äôll find you, you won‚Äôt see me coming, I‚Äôll be there, I‚Äôll be there

When she finally found him asleep in the garden

She flickered just once, and then banished all doubt

She clutched all of her promise and hope in her pocket

Struck steel upon stone, and just let it all out

Just a little red rose, a stain on the snow, blood on her hands and she said:

I‚Äôll be right behind you, in the shards of the mirror, I‚Äôll be there, I‚Äôll be there

I‚Äôll follow, I‚Äôll find you, you won‚Äôt see me coming, I‚Äôll be there, I‚Äôll be there

She let it all out

items.title: Midlothian Mining Song
items.notes:
Midlothian Mining Song

[

; Mudcat 165796

; trad.]

Rod Paterson sang the Midlothian Mining Song

in 1984 on The Easy Club‚Äôs eponymous first album

The Easy Club .

Aaron Jones sang the Midlothian Mining Song

in 2009 on the 2Duos (now Litha) CD

Until the Cows Come Home .

They noted:

Scotland‚Äôs once thriving mining industry employed tens of thousands of
items.lyrics:
Come all Midlothian mining men and listen unto me

You know how we are so oppressed by others‚Äô tyranny

For improvement of the mines no leisure time is found

Our children go neglected while we‚Äôre working underground

Chorus:

From Newtongrange and Arniston, from Polton to Gorebrig

From Birkenside to Lasswade, Cockpen and Bonnyrigg

From Rosewell to Newbattle the Lothian men all say

Were no the likes of other men that work eight hours a day

Our masters are tyrannical that we must confess

For they over tax their workmen and do them so repress

No other occupation so dangerous is found

We cannot call our lives our own when working underground

(chorus)

The sailor he does plough the main and perils does go through

But he sees the danger coming which a miner cannot do

With fallen roofs and fire damp the records can be found

How hundreds yearly lost their lives while working underground

(repeat first verse and chorus)

items.title: Owensboro
items.notes:
Owensboro

[trad.]

Natalie Merchant sang Owensboro

on her 2003 album

The House Carpenter‚Äôs Daughter .

Cara sang Owensboro

in 2010 on their CD

Long Distance Love .

They noted:

This is a lovely traditional song from America about people who worked in

the cotton mills and the conditions they faced every day. Jeana [Leslie] was

given the song by Gavin Firth, a great friend and fantastic guitar player from
items.lyrics:
Well, I lived in a town

Way down south

By the name of Owensboro

And I worked in a mill

With the rest of the ‚Äútrash‚ÄĚ

As we‚Äôre often called

As you know

Well, we rise up early

In the morning

And we work all day real hard

To buy our little meat and bread

Buy sugar, tea, and lard

Well, our children they

Grow, grow up unlearned

With no time to go to school

Almost before they learn to walk

They learn to spin and to spool

Well, the folks in town

They dress so fine

And spend their money free

But they would hardly look

At a factory hand

That dresses like you and me

Would you let them wear

Their watches fine

Let them wear their gems

And pearly strings

But when that day

Of judgement comes

They‚Äôll have to share

Their pretty things

items.title: Sailor Boy / What Does the Deep Sea Say?
items.notes:
[

; Ballad Index DTdeepse

; Mudcat 30063

; trad.]

Woody Guthrie and Cisco Houston recorded

What Did the Deep Sea Say?

in c.1944. It was released on his 1962 Folkways album

Woody Guthrie Sings Folk Songs .

Cara recorded this song as

Sailor Boy

in 2004 for their first CD,

In Colour .
items.lyrics:
Oh captain, tell me true,

Does my sailor sail with you?

No, he does not sail with me,

He sleeps on the bottom of the sea.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

What did the deep sea say?

Tell me, what did the deep sea say?

It moaned and it groaned and it splashed and it foamed

And it rolled on its weary way.

Well he promised he‚Äôd write to me,

But his promise be never kept true

Never a word from my sailor have I heard

Since he sailed on that ocean blue.

A beautiful rose every day,

I placed on the crest of the waves

Said, ‚ÄúTake it please, and let the petals fall,

Above his watery grave.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Cara sing Sailor Boy
versions.lyrics:
Oh, where is my sailor boy?

Where is my sailor boy?

He sleeps at the bottom of the deep blue sea

And he can‚Äôt come back to me.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Oh, what does the deep sea say?

What does the deep sea say?

It moans, it groans, it splashes and foams

And rolls on its weary way.

Oh please tell me, deep blue sea,

Is he sleeping peacefully?

The winds from the north are blowing icy cold,

Please keep him warm for me.

If only my dreaming soul

Some token, a sign could find,

If only the waves would show me where he sleeps

I would leave this world behind.

A beautiful rose, one day,

I placed on the crest of a wave,

I said, ‚ÄúTake it please, and let it settle home

Above his watery grave.‚ÄĚ

Final Chorus:

Oh, what does the deep sea say?

What does the deep sea say?

It guards and holds two lovers in sleep

As it rolls on its weary way.

items.title: The Bonnie Lad
items.notes:
The Bonnie Lad

[

; G/D 6:1112

; words Robert Burns, music Kim Edgar]

Kim Edgar sang Robert Burns‚Äô poem The Bonnie Lad

in 2008 on her CD

Butterflies and Broken Glass .

She also recorded it in 2013 with Cara for their CD

Horizon .

The latter album‚Äôs sleeve notes commented:

As a part of the songwriting collective

‚Äú The Burns Unit ‚ÄĚ
items.lyrics:
O how can I be blythe and glad,

O how can I gang brisk and braw,

When the bonnie lad that I love best,

Is o‚Äôer the hills and far awa?

It‚Äôs no the frosty winter wind,

It‚Äôs no the driving drift and snaw,

But ay the tear comes in my e‚Äôe,

To think on him that‚Äôs far awa.

My father pat me frae his door,

My friends they hae disown‚Äôd me a‚Äô;

But there is ane will take my part,

The bonnie lad that‚Äôs far awa.

A pair o gloves he brought to me,

And silken snoods he gaed me twa,

And I will wear them for his sake,

The bonnie lad that‚Äôs far awa.

O weary winter soon will pass,

And spring will cleed the birken shaw:

And my young babbie will be born,

And he‚Äôll be hame that‚Äôs far awa.

Norman Buchan and Peter Hall:
items.lyrics:
In Perth there live‚Äôd a bonny lad,

A brewer to his trade, O;

And he has courted Peggy Roy,

A young and handsome maid, O.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

With a fol dol diddle, &c.

He courted her for seven long years,

All for to gain her favour;

But there came a lad out of Edinburgh town,

Who swore that he would have her.

‚ÄúO, wilt thou go along with me,

O, wilt thou go, my honey?

And wilt thou go along with me,

And leave your own dear Johnnie?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO yes, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go along with you,

And along with you I‚Äôll ride, O;

I‚Äôll range with you the wide world o‚Äôer,

Tho√Ę¬Ä¬ô I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm to be the brewer√Ę¬Ä¬ôs bride, O.‚ÄĚ

The brewer he came home at e‚Äôen,

Inquiring for his honey;

Her father he made this reply,

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôve never seen her since Monday.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBe it not, or be it so,

Little does it grieve me;

I‚Äôm a young man free, as you can see,

And a small thing will relieve me.

‚ÄúThere is as good fish in the sea

As ever yet were taken,

I‚Äôll cast my net once o‚Äôer again,

Altho√Ę¬Ä¬ô I am forsaken.‚ÄĚ

She‚Äôs rambled up, she‚Äôs rambled down,

She‚Äôs rambled through Kirkaldy,

And many‚Äôs the day she‚Äôs rued the day

She forsook her brewer laddie.

The brewer he set up in Perth,

And often brew strong ale, O;

And he has courted a bonny lass,

And taken her to his sel‚Äô, O.

Ye lovers all, where‚Äôer ye be,

By me now take a warning;

And never slight your ain true love,

For fear ye get a waur ane.

items.title: The Whitby Maid
items.notes:
[

; DT WHITMAID

; Mudcat 96835

; John Leonard]

The earliest recording of John Leonard‚Äôs Whity Maid

that I know of is on his and John Squire‚Äôs 1976 Rubber Records album

Broken-Down Gentlemen .

Curiously enough, all albums listed below claim it is a traditional song.

Leonard and Squire noted:

It‚Äôs nice to lose the head once in a while and on this track we did.

Fred [Hopwood] and Graham [Jones] are really working overtime.

Cheer, lads!
items.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of a maid in Whitby town, she was both fair and clever,

She would sit by her father‚Äôs door no matter what the weather.

A sailor coming home from sea, pockets overflowing,

He saw the maiden sitting there quietly with her sewing.

‚ÄúOh, won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you come along with me, my bonny, oh my honey?

And we will go down to Whitby town and spend a little money.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúFather he would not agree, would be against his wishing.‚ÄĚ

With a twinkle in her eye she said that he‚Äôs gone fishing.

Chorus (repeated after every other verse):

Blow away you northern winds, blow away so cruelly,

Blow away you northern winds.

But mot so cruel as a pretty maid, they deceive you surely,

Blow away you northern winds.

This couple‚Äôs gone to Whitby town and soon were making merry

In every tavern in the town they spent a little money.

The night came down, the stars came out, the lady said, ‚ÄúMy sailor,

Oh won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you come back home with me? I feel I must repay you.‚ÄĚ

They went home and went upstairs, the maid turned down the covers,

Saying, ‚ÄúCome to bed, my sailor boy, let√Ę¬Ä¬ôs you and I be lovers.‚ÄĚ

The sailor jumped out of his clothes, no quicker than he ought to.

When the door broke down and a man came in, saying, ‚ÄúWho√Ę¬Ä¬ôs that with my daughter?‚ÄĚ

Sailor through the window leapt and to his ship‚Äôs gone running,

He‚Äôs left behind his clothes, his watch, and the best part of his money.

Father with the daughter went down to the kitchen table,

They shared the sailor‚Äôs money out as quick as they were able.

Father‚Äôs gone to buy new boots and a new suit from the tailor‚Äôs,

Daughter to the door has gone to watch and wait for sailors.

items.title: The Widow‚Äôs Promise
items.notes:
The Widow‚Äôs Promise

[

; Mudcat 33836

; Mick Ryan]

Mick Ryan recorded his own song

The Widow (later to be renamed to The Widow‚Äôs Promise )

with Crows for their 1986 album

No Bones or Grease .

He also recorded it with Pete Harris for a cassette on the WildGoose label.

This recording was included in 1996 as the title track of their TerraNova

compilation

The Widow‚Äôs Promise .
items.lyrics:
High upon a lonely moor a widow lived alone;

An inn she kept, and as she slept, the pillow heard her moan:

‚ÄúFor many√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the time a traveller has spent the night with me,

But there‚Äôs not a man in all creation gives content to me.

‚ÄúFor some can manage once or twice, some make three or four,

What seems to me a rarity is the man who can do more.

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd give anything to find him, in heaven or in hell!‚ÄĚ

And as these words were spoken, then she heard the front door bell.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

And the wind blew cold and lonely across the widow‚Äôs moor

And she never ever turned away a traveller from her door

The widow boldly ran down the stairs, the door she opened wide,

And as she did a tall and handsome stranger stepped inside.

She gave him bread and brandy, and when that he was fed,

He said, ‚ÄúMy dear, now have no fear, it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs time to go to bed.

‚ÄúFor I heard your call way down below and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve come to see you right.

But you must come to hell with me if I can last the night.‚ÄĚ

She said, ‚ÄúYou randy devil, to this bargain I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm agreed,

For hell on earth, or hell in hell, it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs all the same to me.‚ÄĚ

Then they both fell into bed and the devil was working well,

He thought before the night was through that she‚Äôd be his in hell.

But when they came to number nine the widow cried, ‚ÄúEncore!‚ÄĚ

And when the twelfth time came around again she simply cried for more.

At twenty-five the devil felt compelled to take a rest,

The widow she said, ‚ÄúCome raise your head and put me to the test.‚ÄĚ

At sixty-nine the widow laughed, ‚ÄúAgain! Again!‚ÄĚ she cried.

The devil he declared, ‚ÄúNow I can see just how your husband died.‚ÄĚ

At ninety-nine the devil he began to cry and weep,

He said, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll give you anything but let me go to sleep.‚ÄĚ

Before the morning light was up the devil hobbled home,

And the widow, still unsatisfied, once more was left alone.

She lay there and grumbled as she thought of ninety-nine,

‚ÄúIf only that old devil could have made it one more time!

I‚Äôll call him up again tonight and see what can be done,

With a bit more application, he could make it to the ton.‚ÄĚ

But when she called to him that night, no devil did appear,

For the first time in eternity the devil he shook with fear.

‚ÄúOf all the pain and torment I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve witnessed here in hell,

I never knew what pain was √Ę¬Ä¬ôtill I rang her front door bell.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Bodmin Wassail
items.notes:
Bodmin Wassail

[

; trad.]

Dave Bland recorded a group our wassailers outside a house

in Bodmin, Cornwall, on 6 January 1973. This recording was included in

1998 on the Topic anthology of songs and dance tunes of seasonal events,

You Lazy Lot of Bone-Shakers

(The Voice on the People Volume 16).

Coope Boyes & Simpson, Fi Fraser, Jo Freya and Georgina Boyes

sang the Bodmin Wassail

in 2006 on their CD

Voices at the Door .
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Oh/For singing wassail, wassail, wassail,

And jolly come to our jolly wassail

I wish you a merry Christmas and a happy New Year,

Pockets of money and a cellar of beer.

Here comes the ship out in full sail -

Ploughs the wide ocean in many a gale.

Sometimes it‚Äôs laurel; sometimes it‚Äôs bay.

Come fill up our bowl-dish and we‚Äôll drink away.

If you got an apple, I hope you got ten

To make some sweet cider gainst we comes again.

If missus and master is sitting at ease,

Put your hand in your pocket and give what you please.

Come knock at the knocker and ring at the bell,

I know you‚Äôll reward us for singing wassail.

; Ballad Index R627

I found this gospel in Vance Rudolph‚Äôs

items.lyrics:
Fading away like the stars in the morning,

Losing their light in the glorious sun‚ÄĒ

Thus would we pass from the earth and its toiling,

Only remembered for what we have done.

Only the truth that in life we have spoken,

Only the seed that in life we have sown:

These shall pass onwards when we are forgotten,

Only remembered for what we have done.

Who‚Äôll sing the anthem and who‚Äôll tell the story?

Will the line hold? Will it scatter and run?

Shall we at last be united in glory?

Only remembered for what we have done.

items.title: The Ploughboy‚Äôs Dream
items.notes:
The Ploughboy‚Äôs Dream

[

/ Song Subject MAS960

; Master title: The Ploughboy‚Äôs Dream

; G/D 3:4891

; Ballad Index GrD3491

; VWML RVW2/4/19

, GG/1/12/748

; Bodleian

; Mudcat 90743

; trad.]

Gryphon sang The Ploughboy‚Äôs Dream
items.lyrics:
I dreamt I drove my master‚Äôs team with Dobbin, Bald and Star,

Before a stiff and handy plough, as all my master‚Äôs are.

I found the ground was baked so hard ‚Äôtwas more like bricks than clay;

I could not cut my furrow through, nor would my beasts obey.

Now Dobbin lay down, both Bald and Star they kicked and snorted sore;

The more I lashed and cursed and swore, the less my cattle stir.

Then lo! above me a bright youth did seem to hang in air,

With purple wings and golden hands as angels painted are.

‚ÄúGive over, cruel wretch,‚ÄĚ he cried, ‚Äúnor thus thy beasts abuse

Think, if the ground was not as hard, would they their work refuse?

Besides, I heard thee curse and swear, as if dumb beasts could know,

What all thy oaths and curses meant, or better for them go.

But though they know not there is one who knows thy sins full well,

And what shall be thine after doom another shall thee tell.‚ÄĚ

No more he said, but, light as air, he vanished from my sight;

And with him went the sun‚Äôs bright beams, and all was dark as night.

The thunder roared from underground, the earth did seem to gape;

Blue flames broke forth, and in those flames, a dire, gigantic shape.

‚ÄúSoon shall I call thee mine,‚ÄĚ it cried, with voice so drear and deep,

That, quivering like an aspen leaf, I wakened from my sleep.

versions.title: Hannah James sings Ploughboy‚Äôs Dream
versions.lyrics:
I am a ploughboy stout and tall as ever drove a team,

And three years since as I lay in bed I had a dreadful dream.

I dreamt I drove my master‚Äôs team, both Dobbin, Belle and Star,

Before a stiff and armoured plough as all my master‚Äôs are.

I found the ground was baked so hard, it was more like bricks than clay;

I could not cut my furrow through, nor would my beasts obey.

The more I whipped them, slashed and swore, the less the horses tried;

Dobbin lay down, and Belle and Star ignored my threats and cries.

Till lo! above me appeared a youth, he seemed to hang in air,

And all around a dazzling light which made my eyes to stare,

‚ÄúGive over, cruel wretch,‚ÄĚ he cried, ‚ÄúDo not thy beasts abuse

For, if the ground was not so hard, would they their work refuse?

‚ÄúBesides, I heard you curse and swear, as if dumb beasts could know

What all thy oaths and cursing meant; it‚Äôs better far than gold

That you should know that there is one who knows thy sins full well,

And what shall be thy after doom another shall thee tell.‚ÄĚ

No more he said, but, light as air, he vanished from my sight,

And with him went the sun‚Äôs bright beams, ‚Äôtwas all as dark as night.

Then thunder roared from underground, the earth it seems agape;

Blue flames broke forth and in those flames appeared an awful shape.

‚ÄúI soon shall call you mine,‚ÄĚ he cried, in a voice so clear and deep,

And, quivering like an aspen leaf, I woke out of my sleep.

So ponder well, you ploughboys all, this dream that I have told

And if your work goes well with you, it‚Äôs worth your weight in gold.

items.title: Wexford Carol
items.notes:
Wexford Carol

[

; Mudcat 270

; anon.]

Andrew Cronshaw played the Irish Wexford Carol

in 1982 on his album

The Great Dark Water .

The Voice Squad sang Good People All

on their 1992 album

Holly Wood .

Frank Harte noted:

Otherwise known as the Enniscorthy Christmas Carol , this is one
items.lyrics:
Good people all, this Christmas time

Consider well and bear in mind

What our good God for us has done

In sending His beloved Son.

With thankful heart we should pray

To God with love this Christmas day

In Bethlehem upon that morn

There was a blessed Messiah born!

The night before that happy tide

The noble virgin and her guide

Were long time seeking up and down

To find a lodging in the town.

But mark how all things came to pass

From every door repelled, alas!

As long foretold, their refuge all

Was but an humble oxen‚Äôs stall.

There were three wise men from afar

Directed by a glorious star

And on they wandered night and day

Until they came where Jesus lay.

And when they came unto that place

Where our beloved Messiah was

They humbly cast them at His feet

With gifts of gold and incense sweet.

Near Bethlehem did shepherds keep

Their flocks of lambs and feeding sheep

To whom God‚Äôs angels did appear

Which put the shepherds in great fear.

‚ÄúPrepare and go‚ÄĚ, the angels said

‚ÄúTo Bethlehem, be not afraid

For there you‚Äôll find, this happy morn

A princely babe, sweet Jesus born‚ÄĚ.

With happy heart and joyful mind

The shepherds went the babe to find

And as God‚Äôs angel had foretold

They did our Saviour Christ behold.

Within a manger He was laid

And by His side the virgin maid

Attending on the Lord of life

Who came on earth to end all strife.

Here‚Äôs adieu sweet lovely Nancy, ten thousand times adieu,

items.lyrics:

What a sweet and pleasant gale when Benbow he set sail

And the enemy they turned tail in a fright, in a fright.

Great Reuben and Benbow fought the French, fought the French.

See the boats go up and down and the bullets whizzing round

And the enemy they knocked down, there they lay, there they lay.

Oh, Benbow lost his legs, by chain shot, by chain shot.

Down on his stumps did fall and so loud for mercy called,

‚ÄúOh, fight on my British tars, it is my lot, it is my lot.‚ÄĚ

When the doctor dressed his wounds Benbow cried, Benbow cried,

‚ÄúOh, pray pick me up in haste to the quarter deck my place

That the enemy I might face until I die, until I die.‚ÄĚ

Last Tuesday morning last, Benbow died, Benbow died.

What a shocking sight to see when they carried him away;

Oh, they carried him to Se‚Äôm‚Äôs‚Äôon church, there he lays, there he lays.

items.title: Babes in the Wood / Lost in a Wood
items.notes:

items.lyrics:
O don‚Äôt you remember a long time ago

Two poor little babes, their names I don‚Äôt know

They were stolen away, one bright summer day

Lost in the woods, I‚Äôve heard people say

And when it was night (so sad was their plight?)

The moon settled down and the stars gave no light

They sobbed and they sighed and they bitterly cried

These poor little babes just laid down and died

And when they were dead, the robins so red

Brought strawberry leaves and over them spread

And sung them a song, through all the day long

These poor little babes have never done wrong

(repeat last verse)

versions.title: The Copper Family sing Babes in the Wood
versions.lyrics:

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Pretty babes in the wood, pretty babes in the wood,

O, don‚Äôt you remember those babes in the wood?

Now the day being done and the night coming on

Those two little babies they lay down and died.

Now the robins so red how swiftly they sped,

They put out their wide wings and over them spread.

And all the day long in the branches they throng,

They sweetly did whistle and this was their song.

Copper Family:

items.lyrics:
You Gentlemen of high renown, come listen unto me

That take delight in fox and hounds in ev‚Äôry high degree.

A story true to you I‚Äôll tell concerning of a fox,

In Oxford Town in Oxfordshire there lived some mighty hounds.

Bold Reynard being all in his den and standing on the ground,

Bold Reynard being all in his den and hearing of those hounds.

I think I hear some joyful hounds thinking for me to kill,

But before they catch me by my brush I‚Äôll climb those mighty hills.

Bold Reynard cocked up his head and up the hill he went,

Bold Reynard cocked out his brush and he left a gallant scent.

Your hounds are staunch I know them well, they drive me like the wind,

I will step so lightly on the ground I‚Äôll leave no scent behind.

We drove Bold Reynard five hours or more without a check of speed,

We drove Bold Reynard five hours or more till we came to Oxford Green.

There we caught Bold Reynard all by his brush never to let him go,

He has had so many of our feather-ed fowls down in the valley below.

Our Huntsman blows his joyful sound, Relope, my boys, fulfil

He will have no more of our feather-ed fowls nor lambs on yonder hill.

Oh, pardon, Huntsman, then he cried. No pardon you shall have,

Take off his head likewise his brush and give him three Hurrays.

versions.lyrics:
