items.title: Charming Molly
items.notes:
>

Peter Bellamy >

Songs >

Charming Molly

Charming Molly

[

; Ballad Index CoSea242

; DT CHRMOLLY

; trad.]

The Copper Family:

The Copper Family Song Book .

Charming Molly
items.lyrics:
Charming Molly, fair, brisk and gay like nightingales in May

All round her eyelids young Cupids play,

She has eyes so bright they shine

Black as any berry, cheeks like any cherry.

Charming Molly with sparkling eyes.

See how the swain do admire and desire a pretty little girl,

To hold her hand it burns like sparkling fire,

In her face these things are seen,

Violets, roses, lilies and daffadown-dillies.

Charming Molly she is all divine.

Surely there‚Äôs no one loves a pretty woman if she be not common

Surely such beauty most men admire,

Surely there‚Äôs no one can them despise

Because they are so pretty and they talk so witty,

Charming Molly with sparkling eyes.

versions.title: Peter Bellamy sings Charming Molly
versions.lyrics:

Violets, roses, lilies, daffadown-dillies.

Charming Molly she is all divine.

Surely there‚Äôs no one loves a pretty woman if she be not common, oh.

Surely there‚Äôs no one can them despise

Surely there is no one can them despise

Because they look so pretty and they talk so witty,

Charming Molly with the sparkling eyes.

items.title: Come All You Bold Britons
items.notes:
Come All You Bold Britons

[

; Ballad Index CoSe250

; DT BOLBRIT

; trad.]

This song from the repertoire of the Copper Family is printed in

The Copper Family Song Book .

Bob and John Copper sang

Come All Bold Britons

in 1971 on their Leader box

A Song for Every Season .

The Copper Family also sang Come All Ye Bold Britons
items.lyrics:
Come all you bold Britons wherever you may dwell,

Come listen unto me while a story I will tell,

Great crops of corn our Lord does send,

Where there is one farm there might be ten

And then more cows there might be kept‚ÄĒin old England.

Come all you bold statesmen that are of high renown,

Before the times get better rates and taxes must go down,

Let every farmer hear my thrive

And to the poor some milk might give,

And that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the way we ought to live‚ÄĒin old England.

Oh, yonder shines Phoebe see how she shines so clear,

She will nobly rise and set, my boys, each day throughout the year,

And shiny nights they are so gay,

Both rents and taxes we must pay,

Or else find out some better ways‚ÄĒin old England.

Now some they do say we are made of mortal clay,

While others they do say we were born but yesterday.

But look into old Adam and Eve

And in the scriptures you will read

That we are all one flesh and blood‚ÄĒin old England.

Come all you bold heroes and God speed the plough,

I hope we shall see better times soon in old England now,

And in due time when mercy call

All for the riches great and small

That death will come and seize us all‚ÄĒin old England.

items.title: Corduroy
items.notes:
Corduroy

[

; Bodleian

; Mudcat 32261

; trad.]

Corduroy was a popular song of the mid-19 th century; presumably

it had its origins in the Music Halls.

Bob, Ron and John Copper sang

Corduroy

in 1971 on their Leader Records box set

A Song for Every Season .

Bob Copper also printed this song in 1976 in his book
items.lyrics:
My father was fond of lush and he emptied many a butt

And for to get a suit of togs we had to stinge our guts.

Ofttimes my mother and I dined off a saveloy

To save up the browns to buy me a suit of corduroy.

Chorus (after each verse):

Right tiddy fol lol, fol lol, fol lol

Right tiddy fol lol, fol lay

Right tiddy fol lol, fol lol, fol lol

Right tiddy fol lol, fol lay

When father came home at night and drunk we used him rob

And after the course of a week or two we saved up seven bob.

One day my mother did say, come along with me my boy

We will go to Moses and Sons for a suit of corduroy.

We soon picked out a suit the best that we could find

They fitted me very well in front but rather loose behind,

That‚Äôs no fault said the man for he‚Äôs a growing boy

And I‚Äôll warrant that his behind it will grow to the corduroy.

We quickly fitted them on and my mother she paid the blunt

There was two pockets at the side and a little hole in the front.

As we were walking along all the people they did cry

How very nice he looks in his suit of corduroy.

My mother she used to scold because I had so many faults

And feeling rather queer one day she gave me a dose of salts.

Soon after I was taken short and the buttons did so annoy

And before I could get my trouser down I [raspberry] my corduroy.

When I was out at play one day my pal says to me Jim

Let‚Äôs go to Battersea park and there we‚Äôll have a swim.

As we were swimming about me and this other boy

Some covey hooked it off with my suit of corduroy.

Now as naked as I was born I was taken home in a cart

And when my mother heard the news it nearly broke her heart

She said, you‚Äôve cooked my goose, you very naughty boy,

I shall never forget the day when you lost your corduroy.

versions.title: Charlie Wills sings Corduroy
versions.lyrics:
Most folks when they grow up
at times they do presume

In things that have gone by
it please them to amuse;

How I remember well,
I never felt such joy

Ever since the day when I got birched
in my suit of corduroy

Chorus (after each verse):

Then around me, young and old, around

If ever you wish to join me

If ever you wish to buy a suit

You buy the corduroy.

When father come home at night
if drunk we used to rob

And in the course of a week or so
we had up seven bob

My mother said unto me,
Jack, come with me my boy;

Let‚Äôs go to Groves‚Äôs shop
and buy a suit of corduroy

We soon picked out the suit
the best that we could find;

It fitted very well in front
but hellish slack behind.

Ah, never mind, the shopman said,
he is a growing boy

I warrant his little arse
will soon fill out the corduroy

I felt quite proud
my mother she paid the brunt

It had two pockets by the side
and a little hole in front.

As I went marching down the street
the people they did say,

How very well he looks
in his suit of corduroy!

One day I was taken sick
I told my mother my fault

She said: My boy, you must be ill
and give me a dose of salts

And when the salts begin to work
the buttons begin to fly,

Before I had my trousers down
I [raspberry] my corduroy

As I was walking down the street
some feller shouted, Jim!

Let‚Äôs go to the canal
and there we‚Äôll have a swim!

And while we were swimming about,
me and this other boy,

Some feller runned away
with my suit of corduroy

As naked as I was born
I was drove home in a cab

And when my mother heard of this
it nearly drove her mad.

She said, You silly little goose,
you‚Äôre a very naughty boy!

But I shall never forget the day
when I [raspberry] my corduroy.

items.notes:

items.lyrics:
‚ÄôTwas down in Cupid‚Äôs Garden I wandered for to view

The sweet and lovely flowers that in the garden grew,

And one it was sweet jasmine, the lily, pink and rose;

They are the finest flowers that in the garden grow.

I had not been in the garden but scarcely half an hour,

When I beheld two maidens, sat under a shady bower,

And one it was sweet Nancy, so beautiful and fair,

The other was a virgin and did the laurels wear.

I boldly stepped up to them and unto them did say,

‚ÄúAre you engaged to any young man, come tell to me, I pray?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNo, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm not engaged to any young man, I solemnly declare;

I mean to stay a virgin and still the laurels wear.‚ÄĚ

So, hand in hand together, this loving couple went;

To view the secrets of her heart was the sailor‚Äôs full intent,

Or whether she would slight him while he to the wars did go.

Her answer was, ‚ÄúNot I, my love, for I love a sailor bold.‚ÄĚ

It‚Äôs down in Portsmouth Harbour, there‚Äôs a ship lies waiting there;

Tomorrow to the seas I‚Äôll go, let the wind blow high or fair.

And, if I should live to return again, how happy I should be

With you, my love, my own true love, sitting smiling on my knee.

items.lyrics:
Dame Durden kept five servant maids to carry the milking pail,

She also kept five labouring men to use the spade and flail.

Chorus (after each verse):

‚ÄôTwas Moll and Bet and Doll and Kit and Dolly to drag her tail,

And Kitty she was the charming girl to carry the milking pail.

To rouse her servants maids and men she did begin to bawl.

‚ÄôTwas on the morn of Valentine when birds began to prate,

Dame Durden and her maids and men they altogether meet.

items.notes:

Hard Times of Old England

Hard Times of Old England

[

/ Song Subject MAS1570

; Ballad Index K224
items.lyrics:
Come all brother tradesmen that travel alone,

O, pray come and tell me where the trade is all gone,

Long time I have travelled and cannot find none,

And it‚Äôs O, the hard times of old England,

In old England very hard times.

Provisions you buy at the shop it is true,

But if you‚Äôve no money there‚Äôs none there for you.

So what‚Äôs a poor man and his family to do?

And it‚Äôs O, the hard times of old England,

In old England very hard times.

If you go to a shop and you ask for a job

They will answer you there with a shake and a nod.

That‚Äôs enough to make a poor man to turn out and rob,

And it‚Äôs O, the hard times of old England,

In old England very hard times.

You will see the poor tradesmen a-walking the street

From morning till night for employment to seek.

And scarcely they have any shoes to their feet,

And it‚Äôs O, the hard times of old England,

In old England very hard times.

Our soldiers and sailors have just come from war,

Been fighting for their King and their country sure,

Come home to be starved better have stayed where they were,

And it‚Äôs O, the hard times of old England,

In old England very hard times.

So now to conclude and to finish my song

Let us hope that these hard times they will not last long.

And I may soon have occasion to alter my song,

And sing O, the good times of old England,

In old England very good times.

items.title: Hark the Goddess Diana
items.notes:

[

; Bodleian

Hark the Goddess Diana

is a hunting song from the repertoire

of the Copper Family. It is printed in

The Copper Family Song Book ;

I don‚Äôt know of any published recording of it.

Ben Muriel of the British Library told me that they have a recording by

the Copper Family in their collection; but it is currently unavailable

(as of August 2024).

items.title: John Barleycorn II / Hey John Barleycorn
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS495

; Master title: John Barleycorn II

; Ballad Index K277

; Bodleian

; MusTrad MT232

; DT JBARLEY2

; Mudcat 66785

; Joseph Bryan Geoghegan (c.1816-1889)]

According to the Copper Family website,

Bob Copper collected

John Barleycorn
items.lyrics:
John Barleycorn is an hero bold as any in the land,

For ages good his fame has stood and will for ages stand.

The whole wide world respect him no matter friend or foe,

And where they be that makes too free he‚Äôs sure to lay them low.

Chorus (after each verse):

Hey, John Barleycorn, ho, John Barleycorn,

Old and young thy praise have sung, John Barleycorn.

To see him in his pride of growth his robes are rich and green,

His head is speared with prickly beard fit nigh to serve the Queen.

And when the reaping time comes round and John is stricken down,

He yields his blood for England‚Äôs good and Englishmen‚Äôs renown.

The Lord in courtly castle and the Squire in stately hall,

The great of name in birth and fame on John for succour call.

He bids the troubled heart rejoice, gives warmth to Nature‚Äôs cold

Makes weak men strong and old ones young and all men brave and bold.

Then shout for great John Barleycorn nor heed the luscious vine,

I have no mind much charm to find in potent draught of wine.

Give me my native nut-brown ale, all other drinks I‚Äôll scorn

For true English cheer is English beer, our own John Barleycorn.

Makes weak men strong and old men young and all men brave and bold.

items.lyrics:
‚ÄúO father and mother come tell me this riddle,

Come tell it all to me.

The Brown Girl she has houses and land

Fair Ellen she has none.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThen my advice to you, dear son,

Is to bring the Brown Girl home.‚ÄĚ

He dressed himself in clothes so fine,

Put on a mantle in green.

And every village that he rode through

He was taken to be some king.

He rode till he got to Fair Ellen‚Äôs hall,

He jingled at the ring.

And none was so ready as Fair Ellen herself,

She arose and let him in.

‚ÄúGood news, good news, fair Ellen,‚ÄĚ he said,

‚ÄúGood news I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve brought to you.

I‚Äôve come to ask you to my wedding

For married I must be.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBad news, bad news, Lord Thomas,‚ÄĚ she said,

‚ÄúBad news you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve brought to me.

You‚Äôve come to ask me to your wedding

For married you must be.‚ÄĚ

She dressed herself in clothes so fine,

Put on a diamond ring.

ūĚĄÜ And every village that she rode through

She was taken to be some queen. ūĚĄá

She rode till she got to Lord Thomas‚Äôs hall,

She jingled at the ring.

And none was so ready as Lord Thomas himself,

He arose and let her in.

‚ÄúLord Thomas, Lord Thomas, is this your bride?

She‚Äôs very dark and dim,

When you could have married this fair fine lady

As ever the sun shone on.‚ÄĚ

The brown girl had a little penknife,

It was both keen and sharp.

Betwixt the long rib and the short

She pierced fair Ellen‚Äôs heart.

‚ÄúLord Thomas, Lord Thomas are you blind

And can‚Äôt you very well see?

And can you see my own heart‚Äôs blood

Come trink-eling down my knee?‚ÄĚ

He took the Brown Girl by the hand

And led her through the hall,

And with a sword cut off her head

And kicked it against the wall.

He threw the sword upon the floor,

It flew into his breast.

Here lies two lovers all in a row,

Lord, send their souls to rest.

‚ÄúSo dig my grave under yonder green tree,

Go dig it both wide and deep.

ūĚĄÜ And bury Fair Ellen in my arms

And the Brown Girl at my feet‚ÄĚ ūĚĄá

She dressed herself all in the white,

She‚Äôd four and twenty milk white steeds

She‚Äôd four and twenty milk white swans

And when she came to Lord Thomas‚Äôs gate

He took her by the lilly white hand,

And led her through the Hall, O.

He sat her in a chair of gold,

Amongst her merry maids all, O.

Then up and spoke the dun brown bride

And O so bold spoke she, O.

‚ÄúO where did you get that watery flower

That washed you so white, O?‚ÄĚ

Then up and spoke fair Annie dear

And O so bold spoke she, O.

‚ÄúI got it in my mother√Ę¬Ä¬ôs womb

Where you never got such like, O.‚ÄĚ

The dun brown bride had a small pen knife

That hung down by her side O

She drove it through fair Annie‚Äôs heart

And never a word she cried, O.

Lord Thomas had a gay broad sword

That hung down by his side O

He drove it through the dun brown bride

‚ÄúO dig a grave,‚ÄĚ Lord Thomas said

‚ÄúAnd dig it wide and deep, O

And bury fair Annie in my arms

And the dun bride at my feet, O.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Martin Carthy sings Lord Thomas and Fair Eleanor
versions.lyrics:

items.lyrics:
‚ÄúO why did I leave my home

To go out in this wide world to roam?

If I had have stayed at home

Sure my baby would never been born.‚ÄĚ

O the old man come down in the morn,

With the child still alive at her breast

That was clasped in his dead mother‚Äôs arms.

‚ÄúO father come down let me in,

Come down and you open my door.

My child at my bosom will die

For the winds do blow √Ę¬Ä¬ôcross the wild moor.‚ÄĚ

Sure the old man with silvery hair,

Not a voice nor a sound touched his ear.

And the old clock did chime in the night

And winds do blow ‚Äôcross the wild moor.

For the old man come down in the morn,

Found poor Mary dead at his door

With the child still alive at her breast

It was clapped in his dead mother‚Äôs arms.

versions.title: Bob and John Copper sing Wind Across the Moor
versions.lyrics:
‚ÄôTwas a cold winter‚Äôs night and the wind

Blew bitter across the wild moor.

‚ÄôTwas then that poor Mary returned with her child

Wandering home to her own father‚Äôs door.

Crying, ‚ÄúFather, I pray, let me in!

O come down and open the door!

Or the child that I hold at my bosom will die

As the wind blows across the wild moor.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO why did I leave that fair spot

Where I was happy and free,

Forever to roam without friend or a home?

Pray father, take pity on me!‚ÄĚ

Her father was deaf to the cry

When the sound reached him over the door.

And the watchdog he barked at the wind as it blew

Coldly across the wild moor.

You can‚Äôt think what a father he felt

When he came to the door in the morn.

Poor Mary his daughter lay dead with the child,

Clasped alive in the dead mother‚Äôs arms.

With vengeance he tore his grey hair,

On his Mary he gazed from the door.

‚ÄôTwas on that cold night that she perished and died

As the wind blows across the wild moor.

The father in grief pined away

And the child to its mother went soon.

There‚Äôs no-one alive there to this very day

And the cottage to ruins has gone.

The villager points out the cote

Where the wild rose droops over the door.

‚ÄôTwas there Mary died by the house of her bride

As the wind blows across the wild moor.

items.lyrics:
It is of good ale to you I‚Äôll sing,

And to good ale I‚Äôll always cling.

I like my mug filled to the brim

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Oh Good Ale, thou art my darling,

Thou art my joy both night and morning.

It is you that helps me with my work,

And from a task I‚Äôll never shirk

While I can get a good home brew;

And better than one pint, I like two.

I love you in the early morn,

I love you in daylight, dark, or dawn.

And when I‚Äôm weary, worn, or spent

I‚Äôll turn the tap and ease the vent.

It is you that makes my friends my foes,

It is you that makes me wear old clothes.

But since you come so near my nose

It‚Äôs up you comes and down you goes.

And if all my friends from Adam‚Äôs race

Was to meet me here all in this place,

I could part from all without one fear

Before I‚Äôd part from my good beer.

And if my wife should me despise,

How soon I‚Äôd give her two black eyes.

But if she loved me as I love thee,

What a happy couple we should be.

You have caused me debts and I‚Äôve often swore

I never would drink strong ale anymore.

But you, for all that, I‚Äôll forgive

And I‚Äôll drink strong ale as long as I live.

items.notes:

items.lyrics:
When Adam was first created

And lord of the universe crowned,

His happiness was not completed

Until that a helpmate was found.

He had all things in food that were wanting,

To keep and support him in life,

He‚Äôd horses and foxes for hunting,

Which some men love more than a wife.

He‚Äôd a garden so planted by nature

Men cannot produce in his life,

But yet the all-wise Creator

Still saw that he wanted a wife.

Then Adam he lay in a slumber

And there he lost part of his side,

And when he awoke with a wonder,

He beheld his most beautiful bride.

In transport he gazed upon her,

His happiness now was complete,

He praised his beautiful donor,

Who had thus bestowed him a mate.

She was not took out of his head, sir,

To reign and to triumph o‚Äôer man;

Nor was she took out of his feet, sir,

By man to be trampled upon.

But she was took out of his side, sir,

His equal and partner to be,

But as they‚Äôre united in one, sir,

The man is the top of the tree.

Then let not the fair be despised

By man, as she‚Äôs part of himself,

For woman by Adam was prized

More than the whole world full of wealth.

Man without woman‚Äôs a beggar,

Suppose the whole world he possessed;

And the beggar that‚Äôs got a good woman

With more than the world he is blest.

items.lyrics:
Young Johnny walked out on a sunshiny morn,

And young Johnny followed after with a long and wishing eye.

‚ÄúI have two little lambs stole away from the fold,

And these two little lambs they came this way I‚Äôve been told,

Have you seen two little lambs come a-wandering this way?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO, yes,‚ÄĚ replied the shepherd, ‚ÄúI saw them pass by,

They√Ę¬Ä¬ôre down in yonder valley and that is very nigh.‚ÄĚ

She returned herself with a curtsey and thanked him with a blush

versions.title: George Spicer sings Searching for Young Lambs
versions.lyrics:
As Johnny walk-ed out, one midsummer‚Äôs morn,

And where did he hide himself? all under a thorn.

‚ÄôTwas there he spied a pretty fair maid as she as passing by,

She went down in yonder meadow and that is very nigh.

She searched the meadow over, no lambs could she find.

Oftimes did she cross that young man in her mind.

Then turning round so careless-lie and smiling with a blush,

For young Johnny followed after and hid all in a bush.

‚ÄúHave you seen ere a ewe with its two pretty lambs,

Strayed away from the dame, strayed away from the fold?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh yes, oh yes, my pretty fair maid I saw them passing by,

They went down in yonder meadow and that is very nigh.‚ÄĚ

And now they are got married and living in ‚Äôrocked bands,

Never more to go roaming in search of young lambs.

In searching of young lambs, my friends, no friendships do renew,

And the lambs they skip all round them all in the morning dew.

versions.title: Rob Williams sings Searching for Young Lambs
versions.lyrics:
As Johnny was walking one midsummer‚Äôs morn,

Oh! there he did hide himself all underneath a thorn,

A beautiful damsel by chance to pass him by,

And there he did cast her in his long and wishing eye,

In his long and wishing eye, in his long and wishing eye,

Oh! there he did cast her in his long and wishing eye.

She traced the valleys over, no lamb could she find,

Her Johnny, dear Johnny, so often in her mind.

She turned herself quite curiously, and smiling with a blush,

And there she saw her Johnny all hiding in a bush.

All hiding in a bush, all hiding in a bush,

Oh! There she saw her Johnny, all hiding in a bush.

Oh! then the words of love did begin to overflow,

He tooked her down those meadows, down the meadows down below,

And whilst his arms was round her, her charms to impose,

Then the lambs played all around them all in the morning dew,

All in the morning dew, all in the morning dew,

Oh! The lambs played all around them all in the morning dew.

So now to conclude and to finish my song,

This being the happiest couple and the highest of the folk,

So now they are got married happy in wedlock‚Äôs bands,

No more to go a¬≠roving in searching for young lambs,

In searching for young lambs, in searching for young lambs,

No more to go a¬≠roving, in searching for young lambs.

‚ÄúHave you seen √Ę¬Ä¬ôere a ewe with her young and tender lambs,

Strayed away from the fold, strayed away from their dams?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh yes oh yes my pretty fair maid I saw them passing by

They went down in yonder meadow and that is very nigh.‚ÄĚ

He held her hand, he whispered love, he swore his heart was true

And now they are got married and are joined in wedlock‚Äôs bands

No more to go roving in search of young lambs

In searching for young lambs, kind friends, old friendships to renew

And the lambs they skip all round them, all in the morning dew.

items.title: Sheepshearing Song
items.notes:
Sheepshearing Song

[

English Folk-Songs

Bob Copper:

A Song for Every Season

The Copper Family:

The Copper Family Song Book
items.lyrics:
Come all my jolly boys and we‚Äôll together go,

Together with our masters to shear the lambs and ewes.

All in the month of June of all times in the year

It always comes in season the lambs and ‚Äėyowes‚Äô to shear.

And then we will work hard, my boys, until our backs do break,

Our Master he will bring us beer whenever we do lack.

Our Master he comes round to see our work‚Äôs done well,

And he says, Shear them close, my boys, for there is but little wool,

O, yes, good Master, we reply, we‚Äôll do well as we can.

Our Captain cries, Shear close, my lads, to each and every man,

And at some places still we have this story all day long,

Bend your backs and shear them well, and this is all their song.

And then our noble Captain doth to the Master say,

Come let us have one bucket of your good ale, I pray,

He turns unto our Captain and makes him this reply,

You shall have the best of beer, I promise, presently.

Then with the foaming bucket pretty Betsy she doth come

And Master says, Maid, mind and see that every man has some.

This is some of our pastime while we the sheep do shear,

And though we are such merry boys, we work hard, I declare,

And when ‚Äôtis night and we are done our Master is more free

And stores us well with good strong beer and pipes of tobaccee,

And there we sit a-drinking we smoke and sing and roar,

Till we become far merrier than e‚Äôer we were before.

When all our work is done and all the sheep are shorn

Then home with our Captain to drink the ale that‚Äôs strong.

It‚Äôs a barrel then of hum-cap which we will call Black Ram,

And we do sit and swagger and we swear that we are men.

And yet before the night is through I‚Äôll bet you half-a-crown,

That if you ha‚Äôn‚Äôt a special care that Ram will knock you down.

versions.title: Julie West sings Sheep Shearing

And then we must work hard, boys, until our backs do ache,

And our master he do bring us beer whenever we do lack;

Our master he comes round to see our work is doing well,

And he cries, ‚ÄúShear them close, men, for there is but little wool.‚ÄĚ

And then our noble captain doth to our master say,

‚ÄúCome let us have one bucket of your good ale, I pray.‚ÄĚ

He turns unto our captain and makes him this reply:

‚ÄúYou shall have the best of beer, I promise, presently.‚ÄĚ

And when ‚Äôtis night and we have done, our master is more free,

And stores us well with good strong beer and pipes of tobaccee.

So we do sit and drink, we smoke and sing and roar,

Till we become more merry, far, than e‚Äôer we were before.

When all our work is done and all our sheep are shorn,

Then home to our captain to drink the ale that‚Äôs strong.

√Ę¬Ä¬ôTis a barrel, then, of wine cup we call the ‚ÄėBlack Ram‚Äô,

And we do sit and swagger and swear that we are men.

items.title: The Shepherd of the Downs
items.notes:
The Shepherd of the Downs

[

/ Song Subject MAS1573

; Ballad Index CopSe258

; Bodleian

; DT SHEPDOWN

; Mudcat 131251

; trad.]

Shepherd of the Downs

a.k.a. Shepherd in Love

is believed to be the oldest song in the repertoire of the Copper Family.

James ‚ÄėBrasser‚Äô Copper wrote in the family√Ę¬Ä¬ôs songbook:
items.lyrics:
A shepherd of the downs being weary of his port

Retired to the hills where he used to resort.

In want of refreshment he laid himself down,

ūĚĄÜ He wanted no riches, nor wealth from the Crown. ūĚĄá

He drank of the cold brook, he ate of the tree,

Himself he did enjoy from all sorrow was tree,

He valued no girl be she ever so fair,

ūĚĄÜ No pride nor ambition he valued no care. ūĚĄá

As he was a-walking one evening so clear

A heavenly sweet voice sounded soft in his ear.

He stood like a post not one step could he move,

ūĚĄÜ He knew not what hailed him but thought it was love. ūĚĄá

He beheld a young damsel, a fair modest bride,

She had something amiss and disguised in her face.

Disguised in her face she unto him did say,

ūĚĄÜ ‚ÄúHow now, Master Shepherd, how came you this way?‚ÄĚ ūĚĄá

The shepherd he replied and modestly said,

‚ÄúI never was surprised before at a maid.

When first you beheld me from sorrow I was free,

ūĚĄÜ But now you have stolen my poor heart from me.‚ÄĚ ūĚĄá

He took her by the hand and thus he did say,

‚ÄúWe will get married pretty Betsy today.‚ÄĚ

So to church they did go and were married we hear,

ūĚĄÜ And now he√Ę¬Ä¬ôll enjoy pretty Betsy his dear. ūĚĄá

items.title: Shepherds, Arise!
items.notes:
>

Maddy Prior >

Songs >

Sing, Sing All Earth

Shepherds, Arise!

[

; Ballad Index CopSe212

; Folkinfo 421

; Mudcat 36310

; trad. (Copper, 1971 / Pickard-Cambridge, 1926)]

Bob Copper:

A Song for Every Season
items.lyrics:
Shepherds, arise, be not afraid

With hasty steps repair

To Bethlehem city see the maid,

With her blest Infant there.

Laid in a manger view the Child,

Humility divine:

Sweet innocence (how meek! how mild!),

Grace in His features shine!

For us the Saviour came on earth,

For us His life He gave,

To save us from eternal death

And raise us from the grave.

To Jesus Christ, our glorious King,

Be endless praises given!

Let all the earth His mercies sing,

Who made our peace with Heaven.

To our Redeemer, to our Redeemer and our heavenly King.

Sweet innocence sounds meek and mild.

Grace in his features‚ÄĒgrace in his features shine,

To our Redeemer, to our Redeemer and our heavenly King.

For us the Saviour came on earth, for us his life he gave,

To save us from eternal death

And to raise us from‚ÄĒand to raise us from the grave

To raise us from the grave and to raise us from the grave

Sing, sing, all earth, sing, sing, all earth eternal praises sing

To our Redeemer, to our Redeemer and our heavenly King.

items.title: Shift up a Little Bit Farther
items.notes:
[

; Arthur Aiston]

John Copper sang

Shift up a Little Bit Farther

on the 1995 Veteran anthology

When the May Is All in Bloom .

John Howson noted:

John learned this from his grandfather, Jim Copper‚Äôs song book. It was

composed by Arthur Aiston and when published in 1903 it was arranged by him

and Fred W. Leigh and performed by music hall singer Frank Coyne.
items.lyrics:
My uncle Jimmy has got a beautiful ruby tinted nose

Scotch, of, course, is out of the question,

Uncle says he‚Äôs got indigestion.

Once he stood at a railway crossing to watch the trains go by.

A railway porter standing near came up and shouted, ‚ÄúHi

‚ÄúShift up a little bit farther, shift a little bit farther up.

At this spot there soon will arrive a

Fast express, you‚Äôll worry the driver

With that terrible nose, it‚Äôs like a danger light.

So, shift a little bit farther, father, farther out of sight.‚ÄĚ

Well I went out with some friends of mine in a brand new motor car.

We was going from London to Dover,

But before the journey was over,

Something seemed to go wrong with our machines inside.

Agricultural labourers came to us and shouted ‚ÄúHi

‚ÄúShift up a little bit farther, shift a little bit farther up.

We never thought a travelling tinker

Could be such a tiddley-winker.

We‚Äôve no kettles and pots for you to mend today,

So shift a little bit farther, father, farther on your way.‚ÄĚ

It was down at Winkleton-Super-Mare on a lovely summer‚Äôs day,

People was gazing out in the ocean,

They was causing quite a commotion.

What‚Äôs that strange old object there, a new sea monster p‚Äôraps?

Well I dashed up to them and said ‚ÄúLook here, you girls and chaps

‚ÄúShift up a little bit farther, shift a little bit farther up.

What you seem to think is a sort a

Curiosity in the water,

That‚Äôs my missus and she‚Äôll be out in half a jiff,

So, shift a little bit farther, father, farther round the cliff.‚ÄĚ

My aunt Maria is fairly heavy, she weighs a tidy lump.

Aunty, last bank holiday, tried a

Donkey ride and all of us guide her.

She jumped up on the donkey‚Äôs back, but the brute refused to stir.

He nods his head and winks his eye and then remarks to her:

‚ÄúShift up a little bit farther, shift a little bit farther up.

Please excuse me being so grumpy,

But you are a little bit lumpy.

I can‚Äôt waggle my tail, the flies give me the hump,

So, shift a little bit farther, father, farther off my stump.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Softly the Night
items.notes:
Softly the Night

[

; Mudcat 42376

; words Edward Abiel Washburn (1819-1881)]

Bob and John Copper sang

Softly the Night

in 1971 on their Leader box set

A Song for Every Season .

The Copper Family also sang it on their 2007 CD

Coppers at Christmas .

Andy Turner sang Softly the Night

as the 15 December 2024 entry of his project
items.lyrics:
Softly the night is sleeping on Bethlehem‚Äôs peaceful hill,

Silent the shepherds watching, their gentle flocks are still,

But hark the wond‚Äôrous music falls from the opening sky,

Valley and cliff re-echo, glory to God on high.

Glory to God, it rings again, peace on the earth, goodwill to men.

Come with the gladsome shepherds quick hastening from the fold,

Come with the wise men bringing incense and myrrh and gold.

Come to him poor and lowly all round the cradle throng,

Come with your hearts of sunshine and sing the Angel‚Äôs song.

Glory to God, tell out again, peace on the earth, goodwill to men.

Wave ye, the wreath unfading the fir tree and the pine,

Green from the snows of winter to deck the holy shrine,

Bring ye, the happy children for this is Christmas morn

Jesus the sinless infant, Jesus the lord is born.

Glory to God, to God again, peace on the earth, goodwill to men.

items.title: Spencer the Rover
items.notes:
>

Shirley Collins >

Songs >

Spencer the Rover

>

Peter Bellamy >

Songs >

Spencer the Rover

Spencer the Rover

[

/ Song Subject MAS986

; Master title: Spencer the Rover
items.lyrics:
This song was composed by Spencer the Rover,

Who‚Äôd travelled most parts of Great Britain and Wales,

There had been much reducing which caused great confusion,

And that was the reason a-rambling I went.

In Yorkshire near Rotherham still being on my rambles,

Being weary of travelling I sat down to rest,

At the foot of the mountain there sprung a clear fountain

Of pleasant cool water myself to refresh.

It tasted more sweeter than the food I had wasted,

More sweeter than honey and gave more content,

But the thoughts of my children lamenting for their father

Brought tears to my eyes and caused me to repent.

Now the night fast approaching, to the woods I departed,

With woodbine and ivy my bed for to make,

I heard a bird sighing, lamenting and crying,

Come home to your family and rambling forsake.

On the fifth of November I‚Äôve a reason to remember

‚ÄôTwas then I first returned to my family and wife,

They looked so surprising to see me arriving,

To see such a stranger once more in their sight.

Then me children came round me with their pretty, prattling stories,

With their pretty, prattling stories which drove dull care away,

So we will unite together like birds of a feather,

Like bees in one hive so contented we‚Äôll stay.

So now I am placed in my cottage contented

With woodbine and roses climbing all round the door,

I am happy as those that‚Äôs got plenty of riches,

Contented I‚Äôll stay and go rambling no more.

versions.title: (Ursula Ridley and) Jim Porter sings Spencer the Rover
versions.lyrics:
(This tale was compos√®d by young Spencer the Rover

Who wandered most parts of Great Britain and Wales

He had been so reduc√®d, which caused great confusion

And that was the reason he set off on the rails.

At Yorkshire, near Rotherham, he had been on his rambles

Being tired and hungry, he sat down to rest

At the foot of a mountain, where runs a clear fountain

With bread and with water, himself he refreshed.

And it tasted more sweeter than the honey he had wasted

It tasted more sweeter than the gold he had spent

It was the thought of his children lamenting their father

That caused him to weep and caused him to repent.)

The night fast-lye ‚Äôproaching, to the woods he resorted

With woodbind and ivy for the makin‚Äô a bed

Oh, he dreamt about sighing, lye-ment-anly crying

‚ÄúGo home to your parents, go ramblin√Ę¬Ä¬ô no more.‚ÄĚ

Now on the fifth of November I have reasons to remember,

When first I arrived to my family and wife.

My wife stood surprising just to see me arriving

For to see such a stranger once more in her eye.

Now the children come round me with their prit-prattling stories,

With their prit-prattling stories to drive care away.

We‚Äôre united together like the birds of a feather,

Like a bee in a hive and contented we‚Äôll be.

Now I am placed in my cottage contented,

With the woodbind and the ivy all hang round my door,

I‚Äôm as happy as those who got plenty of riches

I‚Äôll stay at home, I‚Äôll go rambling no more.

These words were composed by Spencer the Rover

Who travelled Great Britain and most parts of Wales.

He had been so reduced which caused great confusion

And that was the reason he went on the roam.

In Yorkshire near Rotherham he had been on his rambles,

Being weary of travel, he sat down to rest.

At the foot of yonder mountain there runs a clear fountain;

With bread and cold water himself did refresh.

It tasted more sweeter than the gold he had wasted,

More sweeter than honey and gave more content.

But the thoughts of his babies lamenting their father

Brought tears to his eyes and made him repent.

Now the night fast approaching to the woods he resorted,

With ivy and woodbine his bed for to make.

Where he dreamt about sighing, lamenting and crying,

Go home to your family and rambling forsake.

On the fifth day of November I‚Äôve a reason to remember,

When first he arrived home to his family and wife.

They stood so surprised when first he arrived

To see such a stranger once more in their sight.

His children came around him with their prittle-prattling stories,

With their prittle-prattling stories to drive care away.

And now they‚Äôre united like birds of one feather,

Like bees in one hive contented they‚Äôll be.

So now he is a-living in his cottage so contented,

With ivy and roses growing all around the door.

He‚Äôs as happy as those that‚Äôs got thousands of riches;

Contented he‚Äôll be and go rambling no more.

items.title: Sportsmen Arouse! / The Innocent Hare
items.notes:
[

; Ballad Index K251

; DT INNOHARE

; Mudcat 58033

; trad.]

The Copper Family:

The Copper Family Song Book

Sportsmen Arouse!

is a song from the repertoire of the Copper Family.

Bob and Ron Copper sang this song in a 1955 recording by Peter Kennedy.

It was published on their EFDSS LP

Traditional Songs From Rottingdean
items.lyrics:
ūĚĄÜ Sportsmen arouse the morning is clear,

The larks are singing all in the air. ūĚĄá

ūĚĄÜ Go and tell your sweet lover the hounds are out, ūĚĄá

Saddle your horses, your saddles prepare,

We‚Äôll away to some cover to seek for a hare.

ūĚĄÜ We searched the woods and the groves all round,

The trial being over the game it is found, ūĚĄá

ūĚĄÜ Then off she springs through brake she flies, ūĚĄá

Follow, follow the musical horn,

Sing follow, hark, forward the innocent hare.

ūĚĄÜ Our huntsman blows his joyful sound,

Tally ho, my boys, all over the downs. ūĚĄá

ūĚĄÜ From the woods to the valleys see how she creeps, ūĚĄá

Follow, follow the musical horn,

Sing follow, hark, forward the innocent hare.

ūĚĄÜ All along the green turf she pants for breath

Our huntsman he shouts out for death. ūĚĄá

ūĚĄÜ Relope, relope, retiring hare. ūĚĄá

Follow, follow the musical horn

Sing follow, hark forward the innocent hare.

ūĚĄÜ This hare has led us a noble run

Success to sportsmen every one, ūĚĄá

ūĚĄÜ Such a chase she has led us, four hours or more, ūĚĄá

Wine and beer we‚Äôll drink without fear,

We‚Äôll drink a success to the innocent hare.

items.lyrics:
As I was a-walking one fine summer‚Äôs morning,

The fields and the meadows they looked so green and gay;

And the birds they were singing so pleasantly adorning,

So early in the morning at the break of the day.

Oh hark, oh hark, how the nightingale is singing,

The lark she is taking her flight all in the air.

On yonder green bower the turtle doves are building,

The sun is just a-glimmering. Arise my dear.

Arise, oh, arise and get your humble posies,

For they are the finest flowers that grow in yonder grove.

And I will pluck them all sweet lily, pink and roses,

All for Sweet Lemeney, the girl that I love.

Oh, Lemeney, oh, Lemeney, you are the fairest creature,

You are the fairest creature that ever my eyes did see.

And then she played it over all on the pipes of ivory,

So early in the morning at the break of the day.

Oh, how could my true-love, how could she vanish from me,

Oh, how could she go and I never shall see her more.

But it was her cruel parents that looked so slightly on me,

All for the white robe that I once used to wear.

versions.lyrics:
Oh early one morning as I was walking

The fields and the meadows they looked so green and gay

The birds sang so sweetly, so pleasant and so charming

So early in the morning at the break of the day.

Oh hark, oh hark how the nightingale is singing

The lark she is taking her flight in the air

The turtle dove in every green bower is building

The sun is just glimmering, arise then, my dear.

Arise, love, arise, I have plucked you a nosegay

The sweetest of flowers that grow in yonder grove

Oh I have plucked them fresh from the lily, pink and rosetree

And it‚Äôs all for my Limadie, the girl that I love.

O Limadie, O Limadie, thou art the fairest flower

Thou art the sweetest flower that e‚Äôer mine eyes did see

And the tunes that I will play to thee shall be on flute of ivory

For my heart is so full of soft love melody.

Oh why should my true love be banished from me?

Oh why should she die and I never see her more?

Because that her parents look so slightingly upon me

I too will die for Limadie, the girl I adore.

items.lyrics:
Twas on one summer‚Äôs evening all in the month of May

Down by a flow‚Äôry garden where Betsy did stray.

I overheard a damsel in sorrow to complain,

All for her absent lover that ploughs the raging main.

I stepped up to this fair maid and put her in surprise,

She owned she did not know me, I being all in disguise.

I said, ‚ÄúMy charming creature, my joy and heart√Ę¬Ä¬ôs delight,

How far have you to travel this dark and rainy night?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAway, kind sir, to the Claudy banks if you will please to show,

Pity a poor girl distracted for there I have to go.

I am in search of a young man and Johnny is his name,

And on the banks of Claudy I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm told he does remain.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIf Johnny he was here this night he would keep me from all harm

But he‚Äôs in the field of battle all in his uniform,

He is in the field of battle his foes he will destroy,

Like a roaring King of honour all in the wars of Troy.

‚ÄúIt was six months or better since my Johnny left the shore,

He‚Äôs a-cruising the wide ocean where the foaming billows roar,

He‚Äôs a-cruising the wide ocean for honour or for gain,

But I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm told his ship got wreck-ed all on the coast of Spain.‚ÄĚ

When Betsy heard this dreadful news she fell into despair,

In a-wringing of her hands and a-tearing of her hair.

‚ÄúSince Johnny has gone and left me no man on earth I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll take,

Down in some lonesome valley I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll wander for his sake.‚ÄĚ

Young Johnny hearing her say so he could no longer stand,

He fell into her arms crying, ‚ÄúBetsy, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm that man,

I am that faithful young man and whom you thought was slain,

And since we met on Claudy banks we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never part again.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Eliza Carthy sings Claudy Banks
versions.lyrics:
As I rode out one evening all in the month of May

Down by the banks of Claudy I carelessly did stray.

Chorus (after every other verse):

All on the walls of Troy

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs six long months and better since my Johnny left the shore

items.title: The Battle of Alma
items.notes:
The Battle of Alma

[

; Ballad Index DalC216

; trad.]

The Cruel Wars

The Battle of Alma

is a song from the repertoire of the Copper Family. It is printed in

The Copper Family Song Book .

Bob and John Copper sang it in 1971 on their Leader box set

A Song for Every Season .

John Copper and Jon Dudley recorded it in 1987 for the Copper Family‚Äôs EFDSS LP

Coppersongs: A Living Tradition .
items.lyrics:
A jolly young soldier a letter did write

To his own dearest jewel, his joy and heart‚Äôs delight,

To tell her of the dangers that he had entered in

At the Battle of Alma where thousands were slain.

Our soldiers and sailors were all prepared for war

To fight those lofty Russians where thousands must fall,

They were commanded by Lord Raglan that man of courage bold,

They fought them sword in hand, my boys, and forced them to yield.

It was the bravest battle that did this world surprise

To see our brave soldiers to stare them in the face,

They marched up to their guns, my boys, and soon they let them know,

They showed to them their needle work and forced them to yield.

The drums they did beat and the trumpets did sound

While thousands of soldiers lay dead on the ground.

There were rifle balls and musket came a-screeching by their ears

And the bomb shells a-bursting and loud cannon roar.

And now let us hope that these wars are all o‚Äôer,

While thousands of soldiers lie bleeding in gore.

May the Lord have mercy on them and save their poor souls,

May the sweet heavens protect them and God be their guide.

items.title: The Birds in the Spring / (Down) By the Green Groves / One May Morning Early
items.lyrics:
ūĚĄÜ One spring morning early I chanced for to rove,

I sit myself down by the side of a grove, ūĚĄá

And there did I hear the sweet nightingales sing.

You never heard so sweet, you never heard so sweet,

You never heard so sweet as the birds in the spring.

ūĚĄÜ All on the cold ground I sit myself down

And the voice of the nightingale re-echoes all round. ūĚĄá

Hark, hark how she warbles her notes, I‚Äôll declare,

No music, no songster, no music, no songster,

No music, no songster could with them compare.

ūĚĄÜ Come all that are here these birds for to hear,

I pray you pay attention and quickly draw near. ūĚĄá

And when you grow old you will have it to sing,

You‚Äôve heard so sweet, you‚Äôve heard so sweet,

You‚Äôve heard so sweet as the nightingales sing.

Their song was so charming their notes were so clear

No music no songster, no music no songster,

No music no songster can with them compare.

All you that come here the small birds to hear,

I‚Äôll have you pay attention so pray all draw near.

And when you‚Äôre growing old you will have this to say,

That you never heard so sweet, you never heard so sweet,

You never heard so sweet as the birds on the spray!

items.lyrics:
‚ÄúMy father is a lawyer, a lord of high renown,

If I should wed a soldier, it would pull my honour down,

Then your part and my part we never shall agree,

So you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll take this as a warning, bold dragoon,‚ÄĚ said she.

‚ÄúNo warning, no warning, no warning will I take,

I will either fight or die in the arm all for thy sweet sake.‚ÄĚ

Then hearing of those words made the lady‚Äôs heart to bleed

And away they went to church and got married with speed.

And when they had a‚Äô been to church and turning home again,

The lady met her father and seven armed men,

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm afraid,‚ÄĚ said the lady, ‚Äúwe both shall be slain.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy fear is not at all,‚ÄĚ said the jolly dragoon.

So the dragoon drew his sword, cut flesh and made the bones to rattle

And the lady held his horse while the dragoon fought the battle.

‚ÄúO, hold your hand, bold dragoon, bold dragoon, hold your hand

And you shall have my daughter, ten thousand pounds in hand.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm afraid,‚ÄĚ said the lady, ‚Äúmy portion is but small.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúSo hold your hand, bold dragoon and you shall have it all.‚ÄĚ

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll either wed or die, my love, all for your sweet sake.‚ÄĚ

And when the lady heard these words it caused her heart to bleed

The lady spied her father with seven armed men.

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm afraid,‚ÄĚ said the lady, ‚Äúwe both shall be slain soon.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO, I fear nothing at all,‚ÄĚ said the jolly, bold dragoon.

‚ÄúThere is no time to prittle, there is no time to prattle,

‚ÄúFight on,‚ÄĚ says the lady, ‚Äúmy portion is but small.

‚ÄúOh, let√Ę¬Ä¬ôs run,‚ÄĚ said the others, ‚Äúor we shall all be slain.

For to fight the valiant soldier we see it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs all in vain.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh, fight on,‚ÄĚ says the lady, ‚Äúfor the pile it is too small.‚ÄĚ

She said, ‚ÄúMy honoured soldier, I fain would be your wife,

But my old Tory father would surely take my life.‚ÄĚ

He took his sword and pistols and he hung them by his side,

And swore that he would marry her, whatever did betide.

As they had been to church and were coming home again

They met her cruel father and seven well-armed men.

‚ÄúLet us flee‚ÄĚ, cried the lady, ‚Äúfor fear we shall be slain.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúFear nothing‚ÄĚ, said the soldier to his charmer again.

But her father then addressed her and unto her did say,

‚ÄúWhat is this behaviour, is this your wedding day?

Since you have been so foolish to be a soldier‚Äôs wife

All in this lonesome valley I will surely end your life.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh no‚ÄĚ, cried the soldier, ‚Äúyou know not what you say,

I have not been defeated and shall not be today!‚ÄĚ

He drew forth his broadsword and his pistol he did rattle

And the lady held the horses while the soldier fought the battle.

Now the first man he came to, he had him quickly slain,

And the second man he came to, he ran him through the same.

‚ÄúLet us flee‚ÄĚ, cried the others, ‚Äúor else we shall be slain,

To fight a valiant soldier is surely all in vain!‚ÄĚ

The father cried, ‚ÄúYou butcher, you make my blood run cold,

But you shall have my daughter and a thousand pounds in gold.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúFight on‚ÄĚ, cried the lady, ‚Äúour portion is too small!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúStay your hand‚ÄĚ, cried the father, ‚Äúand you shall have it all.‚ÄĚ

So he took the soldier home and he made him son and heir,

But not for love he bore him, but just from dread and fear,

There never was a soldier who would ever fire a gun,

Who would ever flinch a hair till the battle it was won.

So don‚Äôt despise a soldier because that he is poor,

He truly is a knight, as he was in days of yore,

He‚Äôs bold, brisk and jolly, both sociable and free,

He‚Äôd soon as fight for love as to fight for liberty.

versions.title: The Dovetail Trio sing The Bold Keeper
versions.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of a bold keeper in the chase of his deer,

He courted a nobleman‚Äôs daughter so fair.

‚ÄúIf you are as willing to that church you will ride

And there we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll get married, brave lady of mine.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy father is a lawyer, a lord of high renown,

If I should wed a keeper, it would pull my honour down.

Then your part and my part we never shall agree,

So you take this as a warning, bold keeper,‚ÄĚ said she.

‚ÄúNo warning, no warning, no warning will I take,

I will fight or die, my love, all for thy sake.‚ÄĚ

And when the lady heard these words it caused her heart to bleed;

And to the church they both went and were married with speed.

As they were a-riding through meadows so wide,

With their broad swords and buckles hung down by their side,

There they met her father with twenty bright men

And their broad glittering swords drawn ready in hand.

The bold keeper said, ‚ÄúNow then, don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you stand to tattle.

There are twenty armed men just fitting for the battle,

For I will draw my broadsword and make their bones to rattle.‚ÄĚ

And the lady held the horse for the bold keeper.

‚ÄúNow then bold keeper, come pray hold your hand.

You shall have my daughter, ten thousand in hand.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúFight on,‚ÄĚ says the lady, ‚ÄúThat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs too small a sum.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh, hold your hand, keeper, and you shall have it all.‚ÄĚ

So come all you young ladies that have gold in store

Never shun a bold keeper although he is poor.

Although he is poor, he will fight for you sake,

So here‚Äôs a health to your love, the bold keeper.

And with it he‚Äôs clipped off the poor tailor‚Äôs ears.

And arse over head, why, he kicked him downstairs,

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôll not have a gamecock,‚ÄĚ said the soldier.

versions.title: Danny Brazil sings The Croppy Tailor
versions.lyrics:
There was an old tailor he lived in Fairmye,

And on this old doughnut he did fix his eye;

He swore he would have her or else he would die,

While the keeper was out upon duty.

He said, ‚ÄúDearest woman your husband√Ę¬Ä¬ôs on the deep,

Ten guineas I‚Äôll give this night with you to sleep;

For into the room I will silently creep,

While your husband is out upon duty.‚ÄĚ

They stripped off their clothes and jumped into bed,

The thoughts of the trooper never ran in his head;

They tossed and they tumbled ‚Äôtil about one o‚Äôclock

Up came the bold bosun the doors for to knock.

It woke the little tailor right out of his sleep

Saying, ‚ÄúWhere shall I run to or where shall I creep?

For I‚Äôve heard the bold knock from the trooper.

O hide me, oh hide me,‚ÄĚ the poor tailor said,

‚ÄúFor I hear the bold knock of the trooper.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThere is an old cupboard stands behind the hall door,

For it‚Äôs in it you‚Äôll get, you‚Äôll be safe and secure;

And I will go down and I‚Äôll open the door,

With kisses and comforts like man and wife should

And welcome my husband the trooper.‚ÄĚ

Then she went down and he opened the door,

With kisses and comforts like man and wife sure;

‚ÄúYour kisses and comforts they make me full sore,

Will you light me fire to my supper?‚ÄĚ

She said, ‚ÄúDearest husband there is no fire stuff,

If you jump in bed with me you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be quite warm enough.‚ÄĚ

He says, ‚ÄúThere is an old cupboard stands behind the hall door,

And I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll burn it tonight,‚ÄĚ said the trooper.

‚ÄúO husband, O husband, O grant my desire,

The old corner cupboard‚Äôs too good for the fire;

And in it I keep my gamecock I admire‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúShow me your gamecock!‚ÄĚ said the trooper.

He hauled the old cupboard from behind the hall door,

And he hauled the little tailor right out on the floor,

Saying, ‚ÄúIs this your gamecock?‚ÄĚ said the trooper.

He asked for the sethers and then for the shears,

And he clipped off the tips of the poor tailor‚Äôs ears;

He give him a kick and a bash and a blow,

And away run the poor croppy tailor.

Said the old tailor, ‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôve done it all right,

But I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve had your old wife for the most of the night.‚ÄĚ

And away run the poor croppy tailor.

items.title: The Brisk Young Ploughboy / The Brave Ploughboy
items.notes:
[

; Master title: The Brisk Young Ploughboy

; Ballad Index PECS004

; DT PLOWBOY

; Mudcat 129655

; trad.]

Brisk Young Ploughboy

is a harvest supper song from the repertoire of the Copper Family.

Bob and Jim Copper sang it in a BBC recording made by Brian George on

1 March 1951 (BBC archive 16063); this was included on the Topic CD

Come Write Me Down: Early Recordings of the Copper Family of Rottingdean .

Bob and John Copper sang it in 1971 on the Trailer 4 LP box
items.lyrics:
‚ÄôTwas of a brisk young ploughboy, come listen to this refrain

And join with me in chorus and sing the ploughboy‚Äôs praise.

My song is of the ploughboy‚Äôs praise and unto you I‚Äôll relate the same,

He whistles and sings and drives his plough, the brave ploughboy.

So early in the morning the ploughboy he is seen

All hastening to the stable his horses for to clean.

Their manes and tails he does comb straight, with chaff and corn he will them bate

And he‚Äôll endeavour to plough straight, the brave ploughboy.

When he goes out in the morning to harrow plough or sow

And with a gentle cast, my boys, he‚Äôll give his corn a throw.

All this I‚Äôll have you understand is just to fill the reaper‚Äôs hand,

Likewise I‚Äôll have you understand, it comes from the ploughboy.

Now seedtime being over the fields look fresh and gay

There‚Äôs merry lads to mow the grass while damsels make the hay.

The small birds sing on every tree, the cuckoo joins sweet harmony,

All welcome here as you may see, the brave ploughboy.

Then haying being over and harvest does draw near,

Our Master he does welcome us with plenty of beef and beer.

We all sit round to drink our beer while Peace and Plenty fill the year

And we‚Äôll be happy while we are here and drink to the ploughboy.

Now harvest being over we start the plough once more,

Our Master has invited us unlocks his cellar door.

With cake and ale we have our fill because we‚Äôve done our work so well

And there‚Äôs no one can despise the skill of the brave ploughboy.

versions.title: Tony Wales sings The Ploughboy
versions.lyrics:
Come all you jolly ploughboys,
come listen to my lays.

And join with me in chorus,
I‚Äôll sing the ploughboy‚Äôs praise;

My song is of the ploughboy‚Äôs fame,
and unto you I‚Äôll relate the same:

He whistles, sings and drives his team,
the brave ploughing boy.

So early in the morning,
this ploughboy he is seen,

He hastens to the stable,
his horses for to clean;

Their manes and tails he will comb straight,
with chaff and corn he does them bait.

Then he‚Äôll endeavor to plough straight,
the brave ploughing boy.

Now all things being ready,
the harness put on, too,

All with smiling countenance,
his work he will pursue;

The small birds sing on every tree,
the cuckoo joins in harmony,

To welcome him they all agree,
the brave ploughing boy.

So early in the morning
to harrow, plough and sow,

And with a gentle cast, my boys,
we‚Äôll give the corn a throw;

This makes the valleys thick to stand,
with corn to fill the reaper‚Äôs hand.

All this you well may understand,
does the brave ploughing boy.

Now the corn it is a-growing,
and seed time it is o‚Äôer.

Our master he does welcome us,
and ope‚Äôs the cellar door;

With cake and ale we have our fill,
because we‚Äôve done our work so well,

There‚Äôs none here can excel the skill
of the brave ploughing boy.

Now the corn it is a-growing,
the fields look fresh and gay,

The cheerful lads come in to mow,
whilst damsels make the hay;

The ears of corn they now appear,
and peace and plenty crown the year,

So we‚Äôll be merry and drink whilst here,
to the brave ploughing hoy.

versions.title: The Watersons sing The Brave Ploughboy
versions.lyrics:
Come all ye jolly ploughboys come listen to my lays

My song is of the ploughboy‚Äôs fame

And unto you I‚Äôll relate the same

He whistles, sings, and drives his team, the brave ploughing boy

So early in the morning, the ploughboy he is seen

He hastens to the stable, his horses for to clean

Their manes and tails he will comb straight

With chaff and corn he does them bait

And he‚Äôll endeavour to plough straight, the brave ploughing boy.

Now all things being ready and the harness that‚Äôs put to

The small birds sing on every tree

The cuckoo joins in harmony

To welcome us, as you may say, the brave ploughing boy.

So early in the morning to harrow, plough, and sow

And with a gentle cast, my boys, we‚Äôll give the corn a throw

Which makes the valleys thick to stand

With corn to fill the reaper‚Äôs hand

All this, you well may understand, comes from the ploughing boy.

Now the corn it is a-growing, and seed-time that‚Äôs all o‚Äôer

Our master he does welcome us and unlocks the cellar door

With cake and ale we‚Äôll have our fill

Because we‚Äôve done our work so well

There‚Äôs none here can excel the skill of the brave ploughing boy.

items.title: The Horse the Missus Dries the Clothes On / The Clothes Horse
items.notes:
[

; Fred W. Leigh]

The Copper Family sang The Horse the Missus Dries the Clothes On

at Will Noble‚Äôs barn in Denby Dale, Yorkshire on 27 September 1986.

This recording was included in 1987 on the EFDSS Holme Valley Tradition cassette

Will‚Äôs Barn .

And John Copper sang

The Clothes Horse

on the 1995 Veteran anthology

When the May Is All in Bloom .

John Howson noted:

Another song which came from Jim Copper‚Äôs song book and another from the
items.lyrics:
I wanted to join the yeomanry, so they told me that I‚Äôd have to ride a horse

Of course

I had to ride a horse.

So I went to the livery stables just along a Charing Cross to hire a horse

Of course

They said it was a horse

And so, of course, I thought it was a horse.

It was a funny animal, the legs it had were four.

There was one in every corner, it could have done with several more.

The men bumped me up and I was on my own,

But I don‚Äôt think I‚Äôll ever get to ride like Sloan

Chorus (after each verse):

Oh, you don‚Äôt catch me on a gee gee‚Äôs back again.

It‚Äôs not the sort of place to sleep and doze on.

The only horse that I think that I can manage is

The one the missus dries the clothes on.

I‚Äôll never forget that moment when that horrible quadruped got on the go,

You know,

I kept on shouting, ‚ÄúWhoa!‚ÄĚ

And what with the wibbly-wobbling and the rocking to and fro

And so and so

You know, I made a rotten show.

And that is why I went to Rotten Row.

At last the bounder stopped a bit and looking over there

He saw a beautiful girl with any amount of carroty hair.

He dashed up behind, before I knew what he‚Äôd done,

He took a great big mouthful of that lady‚Äôs bun.

I had to get off the gee gee‚Äôs back for the lady with ginger hair was feeling sore,

Oh lor,

She soon began to roar.

I tried to get back to my place and perspired at every pore

When something tore

I swore, when I got up once more.

For there I was sat on behind before

I grabbed the bounder by the tail and away he dashed of course.

And soon the saddle began to slip and it took me under the horse.

The crowd gave a cheer they thought I was having a spree

And I said as I came up for the infirmary:

items.title: The Country Lass / Brisk and Bonny Lad
items.notes:
[

; Master title: The Country Lass

117

; trad.]

Brisk and Bonny Lad

is a song from the repertoire of the

Copper Family. Bob and Jim Copper sang it in a BBC recording

( BBC 16062 )
items.lyrics:
I am a brisk and bonny lad and free from care and strife

And sweetly as the hours pass I love a country life.

At wake or fair I‚Äôm often there ‚Äômidst pleasure to be seen,

Though poor I am contented and as happy as a Queen.

I rise up in the morning my labour to pursue

And with my yoke and milking pails I trudge the morning dew.

My cows I milk and then I taste how sweet the nature yields,

The lark she sings to welcome me across the flowery fields.

And in the time of harvest how cheerfully we go,

Some with hooks and some with crooks and some with scythes to mow.

And when our corn is free from harm we have not far to roam,

We‚Äôll all away to celebrate the welcome Harvest Home.

items.title: The Dying Soldier / A British Soldier‚Äôs Grave
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS1040

; G/D 1:110

; Ballad Index MHAp171

; Bodleian

; trad.]

Karl Dallas:

The Cruel Wars

Bob and John Copper sang

The Dying Soldier

in 1971 on their Leader Records box set

A Song for Every Season .
items.lyrics:
The battle it was over and the stars did brightly shine,

The moon shone o‚Äôer the dying and the dead.

And not a sound was heard, save the scream of some wild bird

As it fluttered round the dying soldier‚Äôs head.

And on that spot there lay one who‚Äôd nobly fought that day,

His comrade true to him was standing near.

And with an anguished sigh to his comrade gently cried

And with his hand he wiped away a tear.

Chorus (after each verse):

Then he whispered ‚ÄúGoodbye‚ÄĚ to his comrade so dear,

His head upon his knapsack gently laid.

‚ÄúIf you live to get home you can tell them I am gone,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm lying in a British solder√Ę¬Ä¬ôs grave.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you remember that dear old oak tree,

With my knife I cut my name out in the bark.

And early in the morn I have reaped the golden corn

And listened to the warbling of the lark.

That dear old shady spot it shall never be forgot

For ‚Äôtwas there I used to meet the girl I love.

Tell her not to cry for I‚Äôll meet her bye and bye

In a bright and a better land above.

‚ÄúTell my aged mother that she√Ę¬Ä¬ôs not to weep for me

For in the battle I took a noble part.

Break it gently to her all the news my comrade dear,

I feel that it will almost break her heart.

Tell my sister too I have kept the gift so dear

In parting which she fondly gave to me.

Although I still possess it I have stained with my life‚Äôs blood

This dear locket was a parting gift to me.

‚ÄúI feel that I am dying and my breath is going fast,

Just raise me up once more that I might see.

The moon that give us light and the watch-fires burning bright

And my comrades as happy as can be.

There now goodbye,‚Äú he cried, he then fell back and died,

Beloved by all so noble and so brave.

And early the next day he beneath the turf did lay

In a rude but a British soldier‚Äôs grave.

versions.title: Archer Goode sings A British Soldier‚Äôs Grave
versions.lyrics:
The battle was over; the stars were shining bright,

The moon shone o‚Äôer the dying and the dead.

None could be heard, save the screams of the wild birds

As they fluttered round that dying soldier‚Äôs head.

In his suit there lay one who‚Äôd nobly fought the day

And true to him, his comrade standing by.

As he in anguish cried, his comrade gently sighed,

And with his hand he wiped away a tear.

He whispered ‚ÄúGoodbye‚ÄĚ to his comrades so dear.

With his head upon his knapsack, gently lay.

‚ÄúIf you live to get home, you can tell them I am gone

And I‚Äôm lying in a British soldier‚Äôs grave.

‚ÄúDon√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you remember that dear old oak tree?

With my knife I cut my name out in the bark.

And early in the morn when I reap the golden corn

And listen to the warbling of the lark.

‚ÄúAnd that dear old country spot that will never be forgot

For that was where I used to meet the girl I loved.

Tell her not to cry, for I will meet her bye and bye

In that beautiful and happy land above.

‚ÄúTell my darling mother not to wait for me,

For in the battle I nobly took a part.

Break the news to her gently, my comrade.‚ÄĚ he cried

‚ÄúFor I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm sure it will almost break her heart.

‚ÄúTell my only sister that I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve kept this gift so rare,

That on parting she fondly gave to me.

Although now caressing, ‚Äôtis stained with my life‚Äôs blood,

Dear comrade, this locket I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll give thee.‚ÄĚ

He whispered ‚ÄúGoodbye‚ÄĚ to his comrades so dear.

With his head upon his knapsack, gently lay.

‚ÄúIf you live to get home, you can tell them I am gone

And I‚Äôm lying in a British soldier‚Äôs grave.

‚ÄúI feel that I am dying, my breath is going fast,

Raise me up dear comrade that I may see

The moon that gives us light and the watch-fires burning bright,

And our comrades as happy as can be.‚ÄĚ

Then he heaved a sigh, and then fell back and died

Loved by all with hearts so gentle and so brave.

And at the break of day his corpse they gently laid

items.title: The Echoing Horn
items.notes:
The Echoing Horn

[

/ Song Subject MAS717

The Echoing Horn

is a hunting song from the repertoire

of the Copper Family. It is printed in

The Copper Family Song Book

and in Bob Copper‚Äôs book
items.lyrics:
ūĚĄÜ The echoing horn sounds well in the morn

To call the brave sportsmen away ūĚĄá

With the cry of the hounds,

Makes a musical sound,

So greatly enlightens the day, the day,

So greatly enlightens the day.

ūĚĄÜ We√Ę¬Ä¬ôll away to some shaw to hear some brave noise

Our hounds they will open their throats ūĚĄá

When the fox he breaks cover,

Hark forward, High Over,

We will follow their musical notes, their notes,

We will follow their musical notes.

ūĚĄÜ Hedges, gates and stiles cause us no denials,

Our horses they leap them so well ūĚĄá

With a High Tally Ho,

And away we will go,

We will gallop him over the plain, the plain,

We will gallop him over the plain.

ūĚĄÜ While our hounds are at fault We will hold hard our horse,

Till the scent of the fox we have gained ūĚĄá

With the crying Hark Forward,

High Over, Hark Forward,

What pleasures can hunting excel, excel,

What pleasures can hunting excel.

ūĚĄÜ Over mountains he flies then afterwards dies,

He has led us an excellent chase ūĚĄá

We will take off his brush,

Then home we will push

In order our spirits to raise, to raise,

In order our spirits to raise.

ūĚĄÜ With our bottle and friend an evening we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll spend

We will crown the brave sports of the day ūĚĄá

Our wives will at night

Give us great delight

And solve all our sorrows away, away,

And solve all our sorrows away.

versions.title: Will Noble and John Cocking sing The Echoing Horn
versions.lyrics:
The echoing horn sounds well in the morn,

And calls the brave sportsmen away.

The cry of the hounds with pleasure rebounds,

And bravely enlightens the day, the day,

And bravely enlightens the day.

The people all go to that wonderful show,

Our hounds they do open their throats.

They cry Hark for‚Äôard, Hark for‚Äôard away,

And follow those musical notes, those notes,

And follow those musical notes.

Lets go to yon cover that lies to the south,

Bold Reynard is there, Towler doubles his mouth,

And if he breaks cover, Hark for‚Äôard high over,

We‚Äôll gallop him over yon plain, yon plain,

We‚Äôll gallop him over yon plain.

Over hedge, gate and stile and also beguile,

Our hounds they do open their throats,

Our huntsman does holler, and bravely we‚Äôll follow,

We‚Äôll follow those musical notes, those notes,

We‚Äôll follow those musical notes.

Over mountains he flies and afterwards dies,

He has led us an excellent chase.

We will take off his brush, and homeward we‚Äôll rush,

In order our spirits to raise, to raise,

In order our spirits to raise.

With a bottle and friend this evening we‚Äôll spend,

And crown the brave sport of the day.

Our sweethearts at night give us such delight

And smother all sorrows away, away,

And smother all sorrows away.

items.title: The False Bride / The Week Before Easter / I Once Loved a Lass / I Courted a Wee Girl / The Lambs on the Green Hills
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS190

, CJS2/9/505

items.lyrics:
The lambs on the green hills they sport and they play,

And many strawberries grow round the salt sea,

And many strawberries grow round the salt sea,

And many‚Äôs the ship sails the ocean.

The bride and bride‚Äôs party to church they did go,

The bride she rode foremost, she bears the best show.

But I followed after, with my heart full of woe,

To see my love wed to another.

The first place I saw her ‚Äôtwas in the church stand,

Gold rings on her finger and her love by the hand.

Says I, ‚ÄúMy wee lassie, I will be the man

Although you are wed to another.‚ÄĚ

The next place I seen her was on the way home,

I ran on before her, not knowing where to roam.

Says I, ‚ÄúMy wee lassie, I √Ę¬Ä¬ôll be by your side

Although you are wed to another.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúStop, stop,‚ÄĚ said the groomsman, ‚Äú √Ę¬Ä¬ôtill I speak a word,

Will you venture your life on the point of my sword?

For courting so slowly you‚Äôve lost this fair maid,

So begone, for you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never enjoy her.‚ÄĚ

Oh, make now my grave both large, wide and deep,

And sprinkle it over with flowers so sweet.

And lay me down in it to take my last sleep,

For that‚Äôs the best way to forget her.

versions.title: Bob Copper sings The False Bride / A Week Before Easter
versions.lyrics:
Now a week before Easter the morn bright and clear,

The sun it shone brightly and keen blew the air.

I went up in the forest to gather fine flowers,

But the forest won‚Äôt yield me no roses.

The roses are red the leaves they are green,

The bushes and briars are pleasant to be seen,

Where the small birds are singing and changing their notes

Down among the wild beasts in the forest.

Now the first time I saw my love she was dressed all in white,

Made my eyes run and water quite dazzled my sight,

When I thought to myself that I might have been that man

But she‚Äôs left me and gone with another.

Now the next time I saw my love she was in the church stand

With a ring on her finger and a glove in her hand.

So now she‚Äôs gone from me and showed me false play,

She‚Äôs gone and got tied to some other.

So dig me a grave both long, wide and deep,

And strew it all over with roses so sweet,

That I might lay down there and take a long sleep,

And that‚Äôs the right way to forget her.

versions.title: Sarah Makem sings I Courted A Wee Girl
versions.lyrics:
Well, I courted a wee girl for many‚Äôs a long day,

And I slighted all others that came in my way,

And well she rewarded me to the last day,

For she‚Äôs gone to get wed to another, another;

She‚Äôs gone to get wed to another.

For, the bride and bride‚Äôs party to church they did go.

And the bride she went forward; she bore the best show.

And I followed after with a heart full of woe.

For to see my love wed to another, another;

To see my love wed to another.

And the bride and bride‚Äôs party in church they did stand;

Gold rings on her fingers, her love by the hand,

And the man she is wed to has houses and land.

He may have her since I couldn‚Äôt gain her, gain her;

He may have her since I couldn‚Äôt gain her.

Then, the first time I saw her she was all dressed in white,

And the more I gazed on her she dazzled my sight.

I lifted my cap and I made her good night.

Adieu to all false-hearted lovers, lovers;

Adieu to all false-hearted lovers.

Then the next time I saw her she was leaving down meat.

I sat down beside her; not a bite could I eat,

For I thought my love‚Äôs company far better than meat,

Since love was the cause of my ruin, ruin;

Since love was the cause of my ruin.

Oh, it‚Äôs dig me a grave and dig it down deep,

And strew it all over with the red rose so sweet,

And lay me down silent no more for to weep.

For love was the cause of my ruin, ruin;

For love was the cause of my ruin.

versions.title: A.L. Lloyd sings The False Bride
versions.lyrics:
Oh, when that I saw my love in the church stand,

With the ring on her finger and the glove in her hand,

I jumped in betwixt them and kissed the false bride,

Saying: ‚ÄúAdieu to false loves for ever.‚ÄĚ

Oh, when that I saw my love out the church go,

With the brides-men and bridesmaids they made a fine show,

Then I followed after with my heart full of woe,

For I was the man that ought to had her.

Oh, when that I saw my love sat down to meat,

I sat myself by her but nothing could eat.

I thought her sweet company better than wine,

Although she was tied to some other.

Go dig me a grave both long, wide, and deep,

And strew it all over with flowers so sweet,

That I may lay down there and take my long sleep,

And that‚Äôs the best way to forget her.

versions.title: Shirley Collins sings The False Bride
versions.lyrics:
I courted a bonny girl for many‚Äôs the day,

And hated all people who ‚Äôgainst her did say.

But now she‚Äôs rewarded me well for my pains

For she‚Äôs gone to get tied to another.

The week before Easter, the morn bright and clear,

When the sun it shone brightly and keen blew the air,

I went down to the forest to gather fine flowers

But the forest won‚Äôt yield me no roses.

The first time I saw my love it was to the church go,

The bride and the bridegroom they cut a fine show.

While I followed after, my heart full of woe,

For to see my love tied to another.

The parson that married them aloud he did cry,

All you‚Äôd who‚Äôd forbid it, I‚Äôd have you draw nigh.

Well, thought I to myself, I‚Äôd a good reason why,

Though I had not the heart to forbid it.

The next time I saw my love, it was in the church stand,

A gold ring on her finger, white gloves on her hand.

Thought I to myself, I should have been that man,

Though I‚Äôd never once mentioned to have her.

And the last time I saw my love, she was all dressed in white,

Made my eyes fill with tears, they quite dazzled my sight.

So I picked up my hat and I wished her good night,

Here‚Äôs adieu to all false-hearted true loves.

The ladies and gentlemen they are all asking me,

‚ÄúHow many lilies grow in the salt sea?‚ÄĚ

But I‚Äôll ask them back with a tear in my eye,

‚ÄúHow many ships sail in the forest?‚ÄĚ

Go dig my grave both long, wide and deep,

versions.title: Graham and Eileen Pratt sing Lambs in the Green Hills

versions.title: Rod Stradling sings The Week Before Easter

items.lyrics:
There was an old farmer in Cheshire

To market his daughter did go

And thinking that no one would harm her

For she‚Äôd often been that way before

(repeat last two lines of each verse)

But this time she met with a Highwayman

His pistol he drew to her breast

Saying, ‚ÄúDeliver your money your clothing.

Or you will die in distress.‚ÄĚ

Now he stripped the damsel stark naked

And he gave her the reigns for to hold

And there she stood shivering and shaking

Almost frozen to death in the cold

Then she put her right foot in the stirrups

And saddled her horse like A man

Over hedges and ditches she galloped

crying get me you rogue if you can

Now the rogue he so quickly followed after

And he made his old horse puff and blow

And finding he couldn‚Äôt overtake her

For she reached her father‚Äôs own door

‚ÄúOh daughter, oh daughter what√Ę¬Ä¬ôs happened?

You√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been at the market so long‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh father, I have been in great danger,

But the rogue he has done me no harm.‚ÄĚ

Then she put her old horse in the stable,

And then she sat counting the money,

versions.title: Caroline Hughes sings The Rich Farmer From Chesfield

‚ÄúCome, catch me, bold rogues, af you can!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúDear father, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been in great danger,

versions.title: Harry Green sings The Merchant‚Äôs Daughter and the Highwayman

It‚Äôs of an old merchant‚Äôs fair daughter,

‚ÄúThe girl you may patiently take it,

‚ÄúDear daughter where have you been tarrying,

‚ÄúDear father I have been robb-ed,

‚ÄúThree thousand pound is a good fortune,

versions.title: Joe Jones sings The Farmer of Chester

‚ÄúDear daughter, dear daughter, what√Ę¬Ä¬ôs happened?

versions.title: Bob Copper sings The Farmer in Cheshire
versions.lyrics:

‚ÄúO father, I fell in great danger,
