A thousand, yes a thousand times o‚Äôer.

versions.title: Betsy Renals sings The Farmer from Leicester
versions.lyrics:
There was an old farmer in Leicester,

Had a daughter to market did go;

She was never afraid of no danger,

‚ÄôCos she‚Äôd been on the highway before.

Till she met with a bold and young robber,

Six chamber he held to her breast;

Saying, ‚ÄúDeliver your money and clothing,

Or else you shall die in distress.‚ÄĚ

He stripped the young damsel stark naked,

And the bridle rein gave her to hold;

And there she‚Äôs such shivering and shaking,

And starving to death with the cold.

She put her left foot in the stirrup,

And she mounted her horse like a man;

Over hedges and ditches she galloped,

Saying, ‚ÄúCatch me bold rogue if you can.‚ÄĚ

She rode to the gates of her father‚Äôs,

And shouted all over the farm;

Saying, ‚ÄúDear father I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been in great danger,

But the rogue he have done me no harm.‚ÄĚ

She pulled her grey mare in the stable,

And she spread her white sheet on the floor;

And counted the money twice over,

There were three thousand pounds if not more.

versions.title: Charlie Stringer sings The Farmer From Cheshire
versions.lyrics:
There was a rich farmer in Cheshire,

And his daughter to market would go.

Thinking of no harm or danger,

For she‚Äôd been on the highway before.

She met with an uncivil roadster,

Two pistols he drew from his side.

Saying, ‚ÄúDeliver up your money and clothing,

Or you will die in distress.‚ÄĚ

He stripped that poor lady stark naked,

But she mounted that mare like a man;

She galloped over hedgerows and ditches,

‚ÄôTil she came to her father‚Äôs door.

‚ÄúO daughter, O daughter, what√Ę¬Ä¬ôs happened?

Why stayest you late from the fair?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO father, I have been in great danger,

But the villain has done me no harm.‚ÄĚ

She put the grey mare in the stable,

And spread the white cloth on the floor.

She counted her money twice over,

And she counted a thousand and more.

But now she‚Äôs a carriage to ride in,

And a coachman to ride by her side,

Servants to wait at the table,

And plenty of money besides.

versions.title: Wiggy Smith sings There Was a Rich Farmer at Sheffield
versions.lyrics:
O, there was a rich farmer at Sheffield,

And to market his daughter did go

His daughter not being afraid though

She‚Äôd been on the highway-oh before.

She met with three bold-faced ‚Ä¶ they was robbers,

Three pistols they held at her breast

‚ÄúFor it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs money and clothery we declare,

√Ę¬Ä¬ôr else we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll take your sweet life in distress.‚ÄĚ

They ripped the poor girl stark naked

And they gave her the bridle to hold

‚ÄúNow it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs money and clothes we declare

Else we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll take your sweet life in distress.‚ÄĚ

She put her left leg in the stirrup

And she mounted her horse like a man

It‚Äôs over hedges and ditches she galloped,

‚ÄúNow come catch me bold rogues if you can.‚ÄĚ

She rode to the gates of her father,

And she shouted all over the farm

‚ÄúDear Father, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been in great danger

But those rogues they hanna done me no harm.‚ÄĚ

She put her grey mare in the stable,

And she spread the white sheet on the floor

She counted her money twice over

Five hundred bright pounds and some more.

He said, ‚ÄúDear daughter, if you have that fortune,

On top of that I will give you more,

And if ever you live to get married,

It√Ę¬Ä¬ôll keep the cold wind from your door.‚ÄĚ

items.lyrics:
I would call for liquors plenty,

Spoken: Now that‚Äôs a lovely song

versions.title: Shirley Collins sings The Irish Girl
versions.lyrics:
As I rode out one morning down by the riverside,

Her cheeks were red and rosy and curling was her hair,

Yes, as I rode out that morning, my true love passing by,

I knew her love was changing by the roving of her eye.

By the roving of her eye, by the roving of her eye,

I knew her love was changing by the roving of her eye.

Oh say, don‚Äôt you remember, love, when you gave me our right hand?

You vowed if you ever married that I would be the man.

That I would be the man, that I would be the man,

You said if you ever married that I would be the man.

I wish I was in London or in some sea-port town,

I‚Äôd set my foot on yonder ship and I‚Äôd sail the ocean round.

I‚Äôll sailing on the deep, my love, I‚Äôll sailing on the deep,

I‚Äôll think on handsome married just as I go to sea.

versions.title: Lemmie Brazil sings The Irish Girl
versions.lyrics:
As I walked out one morning clear, it was in the summer time,

I gazed all right round me, and an Irish girl I spied.

Red and rosy was her cheek and coal black was her hair,

Oh I wish I was in old Dublin town a-sittin on the grass,

I‚Äôd call for liquors merrily, I‚Äôd pay before I go,

Sure I‚Äôd roll my lass upon the grass; let the wind blow high or low.

I wish my love was a red, red rose that in the garden grows,

And if I was a gardener along with my love renewn; [or, I‚Äôd go]

And every month all through the year ‚Äôlong with my love renewn,

I‚Äôd sit and sing for you Polly, for once I loved you true.

I wish I was a butterfly, I‚Äôd set on my love‚Äôs breast,

And if I was a linnet I would sing my love to rest;

And if I was a nightingale, I‚Äôd sing the mornings clear,

I‚Äôd sit and sing for you Polly, for once I loved you dear.

Oh her shoes was made of the best Spanish leather and neatly being tied,

The tears rolled down her rosy cheek, until she began to cry;

‚ÄúWhere are you going Johnny,‚ÄĚ cried she, ‚ÄúGive to me your gr√É¬° mo chro√É¬≠.

How can you go a-roamin√Ę¬Ä¬ô to slight your dear Polly?‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Sarah Makem sings I Wish My Love Was a Red Red Rose
versions.lyrics:
I wish my love was a red red rose blowin‚Äô in yon garden fair

And me to be the gardener of her I would take care

There‚Äôs not a month throughout the year but my love I‚Äôd renew

I wish I was a butterfly, I‚Äôd light on my love‚Äôs breast

And if I was a blue cuckoo I‚Äôd sing my love to rest

And if I was a nightingale I‚Äôd sing ‚Äôtil daylight clear

I‚Äôd sit and sing for you, Molly, for once I loved you dear.

I wish I was in Dublin and sitting on the grass

I‚Äôd call for liquor freely and I‚Äôd pay before I‚Äôd go

And I‚Äôd roll her in my arms, let the wind blow high or low.

versions.title: Bob Copper sings As I Walked Out
versions.lyrics:
As I walked out one May morning down by a riverside

I gazed all around me when an Irish girl I spied.

So red and rosy were her cheeks and coal black was her hair

And costly was the robe of gold this Irish girl did wear.

Her shoes were made of Spanish leather, all sprinkled o‚Äôer with dew,

She wrung her hands, she tore her hair crying, ‚ÄúOh, what shall I do?

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm a-going home, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm a-going home, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm a-going home,‚ÄĚ said she,

How can I go a-roving and slight my own Johnny?‚ÄĚ

I wish I was a butterfly, I would fly to my love‚Äôs breast,

And if I were a linnet I would sing my love to rest,

And if I was a nightingale I would sing till the morning clear,

I‚Äôd sit and sing to you, Polly, for once I loved you dear.

I wish I was a red rose bud that in the garden grew

And if I was that gardener to my love I would prove true.

There is not one month throughout the year of what my love I would renew,

With flowers three I would garnish thee, sweet william, thyme and rue.

I wish I was in Dublin town a-romping on the grass

I would call for liquor merrily, I would spend my money free,

One night as I lay on my bed so sick and bad was I

But there‚Äôs many more as bad as me so why should I complain,

For love it is a funny thing, did you ever feel the pain?

versions.title: Walter Pardon sings Let the Wind Blow High or Low
versions.lyrics:
One night when I was walking down by the riverside,

Gazing all around me when an Irish girl I spied.

Red and rosy were here cheeks, lovely coal black was her hair;

Costly were those lovely robes this Irish girl did wear.

Her shoes were black, her stockings white, all sprinkled with dew.

She wrung her hands and tore her hair and cried, ‚ÄúAlas, what shall I do?

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm going home, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm going home, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm going home,‚ÄĚ said she,

‚ÄúO would you go a-roving to slight your own Polly?‚ÄĚ

The very last time I saw my love, he seemed to be in pain,

With heartfelt grief and chilling woe, his heart it seemed near broke in twain.

There√Ę¬Ä¬ôs many a man more true than he, so why should I e√Ę¬Ä¬ôer complain‚ÄĒ

Love it is a killing thing; did you ever feel the pain?

I wish I were a butterfly, I would fly to my true love‚Äôs breast.

I wish I were a linnet, I would sing my love to rest.

I wish I were a nightingale, I would sing to the morning clear.

I‚Äôll sit and sing for you, Polly, the girl I love so dear.

I wish my love were a red rose bud who in the garden grew,

And I to be the gardener; to her I ever would prove true.

There‚Äôs not a month in all the year but my love I would renew,

The lilies I would garnish, sweet William, thyme and rue

I wish I were in Manchester, a-sitting on the grass,

I‚Äôd call for liquor merrily and I would pay before I go,

I‚Äôll roll my lass all on the grass, let the wind blow high or low.

versions.title: Levi Smith sings The Irish Girl

1
For I wish I was in old Dublin Town for a-sitting on the grass

versions.title: James McDermott sings Let the Wind Blow High or Low
versions.lyrics:

Crying, ‚Äú Aroo , what shall I do, for the maid of A St√É¬≥r mo Chroi ?

I wish I was a butterfly, I‚Äôd light on my love‚Äôs breast.

versions.title: Bill Smith sings The Irish Girl

I wish my love was a red rose bud and in the garden grew

items.title: The Jolly Ploughboy / Two Young Brethren / The Two Brothers
items.notes:
[

items.lyrics:
There once was two brothers, two brothers there were,

And they was two brothers born.

The one was a shepherd, a shearer of sheep,

And the other a planter of corn.

God bid them sow fields and the trees for to plant

Their race grew from sturdy fine stock,

Where nothing once dwelt but the wild beasts of prey

Their fortune increased with their flock.

King David whose songs have so often been sung

At first wasn‚Äôt noble and grand,

But only a shepherd boy when he was young

Though afterwards King of the land.

And then there‚Äôs bold Samson in Judges you‚Äôll find,

He delight in his darling so dear.

What a blockhead he was for to tell her his mind

For so quickly his strength she did shear.

Here‚Äôs April, here‚Äôs May, likewise June and July,

It‚Äôs a pleasure to see the corn grow.

In August we‚Äôll moil it, shear low and reap high,

And we‚Äôll bind up our scythes for to mow.

And when we have gathered up every sheaf,

And scrap√®d up every ear,

Let‚Äôs have no more ado but to ploughing we‚Äôll go

To provide for the very next year.

So come all jolly ploughboys and help me to sing

I‚Äôll sing in the praise of the plough.

For if we don‚Äôt labour how shall we get bread?

So do it my jolly boys now.

versions.title: The Copper Family sing Two Young Brethren
versions.lyrics:
Come all jolly ploughmen and help me to sing,

I will sing in the praise of you all,

If a man he don‚Äôt labour how can he get bread?

I will sing and make merry withal.

It was of two young brethren, two young brethren born,

It was of two young brethren born,

One he was a shepherd and a tender of sheep

The other a planter of corn.

We will rile it, we will tile it through mud and through clay,

We will plough it up deeper and low,

Then after comes the seedsman his corn for to sow

And the harrows to rake it in rows.

There is April, there is May, there is June and July

What a pleasure it is for to see the corn grow.

In August we will reap it, we will cut, sheaf and bind it

And go down with our scythes for to mow.

And after we‚Äôve reaped it off every sheaf

And have gathered up every ear,

With a drop of good beer, boys, and our hearts full of cheer

We will wish them another good year.

Our barns they are full, our fields they are clear,

Good health to our master and friends.

We will make no more to do but we‚Äôll plough and we‚Äôll sow

And prepare for the very next year.

versions.title: John Kirkpatrick sings Come All You Jolly Ploughboys
versions.lyrics:
Come, all you jolly ploughboys, and help me to sing;

I‚Äôll sing in the praise of you all.

For if we don‚Äôt labour, how can there be bread?

I will sing and make merry withal.

It was of two young brethren, two brethren of old,

It was of two young brethren born,

Oh, the one was a shepherd and a tender of sheep

And the other a planter of corn.

We will moil it, we will toil it through mire and through clay

We will plough, we cut deeper and low,

And after comes the seedsman, his corn for to sow,

And the harrow to rake it in rows.

Here‚Äôs April, here is May, here is June and July

What pleasure to see the corn grow!

In August it rip‚Äôneth, we reap and sheaves tie

And go down with our scythes for to mow.

And when we have gathered up every sheaf

And gleaned up every ear,

We will make no more to do but to plough and to sow

And provide for the harvest next year.

items.title: The London Heiress / Brisk and Lively Lad / A Rich Farmer‚Äôs Daughter

Brisk and Lively Lad

is a song from the repertoire of the

Copper Family. Bob Copper sang it in a BBC recording

( BBC 21545 )

made by Peter Kennedy at the Central Club , Peacehaven,
items.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of a brisk and lively lad come out of Gloucestershire,

And all his full intention was to court some lady fair.

Her eyes shone bright like the morning dew that does on the lily lie,

She was grace all in her face all mixed with modesty.

As these two lovers were walking they knew each other well

When someone heard them talking and did her father tell.

And when he came for to hear the same and to understand the thing,

Then said he, ‚Äú√Ę¬Ä¬ôTwill never be, I will part them in the spring.‚ÄĚ

It was in the springtime of the year there was a press begun

And all their full intention was to press that farmer‚Äôs son.

‚ÄôTwas to press him and to send him far over the raging sea,

Where I‚Äôm sure he will no more keep my daughter‚Äôs company.

On the twenty-first of August there was a fight begun

And foremost in the battle did they place this farmer‚Äôs son.

There he received a dreadful wound in the hollow of his thigh,

Every vein was filled with pain he got wounded dreadfully.

She went straight to the Captain as Captain‚Äôs handy mate

And everything he said to her she agreed to undertake.

So tenderly she dressed his wounds which so bitterly did smart

Then said he, ‚ÄúA one like thee once was mistress of my heart.‚ÄĚ

She went straight to the Commander and offered very fair,

‚ÄúForty or fifty guineas shall buy my love quite clear.

No money shall be wanted no longer tarry here,

Since √Ę¬Ä¬ôtis so pray let us go, to old England we will steer.‚ÄĚ

She went up to her father‚Äôs gate and stood there for a while

He said, ‚ÄúLord in heaven bless me, there√Ę¬Ä¬ôs my dear and only child.‚ÄĚ

She said, ‚ÄúFather, I have found him and brought him safe on shore.

We will spend our days in England, never roam abroad.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Sarah Makem sings A Rich Farmer‚Äôs Daughter
versions.lyrics:
Oh, there was a rich farmer‚Äôs daughter and she loved a poor farmer‚Äôs son,

And her father‚Äôs only care was to match this nice young man.

Well matched was he, and sent to sea, where the billows do loudly roar.

‚ÄúOh, alas,‚ÄĚ cried he, ‚Äúoh, where is she? Will I ever see her more?‚ÄĚ

It was on the twelfth day of July the battle it began,

And right in front of the enemy they placed this nice young man.

‚ÄôTil he received a deadly wound that nearly pierced his heart.

‚ÄúOh, alas,‚ÄĚ cried he, ‚Äúoh, where is she? Come quickly to my heart.‚ÄĚ

Oh, when this young man got wounded to the cabin he was brought.

And in this little cabin there dwelled a nice wee maid,

And every time she turned around he viewed her in every part.

‚ÄúOh, alas,‚ÄĚ cried he, ‚Äúit was one like she was mistress of my heart.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh,‚ÄĚ she says, ‚Äúyoung man, you are quite right, I approve of your discharge.

Here is five hundred pounds in gold to free yourself at large.

And go unto the captain, and he will set you free,

And we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll both sail home to old Ireland across the stormy sea.‚ÄĚ

Ah then, when they came to her father‚Äôs gates they tarried there for a while.

When out came her father saying, ‚ÄúHere is my only child.

The child that I have been looking for this twelve long months or more,

And the lad I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been in search of is the villain that has sailed o√Ę¬Ä¬ôer.‚ÄĚ

Then he took her by the lily white hand and bade her to come in.

‚ÄúOh no, kind honoured Father, unless that you bid him.

It was for his sake I went away, and proudly faced the war.

You may think your gold is a treasured thing, but love it beats it far.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Mabs Hall sings The Farmer Out of Gloucestershire
versions.lyrics:
There was a jolly farmer came out of Gloucestershire

And all his intentions were to court some lady fair,

Whose eyes shone like the morning star, whose hair was crimp and gay,

She had grace within her face that was mixed with modesty.

As these two lovers stood talking, they loved each other well,

Some person overheard them and her father they did tell.

He promised her he would send him, far over the raging main

That he may no longer keep his fond daughter company.

It was in the springtime of the year, when pressing first began.

In the thickest of the battle then, they placed the farmers son,

Where he did receive a dreadful wound, in the hollow of his thigh.

In the veins, he felt such pains, he was wounded dreadfully.

Then he was safely protected up to a sergeant laid

And the one he fixed his eye upon was the sergeant‚Äôs pretty maid.

Most tenderly she dressed his wounds and bitter they did smart

Then said he, ‚ÄúOne like thee was the mystery of my heart.‚ÄĚ

Straight up to his commander and offered very large,

It was five hundred sovereigns to buy my loves discharge

No money shall be wanted, farewell forever do

You can spend your days in old England and roam abroad no more.

And when she came to her father‚Äôs gate where they had both been before,

So happy that young couple were to think they were safe on shore.

Saying, ‚Äú Father I have found him, and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve brought him safe on shore,

We will spend our days, in old England.‚ÄĚ

items.title: The London Man o‚Äô War / Warlike Seamen / The Irish Captain / The Dolphin
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS209

; Master title: The London Man o‚Äô War

; G/D 1:41

; Henry H560

, ChFRS066

, HHH560

, GG/1/3/85

items.lyrics:
Come all you warlike seamen that to the seas belong,

I‚Äôll tell you of a fight, my boys, on board The Nottingham ;

It was of an Irish captain, his name was Somerville,

With courage bold did he control, he played his part so well.

‚ÄôTwas on the 8 th of June, my boys, when at Spithead we lay,

On board there came an order; our anchor for to weigh;

Bound for the coast of Ireland our order did run so,

For us to cruise and not refuse against a daring foe.

We had not sailed many lengths at sea before a ship we spied,

She being some lofty Frenchman, come a-bearing down so wide,

We hailed her off France, my boys, she asked from whence we came,

Our answer was, ‚ÄúFrom Liverpool and London is our name.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO pray, are you some man-of-war, or pray what may you be?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO then,‚ÄĚ replied our captain, ‚ÄúAnd that you soon shall see;

Come strike your English colours or else you shall bring to;

Since you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre so stout, you shall give out or else we will sink you.‚ÄĚ

The first broadside we gave to them, which made them for to wonder,

Their mainmast and their rigging came a-rattling down like thunder,

We drove them from their quarters, they could no longer stay,

Our guns did roar, we made so sure, we showed them British play.

So now we‚Äôve took that ship, my boys, Godspeed us fair wind

That we might sail to Plymouth town, if the Heavens prove so kind;

We‚Äôll drink a health unto our captain and all such warlike souls,

To him we‚Äôll drink, and never flinch, out of a flowing bowl.

versions.title: Sam Larner sings The Dolphin
versions.lyrics:
Our ship she laid in harbour, in Liverpool docks and more,

Awaiting for fresh orders her anchor for to weigh;

Bound down to the coast of Africa, our orders did run so:

We‚Äôre going to sink and destroy, my boys, no matter where we go.

We had not been sailing scarce fifty leagues or more,

When we espied a lofty ship and down on us he bore;

He hailed us in French colours, he asked us where and whence we came.

‚ÄúWe just come down from Liverpool town and the Dolphin is our name.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAre you a man-of-war, sir? Pray tell me what you be.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI am no man-of-war, sir, but a pirate ship you see.

Come heave up your fore and main yards and let your ship come to,

Our tackles are overhauled and our boats are all lowered, or else we will sink you.‚ÄĚ

Now our Captain stood on the quarterdeck, he was brave and fearless too.

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs three to one against us,‚ÄĚ he cried out to his crew;

‚ÄúIf it hadn√Ę¬Ä¬ôt have been for my younger brother, this battle would never been tried.

Let every man stand true to his guns and we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll give to them a broadside.‚ÄĚ

Now broadside to broadside which caused all hands to wonder,

To see that French lofty‚Äôs mast come rattling down like thunder;

We shot them from our quarterdeck ‚Äôtil they could no longer stay,

Our guns being smart and we played a fine part and we gave them Liverpool play.

Now this large French ship was taken and in Liverpool docks was moored,

We fired shots to our sweethearts with the nice little girls on shore;

We lowered down the French colours, and we hoisted the red, white and blue,

We‚Äôll drink success to the Dolphin and all her jovial crew.

Spoken: How‚Äôs that one? That‚Äôs another one.

versions.title: Tony Rose sings Come All You Warlike Seamen
versions.lyrics:
Come all you warlike seamen that to the seas belong;

I‚Äôll tell you of a fight, my boys, on board the Nottingham.

It was of an Irish captain, his name was Somerville,

With courage bold he did control, he played his part so well.

‚ÄôTwas on the eighth of June, my boys, when at Spithead we lay,

On board there came an order, our anchor for to weigh.

Bound for the coast of Ireland, our orders did run so:

For us to cruise and not refuse against a daring foe.

We had not sailed many lengths at sea before a ship we spied.

She being some lofty Frenchman come a-bearing down so wide.

We hailed her off France, my boys, she asked from whence we came.

Our answer was, ‚ÄúFrom Liverpool, and London is our name.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh pray are you some man-of-war, oh pray, what may you be?‚ÄĚ

Oh then replied our captain, ‚ÄúWhy, that you soon shall see.

Come strike your English colours or else you shall bring to.

Since you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre so stout, you shall give out, or else we will sink you.‚ÄĚ

The first broadside we gave to them, it caused them for to wonder.

Their mainmast and their rigging came a-rattling down like thunder.

We drove them from their quarters, they could no longer stay.

Our guns did roar, we made so sure we showed them British play.

So now we‚Äôve took that ship, my boys, God speed us fair wind

That we might sail to Plymouth town if the heavens prove so kind.

We‚Äôll drink a health unto our captain and all such warlike souls.

To him we‚Äôll drink, and never flinch, out of a flowing bowl.

versions.title: Lillian Lugg sings The Fourteenth of December
versions.lyrics:
On the fourteenth of December in Plymouth Sound we lay

Waiting for fresh orders our anchor for to weigh.

We‚Äôre bound for the coast of Africa, our orders they came slow;

We‚Äôre bound to sink and destroy, my lads, where ever we may go.

We had not been sailing for twenty knots or more,

Before we spied a large ship and down on us she bore;

She hailed us with French colours and asked from whence we came.

‚ÄúWe√Ę¬Ä¬ôve just set sail from Plymouth Sound and the Cambridge is our name.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAre you a man-of-war, sir, or pray what may you be?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm not a man-of-war, sir, but a pirate as you see.

Then pull around your main yards and let your ship come to,

With your boats all lowered and your ropes all torn, or else I will sink you,

With your boats all lowered and your ropes all torn, or else I will sink you!‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke the captain and braveley he did say,

‚ÄúCheer up, cheer up, my bonny lads, we√Ę¬Ä¬ôre bound to win the day!

If this were my own brother this battle I‚Äôll define.

Let every man stand true to his gun and we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll give to them the first broadside!‚ÄĚ

The first broadside was given which caused all hands to wonder,

To see the foretop gallant mast come roaring down like thunder;

We beat her from all quarters till they could no longer stay,

For with powder and shot we peppered them hot and showed them British play.

Now when this large ship was captured for Plymouth Sound we bore,

We hailed off one of our long guns to warn our girls on shore;

They down with her French colours and up with the red, white and blue,

We‚Äôll drink success to the Dolphin and all her saucy crew.

versions.title: Ninebarrow sing Lord Exmouth
versions.lyrics:
Come all you British men of war

That love your native land!

I‚Äôll have you join The Victory

Lord Exmouth gives command

Lord Exmouth does his rights maintain

As you shall quickly see

We fought like many lions bold

To set old England free

So come all you British tars

And maintain your glorious name

May you always leave Lord Exmouth though

To lead you over the main

Oh, the first broadside we gave to them

We took ‚Äôem by surprise

We shot away their ensign staff

And down their colours lie

That‚Äôs very well done our captain cried

That‚Äôs bravely done cried he

Come load your guns, we‚Äôll prime them well

And show them British play

So come all you tars be steady

And maintain your glorious name

May you always leave Lord Exmouth though

To lead you over the main

Oh, the next broadside we gave to them

We struck them such a wonder

Their lower yards, their maintop masts

Came clattering down like thunder

That‚Äôs very well done our captain cried

That‚Äôs bravely done cried he

Come load your guns, we‚Äôll prime them well

And show them British play

Oh, now that we have won the glory

Let us drink a glass of wine

And if you will drink to your true love

Then I will drink to mine

items.lyrics:
As I was a-walking down by the sea-shore

Where the wind and the waves and the billows do roar,

There I heard a strange voice make a terrible sound.

‚ÄôTwas the wind and the waves and the echoes all round.

Chorus (after each verse):

Crying, ‚ÄúO-oh my love has gone

He‚Äôs a youth I adore

He√Ę¬Ä¬ôs gone and I never shall see him no more.‚ÄĚ

She‚Äôd a voice like a nightingale, skin like a dove

And the song that she sung it was all about love.

I asked her to marry me, marry me, please,

But the answer she gave, ‚ÄúMy love√Ę¬Ä¬ôs drowned in the sea.‚ÄĚ

I told her I‚Äôd gold and I‚Äôd silver beside

In a coach and six horses with me she could ride.

‚ÄúNo I never will marry nor yet make a wife;

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll stay constant and true all the time I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve got life.‚ÄĚ

She threw out her arms and she took a great leap

From the cliffs that were high to the billows so deep,

Crying, ‚ÄúThe rocks of the ocean shall make me a bed

And the shrimps of the sea shall swim over my head.‚ÄĚ

And now every night at six bells they appear

When the moon it is shining, the sky it is clear,

These two constant lovers with all their young charms

Rolling over and over in each other‚Äôs arms.

versions.title: Gordon Hall sings The Constant Lovers
versions.lyrics:
Legend gives us a happy ending to this lovely old song

I pray you pay attention, I shan‚Äôt keep you long.

When Phoebe leapt from the clifftops to the wild billows roar

There a bloody big bramble snar√®d up in her drawers

And she cried o-o-o-oh, my love is gone

That sweet youth I adore

And I‚Äôm left a-swinging, by my calico drawers.

A young naval lieutenant, so salt and so true

Was patrolling inshore for King George‚Äôs Revenue

When he spied that young damsel through his open glass

He said: I knows that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs my Phoebe by the size of her ‚ÄĒ

Ah-ah-ah-ah, my lover‚Äôs saved

That sweet girl I adore

She‚Äôs been saved by the bramble and her calico drawers.

Well he lowered a boat and he rode for the shore

And he brought that fair damsel to safety once more

Straight away to the church, where they married in speed

Now in a cot by the seaside they live happy indeed

Crying o-o-o-oh, my lover‚Äôs saved

That sweet love I adore

She‚Äôs been saved by a lawyer, and her calico drawers.

And so now every morn when the sun shines so clear

This constant young couple earn a fortune in gold

By exhibiting the scars where the brambles took hold

Crying o-o-o-oh, my lover‚Äôs saved

We‚Äôve got bright gold in store

And it all came through wearing those calico drawers.

items.lyrics:
‚ÄôTwas in the pleasant month of May in the springtime of the year,

And down by yonder meadow there runs a river clear,

See how the little fishes how they do sport and play

Causing many a lad and many a lass to go there a-making hay.

Then in comes the scytheman that meadow to mow down,

With his old leathered bottle and the ale that runs so brown.

There‚Äôs many a stout and labouring man comes here his skill to try,

He works, he mows, he sweats and blows and the grass cuts very dry.

Then in comes both Tom and Dick with their pitch-forks and their rakes

And likewise black-eyed Susan the hay all for to make.

There‚Äôs a sweet, sweet, sweet and a jug, jug, jug, how the harmless birds did sing,

From the morning till the evening as we were a-haymaking.

It was just at one evening as the sun was a-going down,

We saw the jolly piper come a-strolling through the town.

There he pulled out his tabor and pipes and he made the valley ring,

So we all put down our rakes and forks and left off haymaking.

We call√®d for a dance and we tripp√®d it along,

We danced all round the haycocks till the rising of the sun.

When the sun did shine such a glorious light and the harmless birds did sing,

Each lad he took his lass in hand and went back to his haymaking.

versions.title: Sam Larner sings The Pleasant Month of May
versions.lyrics:
‚ÄôTwas in the pleasant month of May in the springtime of the year,

It was down by yonder meadows there runs a river clear.

Look at those little fishes! See how they sport and play!

Make many a lad and many a lass go there for to make hay.

Now, in comes the jolly scythesman the hay for to mow down,

With his good old leathering bottle and the beer that is so brown,

There‚Äôs many of stout labouring men go there their skill to try,

For they cut, they mow, they lace, they blow, and the grass cut very dry.

Now, in come the jolly scythesmen with a pitchfork and a rake,

And likewise black-eyed Susan, the hay all for to make.

There‚Äôs a long jug jug and sweet jug and the nightingale did sing,

From the morning till the evening we went a-haymaking.

Now, the day being almost over and night was coming on,

We laid among the cocks of hay till rising of the sun,

And the larks did sing most gloriously and each harmless bird did sing,

Then each lad he took his lassie and they went a-haymaking.

There‚Äôs many of stout labouring men go there their skill to try

For they cut, they mow, they lace, they blow and the grass cut very dry.

Spoken: Well, you repeat that. That‚Äôs a little ditty.

versions.title: Levi Smith sings The Haymakers
versions.lyrics:
For it‚Äôs all in the pleasure of month of May in the springtime of the year,

For over yonder meadow, boys, there runs a river clear.

For you see those little fishes, for you see those sport and play,

But there‚Äôs many lads and bonny lads, we was brought to making hay.

Now, there comes Paul and Peter with their pitchforks and their rakes,

For likewise dark-eyed Susan, for the hay we had to make.

For she pulled out the pipe of ivory and this is what she reply,

But we all chucked down our forks and rakes, and we left off making hay.

Chorus

Oh, jug, oh, sweet jug, oh, drink to the morning‚Äôs dew,

That we all chucked down our forks and rakes, and we left off making hay.

versions.title: Joe Holmes sings Tumbling Through the Hay
versions.lyrics:
It being in the month of July in the rosy time of the year,

Down by yon flowery meadows where the waters does run clear,

Where the lambs and little fishes do merrily sport and play,

And the lads and the lasses they go tumbling through the hay.

Chorus (after each verse):

La de dee die de tol da lee

La de dee die la de die

La de dee die de tol da lee.

Then up came lovely Johnny with a pitchfork and a rake,

And up came lovely Molly the hay then for to make,

For they timed their notes so merrily as the nightingale did sing,

And from morning until evening they were at their haymaking.

Then up came the mowers the hay for to cut down,

With their scythes upon their shoulders and their hair a lovely brown,

Then up came the labourers the hay for to shake out,

And when they had it all cut down they tossed it all about.

It was coming up to Saturday and all would get their pay,

And all these jolly haymakers were feeling blithe and gay,

The number of these haymakers as near as I can say

Were five and twenty boys and girls tumbling through the hay.

When one short year were over and all was past and gone,

There were five and twenty fair maids making their sad moan,

Singing, Hush-la ba-baby, these fair maids did say,

And many‚Äôs a time they wished they ne‚Äôer had tossed among the hay.

From morning until evening they were at the haymaking

Then up come the mowers the hay for to cut down

With their scythes upon their shoulders and their hair a lovely brown

Then up come the labourers the hay for to shake out

And when they‚Äôve got it all cut down they tossed it all about

It was coming up to Saturday and all would get their fee

Aye, and all these jolly haymakers were feeling blithe and free

The number of these haymakers as near as I can say

Was five and twenty boys and girls a tumbling through the hay

When nine short months were over and all was passed and gone

There were five and twenty boys and girls a making their sad moan

O hush a bye babby, these fair maids they did say

And many‚Äôs the time they wished they ne‚Äôer had tumbled in the hay

versions.title: Jon Wilks sings Haymaking Song
versions.lyrics:
‚ÄôTwas was in the merry month of May

In the springtime of the year

When down in yonder meadow

There runs a river clear.

To see those little fishes

How they do sport and play,

Calling many a lad and many a lass

All there to making hay.

Then in come both Will and Tom

With pitchfork and with rake,m

And likewise black-eyed Susan

The hay all there to make.

With sweet jug and sweet jug

How the nightingale does sing,

From the morning to the evening

As we go haymaking.

Then just as Bright Phoebus

The sun was going down,

Along came two piping men

Approaching from the town.

They pulled out their tabor and pipes

Which made those pretty girls to sing,

Then they all put down their forks and rakes

And left off haymaking.

items.title: The Nobleman and the Thresherman / The Jolly Thresher / The Honest Labourer
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS510

; Master title: The Nobleman and the Thresherman

; G/D 3:437

; Henry H622, H117

; TYG 37

; Ballad Index R127

; VWML CJS2/9/1774

, CJS2/10/1921

38
items.lyrics:
As I walked out one morning, all in the month of May,

I met a jolly thrasher and unto him did say,

With a staff upon his shoulder and a bottle of good beer,

He was as happy as a lord who had a thousand pounds a year.

‚ÄúThresher, o thresher, come tell unto me how,

Can you maintain your family with only but one cow?

Your family it is large, your wages it is small,

And how you do maintain them I know not at all.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúSometimes a-ditchin√Ę¬Ä¬ô or a-hedgin√Ę¬Ä¬ô I go,

Sometimes I reap, and other times I mow,

Sometimes I follow the harrow and the plough

I earn all of my money by the sweat of my brow.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhen I go home at night just as tired as I can be,

I take my youngest child and dangle it on my knee,

While the others come around me, their rattling and their noise,

And this is all the comfort a poor man enjoys.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Sarah Makem sings The Jolly Thresher
versions.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of a noble sportsman went out to hunt one day

He met a jolly thresher upon the highway

With his flail across his shoulder and his bottle full of beer

He‚Äôs as merry as a lord has ten thousand a year.

‚ÄúOh thresher, dear thresher, will you come tell to me,

How do you maintain your poor wife and family?

Your work it is so heavy, and your wages is so small

Ara, how do you maintain your poor family at all?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell, sometimes I reap, other times I do I mow,

More times a-hedging and a-ditching I do go.

When a mist it comes across me, I harrow and I plough

And I earn all my bread by the sweat of my brow.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhen a poor man comes home at night, wet and weary as you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll see,

He takes the youngest baby and he‚Äôll dandle it on his knee,

While the oldest one comes round him with its prattle and its noise,

And sure, that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs all the comfort a poor man enjoys.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell now that I see you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre so loyal to your wife

Forty acres of good land I‚Äôll give you during life

And if I see you mind it, and of it takes good care,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll bestow it for life both to you and your heirs.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Harry Holman sings The Nobleman and the Thresher
versions.lyrics:
A nobleman there lived in the village of late.

There was a poor thresherman, his family was great.

He had got seven children, the most of them were small,

He‚Äôd nothing but hard labour for to maintain them all.

(The nobleman he met with this poor man one day

And to this poor old thresherman these very words did say

‚ÄúYou are a poor thresherman, I know it to be true

How do you get your living as well as you do?‚ÄĚ)

‚ÄúSometimes I do reap and sometimes I mow

The other times to hedging and a-ditching I do go.

There‚Äôs nothing goes amiss with me, the harrow or the plough,

That√Ę¬Ä¬ôs how I get my living by the sweat of my brow.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAnd when my day√Ę¬Ä¬ôs work√Ę¬Ä¬ôs over I go home late at night,

For in my wife and family I take a great delight.

My children they come round me [with] their pretty flattery toys

And that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs all the pleasure that a poor man enjoys.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy wife she is willing to join me in this yoke,

We‚Äôre just like two young turtle-doves, there‚Äôs ne‚Äôer a one provoked.

Although the times are very bad and we are very poor

But we still kept the wolves and the ravens from our door.

(‚ÄúYou are an honest fellow, you speak well of your wife

And you shall both live happy all the last part of your life

Here‚Äôs forty-five acres of good land I‚Äôll freely give to thee

To maintain your wife and your sweet family.‚ÄĚ)

God bless all you farmers that take to us poor men.

I wish of them with all my heart, their souls in Heaven may spend.

And those that‚Äôs left behind us had better pattern take,

That they may follow after as quick as they can.

versions.title: The Copper Family sing Honest Labourer
versions.lyrics:
It was of an honest labourer as I‚Äôve heard people say,

He goes out in the morning and he works hard all the day.

And he‚Äôs got seven children and most of them are small,

He has nothing but hard labour to maintain them all.

A gentleman one morning walking out to take the air,

He met with this poor labouring man and solemnly declared,

‚ÄúI think you are that thresher-man.‚ÄĚ Said he, ‚ÄúYes, sir, that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs true.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúHow do you get your living as well as you do?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúSometimes I do reap and sometimes I do mow,

At other times to hedging and to ditching I do go.

There is nothing comes amiss to me from the harrow to the plough,

That‚Äôs how I get my living by the sweat of my brow.

‚ÄúWhen I go home at night just as tired as I be

I take my youngest child and I dance him on my knee.

They others they come around me with their prittle-prattling toys

And that‚Äôs the only comfort a working man enjoys.

‚ÄúMy wife and I are willing and we join both in one yoke,

We live like two turtle doves and not one word provoke.

Although the times are very hard and we are very poor

We can scarcely keep the raving wolf away from the door.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell done, you honest labourer, you speak well of your wife,

I hope you will live happy all the days of your life.

Here is forty acres of good land which I will give to thee

Which will help to maintain your sweet wife and family.‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Parson and the Sucking Pig
items.notes:
The Parson and the Sucking Pig

[

; Master title: The Parson and the Sucking Pig

; TYG 45

; Ballad Index WT197

; Bodleian

; Wiltshire

923

; DT TITHEPIG

; trad.]

Sabine Baring-Gould:

Songs of the West
items.lyrics:
A parson dressed all in his best, cocked hat and bushy wig,

He went into a farmer‚Äôs house to choose a sucking pig.

‚ÄúGood morning√Ę¬Ä¬ô, said the parson, ‚Äúgood morning, sir, to you,

I‚Äôve come to choose a sucking pig, you know it is my due.

You know it is my due, you know it is my due,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve come to choose a sucking pig, you know it is my due.‚ÄĚ

Then in the sty the farmer goes, among the pigs so small,

And he chooses for the parson the least amongst them all;

And when the parson saw the same, how he did rant and roar,

He stamped his foot and shook his head and almost cursed and swore.

In the sty the parson ventured without any more ado,

And the old sow ran with open mouth and at the parson flew.

And then she grabbed him by the coat and took off both the skirts;

She ran her head between his legs and rolled him in the dirt.

At length they let the parson out as fast as he could run,

And the farmer almost split his sides for laughing at the fun.

The parson√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wife she saw his plight, ‚ÄúWhere √Ę¬Ä¬ôa you bin?‚ÄĚ she said,

‚ÄúGet out, you bitch,‚ÄĚ the parson cried, ‚Äúfor I am almost dead.

‚ÄúGo fetch me down a suit of clothes, go fetch them down I say,

And bring me my old greasy wig without any more delay.

And for the usage I received all in the cursed sty,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never relish sucking pig until the day I die.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Will Noble sings The Suckling Pig
versions.lyrics:
Come ye that love a bit of fun and listen here awhile,

I‚Äôll tell you of a droll affair that will give you cause to smile.

A parson dressed all in his best, cocked hat and bushy wig,

He went up to a farmer‚Äôs house to choose a sucking pig.

‚ÄúGood morning,‚ÄĚ said the parson, ‚Äúgood morning sir to you,

I‚Äôve come to choose a sucking pig, which you know it is my due.

Therefore I pray go fetch me one that is both plump and fine,

For I have asked a friend or two along with me to dine.‚ÄĚ

So in the sty the farmer goes among the pigs so small,

And he chooses for the parson the least amongst them all.

When the parson saw the same, how he did rant and roar,

He stamped his foot and he shook his wig and he almost cursed and swore.

‚ÄúWell then sir,‚ÄĚ said the farmer, ‚Äúsince my offer you refuse

I pray you go into the sty, there you may pick and choose.‚ÄĚ

In the sty the parson ventured, without any more ado;

The old sow ran with open mouth, and she at the parson flew.

Well the first she grabbed him by the coat and took off both the skirts,

She ran her head between his legs and rolled him in the dirt.

The parson cursed the very hour he‚Äôd ventured for the pig,

You‚Äôd have laughed to see the little ones how they shook his hat and wig.

Well the next she grabbed him by the breeches, as he so loudly cried,

‚ÄúOh! Free me from this cursed pig or I shall surely die!‚ÄĚ

The little pig his waistcoat tore, his stockings and his shoes,

The farmer said, ‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre welcome, I hope you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll pick and choose.‚ÄĚ

Well at length they let the parson out, all in a handsome trim,

The sow and pigs so neatly in the dirt had rolled him

His coat was to a spencer turned, his brogues were ripped behind,

And beside his backside was all bare and his shirt hung out behind.

He‚Äôd lost his stockings and his shoes, which grieved him full sore,

Beside his waistcoat, hat and wig, they were all to pieces tore.

Then of the parson, he scampered home, as fast as he could run,

The farmer almost split his sides with laughing at the fun.

The parson‚Äôs wife stood at the door awaiting his return

But when she saw his awful plight, she into the house did run.

‚ÄúMy dear what is the matter, and where have you been?‚ÄĚ she said.

‚ÄúGet out you slut,‚ÄĚ the parson cried, ‚Äúfor I am almost dead.

‚ÄúGo fetch me down a suit of clothes, go fetch √Ę¬Ä¬ôem down I pray,

And bring me my old greasy wig, without any such delay.

And for the usage I‚Äôve received all in that cursed sty

I never will relish sucking pig, until the day I die.‚ÄĚ

To me folla del lay, folla del lay, folla delara lay

Folla del lay, folla del lay, folla delara lay.

items.title: The Rose in June (Let It Be Early, Late or Soon)
items.notes:
[

; Master title: The Rose in June

Bob Copper collected

Rose in June

in about 1954 from George Fosbury of Axford, Hampshire;

see Chapter 14, pp. 114-122, of his book
items.lyrics:
Was down in the valleys, the valleys so deep,

To pick some plain roses to keep my love sweet.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

So let it come early, late or soon,

I will enjoy my rose in June.

O, the roses red, the violets blue,

Carnations sweet, love, and so are you.

O love, I will carry thy sweet milking pail,

O love, I will kiss you on every stile.

versions.title: Cyril Tawney sings Rose in June
versions.lyrics:
O my love I will cut down my sweet myrtle tree

For to build up a cottage for Sally and me.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

So let it be early, late, or soon,

I will enjoy my rose in June.

My love I will carry your sweet milking pail,

My love I will kiss you at every stile.

Now the roses are red and violets are blue,

Carnations are sweet, love, and so are you.

Cowslips make the meadow look fair,

None with my roses can compare.

Violets make the meadows look gay,

None with my roses can array.

Primroses make the meadows look neat,

None with my roses can compete.

Roses in June are not so sweet

As kisses are, when two lovers meet.

versions.title: June Tabor sings Rose in June
versions.lyrics:
Was down in the valley, the valley so deep,

To pick some plain roses to keep my love sweet.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Let it be early, late or soon,

I will enjoy my rose in June,

Rose in June, rose in June,

I will enjoy my rose in June.

Now I will lead my flock to the fold,

Let it be hot or let it be cold.

Now I will cut the sweet myrtle tree

To build a bower for my love and me.

But roses in June are never so sweet

As kisses can be when true lovers meet.

versions.title: Tim Radford sings Love in June
versions.lyrics:
I‚Äôll harness my horses and follow the plough

While darling Miss Catherine is milking her cow.

Chorus (after each verse):

So let it come early late or soon

I will enjoy my love in June.

Oh love I will carry thy sweet milking pail

Oh love I will kiss you at every stile.

Oh the roses are red and the violets are blue

Carnations are sweet love and so are you.

‚ÄôTwas Down in the valley, the valley so sweet

I will pick some plain roses to keep my love sweet.

Oh I‚Äôll go and cut down that old myrtle tree

And build up a bower for my Catherine and me.

The Rose of Allendale

items.lyrics:
The morn was fair, the sky was clear, no breath came over the sea

When Mary left her highland cot and wandered forth with me.

Though flowers decked the mountain side and fragrance filled the vale,

By far the sweetest flower there was the Rose of Allendale.

Chorus:

Sweet Rose of Allendale, sweet Rose of Allendale,

By far the sweetest flower there was the Rose of Allendale.

Where e‚Äôer I wandered east or west though fate began to lour,

A solace still was she to me in sorrow‚Äôs lonely hour.

When tempests lashed my lonely barque and rent the quivering sail,

One maiden form withstood the storm, ‚Äôtwas the Rose of Allendale.

Chorus:

Sweet Rose of Allendale, sweet Rose of Allendale,

One maiden form withstood the storm, ‚Äôtwas the Rose of Allendale.

And when my fevered lips were parched on Africa‚Äôs burning sands

She whispered hopes of happiness and tales of distant lands.

My life has been a wilderness, unblessed by fortune‚Äôs gale,

Had fate not linked my lot to hers, the Rose of Allendale.

Chorus:

Sweet Rose of Allendale, sweet Rose of Allendale,

Had fate not linked my love to hers, the Rose of Allendale.

items.title: The Seasons Round / The Ploughshare
items.notes:
[

; Master title: The Seasons Round

; Ballad Index BrMa143

; Folkinfo 819

; DT SEASRND

; trad.]

Copper Family:

The Copper Family Song Book .

Lucy E. Broadwood, J.A. Fuller Maitland:

English County Songs

Roy Palmer:

Everyman‚Äôs Book of English Country Songs
items.lyrics:
The sun has gone down and the sky it looks red

Down on my soft pillow where I laid my head,

As I open my eyes for to see the stars shine

Then the thought of my true love runs into my mind.

The sap has gone down and the leaves they do fall,

To hedging and ditching our farmers they‚Äôll call.

We will trim up their hedges, we will cut down their wood,

And the farmers they‚Äôll all say our faggots run good.

Now hedging being over then sawing draws near;

We will send for the sawyer those woods for to clear.

And after he has sawed them and tumbled them down

Then there he will flaw them all on the cold ground.

Now sawing being over then seed-time comes round,

See our teams they are already preparing the ground,

Then the man with his seed-lip he‚Äôll scatter the corn

And the harrows they will bury it to keep it from harm.

Now seed-time being over then haying draws near,

With our scythe, rake, and pitch-fork those meadows to clear.

We will cut down their grass, boys, and carry it away,

We will clip it to the green grass and then call it hay.

Now haying being over then harvest draws near

We will send to our brewer to brew us strong beer,

And in brewing strong beer, boys, we will cut down their corn

We will take it to the barn, boys, to keep it from harm.

Now harvest being over bad weather comes on,

We will send for the thresher to thresh out the corn.

His hand-staff he‚Äôll handle, his swingel he‚Äôll swing,

Till the very next harvest we‚Äôll all meet again.

Now since we have brought this so cheerfully round

We will send for the ploughman who ploughs up and down.

See the boy with his whip and the man to his plough

Here‚Äôs a health to the jolly ploughman who ploughs up the ground.

Now things they do change as the time passes on,

I‚Äôm afraid I will have occasion to alter my song.

You‚Äôll see a boy with a tractor a-going like hell

And whatever farming is coming to there‚Äôs no tongue can tell.

versions.title: Nick Dow sings The Seasons of the Year
versions.lyrics:
The sun it goes down, the sky it looks red,

Down on my pillow I lay down my head.

I lift up mine eyes for to see the stars shine

And thoughts of my true love still run in my mind.

When the sap it goes up the trees they will flaw,

We‚Äôll first branch ‚Äôem round, boys, then put in the saw;

When we have sawn them and tumbled them down

Then we will flaw them all on the cold ground.

When flawing is over haying draws near,

With our scythes and our pitchforks some grass for to clear.

And when we have cleared it and carried it away,

We first called it green grass, we now call it hay.

When haying is over harvest draws near,

We‚Äôll call on the brewer to brew us some beer.

For to brew us strong beer for the hard-working man,

He works late and early till harvest will end.

When the sap it goes down then leaves they will fall,

And the farmer to his hedging and ditching will call.

Ah, but when it‚Äôs hard weather there‚Äôs no working there,

So it‚Äôs into the barn, boys, some corn for to clear.

When the Spring it comes on, the maid to her cow,

The boy to his whip, and the man to his plough.

And so we bring all things so cheerfully round:

Here‚Äôs success to the ploughman who ploughs up the ground!

items.title: The Shepherd‚Äôs Song
items.notes:
The Shepherd‚Äôs Song

[

; Ballad Index CoSea216

; trad.]

Jill Copper sang the

The Shepherd‚Äôs Song

in 1971 on the Copper Family‚Äôs Leader Records box set

A Song for Every Season .

Andy Turner learned The Shepherd‚Äôs Song

from the Copper Family‚Äôs album

and sang it as the 28 November 2011 entry of his project

A Folk Song a Week .
items.lyrics:
There was a young shepherd attending of his sheep

Attending of his sheep on a fine summer‚Äôs day

When a charming young lady by chance should come that way

And on the young shepherd she fixed her bright eye.

He drew forth his pipe and so prettily did play

But this maid turned her head then so modestly away

But so sweet and melodious the shepherd played his tune

She pleaded with this swain to go all on their honeymoon.

Then said this young shepherd of very low degree

I know a young shepherd far richer than me

Don‚Äôt talk about riches or any sort of clothes

Take off your shepherding garments and I‚Äôll dress you in robes.

So straight to the church this young couple they did steer

And were married by asking as you shall quickly hear

They had servants to wait on them, attendants by their side

So who could be more happy than that shepherd and his bride.

items.title: Christmas Song / The Trees Are All Bare
items.notes:
Christmas Song / The Trees Are All Bare

[

/ Song Subject MAS718

; Ballad Index CopSe202

; Folkinfo 203

, 876

; Mudcat 15944

; trad.]

Copper Family:

The Copper Family Song Book .

Ken Stubbs:

The Life of a Man
items.lyrics:
The trees they‚Äôre all bare, not one green leaf to be seen,

And those little doves [indecipherable] at morn;

As for the leaves, they are all fallen from the trees

And the streams are fast, and the streams are fast,

Fast bound down by the hoarfrost.

The poor little small birds to the barn doors fly for food,

Silent they restle on the spray;

Our poor timid hare search the woods all for her food,

Lest her footsteps should, lest her footsteps should,

Her innocence betray.

The poor little pigeons sit shivering on the barn,

Coldly the north winds do blow;

Our poor innocent sheep from the downs unto their fold,

With their fleeces all, with their fleeces all

Quite covered with snow.

The poor little oxen in the yard all foddered on straw

Sends forth their breath like the steam;

Our sweet-looking milkmaid she finds that she must go.

Flakes of ice finds she, flakes of ice finds she,

She finds all on her cream.

Now Christmas is come and our song we have sung,

Soon will come the Springtime of year;

Come hand to me the glass and I‚Äôll drink your healths all round,

And I wish you all, and I wish you all

A bright and happy New Year.

items.title: The Veteran
items.notes:
The Veteran

[

; Ballad Index CoSe234

; Bodleian

; trad.]

The Veteran

is a song from the repertoire of the Copper Family. It is printed in

The Copper Family Song Book

and in Bob Copper‚Äôs book

A Song for Every Season ,

but not on the same-named album.

Andy Turner learned The Veteran
items.lyrics:
Twas on one Sabbath morn, the bells did chime for Church,

The young and gay were gathering there around that rustic porch.

There came an ag-ed man in soldier‚Äôs garb was he,

And gazing on that group he cried, ‚ÄúYou all remember me.‚ÄĚ

The Veteran forgot, his friends were past and gone

The manly forms around him there as children he had known.

He pointed to a spot where his dwelling used to be,

And turning round and smiling said, ‚ÄúYou now remember me.‚ÄĚ

Alas none knew him there. He pointed to a stone

On which a name he breathed was traced, a name to them unknown.

And then the old man wept, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm friendless now,‚ÄĚ said he,

‚ÄúWhere I had many a friend in youth not one remembers me.‚ÄĚ

The old man√Ę¬Ä¬ôs heart seemed broke, said he, ‚ÄúThis is my own.

I hoped with friends to end my days. Alas, that hope has gone.‚ÄĚ

He clutched the moss-grown tomb, ‚ÄúWithout welcome, death,‚ÄĚ said he,

‚ÄúForgotten now by all on earth, Oh, God, remember me.‚ÄĚ

items.lyrics:
‚ÄôTwas on one summer‚Äôs morning as my love walked over the plain,

ūĚĄÜ He advanced ūĚĄá him a shilling all to fight for the Crown.

He is a handsome young man and he‚Äôs going to serve the King,

ūĚĄÜ O my very ūĚĄá heart is breaking all for the loss of him.

versions.title: The Watersons sing The White Cockade
versions.lyrics:
‚ÄôTis true, my love‚Äôs enlisted and he wears a white cockade.

ūĚĄÜ O my very ūĚĄá heart is breaking all for the loss of him.

As I roved out one morning, as I wandered over yon moss

ūĚĄÜ He advanced ūĚĄá me the money two guineas and a crown.

ūĚĄÜ How I wish that ūĚĄá he may perish all in the foaming spray!

versions.title: Charlie Hill sings The White Cockade
versions.lyrics:
‚ÄėTis true my love‚Äôs enlisted, he wears the white cockade.

‚ÄúLeave off your grief and sorrow, likewise your doleful strain.

The white cockade adorns me as I march o‚Äôer the plain.

When I return, I‚Äôll marry by this cockade I‚Äôll swear.

√Ę¬Ä¬ėTil then, my love, be patient and my departure bear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI bring to you sad tidings,‚ÄĚ the sergeant he did say.

‚ÄúYour love was slain in battle, he sends you this today.‚ÄĚ

He handed her the plumage, bedraggled by his gore.

‚ÄúWith his last kiss he sent it, the white cockade he wore.‚ÄĚ

She spoke no words and at her tears, they fell in salten flood,

And from the bedraggled plumage she washed the stains of blood.

‚ÄúOh mother, I am dying, and when I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm in my grave

Just pin it to my bosom, my lover√Ę¬Ä¬ôs white cockade.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: The Witches of Elswick sing The Blue Cockade
versions.lyrics:
Twas on one Monday‚Äôs morning as I crossed over the moss,

ūĚĄÜ And the bounty ūĚĄá they gave me, five guineas and a crown.

My head was full of drink, love, and I didn‚Äôt think of you,

ūĚĄÜ I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll return love ūĚĄá in the spring time I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll make you my bride.

So early the next morning, before the break of day,

All in your ranks and files, boys, all on your native shore,

ūĚĄÜ Fare ye well love, ūĚĄá you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre the lad that I adore.

And the very ground you walk upon, may the grass refuse to grow,

ūĚĄÜ Since you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been the ūĚĄá very cause of all my sorrow, grief and woe.

Well, it‚Äôs true my love is ‚Äôlisted and he wears the blue cockade.

He is a handsome young man, likewise a roving blade.

ūĚĄÜ While my very ūĚĄá heart is breaking all for the love of him.

versions.title: Geoff Lakeman sings The Green Cockade
versions.lyrics:
My love is newly listed

And he wears a green cockade,

He‚Äôs marched away and left me

Chorus:

His team of oxen ploughing

The very ground he trod on

The grass refuse to grow.

If only I had told him

versions.title: Louise and Chris Rogan sing The White Cockade

Think of me ‚Ä¶ (√ó2)

items.lyrics:
Come all you honest labouring men that work hard all the day

And join with me at the Barley Mow to pass an hour away.

Where we can sing and drink and be merry

And drive away all our cares and worry,

When Jones‚Äôs ale was new, my boys, when Jones‚Äôs ale was new.

The first to come in was the ploughman with sweat all on his brow,

Up with the lark at the break of day he guides the speedy plough.

He drives his team, how they do toil

O‚Äôer hill and valley to turn the soil,

When Jones‚Äôs ale was new, my boys, when Jones‚Äôs ale was new.

The next to come in was the blacksmith, his brawny arms all bare

And with his pint of Jones‚Äôs ale he has no fear or care.

Throughout the day his hammer he‚Äôs swinging

And he sings when he hears the anvil ringing,

When Jones‚Äôs ale was new, my boys, when Jones‚Äôs ale was new.

The next to come in was the scythe-man so cheerful and so brown

And with the rhythm of his scythe the corn he does mow down.

He works, he mows, he sweats and he blows

And he leaves his swathes laying all in rows,

When Jones‚Äôs ale was new, my boys, when Jones‚Äôs ale was new.

The next to come in was the tinker and he was no small beer drinker,

And he was no small beer-drinker to join the jovial crew.

He told the old woman he‚Äôd mend her old kettle,

Good Lord how his hammer and tongs did rattle

When Jones‚Äôs ale was new, my boys, when Jones‚Äôs ale was new.

Now here is Jones our landlord, a jovial man is he,

Likewise his wife a buxom lass who joins in harmony.

We wish them happiness and good will

While our pots and glasses they do fill,

When Jones‚Äôs ale was new, my boys, when Jones‚Äôs ale was new.

versions.title: A.L. Lloyd sings When Johnson‚Äôs Ale Was New
versions.lyrics:
There were six jolly tradesmen
come rambling over the hill.

Come ambling over the hill, my boys,
for they were a cheerful crew.

They all sat down for to be merry;

They called for a bottle of wine or sherry.

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre welcome over the hills,‚ÄĚ says Nelly,

When Johnson‚Äôs ale was new.

The first come in was a soldier and no captain ever looked bolder.

His gun on his right shoulder, his good broadsword he drew.

He said he‚Äôd fight with all his might

Before old England should be drunk dry;

And so they spent a rowdy night

When Johnson‚Äôs ale was new.

The next come in was a hatter and he began to chatter.

He asked what was the matter among the jovial crew.

He dashed his hat unto the ground

And he flung the landlord half a crown.

And the company drank his health around

When Johnson‚Äôs ale was new.

Then in come a jolly mason his hammer to put a face on,

No man could be more decent among the jovial crew.

He dashed his trowel against the wall

And he wished every church and steeple would fall,

So there would be work for masons all

When Johnson‚Äôs ale was new.

The last come in was a ragman weary, his ragbag he did carry,

And he was already merry among the jovial crew.

While he was kissing and squeezing the lasses

They burned his ragbag all to ashes,

When Johnson‚Äôs ale was new, my boys,

When Johnson‚Äôs ale was new.

The ale was always improving and nobody thought of moving.

The longer they sat boozing the greater friends they grew.

So rowdily they drunk about

Until the ale had all run out,

And they asked old Johnson to give ‚Äôem a shout

When he had brewed anew.

versions.title: Harry Green sings When Jones‚Äôs Ale Was New
versions.lyrics:
There were three jolly countrymen come over the hills together,

Come over the hills together to join our jovial crew,

They asked for pints and pots of ale,

While Jones‚Äô ale was new my boys, while Jones‚Äô ale was new.

Oh the first to come in was a soldier with his firebrand over his shoulder,

No man could look any bolder to join our jovial crew,

and so she tickled him under his chin,

While Jones‚Äô ale was new, my boys, while Jones‚Äô ale was new.

Oh the next come in was a tinker, he was no small beer drinker,

Nobody could look any bolder to join our jovial crew,

‚ÄúHave you any old pots or kettles to mend,

For my rivets are made of the best of metal.‚ÄĚ

Good lord how his hammers and pincers did rattle,

While Jones‚Äô ale was new, my boys, while Jones‚Äô ale was new.

The next come in was a dyer, he sat himself down by the fire,

No man could look any bolder to join our jovial crew,

He told the landlord to his face

The chimney corner was his place,

And there he‚Äôd sit and scorch his old face,

‚ÄôTil Jones‚Äô ale was new, my boys, while Jones‚Äô ale was new.

The next come in was a mason, his hammer it wanted new facing,

No man could look any bolder to join our jovial crew,

He threw his old hammer at the wall

And wished all the churches and chapels might fall,

And then there‚Äôd be work for masons all,

While Jones‚Äô ale was new, my boys, while Jones‚Äô ale was new.

The next come in was a ragman with his ragbag over his shoulder,

No man could look any bolder to join our jovial crew,

He told the landlord to his heart

While Jones‚Äô ale was new, my boys, while Jones‚Äô ale was new.

Now the last to come in was a cobbler, slapstone over his shoulder,

No man could look any bolder to join our jovial crew,

He dropped his slapstone on his toes

The Copper Family:

The Copper Family Song Book

George Townshend from Lewes in Sussex sang

When Spring Comes In
items.lyrics:
When the Spring comes in the birds do sing, the lambs do play and the bells do ring,

When the Spring comes in the birds do sing, the lambs do play and the bells do ring.

The primrose blooms and the cowslip too,

The violets in their sweet attire, the bluebells shining through the briar,

The daffy-dilly we all admire, while daisies fade away.

From mountains high and meadows green, young men and maidens will be seen,

From mountains high and meadows green, young men and maidens will be seen.

Through woods and groves, they will take their way,

They talks of tales and courts and sales, all little lambs around them play,

And at night they onward bend their way when th‚Äô evening stars appear.

The dairymaid to milking goes with her blooming cheeks as red as a rose,

The dairymaid to milking goes with her blooming cheeks as red as a rose.

She milks, she sings, makes the valleys ring,

The small birds on the branches there are listening to this lovely fair.

For she is her master‚Äôs trust and care, she is the ploughboy‚Äôs joy.

versions.title: The Copper Family sing When Spring Comes In
versions.lyrics:

The lambs do skip and the bells do ring

While we enjoy their glorious charm so noble and so gay.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

And the daffadown-dillies which we admire will die and fade away.

Young men and maidens will be seen

On mountains high and meadows green,

They will talk of love and sport and play

While these young lambs do skip away.

At night they homeward wend their way

When evening stars appear.

The dairymaid to milking goes, her blooming cheeks as red as a rose,

She carries her pail all on her arm so cheerful and so gay,

She milks, she sings, and the valleys ring.

The small birds on the branches there sit listening to this lovely fair.

She is her master‚Äôs trust and care, she is the ploughman‚Äôs joy.

The Old Dun Cow Caught Fire

is a music hall song written by Harry Wincott in 1893.

Harry Champion sang

it in a 1911 recording (Regal G6403; starting in the

video at 2:55):

The Old Dun Cow Caught Fire
items.lyrics:
Some pals and I in a public house were playing dominoes last night,

When all of a sudden in the pot-man runs with a face just like a kite.

‚ÄúWhat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs up?‚ÄĚ said Brown. ‚ÄúWhy you silly old fool! Have you seen old Aunt Mariah?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAunt be blowed,‚ÄĚ then the pot-man cried. ‚ÄúThe blooming pub√Ę¬Ä¬ôs on fire!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOn fire!‚ÄĚ said Brown. ‚ÄúWhat a bit of luck! Come along with me,‚ÄĚ shouts he.

‚ÄúDown in the cellar, if the fire ain√Ę¬Ä¬ôt there, we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll have a fair old spree.‚ÄĚ

So we all goes down ‚Äôlong with good old Brown. Booze we couldn‚Äôt miss.

We hadn‚Äôt been ten minutes there, when I was just like this.

Chorus (after each verse):

And there was Brown upside down mopping up the whisky off the floor.

‚ÄúBooze, booze, booze!‚ÄĚ then the firemen cried as they got banging in the door.

‚ÄúDon√Ę¬Ä¬ôt let √Ę¬Ä¬ôem in till it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs all mopped up!‚ÄĚ someone said to McIntyre,

And we all got blue blind paralytic drunk when the Old Dun Cow caught fire.

Old Johnson flew at the port-wine tub and he gave it just a few hard knocks.

He then starts taking off his pantaloons, likewise his boots and socks.

‚ÄúHold hard,‚ÄĚ said Snooks. ‚ÄúIf you want to wash your feet, there√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a barrel full of four ale here.

Don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt put your trotters in the port wine, Jack, when there√Ę¬Ä¬ôs some old stale beer.‚ÄĚ

Just then there was such a dreadful crash, half the blooming roof gave way.

We got drowned with a fireman‚Äôs hose, but still we were all gay.

We found some sacks and some old tin tacks, shoved ourselves inside.

We all got drinking good old Scotch till we were bleary-eyed.

We got so drunk we did not know the blooming cellar had caught fire.

Poor old Jones had the D.T.‚Äôs bad and wanted to retire.

‚ÄúThere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Old Nick,‚ÄĚ said another poor chap, ‚Äúand he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs poking up the blooming fire.

‚ÄúThat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs no bogey. It√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a fireman, Tom, at least,‚ÄĚ said McIntyre.

‚ÄúLet√Ę¬Ä¬ôs get out,‚ÄĚ said a brown-eyed chap. ‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs getting very dark down here.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúDon√Ę¬Ä¬ôt be a fool,‚ÄĚ said a tipsy chap. ‚ÄúWe haven√Ę¬Ä¬ôt drank the beer!‚ÄĚ

So we filled our hats and we drank like cats ‚Äômidst the flames and smoke.

I had to take my collar off. I thought that I should choke.

items.title: Beacon Park
items.notes:

Beacon Park

Cyril Tawney wrote

Beacon Park

in ca 1968. He sang it in 1970 on his Argo album

A Mayflower Garland .
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Beacon Park is a peaceful place,

If a dog should bark, it‚Äôs a shocking disgrace,

And don‚Äôt you dare cough when the Sun goes down.

We‚Äôre sensitive people on Beacon Down.

Mmmm mmn. Ohmmm mmn.

Mmmm mmn. Ohmmm mmn.

The decibel level determines the rates.

Their cutlery‚Äôs rubber, and so is their plates.

The birds never sing in the undergrowth.

Every baby is born with a cork in his mouth.

At the foot of each garden, there stands a screen

Enclosing a quaint little field latrine.

It may be unhealthy; it may make you blush,

But it won‚Äôt wake you up like that noisy old flush.

The garbage disposal is done without noise.

The bins are constructed of special alloys.

The gates are spring-loaded so they never slam,

And the dustmen take courses with Geraldine Lamb.

This pious fixation for stifling sounds

Is gaining supporters in leaps and bounds.

The church at St. Boniface muffles its peals

And the Albion forwards are whispering, ‚ÄúHeel!‚ÄĚ

items.lyrics:
Come all you brisk young fellows

Who have a mind to range

Into some foreign country

Your stations for to change.

Into some foreign country

Away from home we‚Äôll go.

Chorus (after each verse):

And we lay down on the banks

Where the pleasant waters flow.

Through the wild woods we‚Äôll wander

And we‚Äôll chase the buffalo,

We‚Äôll chase the buffalo.

Through the wild woods we‚Äôll wander

And we‚Äôll chase the buffalo.

Come all you pretty maidens

And spin us up some yarn

To make us some clothing

To keep us snug and warm.

You can card and you can spin, maids,

And we can reap and mow.

There are fishes in the river

That are fitting for our use,

And high and lofty sugar-canes

To yield us pleasant juice,

There are all sorts of game, boys,

Besides the buck and doe.

Suppose though wild Indians

By chance should come too near,

We will link us heart to heart

And have nothing to fear.

We would march through the town, boys,

And give the fatal blow.

A Pinch of Salt: British Sea Songs Old and New .

He recorded it again as

in 1972 for his Argo LP

In Port .
items.lyrics:
It happened on a Summer‚Äôs day in nineteen fifty-four,

We went to greet Her Majesty a-comin‚Äô from the Tour.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

On a British submarine, on a British submarine,

When sixty solid sailors went cheering of the Queen.

Their Lordships said, ‚ÄúNow cheer, my boys, but mind you make it smart,

There√Ę¬Ä¬ôs nothing looks so ragged as the cheering from your heart.‚ÄĚ

Well, when we saw the signal boys, it made our innards freeze

‚ÄúOn the order √Ę¬Ä¬ôone√Ę¬Ä¬ô you hold your hats at forty-five degrees.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAt forty-five degrees‚ÄĚ, my boys, that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs what their lordships said,

‚ÄúOn the order √Ę¬Ä¬ôtwo√Ę¬Ä¬ô you wave your hats three times around your head.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAnd when you go to shout, my boys, be careful what you say,

The word you use for cheerin√Ę¬Ä¬ô is Hurrah, not Hooray.‚ÄĚ

We sailed towards Britannia, boys, the finest ever seen,

But every man was standing like a petrified marine.

But when we reached the moment, boys, that every skipper dreads

A swarm of gnats as big as bats descended on our heads.

The boat was filled with wavin‚Äô arms and the air was filled with cries

As every man cursed and fought to keep‚Äô em from his eyes.

The Duke he scratched his head as he watched us slowly part

‚ÄúThat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the first time I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve seen sailor boys cheerin√Ę¬Ä¬ô from their hearts.‚ÄĚ

We waved our way across the bay till we were out of sight

We waved our way all through the day and on into the night.

Ah! the day we met the Queen, my boys, the day I won‚Äôt forget

If we hadn‚Äôt dived to ninety feet we‚Äôd all be wavin‚Äô yet.

‚ÄúMy fare is sweet, and plenty to spare,

As I go on to Strawberry Fair.‚ÄĚ

Ree-fol, ree-fol, fol-dee-diddle-die-do,

Ree-fol, ree-fol, fol-dee-diddle-dee.

Said I, ‚ÄúA rose will soon fade away.‚ÄĚ

Fol-dee-dee!

‚ÄúSo fare thee well, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll none of your ware,

And I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll not go to Strawberry Fair.‚ÄĚ

O I have a lock, sir,‚ÄĚ she did say.

‚ÄúIf you have a key then come this way

items.title: Down Among the Barley Straw
items.notes:
Down Among the Barley Straw

[

; VWML SBG/3/1/480

; Folkinfo 450

; trad.]

Cyril Tawney recorded Down Among the Barley Straw

in 1971 as the title track of his album of seductions songs from the

Baring-Gould manuscripts,

Down Among the Barley Straw .

However, this album wasn‚Äôt published on the Leader label until 1976.

He noted:
items.lyrics:
As Jan was hurrying down the glade, he met his sweetheart Kit;

‚ÄúO whither so fast?‚ÄĚ the maiden ask‚Äôd, ‚Äúlet‚Äôs bide and talk a bit.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI‚Äôm going to the barn, and if you‚Äôll come, and help me thresh the stro‚Äô,

That task complete, why then my sweet, a ramble we will go.‚ÄĚ

She gave consent, to work they went, as if ‚Äôtwere only play;

The flail he plied, whilst Kit untied, the sheaves, and cleared away.

O willing hands made labour light, and ‚Äôere the sun was low,

With arms entwined, these lovers kind, did down the vallies go.

Said Jan,‚Äúthou art a helpful lass, wilt thou be mine for life?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúFor sure!‚ÄĚ she said. To church they sped, and soon were man and wife.

A lesson then, for all young men who would a courting go,

Your sweetheart ask to share your task, and thresh the Barley Stro‚Äô.

Now many a year, this couple dear, they lived in harmony;

And children had, both lass and lad, i think ‚Äôtwas thirty three.

The sons so hale did wield the flail, and like their father grow;

The maidens sweet, like mother were neat: and clean as the Barley Stro‚Äô.

According to the biography on Cyril Tawney‚Äôs website, he wrote

items.lyrics:
Now don‚Äôt say Jim Johnson weren‚Äôt with ‚Äôee last night,

I heard him as plain as could be:

I was crossing the mound when I heard a strange sound

Down by the sycamore tree.

I thought perhaps a cow had got stuck in the mud

And pulled out her foot with a moo.

But I‚Äôm satisfied now that that noise weren‚Äôt a cow;

It was Jim kissing you.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

You‚Äôm a five foot flirt in the robes of an angel,

You better had leave I alone.

Why, the way you‚Äôre acting it nearly unnerves I,

The thing that preserves I is my joviality.

Though I‚Äôve got trouble as thick as the stubble

‚ÄôTis you that‚Äôs the worst of them all.

Keep out of my track and if you want to come back

You can crawl, crawl, crawl.

Remember what happened last Saturday night,

The air was so peaceful and still.

Like a bulk from the blue came a hullabaloo,

A growling and a cackling so shrill.

It came to my head as I crawled from my bed;

There‚Äôs a fox at my chickens, that‚Äôs true.

I crept out in my socks and bumped into the fox,

It was Jim kissing you.

Now what‚Äôs your excuse for last Sunday in church,

It fair turned the poor vicar grey.

While the organist was rendering Lead Kindly Light

Jim Johnson kept pumping away.

Then all of a sudden the organ stopped short,

The vicar got into a stew.

When he went round behind tell me what did he find:

He found Jim kissing you.

items.lyrics:
Gosport Nancy, she‚Äôs my fancy,

She‚Äôs the girl to make good sport;

How she‚Äôll greet you when she meets you

All the Gosport ladies,

They loves a sailor man,

But for finding a way to spend your pay

There‚Äôs none like Gosport Nan!

Gosport ladies love their gargle,

Gosport girls they goes their tot;

Rum and brandy, gin and shandy,

Gosport girls will drink the lot;

All the Gosport ladies,

They swigs the flowing can,

But for knocking it back with Honest Jack

There‚Äôs none like Gosport Nan!

Gosport girls they‚Äôre good at dancing,

They‚Äôre the best there is, no doubt;

When the music sets them prancing

All the Gosport ladies,

They do the French Can-Can,

But for real high kicks and fancy tricks

There‚Äôs none like Gosport Nan!

Gosport Nancy keeps a parlour

She will do whatever you please;

All the Gosport ladies,

items.lyrics:
The pin-stripe boys have had their say,

But I‚Äôm too old to move away,

I‚Äôm in the sidings now.

I‚Äôve worked this line for many a day,

But that‚Äôs no use when your hair turns grey.

I‚Äôm in the sidings now.

Well, now I know how a wagon feels

When the grass comes creeping round its wheels.

And its timbers turn to a woodworm‚Äôs meals,

I‚Äôm in the sidings now.

So I‚Äôll give my whistle one more blow,

Then I‚Äôll change my pole for a garden hoe,

My bogie fires are burning low,

I‚Äôm in the sidings now.

Good business men have often said,

Always trim your costs if you‚Äôre in the red,

Well, come shake hands with an overhead,

I‚Äôm in the sidings now.

If your money tree will bear no fruit,

Never blame the man who tends the root.

But take your knife to the tender shoot,

I‚Äôm in the sidings now.

items.title: Midsummer Carol
items.notes:
Midsummer Carol

[

; VWML SBG/3/1/439

, SBG/3/1/440

; Folkinfo 505

; trad.]

Rev. Sabine Baring-Gould collected the

Midsummer Carol

in October 1890 from William Aggett of Chagford, Devon

[VWML SBG/3/1/439 ]

,

and printed it in his book
items.lyrics:
‚ÄôTwas early I walked on a midsummer morning,

The fields and the meadows were decked and gay,

The small birds were singing, the woodlands a-ringing,

‚ÄôTwas early in the morning, at breaking of day,

I will play on my pipes, I will sing thee my lay!

It is early in the morning, at breaking of day.

O hark! and O hark! to the nightingales wooing,

The lark is aloft piping shrill in the air.

In every green bower the turtle-doves cooing,

The sun is just gleaming, arise up my fair!

Arise, love, arise! none fairer I spie

Arise, love, arise! O why should I die?

Arise, love, arise! go and get your love posies,

The fairest of flowers in garden that grows,

Go gather me lilies, carnations and roses,

I‚Äôll wear them with thoughts of the maiden I chose

I stand at the door, pretty love, full of care,

O why should I languish so long in despair?

O why my love, O why, should I banished be from thee?

O why should I see my own chosen no more?

O why look your parents so slightingly on me?

It is all for the rough ragged garments I wore,

But dress me with flowers, I‚Äôm as gay as a king,

I‚Äôm glad as a bird when my carol I sing.

Arise, love, arise! in song and in story,

To rival thy beauty was never a may,

I will play thee a tune on my pipes of ivory,

It is early in the morning, at breaking of day,

I will play on my pipes, I will sing thee my lay!

It is early in the morning, at breaking of day.

versions.title: Cyril Tawney sings Midsummer Carol
versions.lyrics:
