‚ÄôTwas early in the morning at the breaking of day.

O hark, and o hark to the nightingales singing,

The lark is aloft, high aloft in the air.

In every green bower are the turtle doves a-building,

The sun is just gleaming, arise up my fair.

Arise love, arise! go and get your love posies,

The fairest of flowers in your garden that grows.

Go gather me lilies and sweet pinks and roses,

I‚Äôll wear them with thoughts of the maiden I chose.

Arise sweet, arise! thou loveliest creature,

Thou loveliest creature I ever did spy.

O let the sun shine on thy beautiful feature,

Arise love, arise, or in sorrow I die.

O why love, o why should I be banished from thee?

O why should I see my own fairest no more?

O why do your parents look sligthingly on me?

It is all for the ragged rough clothes that I wore.

O why love, o why should I be banished from thee?

When I live alone when my true love is near?

Arise love, arise! in song and in story,

To rival thy beauty was never a may,

I will play thee a tune on my pipes of ivory,

‚ÄôTis early in the morning at the breaking of day.

‚ÄôTwas early in the morning at breaking of day.

Go gather me lilies, carnations and roses,

I‚Äôll wear them with pride for the young girl I chose.

Come dress me with flowers, I‚Äôm as gay as a king,

I‚Äôm glad as a bird when my carol I sing.

O hark! love, o hark! love, the nightingales a-wooing,

It‚Äôs early in the morning, at breaking of day.

items.title: Sir Francis Drake
items.notes:
Sir Francis Drake

[

; Ballad Index PaSe007

; Mudcat 163054

; trad.]

Roy Palmer included

Sir Francis Drake; or Eighty-Eight

in his 1986 book

The Oxford Book of Sea Songs

and its 2001 expanded edition

Boxing the Compass ,

where he noted:
items.lyrics:
In eighty-eight, ere I was born,

As I can well remember,

In August was a fleet prepared,

The month before September.

Spain, with Biscayne, Portugal,

Toledo and Granado,

All these did meet and make a fleet,

And called it the Armado.

There was a little man of Spain

That shot well in a gun, a,

Don Pedro hight, as good a knight

As the Knight of the Sun, a.

King Philip made him admiral

And charg√®d him to stay, a

But to destroy both man and boy

And then to run away, a.

The King of Spain did fret amain,

And to do yet more harm, a

He sent along, to make him strong,

The famous Prince of Parma.

When they had sailed along the seas

And anchored upon Dover,

Our Englishmen did board them then

And cast the Spaniards over.

Our Queen was then at Tilbury,

What could you more desire, a?

For whose sweet sake Sir Francis Drake

Did set them all on fire, a.

But let them look about themselves,

For if they come again, a,

They shall be served with that same sauce

As they were, I know when, a.

versions.title: Cyril Tawney sings Sir Francis Drake
versions.lyrics:
In eighty-eight, ere I was born,

As I can well remember,

In August was a fleet prepared,

The month before September.

Spain, with Biscayne, Portugal,

Toledo and Granado,

All these did meet and made a fleet,

And called it the Armado.

Where they had got provision,

As mustard, peas and bacon,

Some say two ships were full of whips,

But I think they were mistaken.

There was a little man of Spain

That shot well in a gun, a,

Don Pedro hight, as good a knight

As any of the Sun, a.

King Philip made him admiral

And charg‚Äôd him not to stay, a

But to destroy both man and boy

And then to run away, a.

The King of Spain did fret amain,

And to do yet more harm, a

He sent along, to make him strong,

The famous Prince of Parma.

When they had sailed along the seas

And anchored upon Dover,

Our Englishmen did board them then

And cast the Spaniards over.

Our Queen was then at Tilbury,

What could you more desire, a?

For whose sweet sake Sir Francis Drake

Did set them all on fire, a.

Away they ran by sea and land,

So well one man slew three score-a,

And had not they all run away,

O my soul, we‚Äôd had killed more-a.

Then let them neither brag nor boast,

For if they come again-a,

Let them take heed they do not speed,

As they did they know when-a.

versions.title: Tom and Barbara Brown sing Sir Francis Drake
versions.lyrics:
In eighty-eight, ere I was born,

Or I can well remember,

In August was a fleet prepared,

A hundred ships in number.

Proud Spain, with Biscayne, Portugal,

Toledo and Granado,

All these did meet and made one fleet,

And called it the Armado.

Their men were young, munitions strong

To do to us more harm, a,

They thought it mete to join their fleet

All with the Prince of Parma.

Their navy was well victualled

With bully beef and bacon,

Some say two ships were full of whips,

But I think they were mistaken.

They sailed round about our shores

And so came into Dover,

Our English lads did board them there

And threw the rascals over.

The Queen was then at Tilbury,

What more could we desire, a?

So Francis Drake, for her sweet sake,

He set them all on fire, a.

So let them look unto themselves

If they should come again, a,

They shall be serv‚Äôd as they were then

Ere ever I was born, a.

items.lyrics:
I went down to Sammy‚Äôs Bar

Hey, the last boat‚Äôs a-leaving

By the shore at Pieta

Call away the di-so

And my real love, she was there

There was sand all in her hair

How did sand get in your hair

Darling Johnny put it there

Been with Johnny all the day

Down at Ghajn Tuffheija Bay

He‚Äôs a better man by far

Because he‚Äôs got a Yankee car

I went out from Sammy‚Äôs Bar

Had to hire a Yankee car

Fourteen days I drank no wine

Saving for that love of mine

Then one day in Paula square

At a paper I did stare

Johnny tried a hairpin bend

For my love, it was the end

Going back to Sammy‚Äôs Bar

I don‚Äôt need no Yankee car

items.title: The Bitter-Sweet Bed (A Girl‚Äôs Song)
items.notes:
The Bitter-Sweet Bed (A Girl‚Äôs Song)

[Cyril Tawney]

Cyril Tawney wrote

The Bitter-Sweet Bed (A Girl‚Äôs Song)

in 1966. It is printed in the

Cyril Tawney Songbook .

Chris While sang

Bitter-Sweet Bed

in 1993 on The Albion Band‚Äôs HTD album

Acousticity ,

and recorded it in between November 1995 and June 1997 for

Tawney‚Äôs 1997 Neptune Tapes cassette
items.lyrics:
They told me ev‚Äôrything and nothing,

They fed me wisdom all the way,

Each friendly word that flowed into my breast

Remained to build a burden there instead.

When I came to my bitter-sweet bed, my love,

When I came to my bitter-sweet bed.

You sent your honey vows before you

To breathe away the clouds of shame.

Then as my fingers fanned a robe around your shoulders

And a halo for your head,

You were God on my bitter-sweet bed, my love,

You were God on my bitter-sweet bed.

However freshly turned the furrow,

However newly sown the seed,

That same sweet soil was ploughed a million times

Before we came by babies old and dead, my love.

Now the pain it pulls upon me

Where are your ivy arms now?

The hand that reaches out for yours

Can only close upon a stranger‚Äôs palm instead

As I lie on my bitter-sweet bed, my love,

As I lie on my bitter-sweet bed.

However freshly turned the furrow,

However newly sown the seed,

That same sweet soil was ploughed a million times

Before we came by babies old and dead, my love.

And now the pain it pulls upon me

Oh, where are your ivy arms now?

The hand that reaches out for yours

Can only close upon a stranger‚Äôs palm instead

As I lie on my bitter-sweet bed, my love,

As I lie on my bitter-sweet bed.

to Peter Kennedy on 3 November 1952 (BBC recording 18703).

This recording was included on the anthology
items.lyrics:
O my dad was a fisherman bold

And he lived till he grew old.

For he opens the pane and he pops out the flame,

Just to see how the wind do blow.

And often he say to me,

You‚Äôd be wise before you go.

Do you open the pane and pop out the flame,

Just to see how the wind do blow.

When the north wind roughly blow

Then I lay right snug below.

But I open the pane and I pop out the flame,

Just to see how the wind do blow.

When the wind come out of the east

You‚Äôll be looking for sleet and snow.

But I open the pane and pops out the flame,

Just to see howthe wind do blow.

When the wind back into the west

That‚Äôll come a rough in at best.

But I open the pane and pops out the flame,

Just to see how the wind do blow.

When the south wind softly blow

It‚Äôs then I love to go.

And I open the pane and pop out the flame,

Just to see how the wind do blow.

And my poor wife say to me,

We shall starve if you don‚Äôt go.

So I open the pane and I pops out the flame,

Just to see how the wind do blow.

Ah, now all you fishermen bold

If you‚Äôd live till you grow old.

Do you open the pane and pop out the flame,

Just to see how the wind do blow.

When the wind comes from the West,

It‚Äôll blow a rough‚Äôn at the best.

When the south soft winds do blow,

My wife says to me, Joem

Come all you fishermen bold

You‚Äôll push out the pane and pop out the flame

items.lyrics:
Dogs in the woods a-skinnin‚Äô up frogs,

To my in come kitty come kymo.

Chorus (after each verse):

Ky-maneero, kymo,

Baw, baw, baw, baw, billy illy income

Carrion crow a-sitting on an oak,

Watching a tailor cutting out a coat.

Fetch my arrow and my bow

That I may shoot yon carrion crow.

He aimed to shoot the carrion crow,

Instead he shot the old grey sow.

Go fetch my glasses and a spoon

That I may heal her gaping wound.

If she dies, we‚Äôll drag her to the house,

We‚Äôll have pork and chitlings and souse.

The old grey sow is dead and gone,

versions.title: Cyril Tawney sings Carrion Crow

‚ÄúO Wife, o wife, bring here my bow

‚ÄúO Wife, o wife, bring some brandy in a spoon

For our old sow√Ę¬Ä¬ôs fallen down in a swoon.‚ÄĚ

items.lyrics:
A ploughman dresses fine, he drinks strong beer ale and wine

‚ÄúPretty maids don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt think amiss a ploughman for to kiss,

For his breath smells as sweet as a rose.‚ÄĚ

Saying, ‚ÄúBe nimble and be quick by the swishing of your whip.‚ÄĚ

And so merrily he‚Äôll rattle them along.

When our shears are shod, to the blacksmith off we wad

items.title: The Cruel Brother / The Three Knights / The Rose and the Lily
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS974

; Child 11

Norman Buchan and Peter Hall:

The Scottish Folksinger
items.lyrics:
There did three Knights come from the West,

With the high and the lily o!

And these three Knights courted one Lady,

As the rose was so sweetly blown.

The first Knight came was all in white,

And asked of her if she‚Äôd be his delight.

The next Knight came was all in green,

And asked of her, if she‚Äôd be his Queen.

The third Knight came was all in red,

And asked of her, if she would wed.

Then have you asked of my Father dear,

Likewise of her who did me bear?

And have you asked of my brother John?

And also of my sister Anne?

Yes, I have asked of your Father dear,

Likewise of her who did you bear.

And I have asked of your sister Anne,

But I‚Äôve not asked of your brother John.

[Here some verses seem to be wanting.]

For on the road as they rode along,

There did they meet with her brother John.

She stooped low to kiss him sweet,

He to her heart did a dagger meet.

Ride on, ride on, cried the serving man,

Methinks your bride she looks wond‚Äôrous wan.

I wish I were on yonder stile,

For there I would site and bleed awhile.

I wish I were on yonder hill,

There I‚Äôd alight and make my will.

What would you give to your Father dear,

The gallant steed which doth me bear.

What would you give to your Mother dear,

My wedding shift which I do wear.

But she must wash it very clean,

For my heart‚Äôs blood sticks in ev‚Äôry seam.

What would you give to your sister Anne,

My gay gold ring, and my feathered fan.

What would you give to your brother John,

A rope and gallows to hang him on.

What would you give to your brother John‚Äôs wife,

A widow‚Äôs weeds, and a quiet life.

versions.title: Polly Johnson sings The Three Maids

There was three maids a-playing ball, I lily-O

There was three maids a-playing ball, I lily-O

The foremost one was dressed in red, I lily-O

The foremost one was dressed in red.

And this is the one I make my wed,

For the rose is sweet I know.

The middle one was dressed in green,

And this is the one I‚Äôll make my queen.

The foremost one was dressed in white,

O this is the one I‚Äôll make my wife.

Her brother John was standing by,

Ride on, ride on, to yonder‚Äės hill,

Ride on, ride on, to yonder‚Äės hill,

Till I get down and make my will.

What do you will your sister Ann?

What do you will your true love dear?

This snow white horse that I rode here.

What do you will your mother dear?

My snow white dress what I wore here.

Tell her to wash it nice and clean,

So my heart‚Äės blood call never be seen.

versions.title: Archie Fisher sings The Cruel Brother
versions.lyrics:
There were three sisters lived in a ha‚Äô

Hech, hey, and the lily gay,

By cam a knicht and he woo‚Äôd them a‚Äô

And the rose is aye the redder aye .

And the first ane she was dressed in green.

‚ÄúWould ye fancy me and be my queen?‚ÄĚ

And the second ane she was dressed in yellow.

‚ÄúWould ye fancy me and be my marrow ?

And the first ane she was dressed in red.

‚ÄúWould ye fancy me and be my bride?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYe may seek me frae my faither dear,

And frae my mither wha‚Äô did me bear.

‚ÄúYe may seek me frae my sister Anne,

And dinna forget my brither John.‚ÄĚ

And he socht her frae her faither, the king,

And he socht her frae her mither, the queen.

And he socht her frae her sister Anne,

But forgot tae speir at her brither John.

And her mither dressed her in her gown,

And her sister tied the flounces ‚Äôround.

Her faither mounted her on her horse,

And her brither led her doon the close .

And he‚Äôs ta‚Äôen a knife baith lang and sharp,

And he‚Äôs pierced the bonnie bride through the heart.

‚ÄúO lead me, lead me up yon hill,

And there I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sit and mak√Ę¬Ä¬ô my will.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat will ye leave tae your faither dear?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe bonnie white steed that brocht me here.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat will ye leave tae your mither dear?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe bloody robes that I do wear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat will ye leave tae your sister Anne?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe gowden ring frae off my hand.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat will ye leave tae your brither John?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe gallows tree for tae hang him on.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Battlefield Band sing The Cruel Brother
versions.lyrics:
There were three ladies played at ba‚Äô,

Hey wi‚Äô the rose and the linsey o,

‚ÄúO lady fair, gie me your hand,

And I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll mak ye lady o√Ę¬Ä¬ôer all my land.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúSir knight, ere you my favour win,

Ye maun gain consent o√Ę¬Ä¬ôer all my kin.‚ÄĚ

He gained consent fae her parents dear,

He‚Äôs gained consent o‚Äôer all her kin,

‚ÄúLead me tae yon high high hill,

‚ÄúAnd what will you gie tae your brother John?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAnd what will you gie to your brother John√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wife?‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Dick Gaughan sings The Cruel Brother
versions.lyrics:
Thair were three sisters lived in a haa

Hie the rose an the lily gay

‚ÄúWad ye come wi me an be ma queen?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs ye maun ask ma faither the king

‚ÄúAn it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs whit will ye leave tae yer faither the king?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe bonnie steed that A ride on‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAn whit will ye leave tae yer mither the queen?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe bluidy robes that A lie in‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAn whit will ye leave tae yer sister Anne?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMa guidlord tae be her man‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAn it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs whit will ye leave tae yer brither John?‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Peggy Seeger sings The Cruel Brother
versions.lyrics:
Three ladies played at cup and ball,

There came three knights among them all,

‚ÄúO, you must ask my father, the king

And you must ask my mother, the queen.

‚ÄúAnd you must ask my sister, Anne,

And you must ask my brother, John.‚ÄĚ

O, he did ask her father, the king,

And he did ask her mother, the queen.

He did ask her sister, Anne,

Her father, the king, led her down the hall,

Her sister, Anne, led her through the door,

Her brother, John put her on her horse.

‚ÄúNow you are high and I am low,

She bent low to give him a kiss

‚ÄúRise up, rise up, my foremost man!

My lady fair looks pale and wan.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúTake me up yon hill so high,

‚ÄúWhat will you leave to your father, the king?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe golden chair that 1 sat in.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat will you leave to your mother, the queen?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe bloody gown that I die in.

‚ÄúShe must wash it in yonder stream,

For my heart√Ę¬Ä¬ôs blood runs in every seam.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat will you say to your sister, Anne?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúDon√Ę¬Ä¬ôt give your heart till you give your hand.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAnd what will you leave to your brother, John?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA pair of gallows to hang him on.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat will you leave to your brother√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wife?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúGrief and misfortune all her life.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Martin Simpson sings The Cruel Brother
versions.lyrics:

versions.title: Eliza Carthy and Norma Waterson sing The Rose and the Lily

The first man came, he was all in white

The last man came and he was dressed in red

‚ÄúAs I have asked your father dear

And also her that did you bear.

And I have asked your sister Anne

Though I never met your brother John.‚ÄĚ

On the road as they rode alone

There they met her brother John;

O she stood low to give him kisses sweet,

Into her heart did a dagger meet.

‚ÄúI wish I were on yonder stile,

There I‚Äôd sit and I‚Äôd bleed awhile.

I wish I were upon yonder hill

For there I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd sit and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd make my will.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your father dear?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThis gallant horse that does me bear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your mother dear?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThis wedding dress that I do wear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give your mother dear?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThis wedding dress that I do wear;

Though she must wash it very, very clean

For my heart√Ę¬Ä¬ôs blood sticks in every seam.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your sister Anne?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy good gold ring and my feathered fan.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your brother John?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA tall, tall tree to hang him on.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your brother John?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA rope and gallows to hang him on.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your brother√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wife?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA widow√Ę¬Ä¬ôs weeds and a peaceful life.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: The Shee sing The Three Knights
versions.lyrics:
There were three knights came from the West,

The first knight came was all in green,

The next knight came was all in white,

And asked of her if she‚Äôd be his delight,

The third knight came was all in red,

And asked of her if she would wed,

‚ÄúThen you must ask of my Father dear,

Likewise of her that did me bear?

‚ÄúAnd you must ask of my brother John?

And also of my sister Anne.‚ÄĚ

So he did ask of her father dear,

Likewise of her that did she bear.

And he did ask of her sister Anne,

‚ÄôTwas on the road as they rode along,

There they did meet with her brother John.

She stooped low to kiss him sweet,

He to her heart did a dagger meet.

‚ÄúRide on, ride on,‚ÄĚ cried the serving man,

‚ÄúMethinks your bride she looks wond√Ę¬Ä¬ôrous wan.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI wish I was on yonder stile,

There I would site and I‚Äôd bleed awhile.

‚ÄúI wish I were on yonder hill,

There I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd alight and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd make my will.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your Father dear?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe gallant steed that doth me bear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your mother dear?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy wedding gown that I do wear.

‚ÄúBut she must wash it very clean,

For my heart√Ę¬Ä¬ôs blood sticks in ev√Ę¬Ä¬ôry seam.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your sister?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy gay gold ring and my feathered fan.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat would you give to your brother?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA rope and gallows to hang him on.‚ÄĚ

What would you give to your brother√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wife?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA widow√Ę¬Ä¬ôs weeds and a quiet life.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Steeleye Span sing Gulliver Gentle and Rosemary
versions.lyrics:
There were three ladies playing at ball,

Gulliver, Gentle and Rosemary,

There came three knights, looked over the wall,

The first young knight was clothed in red,

O the rose, the blood red rose,

He said, ‚ÄúGentle lady will you me wed?‚ÄĚ

O the rose and the pure white lily.

The second young knight was clothed in blue,

O the rose, the blood red rose,

He said, ‚ÄúTo my love I will be true.‚ÄĚ

O the rose and the pure white lily.

The third young knight was clothed in green,

O the rose, the blood red rose,

He said, ‚ÄúFair maiden, will you be my queen?‚ÄĚ

O the rose and the pure white lily.

The lady spoke to the knight in red,

‚ÄúWith you, Sir Knight, I never can wed.‚ÄĚ

The lady spoke to the knight in blue,

She said, ‚ÄúLittle faith I have in you.‚ÄĚ

The lady spoke to the knight in green,

She said, ‚Äú√Ę¬Ä¬ôTis a court you must seek for a queen.‚ÄĚ

The three young knights they rode away,

versions.title: Sophie Crawford sings Green Grow the Lily
versions.lyrics:
There were three ladies played at ball,

Green grow the lily O

And the rose is sweet and merry.

There came a young red knight

‚ÄúYou must ask of my father dear

And the mother that did me bear.

‚ÄúYou must ask of my brother John,

And you must ask my sisters everyone.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI have asked of your father dear

And the mother that did you bear.

‚ÄúI have asked of your lover John,

I have asked your sisters everyone.‚ÄĚ

Her father led her to the door

O the horse she rode it was pitch black

‚ÄúRide on, you false young man,

For I fear your bride looks awf‚Äôlly wan.

‚ÄúRide on and never fail,

For I fear your bride looks awf√Ę¬Ä¬ôlly pale.‚ÄĚ

They rode to the riverside

And there she laid down and died.

‚ÄúWhat√Ę¬Ä¬ôll you leave your mother dear?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO the bridal dress that I do wear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúTake it down to yonder stream,

My heart√Ę¬Ä¬ôs blood runs in every seam‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat√Ę¬Ä¬ôll you leave your brother√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wife?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO to mourn and grieve in all her life.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat√Ę¬Ä¬ôll you leave your brother John?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO a hangman√Ę¬Ä¬ôs noose to hang him on.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Innocent Hare sings Young Viola
versions.lyrics:
As young Viola played outside

Aye and the lilly aye

A fine young man came riding by

Roseys sweet in Mary

This young man was dressed in red

He asked Viola is she would him wed

You must ask my family all

And my brother John so great and tall o

I have asked your family all

And your lover John so great and tall

Her father gave her at the stile

Her mother weeped for her child o

John took a penknife long and sharp

John took a penknife long and sharp

He stabbed his sister to the heart

Roseys sweet in Mary

What will you give your brother John

Aye and the lilly aye

I‚Äôll give him the gallows to hang on

Roseys sweet in Mary

And she cursed her brother then

For wronged love did her life end o

items.title: The Cruise of the Calabar / The Manchester Canal
items.notes:
[

; Master title: The Cruise of the Calabar

; Henry H502

; Ballad Index HHH502

; DT CALABAR2

; Mudcat 167112

; trad.]

Fred Hamer:

Garners Gay

Gale Huntington, Lani Herrmann, John Moulden:

Sam Henry‚Äôs Songs of the People

Colm O Lochlainn:
items.lyrics:
When I were young and in my prime, as lazy as can be;

I stepped on board a Bradford fly, her number were twenty-three.

As we seet sail fro‚Äô Galsey Bay we geet lost in a terrible fog,

And th‚Äôonly mate abooard that flat were Dookey‚Äôs Airredale dog.

Now when we geet a bit further on we met owd twenty-one,

John Dakin he were steering, He‚Äôd a Bradford fly hung on.

That Bradford fly were owd Dolly‚Äôs John, Owd Dolly stood on t‚Äôbow deck

When t‚Äôtow rope broke and catched owd Dolly a belt at t‚Äôback o‚Äôt‚Äôneck.

We sailed along quite merrily till we come to Aintree Val,

And then it started raining, by gum it went dark wi‚Äô cloud,

I catched me foot in t‚Äô stern rail and went slurring reight on t‚Äôdeck.

And I went heead fost down t‚Äô scuttle hole and I nearly broke me neck.

Me fayther were down in t‚Äô cabin, He were hewing his bit o‚Äô scoff.

He said, ‚ÄúNow lad be careful, tha welly knocked t√Ę¬Ä¬ôkettle off.

Tha√Ę¬Ä¬ôd better frame and start pumping for I think we√Ę¬Ä¬ôve sprung a leak.‚ÄĚ

And I wished I‚Äôd never gone booating for my living for to seek.

Soon after that we‚Äôd an awful shock, ee met another ship.

And that were old Tom Rover, ee were on a Sunda‚Äô Schoo‚Äô trip.

He shouted out, ‚ÄúHowd in,you clown!‚ÄĚ I forgeet and howded out.

And we met stem up in t‚Äômiddle o‚Äô cut, by gum and I geet a clout.

We ended up w‚Äôt‚Äô booat i‚Äô t‚Äô pawnshop and our crew went to Walton Jail,

And I‚Äôm th‚Äô only survivor who lived to tell the tale.

I‚Äôve ‚Äôed some happy memories and some bad ‚Äôuns too, I vow,

But I‚Äôll never forget when I war t‚Äô fost mate on booard o‚Äô the Calibar.

versions.title: Cyril Tawney sings The Cruise of the ‚ÄėCalabar‚Äô
versions.lyrics:
Come all you dry-land sailors bold and listen to my song,

There are but forty verses so it won‚Äôt detain you long.

It‚Äôs all about the history of this here British tar

Who shipped as a man before the mast on board of the Calabar.

The Calabar was a clipper flat, copper-fastened afore and aft,

The rudder stuck away out behind and the wheel was a great big shaft.

With half a gale to swell each sail, she‚Äôd make two knots an hour,

The smartest craft on the whole canal, though only one horse-power.

Our captain was a strapping youth, he stood about four foot two.

His eyes was black, and his nose was red, and his cheeks was Prussian blue,

He wore a leather medal that he‚Äôd won in the Crimea war,

And his wife was pilot and passenger‚Äôs cook aboard of the Calabar.

Our vessel ploughed the waters of the Teignmouth to Newton Canal,

All under close three topsails for the glass foretold a squall.

It was in old Salty pool, my lads, we was beaten about the surf,

All bound for the port of Newton, my lad, with a cargo of Dartmoor turf.

We started with a favouring gale, the weather was all sublime,

But just on passing the Teignmouth bridge where you can‚Äôt pass two at a time.

We were struck midships by another flat which gave us a serious check

For she stove in our larboard paddle-box and destroyed our hurricane deck.

While hugging the shore near Netherton Point, a very dangerous spot,

We ran down onto a cob of coal that wasn‚Äôt marked down on the chart.

So to keep the ship from sinking and to save each precious life

We threw the cargo overboard, including the captain‚Äôs wife.

Then all was great confusion while the stormy winds did blow,

Our bosun slipped on an orange peel and he fell in the hold below.

‚ÄúA piratical junk!‚ÄĚ the captain cried, ‚Äúand on us she doth gain!

And when next I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm going go to Newton, my lads, by jaggers I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go by train.‚ÄĚ

We got our arms all ready for to meet the coming foe,

Our grappling arms, our boarding pikes, our Armstrong guns also.

‚ÄúSlap on all speed!‚ÄĚ the captain cried, ‚Äúfor we are sorely pressed!‚ÄĚ

But the engineer replied from the bank that the ‚Äôorse was doing his best.

Full thick and fast the heroes fell, in torrents the blood was spilt.

Many were falling before they were touched to make sure they wouldn‚Äôt be killed.

And when the enemy struck her flag, the crew being laid on their backs

Well, we found that she was a sister‚Äôs craft with a cargo of cobbler‚Äôs wax.

versions.title: Harry Boardman sings The Manchester Canal
versions.lyrics:
O the S.S. Irwell left this port the stormy sea to cross.

They heaved the lead and went ahead on a voyage to Barton Moss.

No fair ship e‚Äôer left the slip from this port to Natal

Than the boats that plough the waters of the Manchester Canal.

The third day out or thereabout, a great storm swept the main.

The captain called his officer, I just forget his name.

‚ÄúYou see that light there on the right?‚ÄĚ ‚ÄúAye, aye,‚ÄĚ he did exclaim.

‚ÄúWell it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the Wilsons√Ę¬Ä¬ô Brewery lightship at the end of Ancoats Lane.‚ÄĚ

The captain‚Äôs brow was darkened for he saw a storm was brewing,

And the engineer reported that the horse it wanted shoeing.

‚ÄúIs there a chart aboard this barque?‚ÄĚ He asked of one or two.

The captain he was ashy pale and so was all the crew.

‚ÄúBy gum, we√Ę¬Ä¬ôve lost our reckoning, whatever shall we do?

We must be near to Bailey Bridge on the banks of Pin Mill Brow.‚ÄĚ

Then all became confusion as the stormy winds did roar,

The captain wished himself and crew were safe again on shore.

‚ÄúLet go the anchor, boys,‚ÄĚ he cried, ‚Äúfor I am sorely puzzled.

The mate is drunk and in his bunk, see that the cook is muzzled.

We‚Äôre short of grub in this ‚Äôere tub, and we are far from land.

There‚Äôs not an oat in this ‚Äôere boat and the engine‚Äôs broken down.

‚ÄúClose reef the sails,‚ÄĚ the bosun cried, ‚Äúwe√Ę¬Ä¬ôre in a great dilemma.

Just row her to Pomona Bay, she cannot stand the weather.

She‚Äôs sprung a leak, now all is lost! Let each man do his best,

For soon she√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be a total wreck on the shoals of Throstle Nest.‚ÄĚ

But soon the storm abated, it was rather overrated

When captain, crew and officers were quickly congregated.

They searched the chart in every part, to find their situation.

They were east-nor-east of Bailey Bridge, just south of Salford Station.

items.title: The Death of Queen Jane
items.notes:

items.lyrics:
Queen Jane was in labour for six days or more;

Till her women got tired and wished it were o‚Äôer.

‚ÄúGood women, good women, good women if you be,

Will you send for King Henery, for King Henery I must see.‚ÄĚ

King Henery was a-sent for and King Henery did come home,

For to meet with Queen Jane. ‚ÄúMy Love, your eyes do look so dim.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúKing Henery, King Henery, King Henery if you be,

If you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll have my right side opened you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll find my dear baby.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúQueen Jane, my Love, Queen Jane, my Love, such a thing was never known.

If you have your right side opened you will lose your dear baby.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWill you build your love a castle, and dig down so deep,

For to bury my body and christen my dear baby?‚ÄĚ

King Henery went a-mourning and so did his men,

And so did his dear baby, for Queen Jane did dyen.

How wide was the mourning, how wide were the bands,

How yellow, yellow were the flamboys they carried in their hands.

There was fiddling, there was dancing on the day the babe was born,

While the royal Queen Jane, beloved, lay cold as a stone.

versions.title: Martin Graebe sings Queen Jane
versions.lyrics:
Queen Jane O! Queen Jane O! What a lady was she!

And she was in labour six weeks and a day.

Queen Jane she was in labour for six weeks and more,

‚ÄôTil the women grew so weary that they fain would give o‚Äôer.

‚ÄúO women! O women! Good women if ye be,

Pray send for King Henry and bring him to me.‚ÄĚ

King Henry was sent for, he came in all speed

In a gown of red velvet from the heel to his head

King Henry was sent for, and to her he came;

‚ÄúDear Lady! Fair Lady! your eyes they look so dim.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúKing Henry! King Henry! If kind you would be,

Pray send for a good doctor and bring him to me.‚ÄĚ

The doctor he was sent for and he came in all speed,

In a gown of black velvet from his heel to his head.

The doctor he was sent for and to her he came,

‚ÄúDear Lady! Fair Lady! Your labour it is vain.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO Doctor! Dear Doctor! If kind you would be,

Pray open my right side and free my baby.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO no!‚ÄĚ cried King Henry ‚Äúthat never can be.

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd rather lose the branches than the top of the tree.‚ÄĚ

The doctor gave her a caudle, and the death sleep slept she,

And he opened her right side and he freed her baby.

The babe it was christened and put out and nursed,

But the royal Queen Jane she lay dead in the dust.

versions.title: Martin Carthy sings Death of Queen Jane
versions.lyrics:
Queen Jane lay in labour full nine days or more

Till the women were so tired, they could stay no longer there,

Till the women were so tired, they could stay no longer there.

‚ÄúGood women, good women, good women as ye be,

Do you open up my right side to find my baby,

Do you open up my right side and find my baby.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO no,‚ÄĚ says the women, ‚Äúthat never may be,

We will send for King Henry we will hear what he say,

We will send for King Henry and hear what he say.‚ÄĚ

King Henry was sent, for King Henry he did come:

‚ÄúWhat do ail you, my lady, for your eyes look so dim?

What do ail you, my lady, your eyes look so dim?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúKing Henry, King Henry, will you do one thing for me?

Will you open up my right side and find my baby?

Will you open up my right side and find my baby?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO no,‚ÄĚ says King Henry, ‚Äúit√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a thing I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never do.

If I lose the flower of England, I shall lose the branch too,

If I lose the flower of England, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll lose the branch too.‚ÄĚ

King Henry went mourning, and so did his men,

And so did the dear baby, Queen Jane did die then,

And so did the dear baby, Queen Jane did die then.

How deep was their mourning, how black were the bands,

How yellow, yellow were the flamboys that they carried in their hands,

How yellow, yellow were the flamboys they carried in their hands.

There was fiddling, there was dancing on the day the babe was born,

But poor Queen Jane beloved lay cold as any stone,

But poor Queen Jane beloved lay cold as any stone.

versions.title: Robyn Stapleton sings Queen Jeanie
versions.lyrics:
Queen Jeanie lay in labour six days and more

Till all her good women were forced to gie her o‚Äôer

With weeping and wailing, lamenting full sore

That the fair flower o England would flourish no more

‚ÄúO women, dear women, good women you be

O send for my mother to come and see me‚ÄĚ

Her mother was sent for and instantly came

Knelt down by the bedside where Jeannie did lay

‚ÄúO Mother, dear Mother, if Mother you be

O send for my father to come and see me‚ÄĚ

Her father was sent for and instantly came

Knelt down by the bedside where Jeannie did lay

‚ÄúO Father, dear Father, if Father you be

O send for my Henry to come and see me‚ÄĚ

King Henry was sent for and instantly came

Knelt down by the bedside where Jeannie did lay

‚ÄúO Henry, dear Henry, if Henry you be

O send for the doctor to come and see me‚ÄĚ

The doctor was sent for and instantly came

Knelt down by the bedside where Jeannie did lay

‚ÄúO Doctor, dear Doctor, if Doctor you be

Open my left side and let my babe free‚ÄĚ

Her left side was opened the young prince was found

‚ÄúO Doctor dear Doctor lay me to the ground‚ÄĚ

Her bones were all broken and laid at her feet

Her body was blessed with ointment so sweet

And ay as they wept they wrung their hands sore

That the fair floo‚Äôer of England will flourish no more

versions.title: Tannara sing Queen Jane
versions.lyrics:
Queen Jane lay in labour full six days or more,

Till the women grew weary and the midwives gave o‚Äôer.

They sent for King Henry to come with great speed

To be with Queen Jane in her hour of need.

King Henry came to her and he sat by her side.

Sing, ‚ÄúWhat ails thee my Jeannie? What ails thee my bride?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO Henry, O Henry, do this one thing for me

Rip open my right side and find my baby.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO Jeannie, O Jeannie, that never will do.

It would lease thy sweet life and thy young baby too.‚ÄĚ

Well, she wept and she wailed til she fell into a swoon

And her right side was opened, and her baby was found.

Well, the baby was christened the very next day

While his poor dead mother a-mouldering lay.

Six men went before her and four more travelled on

While loyal King Henry stood mourning alone.

Well, he wept and he wailed until he was sore,

Saying, ‚ÄúThe flower of all England will flourish no more.‚ÄĚ

He sat by the river with his head in his hands,

Saying, ‚ÄúMy merry England is a sorrowful land.‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Fireship
items.notes:

Cyril Tawney sang The Fireship

in 1964 on the Topic anthology of sea songs and shanties,

Farewell Nancy ,

and in 1992 on his Neptune Tapes cassette of ‚Äúmore songs of seafarers

and the fairer sex‚ÄĚ,
items.lyrics:
As Jack as a-walking Point Beach up and down

He saw pretty Nancy from merry Portsmouth Town.

As soon as he spied her most beautiful face

He hoisted his tops‚Äôl and to her gave chase.

Chorus (after each verse):

T‚Äôme riggin gray!

Come rattle my riggin down!

Come rattle my riggin down Ratcliffe Highway!

He hailed her in Dutch and the signal she knew,

He hoisted his tops‚Äôl and for her hove to.

Jack lowered his jolly boat, and pulled along side,

And he found madam‚Äôs gangway both open and wide!

Jack entered her cabin, and swore, ‚ÄúDamn her eyes!‚ÄĚ

She was nothing but a fireship rigged out in disguise!

Set fire to Jack‚Äôs rigging, likewise to his hull,

And up to the hospital Jack had to skull.

Set fire to Jack‚Äôs rigging, likewise to his hull,

And up to the hospital Jack has to skull,

With his helm hard to starboard as he sailed along,

And his mess-mates cried after him, ‚ÄúYour mainyard is sprung!‚ÄĚ

Now, Jack‚Äôs back in England in merry Portsmouth at last.

And he lies on the lower deck amongst the low-class.

He lies on his hammock, and he cries out, ‚ÄúO, Lord!

Wasn√Ę¬Ä¬ôt that a stiff breeze when I sprung my main yard?‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Grey Funnel Line
items.notes:

The Grey Funnel Line

[

items.lyrics:
Don‚Äôt mind the rain or the rolling sea,

The weary night never worries me.

But the hardest time in sailor‚Äôs day

Is to watch the sun as it dies away.

It‚Äôs one more day on the Grey Funnel Line.

The finest ship that sailed the sea

Is still a prison for the likes of me.

But give me wings like Noah‚Äôs dove,

I‚Äôd fly up harbour to the girl I love.

It‚Äôs one more day on the Grey Funnel Line.

There was a time my heart was free

Like a floating spar on the open sea.

But now the spar is washed ashore,

It comes to rest at my real love‚Äôs door.

It‚Äôs one more day on the Grey Funnel Line.

Every time I gaze behind the screws

Makes me long for old Peter‚Äôs shoes.

I‚Äôd walk right down that silver lane

And take my love in my arms again.

It‚Äôs one more day on the Grey Funnel Line.

Oh Lord, if dreams were only real

I‚Äôd have my hands on that wooden wheel.

And with all my heart I‚Äôd turn her round

And tell the boys that we‚Äôre homeward bound.

It‚Äôs one more day on the Grey Funnel Line.

I‚Äôll pass the time like some machine

Until blue water turns to green.

Then I‚Äôll dance on down that walk ashore

And sail the Grey Funnel Line no more.

And sail the Grey Funnel Line no more.

¬© Cyril Tawney 1959

items.lyrics:
As I were a walking down by the seaside

I saw a red herring washed up by the tide,

And that little herring I took home and dried

Don‚Äôt you think I done well with my jolly Herring.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Toorali ladi rye toorali laye

Toorali ladi rye toorali laye

Toorali ladi rye toorali laye

Don‚Äôt you think I done well with my jolly herring

Now what would you think that I made with his eyes:

The finest of lamps now that ever did shine.

There were big lamps and little lamps and lamps for to shine,

items.title: The Hostess‚Äôs Daughter / I Sowed Some Seeds
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS62

; Master title: The Hostess‚Äôs Daughter

; Ballad Index ReSh045

; VWML HAM/2/2/18

, HAM/4/23/20

, SBG/2/2/144

; Bodleian

; Folkinfo 220

; Mudcat 19261

; trad.]

Sabine Baring-Gould, H. Fleetwood Sheppard:
items.lyrics:
When first to London town I came

I took my lodging all at some inn.

For full five months I did remain

But being a stranger I fell in danger

Doing so, doing so.

The landlord had a daughter fair,

She was a beauty I do declare.

In her bedchamber I dared not go

But being a stranger I fell in danger

Doing so, doing so.

She‚Äôd ruby lips and she‚Äôd eyes of blue,

They caused me to love her so;

I kissed her lips, her bosom too,

But being a stranger I fell in danger

Doing so, doing so.

All in the grove my seed was sown,

All in the grove where grew no green.

The more I kissed her, this girl being young,

The more I kissed her her eyes did glister

Like the rising of the sun.

The seeds of love, they grew apace,

The tears they blossomed all on her face.

All for to reap it I would not stay

But being a stranger I fell in danger,

Ran away, I ran away.

Now when nine long months they were gone and past

This pretty girl had her babe at last.

She must keep it like it was her own

And reap the seeds I myself have sown

Doing so, doing so.

versions.title: Faustus sing The Hostesses Daughter
versions.lyrics:
When first to London town I came

I took my lodging all at some inn.

For five full months I did remain

But being a stranger I fell in danger

By doing so, by doing so.

The landlord had one daughter dear,

She was a beauty I do declare.

But above her garters I dared not go,

But being a stranger I fell in danger

By doing so, by doing so.

Her ruby lips, her eyes so blue,

Which caused me to love her true;

I kissed her rosy lips and cheeks,

For being a stranger I fell in danger

By doing so, by doing so.

And more I kissed her, this girl being young,

Her eyes did glisten like the morning sun.

In yonder grove I sowed my seed

For being a stranger I fell in danger

By doing so, by doing so.

The seeds of love, they grew apace,

The tears they ever ran down her face.

All for to reap it I could not stay

For being a stranger I fell in danger

By doing so, by doing so.

And when this pretty little babe is born

Oh she must keep it, it is her own,

And reap the seeds which I have sown

For being a stranger I fell in danger

By doing so, by doing so,

By doing so, by doing so.

versions.title: Salt House sing I Sowed Some Seeds
versions.lyrics:
I sowed some seeds all in,

Some grove there grows no green.

For to repeat I could not stir,

I being a stranger, I fell in danger

For doing so, for doing so.

The seeds they grew apace,

The tears were on her face.

All for to reap I could not stay

For being a stranger I fell in danger

For doing so, for doing so.

When nine long months was gone this girl had a fine son.

Now she must keep and call her own

And reap the seed that I have sown.

For being a stranger I fell in danger

For doing so, for doing so.

I sowed on yonder hills

And how to reap I dare not,

And how to reap I dare not say.

For being a stranger I felt in danger

I runned away, I runned away for doing so.

items.title: The Knight‚Äôs Dream / A Cornish Young Man / The Labouring Man‚Äôs Daughter

Cyril Tawney sang A Cornish Young Man

items.lyrics:
A Cornish young man he dreamed a dream

So put me not under no trial

No whore should you be, no any such thing

So take this sweet kiss as a token

For love, oh my dear, is a stone in a sling

And it‚Äôs hard to believe when its spoken

Oh, take up this ring and a guinea in gold

For love, oh my dear, is a stone in a sling

And it‚Äôs hard to believe till its spoken

versions.title: Danny Stradling sings The Labouring Man‚Äôs Daughter
versions.lyrics:

Sure, I hope that you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never deny me.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúDeny you‚ÄĚ, said she, ‚Äúhow could that come to be,

And you being so highly above me.

Oh, but just ‚Äôcos I‚Äôm poor, you don‚Äôt think me a fool,

And you don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt put me under a trial.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh, love it is a thing that just hangs like a string,

And a string that is easily broken.

Oh, but you can be sure that to you I‚Äôll be true,

‚ÄúI don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt want your guinea gold ring no nor any such a thing,

Nor I won‚Äôt take your store though I‚Äôm lowly and poor,

items.title: The Lone Shepherd
items.notes:

The Lone Shepherd

[Cyril Tawney]

Cyril Tawney sang his song

Bah! Humbug: The Alternative Christmas Album .

Ian Green noted in its booklet:

This song was written by Cyril 40 years after
items.lyrics:
If I‚Äôd been seated all on the ground

Last night when glory it shone all around

I‚Äôd be below now with the rest of them

Not up here freezing on the heights of Bethlehem.

It sounds so stupid in the light of day

But nature‚Äôs call I found I must obey

So when that angel bright his moment chose

He caught me vertical instead of in repose.

My eyes were dazzled, I stepped back with shock

I turned my ankle on a well-placed rock

Now I‚Äôm immobile, which is why you‚Äôll find

They‚Äôre down their at their ease, while I the flocks do mind.

They‚Äôve just got back now, and I‚Äôve heard it all

Of this holy infant in an ox‚Äôs stall

Of myrrh and frankincense and even gold

I missed the whole damn lot, as well as catching cold.

But on reflection it occurs to me

My whole misfortune it was meant to be

There was an inn attached, and inns have wine

And self-denial, well, that‚Äôs no strong point of mine.

He knows my weakness, and it‚Äôs my belief

He planned that moment when I sought relief

For smooth fulfilment of the prophecies

No place for tosspots at nativities.

He‚Äôd want no boozy breath, or belching gut

No drunken choruses all full of smut

No sacred air defiled by bawdy jokes

Or shepherds throwing up on wise men‚Äôs costly cloaks.

Too late for ‚Äėsorries‚Äô, that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a certain bet

When you reel sideways and the crib‚Äôs upset

And no point bending knee to genuflect

If once you‚Äôre down there you need help to stand erect.

Ah yes, I see now my bad luck was meant

Such dire calamities to circumvent

As it is now, so shall it ever be

He with his wondrous ways moves most mysteriously.

items.title: The Man at the Nore / Keeper of the Eddystone Light
items.notes:
[

; Ballad Index PBB120

; Bodleian

; DT EDDYNORE

; Mudcat 60153

; trad.]

Cyril Tawney sang The Man at the Nore

in 1963 on his EFDSS album

Between Decks

and in 1990 on his Neptune Tapes cassette of

songs of seafarers and the fairer sex,

Sailor‚Äôs Delight .
items.lyrics:
Now my father was the keeper of the Eddystone light

And he married a mermaid one fine night

From this union there came three

Two of ‚Äôem was fishes and the other was me

Now when I was but a bit of a slip

I was put in charge of the Nore lightship

I kept my lamps in very good style

Doing of the work according to Hoyle:

Chorus (after each verse):

Oh the raging Nore, the rolling Nore

The waves they tumble o‚Äôer and o‚Äôer

There‚Äôs no such a life to be had on shore

As the one that‚Äôs led by the Man at the Nore.

Well, one evening as I was a-trimming of the glim

Singing a verse from the Evening Hymn

I spied by the light of my signal lamp

The form of my mother looking awfully damp

Just then a voice cried out, Ahoy

And there she was just a-sitting on a buoy

That‚Äôs meaning a buoy for the ships that sail

And not a boy that‚Äôs a juvenile male:

Says I to my mother, Now how do you do

And how‚Äôs my father and my sisters two?

Says she, It‚Äôs an orph-i-an you are

You‚Äôve only one sister and you‚Äôve got no pa

Your father was drowned with sever-i-al pals

And digested by the cannibals

Of your sisters, one was cooked in a dish

The other one is kept as a talking fish:

Well, at that I wept like a soft-eyed scamp

My tears, they made the waters damp

Says I to my mother, Won‚Äôt you step within

You look so wet, just to dry your skin

Says she, I likes the wet, my dear

Says I, Let me offer you the cabin chair

My mother, she looks at me with a frown

It‚Äôs owing to my nature that I can‚Äôt sit down:

Says my mother, Now never you go on shore

But always remain the Man at the Nore

With that, I caught a glittering scale

And that was the end of my mother‚Äôs tale

Now in deference to this maternal wish

I can‚Äôt visit my sister, the talking fish

So if you sees her when you gets on shore

Give her the regards of the Man at the Nore:

items.title: The Nightingale
items.notes:
>

Frankie Armstrong >

The Nightingale

>

Norma Waterson >

Songs >

The Nightingale

>

Eliza Carthy >

Songs >

The Nightingale
items.lyrics:
Both old and young, I pray lend an ear

To a lovesick maiden in deep despair

Whose heart was light, but whose courage failed

When her true love sailed in the Nightingale .

Her parents were of high degree,

Her true love not so rich as they.

So they sent a press gang which did not fail

To press her true love in the nightingale.

As she that night on her pillow lay,

A form before her these words did say:

‚ÄúGo, tell your parents they may bequail

For the loss of your true love on the Nightingale .

‚ÄúOn the fourteenth day of December last,

The storm did blow a most fearful blast.

We lost our spars, likewise every sail.

What a dismal wreck was the Nightingale !‚ÄĚ

As she awoke in a terrible fright,

It being the hour of twelve at night,

To see his ghost standing cold and pale,

Just as he was drowned on the Nightingale ,

These words he spake in lamenting cries:

‚ÄúIn the Bay of Biscay my body lies

To become the prey of the shark and whale,

With my drowned shipmates in the Nightingale .‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Oggie Man
items.notes:
The Oggie Man

[

; DT OGGYMAN

; Mudcat 34833

; Cyril Tawney]

Cyril Tawney wrote this song about a Cornish pasty seller in 1959.

His own recordings of

The Oggie Man

can be found on the Elektra LP

A Cold Wind Blows: Songs in Traditional Styles

(1966), on the Argo LP

A Mayflower Garland (1970)
items.lyrics:
Well the rain‚Äôs softly falling and the Oggie Man‚Äôs no more;

I can‚Äôt hear him calling like I used to before.

I came through the gateway and I heard the sergeant say,

‚ÄúThe big boys are coming, see their stand across the way.‚ÄĚ

Yes the rain‚Äôs softly falling and the Oggie Man‚Äôs no more.

It was there that she told me when she bade me good bye,

‚ÄúThere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs no one will miss you one half as much as I

My love will endure, dear, like a beacon in the squall

Eternal as that Oggie Man beneath the dockyard wall.‚ÄĚ

Well the rain‚Äôs softly falling and the Oggie Man‚Äôs no more.

items.title: There Are No Lights on Our Christmas Tree
items.notes:
>

The Watersons >

Songs >

There Are No Lights on Our Christmas Tree

There Are No Lights on Our Christmas Tree

[

; DT NOLIGHTS

; Mudcat 64860

; Cyril Tawney]

Cyril Tawney wrote

There Are No Lights on Our Christmas Tree

in 1962. He sang it in 1997 on his cassette of non-maritime songs,
items.lyrics:
The time has come for festivity

For Christmas pudding and revelry

But as I passed a house the other night

I heard this little voice so clear and bright:

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

There are no lights on our Christmas tree

We must not spoil the televee

No party games, no mistletoe

Just whistle ‚ÄúWenceslas‚ÄĚ and out you go

Just once a year I become a square

I like to feel the tinsel in my hair

I like to sing the songs of days gone by

But dad and me we don‚Äôt see eye to eye

The box of crackers from Uncle Alf

It lies unopened upon the shelf

Dad has forbid them but we‚Äôre hoping he

Won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt notice one more bang in ‚ÄúLaramie‚ÄĚ

Some carol singers came to the door

I‚Äôve never seen Dad so mad before

He grabbed the leader by the coat

And tried to ram his lantern down his throat.

The latest boy-friend of sister Bette

Was simply gasping for a cigarette

He looked a proper case I do declare

A-striking matches down behind a chair.

When I grow up to be a man

There‚Äôll be no television in my plan

With laughter gay my house will ring

I never want to hear my family sing

items.title: The Roving Journeyman / The Rambling Irishman
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS2

; Master title: The Roving Journeyman

; G/D 7:1397

; Ballad Index MA062

; VWML SBG/3/2/66

; Bodleian

; GlosTrad

453

items.lyrics:
I am a rambling Irishman; I travel the country o‚Äôer

In search of an occupation like I never done before.

I turned across the ocean and I thought it a very good plan

To take a trip to Americay and unto your happy land.

When I landed in Philadelphia all the girls all jumped with joy.

Says one unto the other, ‚ÄúHere comes an Irish boy.‚ÄĚ

They wanted me to join with them, and took me by the hand;

They took one merrily round the room: ‚ÄúLong life to the Irishman!‚ÄĚ

They took me to a big hotel; was there to spend the night.

The landlord‚Äôs wife and daughter ‚Äôtwas me took great delight.

She never took her eyes off me ‚Äď √Ę¬Ä¬ôtwas on the floor I stand ‚Äď

And she shouted to her mother, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm in love with the Irishman.‚ÄĚ

It√Ę¬Ä¬ôs ‚ÄúDaughter, dear daughter, what√Ę¬Ä¬ôs this you are going to do:

To fall in love with an Irishman, the laddie you never knew,

With his knapsack on his shoulder, his shillelagh in his hand?‚ÄĚ

Saying, ‚ÄúMother dear, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd roam the world with a rambling Irishman.‚ÄĚ

It‚Äôs now that we are married and we‚Äôre settled down for life,

It‚Äôs happy as the turtle dove myself and my wee wife.

I‚Äôll work for her, I‚Äôll toil for her, I‚Äôll do the best I can,

But she never say she rue the day she married the Irishman.

versions.title: Paddy Doran sings The Roving Journeyman
versions.lyrics:
O there was a little beggarman that goes from town to town,

And wherever he gets a job of work he‚Äôs willing to sit down;

With his bundle on his shoulder, his stick was in his hand,

And it‚Äôs round the country I‚Äôd go with my roving journey-man.

And from the County Carlow, the girls jump for joy,

Said one unto the other, ‚ÄúNow here comes a travelling boy.‚ÄĚ

And they wanted me to marry her and took me by the hand,

She went home and told her mother, ‚Äôdeed, she loves a journey-man.

versions.title: Tom Willett sings The Roving Journeyman
versions.lyrics:
I am a roaming journeyman, I roam from town to town,

And when I get a job of work I‚Äôm willing to sit down.

With my bundle on my shoulder, with my stick all in my hand,

And it‚Äôs round the country I will go, like a roaming journeyman.

Now when I get to Brighton Town, the girls they jump for joy,

Saying one unto the other, ‚ÄúThere comes the roaming boy.‚ÄĚ

One hands to me the bottle, and the other holds the glass,

And the toast goes round the table, ‚ÄúHere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs good luck to the journeyman.‚ÄĚ

I cannot think the reason why my love she looks so sly,

I never had any false heart to any young female kind.

I never had a false heart to any young female kind,

But I always went a-roaming for to leave my girl behind.

versions.title: John Kirkpatrick sings The Roving Journeyman
versions.lyrics:
I am a roving journeyman and I roam from town to town,

And when I‚Äôve done a job of work I‚Äôm willing to sit down.

With my kit all on my shoulder and my grafting tool in hand,

And it‚Äôs round the country I will go a roving journeyman.

And when I came to London town the maidens jumped for joy;

Said one unto the other; ‚ÄúO here comes a roving boy.‚ÄĚ

They treat me to the bottle and they treat me to the can,

And it‚Äôs let the toast go round, boys, to the roving journeyman.

Well I had not been in London town the days were scarcely three

Before my master‚Äôs daughter she fell in love with me.

She asked me for to dine with her and she took me by the hand,

And she proudly told her mammy that she loved a journeyman.

‚ÄúO get away you foolish maid such folly speak no more;

How can you love a roving man you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve never seen before?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO mother sweet I do entreat, I love him all I can,

And it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs round the country I will go with my roving journeyman.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Danny Brazil sings The Rambling Irishman
versions.lyrics:
Sure I am a bit of an Irishman,
and I‚Äôve travelled the country round;

To seek for work in foreign parts,
I‚Äôve took it to be a plan.

With me bundle on me shoulder,
me shillelagh blackthorn in me hand,

Sure I bless the day as I sailed away
as a rambling Irishman.

When I landed in Castlecomerol
the girls did jump for joy;

There was one unto the other,
‚ÄúHere comes an Irish boy.‚ÄĚ

One treated me with a bottle
and the other one with a can,

And the toast went round the table,
‚ÄúHere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs good luck to an Irishman.‚ÄĚ

Sure I hadn‚Äôt been in Philadelphie,
it was scarce likely days, no more;

All for the landlady‚Äôs daughter,
she fell in love with me.

She asked me if I‚Äôd dine with her;
she kissed and squoze my hand

And she whispered to her mummy,
‚ÄúSure, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm in love with the Irishman.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs daughter, dearest daughter,
you must be mad I‚Äôm sure,

To fall in love with an Irishman
you never known before.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs hold your tongue, dear mother‚ÄĚ, she said,
‚ÄúI will do the best I can,

And I mean to ramble the world around
with me rambling Irishman.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: David Stacey sings The Roving Journeyman
versions.lyrics:
I am a roving journeyman and
I roam from town to town.

O and when I gets a job of work
I‚Äôm ready to set down.

With me bundle all on me shoulder
and me stick all in me hand,

And round the country I shall go
like a roving journeyman.

Now when I arrived in Brighton Town
all the girls they jumped for joy

Said one unto the other,
‚ÄúHere comes the roving boy.‚ÄĚ

Now one handed me the bottle,
the other held the glass

And the toast goes round the table,
‚ÄúHere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs good luck to a journeyman.‚ÄĚ

Now I hadn‚Äôt been in Brighton Town
but days scarce two or three

Before me master‚Äôs daughter
she fell in love with me.

She asked me for to dine with her
and she took me by the hand

And boldly she told her parents
that she loved the journeyman.

‚ÄúO get away you foolish maid,
such folly speak no more,

For he‚Äôs only a roving journeyman
that you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve never seen before.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBut Mother sweet, in do entreat
I‚Äôll love him all I can,

And round the country I shall go
like a roving journeyman.‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Song Goes On
items.notes:
The Song Goes On

[

; Mudcat 159180

; Mick Ryan]

Mick Ryan wrote The Song Goes On after Cyril Tawney‚Äôs death and

dedicated it to him. Mick Ryan, Pete Harris, Keith Kendrick, Sylvia Needham,

and Doug Bailey sang it at a concert in memory of Cyril Tawney which was held

at the Sidmouth Folk Festival in 2005. This live recording was included

in 2007 on the Cyril Tawney anthology

The Song Goes On .

Mick Ryan and Pete Harris also recorded it in 2006 for their WildGoose

album
items.lyrics:
There are singers that we love as we sing our lives away

And though we all fall silent in the end,

They will sing with us forever they‚Äôll be singing every day

When we sing the songs they sang.

Chorus:

For the song goes on in the songs we sing,

And when one song ends then another song begins,

So the singers who are gone will be singing once again

When we sing the songs they sang.

Some are heard by many and some are heard by few,

And though we all fall silent in the end,

Someone will remember both the song you sing and you,

When they sing the songs you sang.

(Chorus)

Refrain:

Sing the chorus, sing the song,

And the singers gone before us will be singing right along.

Sing the chorus and refrain,

And the singers gone before us will be singing once again.

We can drive away the silence of the grave that we all fear,

And though we all fall silent in the end,

Sing and you‚Äôll be singing with the singers you hold dear

When you sing the songs they sang.

(Chorus)

So if you sing along you bring along the singers who are gone

Though we all fall silent in the end.

Sing and you‚Äôll be singing both the singer and the song

When you sing the songs they sang.

(Chorus, Refrain and Chorus)

items.lyrics:
On yon lofty mountain a castle do stand,

It‚Äôs built up with ivy and diamonds so grand.

It‚Äôs built up with ivy and diamonds so bright,

It‚Äôs a pilot for the sailors on a dark stormy night.

versions.title: Turp Brown sings The Streams of Lovely Nancy
versions.lyrics:
The streams of lovely Nancy were divided in three parts,

Where young men and maidens go to meet their sweethearts.

Drinking of strong liquors caused my heart to sing.

It‚Äôs the knives of yonder valley made the rocks for to ring.

In yonder‚Äôs high mountain there‚Äôs an high castle stands,

Builded up with ivory all near the black sand,

Builded up with ivory and the diamonds so bright.

It‚Äôs a pilot for the sailor on a dark winter‚Äôs night.

‚ÄúI will go, love, in some nunnery and there end my life.

Never will get married nor yet made the wife.

Constant and true-hearted from ever I‚Äôll remain.

I never will get married till my sailor come again.‚ÄĚ

Spoken: Thank you very much.

versions.title: The Streams of Lovely Nancy in Canow Kernow
versions.lyrics:
The streams of lovely Nancy divide in three parts,

Where young men and maidens do meet their sweethearts,

In drinking good liquor which makes me to sing

And the sound of the valleys makes my heart to ring.

On yonder high mountain a castle there does stand,

It is built with ivory near to the black sand.

It is built up with ivory and diamonds so bright,

it is a pilot for sailors in a dark stormy night.

As a sailor was walking, a-walking along,

Says a sailor to his true love, I will sing you a song.

It is a false-hearted woman which makes me to say‚ÄĒ

Fare you well, lovely Nancy, for I must away.

On yonder high mountain the wild fowl fly,

There is one amongst them that flies very high.

My heart is the eagle‚Äôs wings when they are spread

So on high when I think on my angelic maid.

We sailed from London to fair Liverpool town,

Where the girls they are plenty, some white and some brown.

But of all the bonnie lasses that ever I did see,

At the sign of the Angel is the darling for me.

I‚Äôll go down to the nunnery and there end my life,

And I‚Äôll never be married nor yet be made a wife.

So constant and true hearted for ever I‚Äôll remain

And I never will be married till my love comes again.

versions.title: Cyril Tawney sings The Sweet Streams of Nancy
versions.lyrics:
O the sweet streams of Nancy they divide in two parts,

Where the young men in dancing they do meet their sweethearts.

There in drinking strong liquor it makes my heart sing,

And the sound of the viol it does make my heart ring.

On yonder tall mountain a castle doth stand.

It is built of white ivory all above the black sand.

All of ivory builded and of diamonds bright,

All with gold it is gilded and it shines in the night.

On yonder high moorland the wildfowl do fly,

There is one fair among them that soars very high.

My heart is an eagle with wings wide outspread,

It soareth and flieth in pursuit of my maid.

versions.title: Jo Freya sings The Streams of Lovely Nancy
versions.lyrics:
O the streams of lovely Nancy are divided in three parts,

Where the young men and maidens they do meet their sweethearts.

It is drinking of good liquor caused my heart for to sing,

And the noise in yonder village made the rocks for to ring.

At the top of this mountain, there my love‚Äôs castle stands.

Its all overbuilt with ivory on yonder black sand,

Fine arches, fine porches, and diamonds so bright.

It‚Äôs a pilot for a sailor on a dark winter‚Äôs night.

On yonder high mountain, where the wild fowl do fly,

There is one there amongst them that flies very high.

If I had her in my arms, love, near the diamond‚Äôs black land,

How soon I would secure her by the sleight of my hand.

At the bottom of this mountain there runs a river clear.

A ship from the Indies did once anchor there,

With her red flags a-flying and the beating of her drum,

Sweet instruments of music and the firing of her gun.

So come all you little streamers that walk the meadows gay,

I‚Äôll write unto my own true love, wherever she may be.

For her rosy lips entice me, with her tongue she tells me ‚ÄúNo‚ÄĚ,

And an angel might direct us right, and where shall we go?

versions.title: Linda Adams sings The Streams of Lovely Nancy

It‚Äôs all overbuilt with iv‚Äôry on yonder black sand.

On yonder high mountain where the wild fowl do fly

So come all you little streamers that walk the meadows gay.

versions.title: Nancy Kerr and James Fagan sing The Streams of Lovely Nancy
versions.lyrics:
The streams of lovely Nancy divide in three parts,

Where the lads and the lasses do meet their sweethearts,

In drinking good liquor which makes me to sing

And the bells in the valley make my heart for to ring.

A sailor was walking, a-walking along,

Said he to his true love, I‚Äôll sing you a song.

√Ę¬Ä¬ôTis a false-hearted lady which makes me to say‚ÄĒ

Fare you well, lovely Nancy, for I must away.

On yonder high mountain the wild fowl fly,

There is one dove amongt them that flies oh so high.

My heart‚Äôs on her wings as the flies far and near

Sweet vows have I taken till end of my life

So fair and true hearted for e‚Äôer I‚Äôll remain

(repeat first verse)

versions.title: John Kirkpatrick sings The Streams of Lovely Nancy
versions.lyrics:
Oh, the streams of lovely Nancy they divide in three parts

Where young men and maidens do meet their sweethearts.

It‚Äôs drinking of strong liquor makes the hearts for to sing

And the noise in yonder valley makes the rocks for to ring.

On yonder high mountain a castle does stand.

It‚Äôs builded of ivory near to yonder black strand,

It‚Äôs builded of ivory and diamonds so bright,

It‚Äôs a pilot for a sailor on a dark winter‚Äôs night.

On yonder high mountain where the wild fowl do fly

There is one amongst them that flies very high.

If I had my true love for a night on that strand,

How soon I would secure her by the slight of my hand.

At the foot of yonder mountain there runs a river clear.

A boat from the Indies did once anchor there,

With red flags a-flying and the beating of the drum,

Sweet instruments of music and the firing of her gun.

Our sailor and his true love were a-walking along

Said the sailor to his true love, ‚ÄúI will sing you a song

You√Ę¬Ä¬ôre a false-hearted woman for your tongue it tells me ‚ÄúNo‚ÄĚ

Fare the well, my lovely Nancy, far away from you I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWe sailed away from London to Liverpool town

And there we spied lasses, some fair and some brown.

But of all the fine lasses I ever did see,

Oh, the voice of my angel is the darling for me.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI will go, love, to some nunnery and there end my life,

I never will marry nor yet make a wife.

True-hearted and constant forever I‚Äôll remain.

I never shall be married till my sailor comes again.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Damien O‚ÄôKane sings The Strands of Magilligan
versions.lyrics:
I‚Äôm a stranger in this country, from Amerikay I came,

There‚Äôs few in it knows me or can tell my name.

And here among strangers I will stay for a while

For the sake of my darling I‚Äôll go many a mile.

The Strands of Magilligan divide in two parts

Where young men and maidens do meet their sweethearts.

They will take no denial, we must dance and sing,

And the sound of the fiddle O it makes my heart ring.

On the top of a cuff where her castle does stand,

It is well built with ivy down to the back strand.

It is well built with ivy and diamonds so bright,

It‚Äôs a pilot for sailors on a bright and stormy night.

On Magilligan top where the wild birds do fly

There is one amongst them that flies so high.

On eagles wing soaring I‚Äôll speed as the wind,

The wild deep exploring my true love I‚Äôll find,

The Strands of Magilligan divide in two parts

And rejoin as in dancing do lads their sweethearts.

So the strands bright and shining though parted in twain,

Reunited like two lovers where the Foyle meets the main.

Roy Palmer wrote in his 1975 book

an Truro Agricultural Show :

Though the Highland and Agricultural Society had been holding shows in

Scotland for some time, the first large-scale agricultural show in England was
items.lyrics:

Chorus (after each verse):

But/So/And don‚Äôt go kissing the girls you know

At Truro Agricultural Show.

Fat farmers and their wives so rare;

Their bouncing daughters neat and clean,

With a pork-pie hat and a crinoline.

From Newlyn East, St Columb, too,

There‚Äôs hump-backed Jim and carroty Joe;

And a special train upon the rail

To bring all the thieves from Bodmin Jail.

They‚Äôve got a band from Plymouth down,

The best that ever was in the town;

All the gentry will be there:

It‚Äôs twice as pretty at Whitsun Fair.

There‚Äôs horses, ponies, cows and calves,

Truro don‚Äôt do things by halves.

Devon bulls, sheep, pigs and geese:

You can see it all for a shilling apiece.

There‚Äôs things up there that‚Äôll make you laugh;

There‚Äôs a two-legged cow and a nine-legged calf,

A billy-goat that comes from Wales

With sixteen eyes and seventeen tails.

Now all around I hear them say,

‚ÄúWe√Ę¬Ä¬ôll see this show this very day;

Off we go, all in a row,

To Truro Agricultural Show.‚ÄĚ

I‚Äôm glad you‚Äôre come, I see you‚Äôre here;

There‚Äôs thousands come from everywhere:

Rich and poor and high and low,

At Truro Agricultural Show.

‚ÄôTwas on one April morning, just as the sun was rising,

‚ÄôTwas on one April morning I heard the small birds sing.

For love it is a fancy,

So sweet were the notes that I heard the small birds sing.

Young men are false and full of all deceiving

Young men are false and seldom do prove true,

For they‚Äôre roving and they‚Äôre ranging

And their minds are always changing

For they‚Äôre thinking for to find out some pretty girl that‚Äôs new.

O if I had but my own heart in keeping,

O if I had but my own heart back again.

Close in my bosom

I would lock it up for ever,

And it should wander never so far from me again.

Why would you spend all your long time in courting?

Why would you spend all your long time again?

For I don‚Äôt intend to marry,

I‚Äôd rather longer tarry,

So young man don‚Äôt you spend all your long time in vain.

items.title: Vanity
items.notes:
>

The Watersons >

Songs >
