[

; DT VANITTY

; Cyril Tawney]

Cyril Tawney sang

Vanity

in 1997 on his cassette of non-maritime songs,

Man of Honour .
items.lyrics:
Oh love is a damn fine splendid thing

It grows on every tree

Don‚Äôt be so grand come take my hand

And walk through the grove with me

And walk through the grove with me.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

And its vanity, vanity all the world around

And it‚Äôs vanity will get you in the end, I‚Äôll be bound.

I‚Äôve met your kind of girl before

And money‚Äôs all you crave

But you won‚Äôt have silks and satins love

When they take you to your grave

When they take you to your grave.

You clutch your perm at every breeze

You‚Äôre scared it might unroll

You‚Äôre so took up with how you look

Have you thought about your soul?

Have you thought about your soul?

lt never does to walk through life

With your nose up in the air

For sure enough you‚Äôll tread in stuff

That you won‚Äôt see lying there

That you won‚Äôt see lying there.

One day you‚Äôll know just what it‚Äôs like

When love brings on the pain

And your heart feels like a ponder-ball

Upon an idle crane

Upon an idle crane.

If I was a bolder kind of chap

I‚Äôd take you on my knee

I‚Äôd grab you round and shake the fruit

Down off your loving-tree

Down off your loving-tree.

So fare you well, me lofty maid

Forever fare you well

I never knew a snail to rate

When it stayed inside its shell

When it stayed inside its shell.

items.title: West Yorkshire Lullaby
items.notes:
>

The Watersons >

Songs >

West Yorkshire Lullaby

West Yorkshire Lullaby

[

; Mudcat 129046

; Cyril Tawney]

Cyril Tawney wrote

West Yorkshire Lullaby

in 1995. He sang it in 1997 on his cassette of non-maritime songs,

Man of Honour .
items.lyrics:
Hush-a-bye, my little one,

Close your pretty mouth.

I know things aren‚Äôt so peaceful

Since we moved up from the south.

The folks up here are nice enough,

As kind as people go,

But oh! oh! oh! don‚Äôt their voices carry so!

Oh! oh! oh! don‚Äôt their voices carry so!

They√Ę¬Ä¬ôre fond of conversation ‚Äď

Ah, but that‚Äôs the very catch,

For they turn ev‚Äôry tete-a-tete

Into a shouting match.

You address them sotto voce;

They reply fortissimo ‚Äď

Oh! ‚Ä¶

What seemed to be an argument

Turns out quite the reverse.

You‚Äôd think two auctioneers

Were taking time off to rehearse.

You‚Äôd hear less noise if a carthorse

Trod upon a gouty toe.

Oh! ‚Ä¶

It probably goes back to days

When they were in the pram

With a foghorn of a father

And a klaxon of a ma‚Äôam.

With larynxes like timberwolves

And lungs like buffalo.

Oh! ‚Ä¶

In days gone by, town criers cried

And [hoisted?] every word.

Wesley never preached outside,

But no one would have heard,

And Captain Oates unplugged his ears

And sailed off to the snow,

Crying ‚ÄúOh! ‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ

There‚Äôs lots of ruined abbeys

In the countryside around,

But the reason wasn‚Äôt Cromwell

Or King Henry, I‚Äôll be bound.

It was much more like what happened

To the walls of Jericho.

‚ÄúOh! ‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ

But hush-a-bye, my little one;

We really ought to smile,

‚ÄôCos daddy‚Äôs selling hearing aids

And making quite a pile.

His business never slackens,

So it‚Äôs not all bad, you know.

‚ÄúHo! ‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ

items.title: William Coombe / Crantock Games
items.notes:
William Coombe / Crantock Games

[

; Ballad Index Gund048

; VWML SBG/1/3/205

, SBG/3/1/817

; trad.]

Sabine Baring-Gould, Henry Fleetwood Sheppard,

Songs of the West

Inglis Gundry:

Canow Kernow

Cyril Tawney sang

William Coombe (Crantock Games)
items.lyrics:
Twas in the month of May, when flowers spring,

And pretty lambkins play and thrushes sing,

When young men do resort to walk about in sport,

Not thinking any harm at Crantock Games.

Crantock and Newlyn men, all in one room,

The first shot was then that caused my doom.

My name is Willy Coomb, just twenty in my bloom,

Just twenty in my bloom when I was shot.

My brother swift did fly to Truro town,

A surgeon for to find to heal my wound.

The surgeon says, ‚ÄôTis o‚Äôer, no man then can him cure,

Great pains he must endure all through the night.

Father, thy son is dead too true to hear,

Mother, don‚Äôt weep no more, oh mother dear.

Sister, don‚Äôt cry nor grieve, since there is no relief,

No warning did he give when he shot me.

My God who rules on high, behold my misery,

And take my sins away before I die.

I‚Äôll swear until I die I was shot wilfully,

And from that wound I‚Äôll die at Crantock Games.

items.title: Windy Old Weather / Up Jumped the Herring
items.notes:
[

; Master title: Windy Old Weather

; Ballad Index CoSB204

; Bodleian

; Folkinfo 763

; DT WINDYWEA

, WINDYWE2

; Mudcat 149445

; trad.]

Harry Cox of Catfield, Norfolk, sang

Windy Old Weather

to Alan Lomax (and Peter Kennedy? see lyrics below)
items.lyrics:
As we were a-fishin‚Äô off Happisburgh Light

Shootin‚Äô an‚Äô haulin‚Äô an‚Äô trawlin‚Äô all night.

Up jumped the herring, the queen of the sea,

She sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, o, you can√Ę¬Ä¬ôt catch me.‚ÄĚ

Chorus (after each verse):

In this windy old weather, stormy old weather,

When the wind blow, we‚Äôll all pull together!

Up jumped the mackerel, with spots on his back,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre on your own track.‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the sprat, the smallest of all,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, come haul your trawl haul.‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the crab, with his great long claws,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, you √Ę¬Ä¬ôll run her ashore.‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the rooker, his back hard and tough,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld steward, you will bum the duff.‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the mackerel, with spots on his back,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, come square your main tack.‚ÄĚ

Harry Cox: That‚Äôs all I know of that.

Alan Lomax: Oh, God! That‚Äôs one of the nicest songs I‚Äôve heard in England, Peter!

One night we were fishing Happisburgh* Light,

Fishing and trawling, all through the night

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

In this windy old weather, stormy old weather, stormy old weather,

When the wind blow, we‚Äôll all pull together.

Up jumped the herring, the queen of the sea,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, O you can√Ę¬Ä¬ôt catch me!‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the mackerel, with spots on his back,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, come square your main tack!‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the crab, with his great long claws,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll run her ashore!‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the rooker, his back hard and tough,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, you will burn the duff!‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the sprat, the smallest of all,

He sang out, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, you will loose your trawl!‚ÄĚ

Up jumped the whiting, with silvery eyes

Said, ‚ÄúYou haven√Ę¬Ä¬ôt gt long on the sea for to ride!‚ÄĚ

Up spoke the skipper, ‚ÄúThe saying is right,

We√Ę¬Ä¬ôll haul up our trawl and we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go home tonight!‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Bob Roberts sings Windy Old Weather
versions.lyrics:
As we were a-fishin‚Äô off Happisburgh Light,

Shootin‚Äô an‚Äô haulin‚Äô an‚Äô trawlin‚Äô all night.

Chorus (after each verse):

It was windy old weather, stormy old weather,

When the wind blows we all pull together.

When up jumped a herring, the queen of the sea,

Says now, ‚ÄúOld Skipper, you cannot catch me,‚ÄĚ

Chorus:

In this‚Ä¶

We sighted a thrasher a-slashin‚Äô his tail,

‚ÄúTime now Old Skipper to hoist up your sail.‚ÄĚ

Then up jumps a slipsole as strong as a horse,

Says now. ‚ÄúOld Skipper, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre miles off your course.‚ÄĚ

Then along comes a plaice who‚Äôs got spots on his side.

Says, ‚ÄúNot much longer√Ę¬Ä¬Ēthese seas you can ride.‚ÄĚ

Then up rears a conger as long as a mile,

‚ÄúWinds cornin√Ę¬Ä¬ô east√Ę¬Ä¬ôly,‚ÄĚ he says with a smile.

I think what these fishes are sayin‚Äô is right,

We‚Äôll haul up our gear now an‚Äô steer for the light.

Chorus:

Cos it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs‚Ä¶

versions.title: Sam Larner sings Up Jumped the Herring
versions.lyrics:
(Spoken:)
When you‚Äôre a-hauling, you say, well this is a poor shimmer, that mean a,
‚Ä¶ a multitude, a, ‚Ä¶ a good lot, you know, a shimmer. A big
shimmer or a little shimmer, we call that a shimmer o‚Äô herring. And when we‚Äôre
a-hauling mackerel, we call them‚Ä¶ scrunks, big scrunks for mackerel,
shimmer for herring.

Then up jumped the herring, he‚Äôs the king of the sea,

He said to the skipper: ‚ÄúLook under your lee.‚ÄĚ

Chorus (after each verse):

Windy old weather, boys, squally old weather, boys,

When the wind blow, we‚Äôll all go together.

Then up git the garnet with pricks on his back,

He jumped on the foredeck to hook on the jib tack, singing

Then up git the codfish with his great old head,

He said to the deckie, ‚ÄúGet a cast of lead,‚ÄĚ singing

Then up git the haddock, so sharp and so shy;

He said to the deckie: ‚ÄúHook on the lee guy.‚ÄĚ

Then up git the roker, so sharp and so rough,

He said to the cook: ‚ÄúYou are burning the duff.‚ÄĚ

2 nd Chorus:

So after it, you bugger, so after it, you bugger,

When the wind blow, we‚Äôll all go together.

Then up jumped the sprat, the smallest of them all,

He said to the skipper; ‚ÄúHaul, haul the man√Ę¬Ä¬ôs trawl.‚ÄĚ

(Laughs, spoken:)
That‚Äôs what we used to sing, little boys used to sing that, we went to school.
Course, we were all connected with the fishing, our fathers and our
forefathers, we were ‚Ä¶ that was bred in us to be fishermen.
Course we were brought up the hard way we were, there‚Äôs no mistake about it.
We see more Sundays than we did Sunday dinners when I was a little boy.
That we did. Why, for me and my brothers that was either sea or Jail,
and that for my sisters that was service or Jail.

versions.title: Peggy Seeger sings The Boston Come-All-Ye
versions.lyrics:
Come all you young sailormen, listen to me,

I‚Äôll sing you a song of the fish of the sea.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Then blow ye wins westerly, westerly blow,

We‚Äôre bound to the south‚Äôard, so steady she goes

O first come the whale, the biggest of all,

He clumb up aloft and let every sail fall.

And next comes the mackerel with his striped back,

He hauled at the sheets and he boarded each tack.

Then come the porpoise with his short snout,

He went to the wheel calling, ‚ÄúReady about!‚ÄĚ

Then come the smelt, the smallest of all,

He clumb up the pool and sung out, ‚ÄúTopsail haul!‚ÄĚ

The herring come saying, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm king of the seas,

If you want any wind, why I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll blow you a breeze.‚ÄĚ

Next come the cod with his chucklehead,

He went to the mainsheet to heave at the lead.

Last come the flounder as flat as the ground,

Says, ‚ÄúDamn your eyes, chucklehead, mind how you sound!‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Johnny Doughty sings Windy Old Weather
versions.lyrics:
As I was a‚Äôfishing off Dungeness light

Shooting and hauling all through the long night

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

In this windy old weather, stormy old weather,

When the wind blows, we‚Äôll all pull together.

When up spoke the cod with his great big head,

‚ÄúHold hard there Skipper I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go chuck the lead!‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke the herring, the king of the sea,

‚ÄúIn this stormy weather you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never catch me!‚ÄĚ

When up spoke the place with spots on his side,

‚ÄúNow look here skipper these sea you can√Ę¬Ä¬ôt ride!‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke the mackerel with stripes on his back,

‚ÄúHold hard there Skipper I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll shift the jib tack!‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke the sprat, the smallest of all,

‚ÄúCome on there Skipper let√Ę¬Ä¬ôs give the mans trawl!‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke the Skipper, ‚Äúit√Ę¬Ä¬ôs true what they say,

We√Ę¬Ä¬ôll all hump our trawl and we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll get underway!‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke the crew, ‚Äú If these fish are right

We√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sail in royal harbour and we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be alright!‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Tom Brown sings Windy Old Weather
versions.lyrics:
As I was a-sailing round Happisburgh Light,

We shot all our gear and we toiled through the night

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

In this windy old weather, stormy old weather,

When the wind blow, we‚Äôll all pull together.

Then up jumped the herring, king of the sea,

Saying, ‚ÄúCome on, old skipper, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never catch me!‚ÄĚ

Then up jumped the plaice, as big as a plate,

Saying, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sign as skipper, if you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sign as mate.‚ÄĚ

Then up jumped the whiting, easy and free,

Saying, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sign as driver, if you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll fire for me.‚ÄĚ

Then up jumped the whalefish that we call the blower,

Saying, ‚ÄúCome on, old skipper, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sign as net-stower.‚ÄĚ

Then up jumped the skate with his long slimey tail,

Saying, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sign to the hawse, boy, if you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sign to the whale.‚ÄĚ

Then up jumped the coley, back as a rook,

Saying, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sign as yunker , if you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sign as cook.‚ÄĚ

Now I think what the fishes are saying is right,

We‚Äôll heave in the gear and we‚Äôll steer for the light.

items.title: Anac Cuan (Annaghdown)

[Anthony Raftery]

The full text of

Anac Cuan

both in Irish and in its English translation

Annaghdown

can be found in Eibhl√≠n Mhic Choisdealbha (Mrs Costello)‚Äôs book

Amr√°in muige se√≥la: Traditional Folksongs From Galway and Mayo

(Dublin: Talbot Press, 1923, p. 98).

There appear to be several spellings of the title,

The lyrics are ascribed to the poet Anthony Raftery.

versions.title: Annaghdown (English translation)
versions.lyrics:
If my health is spared I‚Äôll be long relating,

Of the boat that sailed out from Annaghdown,

And the keening after of mother and father,

As the laying out of each corpse was done.

Oh King of Graces, who died to save us,

It was a small affair but for one or two,

But a boat-load bravely on a calm sailing,

Without storm or rain to be swept to doom.

The boat sprang a leak and left all those people,

And frightened sheep out adrift on the tide,

It beats all telling what fate befell them,

Eleven strong men and eight women died.

Men who could manage a plough or harrow,

For to break the fallow or scatter seed,

And the women whose fingers could move so nimbly,

To spin fine linen or cloth to weave.

Young boys they were lying where crops were ripening,

From the strength of youth they were borne away,

In their wedding clothes for their wake they robed them,

Oh King of Glory man‚Äôs hope is vain.

May burning mountains come tumbling downward,

On that place of drowning may curses fall,

Full many the soul it has left in mourning,

And left without hope of a bright day‚Äôs dawn.

The cause of their fate was no fault of sailing,

It was the boat that failed them the Caislean Nuadh,

And left me to make with a heart that‚Äôs breaking,

This sad lamentation for Annaghdown.

items.title: Anderson‚Äôs Coast
items.notes:
Anderson‚Äôs Coast

[

; DT ANDRCST

; Mudcat 167344

; John Warner]

John Warner and Margaret Walters sang Warner‚Äôs song

Anderson‚Äôs Coast

on their 1994 album

Pithead in the Fern .

They noted:

Convicts frequently stole ships to escape from the horrors and savage

treatment in Van Diemen‚Äôs Land, and some were wrecked on the Gippsland coast
items.lyrics:
Old Bass Strait roars like some great millrace,

And where are you, my Annie?

And the same moon shines on this lonely place,

As shone one day on my Annie‚Äôs face.

Chorus (after each verse):

But Annie, dear, don‚Äôt wait for me,

I fear I shall not return to thee,

There‚Äôs nought to do but endure my fate,

And watch the moon, the lonely moon

Light the breakers on wild Bass Strait.

We stole a vessel and all her gear,

And where are you, my Annie?

And from Van Diemen‚Äôs we north did steer,

Till Bass Strait‚Äôs wild waves wrecked us here.

A mile inland as our path was laid

And where are you, my Annie?

We found a government stockade

Long deserted but stoutly made.

And somewhere west, Port Melbourne lies,

And where are you, my Annie?

Through swamps infested with snakes and flies,

The fool who walks there, he surely dies.

We hail no ships though the time, it drags,

And where are you, my Annie?

Our chain gang walk and our government rags

All mark us out as Van Diemen‚Äôs lags.

We fled the lash and the chafing chain,

And where are you, my Annie?

We fled hard labour and brutal pain,

And here we are, and here remain.

versions.title: John Roberts and Debra Cowan sing Anderson‚Äôs Coast
versions.lyrics:
Old Bass Strait roars like a great mill race

And where are you, my Annie?

And the same moon shines on this distant place

As shone that night on my Annie‚Äôs face.

Chorus (after each verse):

And Annie dear, don‚Äôt wait for me,

I fear I‚Äôll never return to thee.

There‚Äôs naught to do but endure my fate

And watch the moon, the lonely moon,

Light the breakers of wild Bass Strait.

We stole a vessel and all her gear

And where are you, my Annie?

And from Van Diemen‚Äôs north did steer

Till Bass Strait‚Äôs wild waves, they wrecked us here.

A mile inland as our path was laid

And where are you, my Annie?

We found a government stockade,

Long, long deserted, but stoutly made.

And somewhere‚Äôs west port Melbourne lies

And where are you, my Annie?

Through swamps infested with snakes and flies

And the fool who walks there, the fool he dies.

We hail no ships, though time it drags,

And where are you, my Annie?

For our chain gang roll and government rags,

They mark us out as Van Diemen‚Äôs lags.

We fled the lash and the chafing chain,

And where are you, my Annie?

We fled starvation and brutal pain,

But here we are, and here remain.

items.title: And When They Dance
items.notes:
And When They Dance

[Roy Abbott]

Roy Abbott of Mucky Duck Bush Band wrote And When They Dance .

They sang in on their 2004 album

Mucky Duck Bush Band 1974-2004: Three Ducking Decades .

Danny Spooner sang And When They Dance

on his 2007 CD of fairly contemporary Australian songs,

Emerging Tradition .

He also sang it live in June 2007 in concerts with the

Australian Chamber Orchestra.

A recording of these concerts was released in 2017 on his download album

Enchanted: Live in Concert .
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

And when they dance their dresses spin round,

They travel so light that they scarce touch the ground,

And the smiles on their faces would win any crowd

The lasses who dance ‚Äôtil the morning.

I play in a band, I‚Äôve played all around,

From Perth in the west to old Melbourne Town,

But one thing delights me each time I look down

It‚Äôs the lasses who dance ‚Äôtil the morning.

I‚Äôve played for the gentry I‚Äôve played for them all,

From the old bush hut to the debutante‚Äôs ball,

But one thing unites them the great and the small

It‚Äôs the lasses who dance ‚Äôtil the morning.

And when the dance ends and they all leave the floor

Their legs are so weary tired and sore

But who are the ones that keep yellin‚Äô for more?

It‚Äôs the lasses who dance till the morning.

So, long may I travel and far may I roam

Around this big country we call our home

Playing for people that I‚Äôll never know

And the lasses who dance till the morning.

items.notes:

Ewan MacColl and Peggy Seeger sang

items.lyrics:
As I came in by Fisherrow, Musselburgh was near me

I threw off my mussel-pock , courted wi‚Äô my dearie.

Chorus:

Up stairs, doun stairs, timmer stairs fears me

I thought it lang to lie my lane when I‚Äôm sae near my dearie!

Oh had her apron bidden doun , the kirk wad ne‚Äôer hae kent it

But since the word‚Äôs gane through the toon, my dear I canna mend it

But ye maun mount the cutty-stool and I maun mount the pillar

That‚Äôs the way the poor folks dae, because they hae nae siller

items.title: Bartholomew Fair / Room for Company
items.notes:
[trad.]

Ewan MacColl sang Room for Company

in 1962 on his Folkways album

Broadside Ballads (London: 1600-1700) .

His album‚Äôs liner notes commented:

Imprinted at London for E. W.

The ballad was registered by John Trundle under the title of

Rome for Company in Bartholomew Faire on 22 October 1614, the year

in which Ben Johnson‚Äôs hilarious play, Bartholomew Fair , was first

presented to London theatre-goers. The earliest printed version of the melody

is in Playford‚Äôs Musick‚Äôs Recreation on the LYRA VIOL (1652).

Seven of the twenty-one verses are given here.
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Room for company, here come good fellows,

Room for company, Bartholomew Fair.

Cobblers and broom-men, jailers and loom-men,

Room for company, Bartholomew Fair.

Botchers and tailors, shipwrights and sailors,

Room for company, well may they fare.

Paviers, bricklayers, potters and brickmakers,

Pinders and pewterers, plumbers and fruiterers.

Pointers and hosiers, salesmen and clothiers,

Horse coursers, carriers, blacksmiths and farriers.

Colliers and carvers, barbers and weavers,

Sergeants and yeomen, farmers and ploughmen.

Billfolds, fellmongers, bellowsmenders, woodmongers,

Pumpmakers, glassmakers, chamberlains and matmakers.

Collarmakers, needlemakers, buttonmakers, fiddlemakers,

Fletchers and bowyers, drawers and sawyers.

Cutpurses, cheaters, bawdy-house door-keepers,

Room for company, Bartholomew Fair.

Punks, ay, and panderers, and cashier‚Äôd commanders,

Room for company, ill may they fare.

versions.title: Regal Slip sing Room for Company

items.title: Big Poll the Grog Seller
items.notes:
Big Poll the Grog Seller

[

; Ballad Index AnSt085

; Charles Thatcher (1831-1878)]

From Thatcher‚Äôs Colonial Minstrel (1864),

published with the note:

A new parody of Philip the Falconer as written and sung by

Thatcher at the Shamrock.

John Thompson sang Poll the Grogseller

as the 9 November 2011 entry of his blog

An Australian Folk Song a Day .

Danny Spooner sang Big Poll the Grog Seller
items.lyrics:
Big Poll the grog seller gets up every day

And her small rowdy crib she sweeps out;

She‚Äôs turning in plenty of tin, people say,

For she knows what she‚Äôs about,

Aye yes, she knows what she‚Äôs about.

Well, Polly‚Äôs good-looking, and Polly is young,

Polly‚Äôs possessed of a smooth oily tongue;

She‚Äôs an innocent face and a good head of hair,

And a lot of young fellows will often go there.

And they keep dropping in, handsome Polly to court,

And Poll, she supplies them with brandy and port

And the neighbours all say that the whole blessed day,

She‚Äôs a-grog-selling late and early,

She is a-grog-selling late and early.

Two sly-grog detectives have come up from town,

And they both roam around in disguise;

And several retailers of grog are done brown,

And have reason to open their eyes,

And have reason to open their eyes.

Of her small rowdy crib they are soon on the scent;

But Polly‚Äôs prepared when they enter her tent;

They call for some brandy - ‚ÄúOh, we don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt sell it here,

To your fine artful dodge and disguises I‚Äôm fly;

But if Polly you‚Äôd nail, you‚Äôd have, without fail,

To get up in the morning early.

Yes, if Polly you‚Äôd nail, you‚Äôd have, without fail,

items.title: Bridges
items.notes:
Bridges

[

; Mudcat 161695

; Erik Gooding]

Danny Spooner sang Erik Gooding‚Äôs song Bridges

This track was also included in 2007 on his anthology

He noted:

About 1972 Melbourne played host to an English academic by the name of

Erik Gooding. Erik was a fine singer and song-writer, and it was a sad loss
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after every other verse):

If you‚Äôre looking for fine bridges then on Teesside we‚Äôve got two,

To show what local engineers and labourers can do,

There‚Äôs one that lifts a stretch of road up out of shipping‚Äôs way

And the other shuffles back and forth a hundred times a day.

Now when I was just a student lad and working on a grant,

I got myself a summer job in Dorman‚Äôs Ackland Plant

Where conditions for the workers hadn‚Äôt changed in fifty years

And the smell of oil and manganese hung in the furnace glare.

Well the output of that plant you would be very hard to beat,

Wi‚Äô weary tykes a-toiling in the grim and bloody heat;

But a firm that makws a better steel just find one if ye can

For this very furnace forged the steel for Sydney Harbour‚Äôs span.

One day the foreman and myself were walking to the mess.

We passed five hundred ton o√Ę¬Ä¬ô steel‚ÄĒI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm sure it was no less,

Said he to me, ‚ÄúThe job looks hard, of ingots there√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a glut.‚ÄĚ

And sure enough a twelve-month and that Ackland Plants were shut.

So al you lads of Brumagem what mak‚Äôs us fancy cars

And fittings fine electrical and fruit and nut-cake bars,

There‚Äôs not a single one of you can knows the way we feel

About our area up The North, where we make British steel.

items.title: Bring Out the Banners
items.notes:
Bring Out the Banners

[words John Warner, music trad.]

Dave Webber and Anni Fentiman sang John Warner‚Äôs song

Bring Out the Banners

in 2006 on their CD

Unity .

They commented:

John Warner wrote this song to sing at an exhibition of Union Banners at

Trade Hall in Sydney in September 1997 around the time of the Australian

maritime workers‚Äô dispute. The union movement has a tradition of using hymn

tunes as a vehicle for songs of struggle as the tunes were often well-known

and, as in this case, powerful musically, and ideal for the function.
items.lyrics:
In a faded photo like a dream,

A locomotive under steam

Rolls with the ranks of marching feet

And union banners on the street.

Chorus (after each verse):

Bring out those banners once again,

You union women, union men,

That all around may plainly see

The power of our unity.

I‚Äôve seen those banners richly made

Proud symbols all of craft and trade,

The union names in red and gold,

Their aspirations printed bold.

Boilermakers, smiths and cooks

Stevedores with cargo hooks,

Declare their union stout and strong

Rank on rank before the crowd.

They won for us the eight-hour day,

The won our right to honest pay,

Victorious their banners shone

How dare we cede what they have won?

Today, when those who rule divide,

We must be standing side by side,

Our rights were bought with tears and pain,

Bring out those banners once again.

items.title: Dark Old Waters
items.notes:
Dark Old Waters

[Gordon Bok]

Gordon Bok wrote Dark Old Waters in 1992

‚Äúfor the film documentary of the short life of the schooner

items.lyrics:
Don‚Äôt be thinking of me,

All away and alone,

Where you go, go well, and a fair wind home.

Don‚Äôt be thinking of me on the rolling old sea

Don‚Äôt be thinking of me on the rolling old sea,

Deep Sea Tug is the work of the great song-crafter Harry Robertson

He noted:

In his book The Highland Clearances , John Prebble tells how,

after the defeat at Culloden (1745), the chieftains sold out the highlanders

who had fought so valiantly by driving them off their lands so they could rent

items.lyrics:
In the year o‚Äô the sheep and the burning time

They cut our young men in their prime,

And the old Scots ways was a hanging crime

For the Gaels of Caledonia.

There‚Äôs a den for the fox and a set for the hare,

A nest in the tree for the birds of the air;

But in old Scotland there‚Äôs no place there

For the Gaels of Caledonia.

Chorus (after each verse):

But there‚Äôs no use getting frantic

It‚Äôs time to hump your load

Well the bailiffs came wi‚Äô a writ and a‚Äô

And the gallant lads o‚Äô the Forty Twa;

And they drove ye oot in the cold and snaw,

The Gaels o‚Äô Caledonia.

And they burned your hame and ye‚Äôr crops as well,

Ye stood and wept in the blackened shell,

And the winter‚Äôs moor was a living hell

For the Gaels o‚Äô Caledonia.

Well, the famine and the plague they dragged you down

As you made your way to Glasgow Town,

For you‚Äôd heard o‚Äô a ship that was sailing soon

For the shores o‚Äô Nova Scotia.

And you sold your gear and you paid your fare,

Your head held high, though your heart was sair.

And ye bid farewell for evermair

To the shores o‚Äô Caledonia.

The land was cleared and a deal was made,

Now an English lord in a tartan plaid

Of the Gaels o‚Äô Caledonia.

And he hunts the deer in the lonely glen

That once was a home for a thousand men,

And the wind on the moor sings a sad refrain

For the Gaels o‚Äô Caledonia.

‚ÄėDinki Di‚Äô is Australian slang for something that is true,

items.lyrics:
He came over to London and straight away strode,

Dinki di, dinki di,

A lousy lance-corporal said, ‚ÄúPardon me, please,

You‚Äôve mud on your tunic and blood on your sleeve,

You look so disgraceful the people will laugh,‚ÄĚ

Said the lousy lance-corporal on the headquarters staff.

Dinki di, dinki di,

Said the lousy lance-corporal on the headquarters staff.

The digger then shot him a murderous glance;

He said, ‚ÄúWe√Ę¬Ä¬ôre just back from the balls-up in France,

Where bullets are flying and comforts are few,

And brave men are dying for bastards like you.

Dinki di, dinki di,

And brave men are dying for bastards like you.

‚ÄúWe√Ę¬Ä¬ôre shelled on the left and we√Ę¬Ä¬ôre shelled on the right,

We‚Äôre bombed all the day and we‚Äôre bombed all the night,

And if something don‚Äôt happen, and that pretty soon,

There‚Äôll be nobody left in the bloody platoon;

Dinki di, dinki di,

There√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be nobody left in the bloody platoon.‚ÄĚ

This story soon got to the ears of Lord Gort ,

Who gave the whole matter a great deal of thought,

He awarded the digger a V.C. and two bars,

For giving that corporal a kick up the arse;

Dinki di, dinki di,

For giving that corporal a kick up the arse.

Now when this war‚Äôs over and we‚Äôre out of here,

We‚Äôll see him in Sydney town begging for beer.

He‚Äôll ask for a dina to buy a small glass,

But all he‚Äôll get is a kick in the arse.

Dinki di, dinki di,

But all he‚Äôll get is a kick in the arse.

items.title: Follow Me Up to Carlow
items.notes:
Follow Me Up to Carlow

[

; Ballad Index PGa090

; DT FLLWCRLO

; Mudcat 52454

; Patrick Joseph McCall (1861-1919)]

Follow Me Up to Carlow is an Irish song celebrating the

defeat of an army of 3,000 English soldiers by Fiach Mac Aodh √ď Broin

(anglicised Fiach MacHugh O‚ÄôByrne) at the Battle of Glenmalure,

by Patrick Joseph McCall (1861‚Äď1919) and appear in his

Songs of Erinn (1899) under the title
items.lyrics:
Lift MacCahir √ďg your face brooding o‚Äôer the old disgrace

Chorus (after each verse):

Follow me up to Carlow.

See the swords of Glen Imayle, flashing o‚Äôer the English Pale

Rooster of a fighting stock, would you let a Saxon cock

Crow out upon an Irish rock, fly up and teach him manners.

From Saggart to Clonmore, there flows a stream of Saxon gore

O, great is Rory √ďg O‚ÄôMore, sending the loons to Hades.

White is sick and Lane is fled, now for black Fitzwilliam‚Äôs head

We‚Äôll send it over dripping red, to Queen Liza and the ladies.

Cilla & Artie .
items.lyrics:
My faither is a baillie frae a wee fairm at Caiplie ,

He worked on the land a‚Äô the days of his life.

By the time he made second he aye said he reckoned

He‚Äôd ploughed near on half o‚Äô the East Neuk o‚Äô Fife.

He feed on at Randerston , Crawhill and Clephanton ,

Cambo and Carnbee and Kilrennie Mill ,

At Kingsbarn he married, at Boarhills he‚Äôs buried.

But man, had he lived he‚Äôd be ploughing on still,

Chorus:

For those days were his days, those ways were his ways

Tae follow the ploo while his back was still strong,

But those days have passed and the time came at last

For the weakness of age to make way for the young.

I wisnae fir plooin‚Äô, tae the sea I wis goin‚Äô,

Tae follow the fish and the fisherman‚Äôs ways.

In rain, hail and sunshine I‚Äôve watched the lang run-line,

Nae man mair contented his whole workin‚Äô day.

I‚Äôve lang-lined the Fladen Ground, the Dutch and the Dogger Bank,

Pulled the big fish frae the deep Devil‚Äôs Hole.

I‚Äôve side-trawled off Shetland, the Faroes and Iceland,

In weather much worse than a body could thole.

Chorus:

For that day was my day, that way was my way,

Tae follow the fish while my back was still strong,

But that day has passed and the time come at last

For the weakness of age to make way for the young.

My sons they have grown an‚Äô away they have gone,

Tae search for black oil, in the far northern sea.

Like oilmen they walk an‚Äô like Yankees they talk,

There‚Äôs no much in common ‚Äôtween my sons an‚Äô me.

They‚Äôve rough rigged on Josephine, Forties and Ninian,

Claymore and Dunlin, Fisher and Awk.

They‚Äôve made fortunes for sure for in one run ashore

They spend more than I earned in a whole season‚Äôs work.

Chorus:

But this day is their day, this way is their way,

Tae ride the rough rigs while their backs are still strong,

But this day will pass and the time come at last

For the weakness of age to make way for the young.

My grandsons are growing, to the school they‚Äôre soon going,

But the lang weeks of summer they spend here wi‚Äô me.

We walk through the warm days, talk o‚Äô the auld ways,

The cornfield and cod-fish, the land and the sea.

We walk through the fields that my father once tilled,

Talk wi‚Äô the auld men that once sailed wi‚Äô me.

Man, it‚Äôs been awfae good, l‚Äôve shown them all I could

O‚Äô the past and the present, what their future might be.

Chorus:

For the morn will be their day, what will be their way?

What will they make of their land, sea and sky?

Man, I‚Äôve seen awfae change but it still seems gey strange,

Tae look at my world through a young laddie‚Äôs eyes.

items.title: Geordie Black
items.notes:
Geordie Black

[

; DT GEORDBLK

; Mudcat 149874

, 170190

; Rowland ‚ÄėRowie‚Äô Harrison]

Karl Dallas:

One Hundred Songs of Toil

The Ian Campbell Folk Group sang Geordie Black

in 1965 on their Transatlantic album

Coaldust Ballads .

This was also included in 2005 on their anthology
items.lyrics:
Ma name is Geordie Black, aa‚Äôm ‚Äôm getting very awd,

Aa‚Äôve hewed tons of coal in my time,

An‚Äô when aa wes young, aa could either put or hew,

Oot o‚Äô other lads aa always took the shine.

Aa‚Äôm going doon the hill, I canna use the pick,

The master hes ne pity on auld bones.

Aa‚Äôm noo on the bank, an‚Äô aa pass me time away

Amang the bits of lads wi‚Äô pickin‚Äô oot the stones.

When aa was a bairn, carried on ma feither‚Äôs back,

He would take me away te the pit,

Aye, an‚Äô gettin‚Äô to the cage, aye, an‚Äô gangin‚Äô doon belaw

Were enough to mak a youngster tak a fit.

Te sit an‚Äô keep a door midst darkness an‚Äô gloom,

Aye, mony a lonely ‚Äôoor by messel,

And te hear the aaful shots that rummelled through the pit,

An‚Äô lumps o‚Äô roondy coal fell down pellmell.

Ma name is Geordie Black, in maw time aa‚Äôve been a crack,

And aa‚Äôve worked byeth the Gyuss and the Betty.

An‚Äô the coals on the Tyne oot o‚Äô uthers tyek the shine,

An‚Äô we lick them a‚Äô for iron doon at Hawks‚Äôs.

Aye but here‚Äôs success te trade baith on the Wear an‚Äô Tyne!

Aa dinna like te see the place se slack.

For when work pit gang idle, ne wages come teneet,

An‚Äô it grieves the heart o‚Äô poor old Geordie Black.

items.title: Hangin‚Äô on for the Rain
items.notes:
Hangin‚Äô on for the Rain

[Ann Infante]

Danny Spooner sang Hangin‚Äô on for the Rain

in 2017 on his final CD,

Home .

He noted:

Australia is a country of extreme weather patters: flood and fire, wind and

drought are part of the rural weather cycle. In Ann Infante‚Äôs song we hear a

farmer enduring these devastating extremes to restock when conditions

improve.
items.lyrics:
Oh, Christmas is coming across this dry land,

I‚Äôm hanging on, hanging on.

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve drawn my line and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm making my stand‚ÄĒ

Hanging on for the rain.

The shepherds who watched o‚Äôer my flock are all gone,

I‚Äôm hanging on, hanging on.

The few I‚Äôve got left I can watch on my own,

Hanging on for the rain.

Chorus (after each verse):

Hanging on, hanging on, this drought can‚Äôt last forever,

I‚Äôm searching the skies, blinking sweat from my eyes,

While I pray for a change in the weather.

The wise men flew in to the farms cracked and parched,

Said the drought wouldn‚Äôt end till this time next March.

I‚Äôve sold all the cattle that I could afford

And now I‚Äôm hand-feeding the best of my herd.

The kids are excited now Christmas is near,

They‚Äôll think Santa‚Äôs a mean old bugger this year.

Cos Jane wants a doll and Jack wants a train,

But my Christmas wish is for good summer rain.

When they close the long paddock, you know times are bad,

No change to go droving, there‚Äôs no grass to be had.

I‚Äôve thought about walking off hundreds of times,

But I‚Äôm tied to this land with invisible chains.

items.title: Hey Rain (The Innisfail Song)
items.notes:
Hey Rain (The Innisfail Song)

[

; Mudcat 109812

; Bill Scott]

Dave de Hugard sang Bill Scott‚Äôs song Hey Rain

in 1986 on his Larrikin album

The Magpie in the Wattle .

He noted:

Hey Rain was written by Bill Scott, author, songwriter and story

teller from Queensland. Bill‚Äôs simple and powerful images will strike a chord

with anyone who has had any experience of the tropical Queensland wet season.

Gerry Hallom sang Hey Rain
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Hey rain,

rain coming down,

On the cane,

On the roofs of the town.

Rain in my beer and rain in my face

Old Innisfail is a bloody wet place,

Hey rain, hey rain.

Rain in my beer and rain in my grub

And they‚Äôve just fitted anchors to the Gurradunga pub,

Hey rain, hey rain.

I‚Äôve got a Johnson River crocodile livin‚Äô in my ‚Äôfridge

And there‚Äôs a bloody great tree down on the Jubilee Bridge,

Hey rain, hey rain.

The monsoon sky‚Äôs so dark and big,

There‚Äôs an old flying fox in a Moreton Bay Fig,

Hey rain, hey rain.

And a bloke from the west nigh died of fright

The river rose thirty five feet last night,

Hey rain, hey rain.

It‚Äôs the worst wet season we‚Äôve ever had,

I‚Äôd swim down to Tully but it‚Äôs just as bloody bad,

Hey rain, hey rain.

items.title: Hieland Laddie
items.notes:
Hieland Laddie

; Ballad Index Doe050

Joanna C. Colcord:

items.lyrics:
Was ye ever in Quebec?

Bonny laddie, hieland laddie,

Stowing timber on the deck,

Me bonny hieland laddie;

Way hey and away we go

Bonny laddie hieland laddie,

Way hey and away we go,

Me bonny hieland laddie.

Was ye ever in Mobile Bay?

Screwing cotton all the day.

Was ye ever off Cape Horn?

Where the weather‚Äôs never warm.

Was ye ever in Mirrimashee?

Where yer tie up to a tree.

Was yeever in Valipo?

Where them gals is never slow.

Was ye ever in Dundee?

There some pretty ships ye‚Äôll see.

items.title: Hills of Isle au Haut
items.notes:
Hills of Isle au Haut

[

; DT IHAUTHIL

; Mudcat 111913

; Gordon Bok]

The Isle au Haut is part of Acadia National Park in Maine, supported

mainly by fishing.

Gordon Bok sang his own song The Hills of Isle au Haut

in 1970 on his Folk-Legacy album A Tune for November .

Fraser and Ian Bruce sang The Hills of Isle au Haut

on their 1980 album

Mrs Bruce‚Äôs Boys Vol 1 .
items.lyrics:
Away to the westward

Is the place a man should go,

Where the fishing‚Äôs always easy

And there ain‚Äôt no ice or snow.

Chorus (after each verse):

I‚Äôll haul down the sail

Where the bays run together,

Bide away my days

On the hills of Isle au Haut.

Them Plymouth girls are fine,

Put their hearts in your hand;

And the Plymouth boys are able,

First-class sailors, every man.

And the trouble with old Martir,

You don‚Äôt try her in a trawler,

Them Bay of Biscay gales

Roll yer head off yer shoulder.

And the winters drive you crazy,

And the fishing‚Äôs hard and slow;

You‚Äôre a damned fool if you stay,

But there‚Äôs no better place to go.

items.title: Humpin‚Äô Me Drum
items.notes:
Humpin‚Äô Me Drum

[

; Mudcat 86350

; words adapted by Graham Seal]

Danny Spooner sang Humpin‚Äô Me Drum

in 2017 on his final CD,

Home .

He noted:

I like the way each verse seems to be sung by another travelling Australian

character. I can‚Äôt remember from whom I first heard it but it was a folk song

aficionado whom I met along the way and was blown away by his song. I can see

his face and hear his voice but I can‚Äôt remember his name. I think it was Paul.
items.lyrics:
I‚Äôve humped my drum from Kingdom Come

To the back of the Milky Way,

I‚Äôve boiled my quart out on Cape of York

And i starved last Christmas Day.

I cast a line on the old Condamine

And one on the Nebine Creek.

I‚Äôve driven through bog, so help be Bob,

Up Mungindi‚Äôs main street.

I crossed the Murray and drank at Cloncurry

Where they charged me a bob for a nip.

I worked in the Gulf where the cattle they duff

And the squatters they give ‚Äôem jip.

I‚Äôve worked from morn in the fields of long corn

Till the sun was out of sight.

I‚Äôve caused to know the Great Byno

And the Great Australian Bight.

I danced with Kit when the lamps was lit

And Doll when the dance broke up.

I flung my ‚Äôat on the Myall Track

When Bowman won the Cup.

I laughed aloud with the merry crowd

In the city of the plains;

I sweated too on Omdooroo

While bogged in those big bore-drains.

I wheeled my bike from the Shearer‚Äôs Strike,

Not wanting a funeral shroud.

And I made the weights for the Flying Stakes

But I dodged the lynchin‚Äô crowd.

I carried a gun through World War One,

Then went on the track again;

From Omeo to Bendigo,

To Bourke and back again.

I lost some tears in the hungry years

When jobs were short and few,

And I packed up my swag and my old tucker bag,

There was nothing else to do.

Yes, I‚Äôve humped my drum from Kingdom Come

To the back of the Milky Way.

items.title: Jack Robinson
items.notes:
Jack Robinson

[

; Ballad Index CrNS040

; trad.]

Tim Laycock sang Jack Robinson

in 1999 on his WildGoose CD

Fine Colours .

He noted:

This gentle parody of

The Sailor‚Äôs Return

was in the repertoire of Sam Cowell (1820-1864)

[VWML RoudBS/B148690 ]
items.lyrics:
With the perils and the dangers of the voyage past

Our ship at Portsmouth arrived at last

The sails all furled and the anchors cast

The happiest of the crew was Jack Robinson.

For his Polly he had trinkets and gold galore

Besides prize money, a weight of store

Along with the crew he went on shore

As bosun to the boat did Jack Robinson.

Well he met with a man and he says, I say

Perhaps you know me sweetheart, the lovely Polly Gray

Does she live somewhere hereabout? the man says, Nay,

I don‚Äôt know her at all, Jack Robinson.

Says Jack to him, I‚Äôve left my ship

All of my messmates have given the slip

Perhaps you‚Äôd like to join me for a decent can of flip

For you‚Äôre a handsome fellow, says Jack Robinson.

In a public house, well, they both sat down

The talked of admirals of high renown

They drank as much grog as would cost half a crown

This here fellow and Jack Robinson.

Jack he called out the reckoning to pay

The landlady came in, in fine array

Damn ye! says Jack, it‚Äôs me own Polly Gray,

Who‚Äôd have thought of meeting here? says Jack Robinson.

Well the landlady staggered back against the wall

Says she, Young sailor lad, I don‚Äôt know you at all.

Shiver me, says Jack, here‚Äôs a pretty squall,

Damn ye, don‚Äôt you know me, I‚Äôm Jack Robinson!

Don‚Äôt you see the handkerchief that you gave to me

Three years ago, me love, before I went to sea,

Every time I blew my nose it made me think of thee.

On me soul it did, said Jack Robinson.

Says the lady, says she, I have changed my state.

What! You don‚Äôt mean you‚Äôve got another mate?

You know you promised me. She says, I couldn‚Äôt wait,

No tidings could I gain of you, Jack Robinson.

Someone, somewhere, someday said

Someone else had somewhere read

In some almanac as how you was dead.

I ain‚Äôt been dead at all! says Jack Robinson.

Well, he turned his quid and he finished off his glass

He hitched up his trousers, alas, alas,

That ever I should come to such a pass

To be bilked by a woman, says Jack Robinson.

But to fret and stew about it much would be in vain

I shall get a ship and go to Holland, France or Spain

But to Portsmouth Harbour I shall never come again.

He was off before they could say ‚ÄôJack Robinson.‚Äô

items.title: John Cherokee
items.notes:

; Ballad Index Hugi439

items.lyrics:
This is the story of John Cherokee,

Alabama John Cherokee

Nigger man from Mirramashee,

Alabama John Cherokee

Chorus (after each verse):

Way, ay, yah ugh! Alabama John Cherokee

Way, ay, yah ugh! Alabama John Cherokee

They made him a slave in Alabam‚Äô,

He run away every time he can.

They shipped him ‚Äôboard of a whaling ship,

Again an‚Äô again, he gave ‚Äôem the slip.

They catched him and they chained him tight,

Stowed him below without a light.

And he got nothing to eat or drink,

Soon his bones they starts to shrink.

And now his ghost is often seen

Up on the main truck wet and green.

So dat is de tale of John Cherokee,

Nigger man from Mirramashee.

‚ÄúI hear,‚ÄĚ I heard the Old Man say,

Chorus (after each verse):

Too lai ay, ooohh, too lai ay,

I thought I heard the bosun say,

‚ÄúWork tomorrow but not today.‚ÄĚ

The blower says, ‚ÄúBefore I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm done

items.title: Johnny Bowker
items.notes:
Johnny Bowker

[

; Ballad Index Doe009a

; DT JONBOKER

; Mudcat 67154

; trad.]

Paul Clayton and chorus sang Do My Johnny Booker

items.lyrics:
O do my Johnny Booker, come rock and roll me over,

O do my Johnny Booker, do.

O do my Johnny Booker, come rock and roll me over,

O do my Johnny Booker, come rock and roll me over,

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings Johnny Bowker
versions.lyrics:
Do me Johnny Bowker come rock and roll me over,

Do me Johnny Bowker do.

Do me Johnny Bowker from Calais up to Dover.

Do me Johnny Bowker, the mate he is a croaker.

Do me Johnny Bowker, the bosun‚Äôs never sober.

items.title: Johnny Stewart, Drover
items.notes:
Johnny Stewart, Drover

[Chris Buch]

Danny Spooner sang Chris Buch‚Äôs song Johnny Stewart, Drover

in 2008 on his CD

Brave Bold Boys .

He noted:

Chris Buch came to Australia from London in 1967 seeking the Australian

Legend. He found it in 1981 when he was encouraged to join Johnny Stewart

droving fifteen hundred head of cattle from Rocklands down along the Georgina

River across the Diamantina River to a station on the Cooper River, a journey

that took Johnny thirteen weeks. Chris joined the drove for ten days but the

experience was enough to create this cracking song about an Australia which
items.lyrics:
The mob is dipped, the plant is moving out,

They‚Äôre leaving Rockland‚Äôs dusty yards behind them.

The whips are cracking and the drovers shout;

Along the Queensland stock-roads you will find them.

Droving ways have been like this for years,

Modern times don‚Äôt mean those days are over.

For the diesel road trains cannot know the steers

Or walk them down like Johnny Stewart, drover.

Chorus (after every other verse):

On the banks of the Georgina, down the Diamantina,

To where the grass is greener, down in New South Wales.

Johnny Stewart, drover, taking cattle over,

His life story moving on miles of dusty trails.

The cook is busy by the campfire light,

Above the coals a billy gently swinging.

The mob is settled quiet for the night

And Johnny Stewart rides around and singing.

Johnny doesn‚Äôt spend much time in town,

Impatient for the wet to be over.

Most of the year he‚Äôs just walking cattle down,

Stock roads are home for Johnny Stewart, drover.

Well, dawn will surely bring another day,

The sun still chasing moon, never caught her.

Morning light will see them on their way,

Another push to reach the welcome water.

Well, they‚Äôre counted in now, Johnny‚Äôs work is done,

Fifteen hundred head are counted over.

items.title: La-di-da
items.notes:
La-di-da

[Jake Thackray]

Danny Spooner sang La-di-da

on his 1987 album

When a Man‚Äôs in Love .

He noted:

This song is a classic plaint against the extended family that one inherits

with marriage. Yet for all its vitriolic humour and honest fear, there is a

gentle understanding and acknowledgement of human frailties to be found in

this masterpiece by the singer-songwriter [Jake] Thackray.
items.lyrics:
Now that we‚Äôve agreed that we‚Äôre in love,

We‚Äôll have to face the la-di-da, the eye wash,

All of the fancy pantomime, I love you very much, I‚Äôll try love

I‚Äôll bill and coo with your gruesome auntie Susan cross me heart, I‚Äôll play it cool;

And I‚Äôll let your tetchy uncles get me back up cross me heart,

And I won‚Äôt get shirty when they say I look peculiar.

I‚Äôll be nice to your mummy, I‚Äôll come all over la-di-da

Although she always gets up me nose, I love you very much,

And so I‚Äôll smile and I‚Äôll acquiesce, when she invites me to caress, her scabby cat;

I‚Äôll sit still while she knits and whitters cross me heart,

And I won‚Äôt lay a finger on the crabby old bat-face.

I‚Äôll be polite to your daddy, frightfully la-di-da

Although he always bores me to my boots, I love you very much,

And so I won‚Äôt boo and hiss when he starts to reminisce, I won‚Äôt flair up, I won‚Äôt drop of

The runs he used to score and how he won the war cross me heart;

But I‚Äôll have to grit me teeth when he goes on about his rupture.

I‚Äôll behave at the wedding breakfast I‚Äôll be la-di-da, I promise,

Hearty toasts and risqu√© jokes, I love you very much, so help me,

I‚Äôll force a laugh for the flicking photographs, so have no fear, I won‚Äôt turn tail,

And I won‚Äôt run amuck when the females chuck confetti in my ears,

And cross me heart love, I‚Äôll keep off the Pale Ale.

When we‚Äôre off on our own, no more la-di-bloody-da, I promise,

We just won‚Äôt have the time, we won‚Äôt have time for such, fancy pantomime,

I love you far too much.

When I went to Brittany in 2002 I heard wonderful shanties and songs from

had sung English and American shanties for years and wondered

why‚ÄĒ‚ÄúIf we are a maritime nation, which we are, where are our

researched, collected and published by

items.title: Logs to Burn
items.notes:
Logs to Burn

[

RoudBS B198717

; Honor Goodhart, 1920]

Logs to Burn is a poem attributed to Honor Goodhart

that was printed in Punch in 27 October 1920.

Johnny Collins sang Logs to Burn

in 1975 on his Traditional Sound Recordings album

Johnny‚Äôs Private Army .

This track was also included in 1998 on his Fellside compilation

The Best of the Early Years .

West of Eden sang Logs to Burn
items.lyrics:
Logs to burn; logs to burn;

Logs to save the coal a turn.

Here‚Äôs a word to make you wise

when you hear the woodman‚Äôs cries;

Never heed his usual tale

That he‚Äôs splendid logs for sale

But read these lines and really learn

The proper kind of logs to burn.

Oak logs will warm you well,

If they‚Äôre old and dry.

Larch logs of pinewoods smell

But the sparks will fly.

Beech logs for Christmas time;

Yew logs heat well;

‚ÄėScotch‚Äô logs it is a crime

For anyone to sell.

Birch logs will burn too fast;

Chestnut scarce at all;

Hawthorn logs are good to last

If cut in the fall.

Holly logs will burn like wax,

You should burn them green;

Elm logs like smouldering flax,

No flame to be seen.

Pear logs and apple logs,

They will scent your room;

Cherry logs across the dogs

Smell like flowers in bloom,

But ash logs all smooth and grey

Burn them green or old,

Buy up all that come your way

They‚Äôre worth their weight in gold.

versions.title: Danny Spooner and Duncan Brown sing Logs to Burn
versions.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Logs to burn, logs to burn,

Logs to save the coal a turn.

Here‚Äôs a word to make you wise

When you hear the woodman‚Äôs cries.

Beech wood fires burn bright and clear,

Hornbeam blazes too,

If the logs are kept a year

To season through and through.

Oak logs will warm you well

If they‚Äôre old and dry.

Larch logs and pinewoods smell

But the sparks will fly.

Pine is good and so is yew

For warmth through wintry days.

The poplar and the willow too,

They take too long to blaze.

Birch logs will burn too fast,

Alder scarce at all.

Chestnut logs is good to last,

Cut them in the fall.

Holly logs will burn like wax,

You should burn them green.

Elm logs like smouldering flax,

No flames with them are seen.

Pear logs and apple logs,

They will scent your home.

Cherry logs across the dogs

They smell like flowers in bloom.

Ash logs so smooth and grey

Burn them green or old.

Buy up all that come your way

They‚Äôre worth their weight in gold.

items.title: London‚Äôs Ordinary
items.notes:
London‚Äôs Ordinary

[

; Bodleian

; EBBA 20086

; trad.]

John Faulkner sang London‚Äôs Ordinary, or Every Man in His Humour

in 1966 on The Critics Group‚Äôs album

A Merry Progress to London .

Lis Johnston and Danny Spooner sang

London‚Äôs Ordinary

on the 1978 album

Danny Spooner and Friends .
items.lyrics:
Through the Royal Exchange as I walked,

Where gallants in satin do shine,

At midst of the day they parted away

To several places to dine.

The gentry went to the King‚Äôs Head,

The nobles unto the Crown;

The knights unto to the Golden Fleece,

And the ploughmen unto the Clown.

The clergy will dine at the Mitre,

The vintners at the Three Tuns;

The usurers to the Devil will go,

And the friars into the Nuns.

The cheater will dine at the Checquer,

The pick-pocket at the blind Ale-house.

Till taken and tried up Holborne they ride

And make their end at the Gallows.

The plumbers will dine at the Fountain,

The cooks at the Holy Lamb;

The drunkards at noon to the Man in the Moon,

And the cuckolds into the Ram.

The rovers will dine at the Lion,

The watermen at the Old Swan,

The bawd will to the Negro go,

And the whores to the Naked Man.

The fishmongers unto the Dolphin,

The bakers to the Cheat Loaf;

The turners into the Ladle will go,

Where the may merrily quaff.

The hosiers will dine at the Leg,

The drapers at the signe of the Brush;

The fletchers to Robin Hood will go,

And the spend-thrift to Beggers Bush.

The goldsmiths will to the Three Cups,

Their money they count it as dross;

Your puritan to the Pewter Can,

And your papist to the Cross.

The weavers will dine at the Shuttle,

And the glovers will into the Glove;

The maidens all to the Maidenhead,

And the true lovers unto the Dove.

And thus every man in his humour,

From the North unto the South;

But he that hath no money in his purse,

May dine at the sign of the Mouth.

items.title: Look Out Below
items.notes:
Look Out Below

[

;

AFS 56

Danny Spooner sang

items.lyrics:
A young man left his native shore

The work was bad at home

And to Australia‚Äôs sunny land

He crossed the briny foam

And when he came to Ballarat

It put him in a glow

The cry, ‚ÄėLook out below‚Äô

Where‚Äôer he turned his wondering eye

And peace and plenty hand in hand

Says he, ‚ÄúAnd I am young and strong

To the diggings I will go

For I love the sound of windlass and

The cry, ‚ÄėLook out below‚Äô ‚ÄĚ

So among the rest he took his chance

His luck at first was vile

But he did work and persevere

And at length he made a pile

Says he, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôve made my fortune

So home again I‚Äôll go

I‚Äôll bid farewell to the windlass and

The cry, ‚ÄėLook out below‚Äô ‚ÄĚ

But arrived in London once again

His gold he freely spent

And into every gaiety

And dissipation went

But pleasures if prolonged too much

Oft causes pain you know

And he missed the sound of the windlass and

The cry, ‚ÄėLook out below‚Äô

And thus he reasoned with himself

‚ÄúOh why do I return

For the digger‚Äôs independent life

For that I surely yearn‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúHere purse-proud lords the poor oppress

But there it is not so

Give me the sound of the windlass and

The cry, ‚ÄėLook out below‚Äô ‚ÄĚ

And so he started back again

With his own little wife

And he says there‚Äôs nothing can compare

To a jolly digger‚Äôs life

Ask him if he‚Äôll go back again

He√Ę¬Ä¬ôll quickly answer, ‚ÄúNo,

For I love the sound of the windlass and

The cry, ‚ÄėLook out below‚Äô ‚ÄĚ

[

; trad.]

Karl Dallas:

The Cruel Wars

Swan Arcade sang

Lord Willoughby

in 1973 on their eponymous first album on the Trailer label,

Swan Arcade .
items.lyrics:
The fourteenth day of July,
with glistering spear and shield,

A famous fight in Flanders
was foughten in the field;

The most courageous officers
were English captains three,

But the bravest man in battle
was brave Lord Willoughby.

The next was Captain Norris‚Äô,
a valiant man was he;

The other, Captain Turner,
from field would never flee.

-->

With fifteen hundred fighting men,
alas, there were no more,‚ÄĒ

They fought with fourteen thousand men
upon the Flanders shore.

‚ÄúStand to it, noble pikemen,
and look you round about!

And shoot you straight, you bowmen,
and we will keep them out!

You musquet and caliver men,
do you prove true to me;

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be the foremost man in fight!‚ÄĚ
says brave Lord Willoughby.

And then the bloody enemy
they fiercely did assail;

And fought it out most furiously,
not doubting to prevail.

The wounded men on both sides fell,
most piteous for to see,

Yet nothing could the courage quell
of brave Lord Willoughby.

For seven hours, to all men‚Äôs view,
the flight endured sore;

Until our men so feeble grew
that they could fight no more.

And then upon dead horses
full savourly they ate,

And drank the puddle water‚ÄĒ
they could no better get.

When they had fed so freely,
they kneeled on the ground,

And praised God devoutly
for the favour they had found;

And beating up their colours,
the fight they did renew,

And turning tow‚Äôrds the Spaniard,
a thousand more they slew.

The sharp steel-pointed arrows
and bullets thick did fly;

Then did our valiant soldiers
charge on most furiously;

Which made the Spaniards waver,
they thought it best to flee;

They feared the stout behaviour
of brave Lord Willoughby.

Then quoth the Spanish general,
‚ÄúCome let us march away.

I fear we shall be spoiled all
if that we longer stay.‚ÄĚ

For yonder comes Lord Willoughby
with courage fierce and fell.

He will not give one inch away
for all the devils in Hell.

And then the fearful enemy
was quickly put to flight;

Our men pursued courageously
and roused their forces quite.

Till then at last they gave a shout
which echoed through the sky;

‚ÄúGod and Saint George for England!‚ÄĚ
the conquerors did cry.

This news was brought to England,
with all the speed might be,

And soon our gracious Queen was told
of this same victory.

‚ÄúO this is brave Lord Willoughby,
my love that ever won;

Of all the Lords of honour
√Ę¬Ä¬ôtis he great deeds hath done.‚ÄĚ

For soldiers that were maimed
and wounded in the fray,

The Queen allowed a pension
of eighteen pence a day:

Likewise all costs and charges
she quit and set them free;

And this she did all for the sake
of brave Lord Willoughby.

Then, courage! noble Englishmen,
and never be dismayed:

If that we be but one to ten
we will not be afraid

To fight with foreign enemies,
and set our nation free;

And thus I end the famous bout
of brave Lord Willoughby.

items.title: Man of the Earth
items.notes:
Man of the Earth

[Bernie Parry]

Bernie Parry sang his own song

Man of the Earth

in 1977 on his Free Reed album

Sailing to the Moon .

It was also included in 2002 on the Free Reed anthology

This Label Is Not Removable .

This video shows him singing it in about 2011:

Pat Ryan sang Man of the Earth

on her 1977 Folk Heritage album

Leaboy‚Äôs Lassie .
items.lyrics:
Every day as I go through the old shanty town

Where the sheds and allotments all stand,

I see an old man on his land

With a rake or a spade in his hands.

And he‚Äôs there in all weather,

In sunshine or rain and I hesitate as I go past.

Is he happy or sad with his task?

Oh, I haven‚Äôt the time for to ask.

Chorus (after each verse):

The man of the earth, the man of the soil,

In his lonely allotment he labours and toils.

There‚Äôs not much to do since he turned sixty-five.

So he took to his garden to keep him alive

And I think it‚Äôs his joy and his pride.

Fifty years in the ironworks broke his will

And his back and his shoulders are round.

There was no other work in the town

So they had him both fettered and bound.

Then all of a sudden he turned sixty-five

And his bosses said, ‚ÄúThank you my man.‚ÄĚ

And they stuck a gold watch in his hand

And behind him the door quickly slammed.

Every Saturday evening he‚Äôs down at the pub

And he stands by himself at the bar,

Slowly sipping a solitary jar

For the pension won‚Äôt go very far.

So he sells a few things to his neighbours and friends,

A few of the things that he grows.

But he‚Äôs got to watch out how he goes

Or they‚Äôll stop all his pension, he knows.

Every day as I go through the old shanty town

Where the sheds and allotments all stand,

I see an old man on his land

With a rake or a spade in his hands.

But I really can‚Äôt linger, I must be gone,

For I work in the ironworks too.

I started there five years ago,

Only forty-five more to go.

items.title: Mothers, Daughters, Wives
items.notes:
Mothers, Daughters, Wives

[Judy Small]

Danny Spooner sang Judy Small‚Äôs song Mothers, Daughters, Wives

on his 2007 CD of fairly contemporary Australian songs,

Emerging Tradition .

He noted:

Often they are victims of conflicts, yet some women have been prepared to

hand out the ‚Äėwhite feather‚Äô to men who stayed away from the fight

without enquiring the reason. In this song, Judy Small suggests that while

social conditioning might have been the cause of past acceptance, many modern

women refuse to be so stereotyped.
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after every other verse):

The first time it was fathers, the last time it was sons,

And in between your husbands marched away with drums and guns.

And you never stopped to question, you just went on with your lives,

For all they‚Äôd taught you who to be was mothers, daughters, wives.

You can only just remember the tears your mother shed;

As she sat and read their papers, through the lists and lists of dead.

And the gold frames held the photographs that mothers kissed each night,

And the doorframes held the shocked and silent strangers from the fight.

And twenty-one years later, with children of your own,

The trumpets sounded once again and the soldier boys were gone.

And you drove their trucks and made their guns and tended to their wounds,

And at night you kissed the photographs and prayed for safe returns.

And after it was over, you had to learn again

To just be wives and mothers when you‚Äôd done the work of men,

So you worked to help the needy and you never trod on toes

And the photos on the pianos they struck a happy family pose.

Then your daughters grew to women and your little boys to men,

And you prayed that you were dreaming when the call-up came again.

But you proudly smiled and held your tears as they bravely waved goodbye

And the photos on the mantelpiece, they always made you cry.

And now you‚Äôre getting older and with times the photos fade

And in widowhood you‚Äôre sitting, and reflect on the parade,

Of the passing of your memories as your daughters change their lives,

Seeing more to their existence than just mothers, daughters, wives.

Final chorus:

The first time it was fathers, the last time it was sons,

And in between your husbands marched away with drums and guns.

And you never stopped to question, you just went on with your lives,

For all they‚Äôd taught you who to be was mothers, daughters, wives,

And you believed them.

items.title: My Jolly Sailor Bold
items.notes:
My Jolly Sailor Bold

[

; Bodleian

; trad.]

Sandra Kerr sang My Jolly Sailor Bold

in 1966 on The Critics Group‚Äô Argo anthology of London songs,

Sweet Thames Flow Softly .

The album‚Äôs booklet commented:

The exploits of Jolly Jack at sea and of his solace and loves ashore occupy

a major place in English folk music tradition. Taken from J. Ashton‚Äôs

Real Sailor Songs this is typical of many 19 th Century

songs in which a maiden of high degree defies her parents and forsakes a
items.lyrics:
Upon one summer‚Äôs morning, I carelessly did stray,

Down by the Wall of Wapping, where I met a sailor gay,

Conversing with a bouncing lass, who seem‚Äôd to be in pain,

Saying, William, when you go, I fear you‚Äôll ne‚Äôer return again.

His hair it did in ringlets hang, his eyes as black as sloes,

May happiness attend him wherever he goes,

From Tower Hill, down Blackwall, I will wander, weep, and moan,

All for my jolly sailor bold, until he does return.

My father is a merchant‚ÄĒthe truth I now will tell,

And in great London City in opulence doth dwell,

His fortune doth exceed ¬£300,000 in gold,

And he frowns upon his daughter, cause she loves a sailor bold.

A fig for his riches, and his merchandise, and gold!

True love is grafted in my heart; give me my sailor bold;

Should he return in poverty, from o‚Äôer the ocean far,

Onto my tender bosom, I‚Äôll fondly press my jolly tar.

My sailor is a smiling as the pleasant Month of May,

And oft we have wandered through Ratcliffe Highway,

Where many a pretty blooming girl we happy did behold,

Reclining on the bosom of her jolly sailor bold.

Come all you pretty fair maids, whoever you may be,

Who love a jolly sailor bold that ploughs the raging sea,

While up aloft in storm and gale, from me his absence mourn,

And firmly pray, arrive the day, he home will safe return.

My name it is Maria, a merchant‚Äôs daughter fair,

And I have left my parents and three thousand pounds a year,

Her heart is pierced by Cupid, I disdain all glittering gold,

There is nothing can console me but my jolly sailor bold.

Her name it is Maria, she‚Äôs a merchant‚Äôs daughter fair,

Her heart is pierced by Cupid‚Äôs dart; she shuns all glittering gold,

And there‚Äôs nothing can console her but her jolly sailor bold.

items.title: Newell Highway
items.notes:
Newell Highway

[words John Warner, music G.H.H. Parry (1872)]

Margaret Walters sang John Warner‚Äôs song

Newell Highway

on their 1997 album

Who Was Here? .

They noted:

This song celebrates the Warrumbungle Ranges in inland New South Wales.

John first heard the tune, C.H.H. Parry‚Äôs melody for the hymn

Dear Lord and Father of Mankind , in his earliest childhood as a theme

to a BBC radio programme, and it is adapted here from that memory.

Danny Spooner sang John Warner‚Äôs song
items.lyrics:
Awake before the dawn, within the spires of range

Where magpies ornate melodies

Engrave the chilly morning breeze

Beneath the towering stone,

Beneath the towering stone.

On nights of silver moon, too rich to waste on sleep,

In silence make your way to seek

The choirs of frog in swamp and creek

That sing beneath the stars,

That sing beneath the stars.

Out on the Western Plain beside the roaring road,

Where trucks snarl by without a care,

Are billabongs with ibis there

And wedge-tail eagles soar,

And wedge-tail eagles soar.

All you who love the earth and make her ways your choice,

Cry out against the noise of trade

Demand that silence should be made

So that all might hear her voice,

Her ancient, matchless voice.

items.title: On the Banks of the Reedy Lagoon
items.notes:
On the Banks of the Reedy Lagoon

[

;

AFS 149

; Ballad Index PASB144

; DT REEDYLAG

; Mudcat 154639

; Jimmy Connors]

Martyn Wyndham-Read sang Reedy Lagoon

on his 1984 Greenwich Village album

A Rose From the Bush .

He commented in his sleeve notes:
items.lyrics:
The sweet-scented wattle sheds perfume around,

Enticing the bird and the bee;

As I lie at my rest in a fern-covered rest

In the shade of a currajong tree;

High up in the air I can hear the refrain

Of a butcher-bird piping its tune,

For the spring, in her glory, has come back again

To the banks of the Reedy Lagoon.

I‚Äôve carried my bluey for many a mile,

My boots they are worn out at the toe;

And I‚Äôm dressing, this season, in a far different style,

To that of last season, God knows!

My cooking utensils, I‚Äôm sorry to say,

Consist of a knife and a spoon.

And I‚Äôve dry bread and tea, in my battered jack-shay

On the banks of the Reedy Lagoon.

Where is old Frankie, man how could he ride,

And Johnny, the kind-hearted boy;

They tell me that lately he‚Äôs taken a bride,

A benedict‚Äôs life to enjoy.

And Big Mac, the Scotchman; I once heard him say,

That he wrestled the famous Muldoon:

But they‚Äôre all far away, and I‚Äôm lonely today

On the bank of the Reedy Lagoon.

Now where is that lassie I oft-times caressed,

The girl with the sad dreamy eyes?

She pillows her head on another man‚Äôs breast,

While he tells her the very same lies.

My bed she would hardly be willing to share,

Where I camp by the light of the moon.

But it‚Äôs little I care, cos I couldn‚Äôt keep square

On the bank of the Reedy Lagoon.

‚ÄôTis the butcher-bird piping his tune,

To the banks of the Reedy Lagoon.

Oh, where is young Frankie, oh how he could ride!

And Johnny, the kind-hearted boy;

A benedict‚Äôs life to enjoy.

And Big Jock, the Scotsman; I once heard ‚Äôem say,

They‚Äôre all far away, and I‚Äôm lonely today

I think of Bob Billy and Willie the brave,

Together we oft sang a song;

They‚Äôre wrapped in the slumbers that comes with the grave

Down there neath the shades of Toowong.

That dark road I‚Äôll travel, be it sooner or late,

Let death be tardy or soon,

My probable fate I‚Äôll not contemplate

On the bank of the Reedy Lagoon.

Oh, where is the lady I oft times caressed‚ÄĒ

The girl with the sad dreamy eye;

She nestle her head on another man‚Äôs breast,

My bed she would hardly be willing to share

But it‚Äôs little I care, for I couldn‚Äôt keep square

items.title: On the Wallaby / The Tentpoles Are Rotting
items.notes:
[

; Henry Lawson]

On the Wallaby is a poem from Henry Lawson‚Äôs book

When I Was King (Sydney: Angus and Robertson, 1906).

The Bushwackers sang The Tent Poles are Rotten

in 1981 on their album

Faces in the Street

which was produced by Trevor Lucas.

Martyn Wyndham-Read sang The Tent Poles are Rotten

in 1986 on his Greenwich Village album of Australian songs,

Across the Line .

Danny Spooner sang On the Wallaby
items.lyrics:
Now the tent poles are rotting, the camp fires are dead,

And the possums may gambol in trees overhead;

I am humping my bluey far out on the land,

And the prints of my bluchers sink deep in the sand:

I am out on the wallaby humping my drum,

And I came by the tracks where the sundowners come.

It is nor‚Äô-west and west o‚Äôer the ranges and far

To the plains where the cattle and sheep stations are,

With the sky for my roof and the grass for my bunk,

And a calico bag for my damper and junk;

And scarcely a comrade my memory reveals,

Save the spiritless dingo in tow of my heels.

But I think of the honest old light of my home

When the stars hang in clusters like lamps from the dome,

And I think of the hearth where the dark shadows fall,

When my camp fire is built on the widest of all;

But I‚Äôm following Fate, for I know she knows best,

I follow, she leads, and it‚Äôs nor‚Äô-west by west.

When my tent is all torn and my blankets are damp,

And the rising flood waters flow fast by the camp,

When the cold water rises in jets from the floor,

I lie in my bunk and I list to the roar,

And I think how to-morrow my footsteps will lag

When I tramp ‚Äôneath the weight of a rain-sodden swag.

Though the way of the swagman is mostly up-hill,

There are joys to be found on the wallaby still.

When the day has gone by with its tramp or its toil,

And your camp-fire you light, and your billy you boil,

There is comfort and peace in the bowl of your clay

Or the yarn of a mate who is tramping that way.

But beware of the town‚ÄĒthere is poison for years

In the pleasure you find in the depths of long beers;

For the bushman gets bushed in the streets of a town,

Where he loses his friends when his cheque is knocked down;

He is right till his pockets are empty, and then‚ÄĒ

He can hump his old bluey up country again.

items.title: Our Jack
items.notes:
Our Jack

[

; Ballad Index Doe169

; Mudcat 7388

; trad.]

Martyn Wyndham-Read and Danny Spooner sang Our Jack

in 1989 on their album

All Around Down Under ,

and Danny Spooner sang

Our Jack

in 2008 on his CD

Brave Bold Boys .
items.lyrics:
Our Jack‚Äôs come out of gaol today

To Pentridge he has been

For many a day he‚Äôs been away

His face we ne‚Äôer have seen

McMenamy arrested Jack

And with a gentle jerk

Comes down upon our old friend Jack

With jemmy hard at work

Chorus (after each verse):

Our Jack‚Äôs come home today

Our Jack‚Äôs come home today

Quite wan and pale from out of gaol

Our Jack‚Äôs come home today

When Jack came out of gaol today

It made his Polly glad

She had to pawn the things he‚Äôd shook

And found that she‚Äôs been had

The price she got it weren‚Äôt enough

To keep her for a day

But all is past, she‚Äôs right at last

Our Jack‚Äôs come home today

When Jack came out of quad today

We had a glorious spree

And did a tour of Melbourne pubs

As jovial as could be

With wine and beer and brandy punch

We started out ad lib

When Jack proposed a partnership

To crack another crib

With jemmies and with skeleton keys

Of cribs we went in search

But seeing Dave O‚ÄôDonnell there

We left Jack in the lurch

Undaunted Jack he set to work

Another crib to crack

O‚ÄôDonnell like a ton of bricks

Came down to lumber Jack

Final chorus 1989:

Our Jack‚Äôs gone back today

Our Jack‚Äôs gone back today

White wan and pale back into gaol

Our Jack‚Äôs gone back today

Final chorus 2008:

Our Jack‚Äôs gone back today

Set up he‚Äôll be they say

A nice record he‚Äôll get from Ward

Our Jack‚Äôs gone home today

items.title: Peter‚Äôs Private Army
items.notes:
Peter‚Äôs Private Army

[Martin Graebe]

Martin Graebe wrote Peter‚Äôs Private Army in 1972

Parallel Strands .

Martin noted in his songbook

Jack in the Green and Other Heroes (PDF document):

At one point in my career my interest in historical oddities and my

professional life as a developer of new food products came together when I was

working on Ambrosia ‚ÄėTraditional Rice Pudding‚Äô.

In establishing the background for that product I used information on the

retail market in milk products in the middle of the last century from
items.lyrics:
Now people come and gather round,

Though our coats are ragged and our faces brown.

You see we are no common band

At the call of our country we all did stand.

Chorus (after each verse):

Tip-a-tap tip, hop and skip

It‚Äôs Peter‚Äôs Private Army

Number one is Peter Dunn,

His arm blown away by a big French gun.

It‚Äôs him our little band does lead

With the money bag tied to his sleeve.

And number two is Peg-leg Hugh,

His dancing done since Waterloo.

With his fiddle tucked beneath his chin

He‚Äôll wave his stump to keep the time.

And number three is Blind Jack Bree,

Lost both his eyes in the King‚Äôs navy.

But still his squeeze-box he can play

And the notes roll out like an ocean wave.

And number four is Harry Taw,

Caught a bullet in the throat in the Peninsula.

But he‚Äôs wind enough on his fife to toot,

He can say as much as we although his voice is mute.

And number five is Mad Jim Ives,

We sometimes wonder if he‚Äôs dead or alive.

But when he hears the fiddle thrum

He‚Äôll beat like hell on his pigskin drum.

And now we‚Äôre marching off again,

You can hear us fading down the lane.

And we hope that you‚Äôve been kind to us

‚ÄôCos we‚Äôve given much for you in the foreign wars.

He noted:

Between 1949-51 Harry Robertson worked on whaling ships out of the misty, icy

items.lyrics:
Please find the lyrics for this song at
Harry Robertson‚Äôs website .

items.title: Rabbit Trapper
items.notes:
Rabbit Trapper

[trad.]

Danny Spooner sang Rabbit Trapper

on his 2007 anthology

Years of Spooner .

He noted:

I‚Äôve always loved the laconic humour to be found in Australian folk songs,

and this is a great example. The Aussie bushman was a law unto himself, proud,

independent and capable. A man to be admired. I got this song from Alan Scott,

a wonderful singer and folklorist, who inspired many a young singer and whose

passing left a large gap in the Australian folk scene.
items.lyrics:
Now my traps is all a-jangle in an easy swinging tangle,

And I‚Äôm setting in a circle keeping to a fringe of trees;

Though I‚Äôm mud and gory spattered and my clothes is torn and tattered,

I‚Äôm as carefree as the bunnies, till they fall for one of these.

I‚Äôm under no man‚Äôs orders and I recognize no borders,

Amd there‚Äôs a welcome every where fer me and my old dungarees;

For I am a rabbit trapper, a canny bunny snapper,

And I whistle through the bushland like the birds up in the trees.

While some blokes talk to tabbies, I am out among the rabbies,

I can hear ‚Äôem buck and squealin‚Äô, a dozen traps ahead,

And again while they are flirtin‚Äô at my last trap I‚Äôll be certain

To be baggin‚Äô up my bunnies, keeping tally as I tread.

So come on my old cobber, we‚Äôll put on some decent clobber

And we‚Äôll leave the bunnies playin‚Äô in and out among the trees;

We‚Äôll get to the railway early, there‚Äôs a shy and dinkum girlie

And i‚Äôll juggle with the cream cans while she writes out cheques for me.

items.title: Row On
items.notes:
[

; Mudcat 13562

; words trad., tune Tim Laycock]

George Payne Rainsford James:

Arabella Stuart: A Romance from English History

Gale Huntington:

Songs the Whalemen Sang

Row On

is a song from George Payne Rainsford James‚Äô book

Arabella Stuart: A Romance from English History

(New York: Harper & Brothers, 1844, page 103).

At first glace it looks like a lover‚Äôs lament, but from the context it is clear
items.lyrics:
Row on, row on! Another day

May shine with brighter light;

Ply, ply the oars, and pull away,

Thou must not come to-night.

Clouds are upon the summer sky,

There‚Äôs thunder on the wind;

Pull on, pull on, and homeward hie,

Nor give one look behind!

Bear where thou go‚Äôst the words of love;

Say all that words can say,

Changeless affection‚Äôs strength to prove,

But speed upon the way.

Oh! like yon river could I glide

To where my heart would be;

My bark should soon outsail the tide,

That hurries to the sea.

But yet a star shines constant still

Through yonder cloudy sky,

And hopes as bright my bosom fill,

From faith that cannot die!

Row on, then, row! God speed thy way!

Thou must not linger here;

Storms hang about the closing day;

To-morrow may be clear.
