items.title: Barney Buntline / Sailor‚Äôs Consolation
items.notes:
[

; Ballad Index Hugi460A

; Charles Dibdin]

Gordon Bok sang Charles Dibdin‚Äô song A Sailor‚Äôs Consolation

on his 1992 album Schooners .

He noted:

Dibdin wrote many ‚Äėsailors√Ę¬Ä¬ô songs‚Äô back in the 1700s in England.

Not many have gone into tradition. Bob Zentz of Norfolk, Virginia, sent me

this one last summer; he had set it to the tune of

The Recruited Collier ,

which it fits handsomely. He played it on a concertina, which the cellamba here

reflects. Thanks for another good one, brother Bob.
items.lyrics:
One night came on a hurricane

The sea was mountains rolling,

When Barney Buntline turned his quid,

And said to Billy Bowline:

‚ÄúA strong nor√Ę¬Ä¬ôwester√Ę¬Ä¬ôs blowing, Bill,

Hark! Don‚Äôt you hear it roar now?

Lord help them! How I pities all

Unhappy folks on shore now.

‚ÄúFoolhardy chaps that live in towns;

What dangers they are all in,

And now lie shaking in their beds

for fear the roof should fall in.

Poor creatures, how they envy us

And wishes, I‚Äôve a notion,

For our good luck in such a storm

To be upon the ocean.

‚ÄúAnd as for them who√Ę¬Ä¬ôre out all day

On business from their houses,

And late at night are coming home

To cheer their babes and spouses;

While you and I, Bill, on the deck

Are comfortably lying,

My eyes! What tiles and chimney-pots

Around their heads are flying!

‚ÄúAnd very often have we heard

How men are killed and undone

By overturns of carriages,

By thieves and fires in London.

We know what risks all landsmen run

From noblemen to tailors;

Then Bill, let us thank Providence

That you and I are sailors.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Scrumptious Young Gals
items.notes:
Scrumptious Young Gals

[Charles Thatcher (1831-1878)]

Danny Spooner sang Scrumptious Young Gals

on his 2013 CD

Gorgeous, Game Girls .

He commented:

Another

[see also

Big Poll the Grog Seller ]

of Thatcher‚Äôs offerings, this song reflects how things were changing

on the [Australian] goldfields by the 1860s. The numbers of womenfolk were

growing, and as towns grew more prosperous they tried to move away from the
items.lyrics:
Scrumptious young girls, oh, you‚Äôre togged out so finely

Adorning the diggings so charming and so gay;

With your beautiful smiles, oh, you look so divinely,

Thus lovers crowd around you their homage for to pay.

Australia may yield her profuse golden treasures

And her bright summer skies shine with radiance too;

But women alone can afford us real pleasures -

Say what would the goldfields now be without you?

Chorus (after each verse):

Scrumptious young girls, oh, you‚Äôre togged out so finely

Adorning the diggings so charming and so gay;

With your beautiful smiles, oh, you look so divinely,

And lovers crowd around you their homage for to pay.

Gals, gals, glorious gals, beautiful, beautiful, beautiful,

Scrumptious young girls.

Scrumptious young girls thanks to free emigration

Out to the diggins for husbands you fly;

Mrs Chisholm, we hold on in great veneration,

Because you‚Äôve kept up such a constant supply.

Five years ago and how dull was this quarter,

And how very monotonous, too, was the life;

The sight of a female would our mouths water,

And only rich diggers could pick up a wife.

Scrumptious young girls, oh, how flash you are growing,

Quiet print dresses you‚Äôve left upon the shelf;

Wearing rich satins and silks and thereby a-showing

That each of you thinks no small beer of herself.

Balls and casinos and concerts frequenting,

Oh, still may you flourish so queenly and grand:

And ever such charming new pleasures inventing,

Grace with your presence this fair golden land.

items.title: Sean O‚ÄôDwyer of the Glen
items.notes:
[

; Ballad Index OLoc081

; trad.]

Danny Spooner, accompanied by Mick Farrell, sang and played

the air and song

Se√°n √ď Du√≠bh√≠r an Ghleanna (Sean O‚ÄôDwyer of the Glen)

in 1978 on their album

Limbo .

He noted:

These two marvellous pieces of Irish musical and lyric brilliance were

inspired by a guerrilla leader in Ireland at the time of Oliver Cromwell‚Äôs

attempted conquest. Se√°n √ď Duinhr carried on a fierce resistance against the
items.lyrics:
How oft at sunny morning
sunlight all adorning

I hear the horn give warning
‚Äômid the birds mellow call.

Badgers flee before us,
woodcocks startle o‚Äôer us

And guns give ringing chorus
‚Äômid the echoes all.

The fox runs higher and higher,
huntsmen shouting nigh her

A maiden lying by her fowl
left wounded in his gore.

Now they fell the wildwood,
farewell home of childhood

Se√°n √ď Duibhir an Gleanna
your day it is o‚Äôer.

‚ÄôTis my sorrow sorest,
sad the falling forest

The north wind brings me no rest
and death is in the sky.

My noble hounds tied tightly
never sporting brightly

Would make a child laugh lightly
with a tear in its eye.

The antlered noble-hearted,
stags are never started

Never chased nor parted
from the furzy field.

If peace comes but a small way
I‚Äôll journey down to Galway

I‚Äôll leave, but not for always,
my Erin of ills.

Land of streams and valleys
has no head nor rallies

In city, camp or palace
they never toast her name.

Where the warrior column
from Clyne to peaks of Collum

All wasted hills and solemn
the wild hare grows tame.

When will come the routing
shocks of churls and flouting?

I hear no joyful shouting
from the blackbird brave.

Ne‚Äôer warlike is the yeoman,
justice comes to no man

And priests must flee the foeman
to the mountain cave.

‚ÄôTis my woe and ruin,
sinless death‚Äôs undoing

Came not to the strewing
of all my bright hopes.

How oft of sunny morning
I watched the sun returning

The autumn maples burning
and dew on the woodland slopes.

But now my lands are plundered
far my friends are sundered

And I must hide me under
the branch and bramble screen.

If soon I cannot save me
from flights of foes who crave me

Oh death at last I‚Äôll brave thee
my bitter foes between.

For now they fell the wildwood,
farewell home of childhood

Se√°n √ď Duibhir an Ghleanna
your day it is o‚Äôer.

items.title: Shearing in a Bar
items.notes:
>

Martyn Wyndham-Read >

Songs >

Shearing in a Bar

Shearing in a Bar

[

;

AFS 145

; Ballad Index TrMe069

; Duke Tritton]

Martyn Wyndham-Read sang Shearing in a Bar

on his 1981 Fellside album
items.lyrics:
My shearing days are over, though I never was a gun,

I could always count my twenty at the end of every run.

I used the old ‚ÄúTrade Union‚ÄĚ shears, and the blades were always full

As I drove ‚Äôem to the knockers, and I clipped away the wool.

I shore at Goorianawa and didn‚Äôt get the sack;

From Breeza out to Compadore I always could go back.

And though I am a truthful man, I find when in a bar

My tallies seem to double, but I never call for tar.

Shearing on the western plains where the fleece is full of sand,

And the clover burr and corkscrew grass, is the place to try your hand.

For the sheep are tall and wiry where they feed on the Mitchell grass,

And every second one of them is close to the cobbler class;

And a pen chock full of cobblers is a shearers dream of hell,

So loud and lurid are their words when they catch one on the bell.

But when we‚Äôre pouring down the grog you‚Äôll have no call for tar,

For a shearer never cuts ‚Äôem when shearing in a bar.

At Louth I caught the bell sheep, a wrinkled, tough wooled brute,

Who never stopped his kicking till I tossed him down the chute.

My wrist was aching badly, but I fought him all the way;

Couldn‚Äôt afford to miss a blow; I must earn my pound a day.

So when I‚Äôd take a strip of skin I‚Äôd hide it with my knee,

Turn the sheep around a bit where the right bower couldn‚Äôt see,

Then try and catch the rousie√Ę¬Ä¬ôs eye and softly whisper, ‚Äútar‚ÄĚ;

But it never seems to happen when I‚Äôm shearing in the bar.

I shore away the belly wool and trimmed the crutch and hocks,

Opened up along the neck while the rousie swept the locks,

Then smartly swung the sheep around and dumped him on his rear

Two blows to clip away the wig‚ÄĒI also took an ear‚ÄĒ

Then down around the shoulders and the blades were opened wide

As I drove ‚Äôem on the long blow and down the whipping side.

And when the fleece fell on the board, he was nearly black with tar,

But this is never mentioned when I‚Äôm shearing in a bar.

Now when the season‚Äôs ended and my grandsons all come back,

In their buggies and their sulkies‚ÄĒI was always on the track‚ÄĒ

They come and take me into town to fill me up with beer,

And I sit on a corner stool and listen to them shear.

There‚Äôs not a bit of difference; it must make the angels weep

To hear a mob of shearers in a barroom shearing sheep,

For the sheep go rattling down the race with never a call for tar,

For a shearer never cuts ‚Äôem when he‚Äôs shearing in a bar.

Then memories come a-crowding and they wipe away the years,

And my hand begins to tighten and I seem to feel the shears.

I want to tell them of the sheds, the sheds where I have shorn,

Full fifty years and sometimes more, before these boys were born.

I want to speak of Yarragin, Dunlop or Wingadee,

But the beer has started working and I‚Äôm wobbling at the knee;

So I‚Äôd better not start shearing, I‚Äôd be bound to call for tar,

Then be treated as a blackleg when I‚Äôm shearing in a bar.

items.title: Shelter
items.notes:
Shelter

[Eric Bogle]

Danny Spooner sang Eric Bogle‚Äôs song Shelter

on his 2017 final CD,

Home .

He noted:

Eric Bogle is a much-loved singer songwriter who has over and over again

written down and sung what we know in our hearts. To be Australian today

is to be fortunate and gives us reason to welcome the homeless, the hungry,

the restless and the weary. (After a roud tour from southern NSW through

central NSW and on the Fleurieu Peninsula SA, the aptness of this song caused

Danny to call his album ‚ÄėHome‚Äô.)
items.lyrics:
Please find the lyrics for this song at
Eric Bogle‚Äôs website (PDF document).

Sorry, I can't show the lyrics here as I don't have the copyright
owners' permission to publish them.
But please feel free and ask me to send you the song's lyrics.

items.title: Sixteen Million People
items.notes:
Sixteen Million People

[Don Henderson]

Danny Spooner sang Don Henderson‚Äôs Sixteen Million People

on his 2007 CD of fairly contemporary Australian songs,

Emerging Tradition .

He noted:

I remember falling about myself when I first heard Don Henderson sing this

at the Troubadour in Sydney when Australia only had sixteen million population.

The experience of being mistaken for someone else might be common enough, but

Don‚Äôs exquisite sense of humour and imagination suggests endless possibilities.
items.lyrics:
Have you ever had the feeling, being introduced to someone,

You think that you‚Äôve already met.

But you really can‚Äôt be certain, ‚Äôcause the names aren‚Äôt familiar

But there‚Äôs something about the face you can‚Äôt forget.

And it turns out that really, after quite a bit of talking,

You went to kindergarten and such,

And the people that surround you, there‚Äôs only sixteen million

And sixteen million people isn‚Äôt much.

Well you walk into a bar and a bloke says, ‚ÄúG√Ę¬Ä¬ôday Charlie.‚ÄĚ

And you tell him that Charlie‚Äôs not yer name,

And he says that he is sorry but he thought yer name was Charlie,

But he reckons that he knows yer just the same.

And it turns out that his sister‚Äôs married to your uncle‚Äôs second cousin,

Yes, of course now he remembers you,

You were seated four rows down at the table in a grey suit

At the wedding in nineteen fifty-two.

Well, you‚Äôre at the country-dance and you‚Äôre dancin‚Äô with a stranger

To tell the truth you wouldn‚Äôt know from Eve,

But with faint heart and all that stuff you say, ‚Äú√Ę¬Ä¬ôaven√Ę¬Ä¬ôt we met somewhere?‚ÄĚ

And she says, ‚ÄúWhy yes! I do believe.‚ÄĚ

And it turned out that once you were on a train to Brisbane

And it didn‚Äôt have a dining car, don‚Äôt cry,

And she was the waitress at South Grafton Station

And you ordered black coffee and a pie.

Well, you are in the one horse town and the horse has long since bolted,

There‚Äôs nothing but a hotel and a jail,

And a copper and a publican and a liver-coloured kelpie

And the dog comes up to you and wags his tail.

Now it turns out that really the dog‚Äôs never met yer

Just thought that he‚Äôd come over and say hi!

But the copper and the publican, they reckon they both know yer

But they didn‚Äôt want to say so, they were shy.

items.title: The Antarctic Fleet
items.notes:
[Harry Robertson (1923-1995)]

Scottish born, Australian Folk singer Harry Robertson recorded his own

song The Antarctic Fleet in 1971 for his album

Whale Chasing Men .

Danny Spooner sang The Antarctic Fleet

on his 2002 CD

Launch Out on the Deep .

He noted:

Harry Robertson was born at Barrhead in Scotland and was a typical Scots

engineer, tough, resilient and capable. He was also a great singer/songwriter.

He spent time in Royal Naval tugs, tankers, whalers, and did time in the

graving docks of Brisbane. Like many of his songs, this one explores just a
items.lyrics:
Please find the lyrics for this song at
Harry Robertson‚Äôs website .

Sorry, I can't show the lyrics here as I don't have the copyright
owners' permission to publish them.
But please feel free and ask me to send you the song's lyrics.

items.title: The Apprentice Song
items.notes:
The Apprentice Song

[

; Mudcat 9308

; Ian Campbell]

Martyn Wyndham-Read sang Ian Campbell‚Äôs The Apprentice Song

in 1966 on his, Danny Spooner, Gordon McIntyre and Peter Dickie‚Äôs album

A Wench, a Whale and a Pint of Good Ale .

Danny Spooner and Duncan Brown sang The Apprentice Song

in 2016 on their CD of songs of the working life,

Labour and Toil .

The album‚Äôs liner notes commented:

Apprenticeship‚ÄĒthe commencement of the working life in most trades
items.lyrics:
Come on lad and bring your tool bag,

Keep your eyes peeled, use your head.

Fetch your footprints, spanners, chisels,

From now on they‚Äôll earn your bread.

Keep your eyes on the older fitters,

They‚Äôre the boys who know their stuff.

One day you will do their job

If you‚Äôre smart and keen enough.

Come on boy and take your place

Among the men who serve the trade.

Scalers, cokers, valvesmen, stokers,

This is where the gas is made.

Keep your eye on the old Fairweather gauge,

The gas that‚Äôs on the town.

Send it out bang on four fifty

Or else you‚Äôll let the housewife down.

Wake up, son, and mind your setting,

B-range is the one to watch.

Number three is due for scaling,

See she don‚Äôt get too much ash.

Mind your eye with that red hot poker,

Read your heat and see it‚Äôs right.

Leave your range in decent order

For the lads on shift tonight.

items.title: The Backblocks Shearer / Widgegoera Joe
items.notes:
[

;

AFS 96

; Ballad Index MA038

; DT BACKBLCK

; Mudcat 2810

; trad.]

According to the note of the Mudcat Caf√© lyrics of

The Backblocks Shearer ,

this song was written by W. Tully at Nimidgee, NSW,

and can be found in most Australian collections.

Martyn Wyndham-Read sang Widgegoara Joe
items.lyrics:
I‚Äôm only a backblocks shearer, as easily can be seen,

I‚Äôve shore in almost every shed in the plains of the Riverine.

I‚Äôve shore in most of the famous sheds, I‚Äôve seen big tallies done,

But somehow or other, I don‚Äôt know why, I never became a gun.

Chorus (after each verse):

Hurrah, my boys, my shears are set, I feel both fit and well;

Tomorrow will find me at my pen, when the gaffer rings the bell,

With Haydon‚Äôs patent thumb-guards fixed and both my blades pulled back.

Tomorrow I go with my siding blow for a century or the sack.

I‚Äôve opened up the windpipe straight, I‚Äôve opened behind the ear;

I‚Äôve practised every possible style in which a man can shear;

I‚Äôve studied all the cuts and drives of the famous men I‚Äôve met,

But I‚Äôve never succeeded in plastering up those three little figures yet.

As the boss walked down this morning, I saw him stare at me,

For I‚Äôd mastered Moran‚Äôs great shoulder-cut, as he could plainly see,

But I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve another surprise for him that√Ę¬Ä¬ôll give his nerves a shock ‚Äď

Tomorrow he‚Äôll find that I have mastered Pierce‚Äôs rang-tang block.

If I succeed as I hope to do, then I intend to shear

At the Wagga demonstration which is held there every year.

And there I‚Äôll lower the colours, the colours of Mitchell & Co.

Instead of Deeming, you will hear of Widgegoara Joe!

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings The Backblocks Shearer
versions.lyrics:
I‚Äôm only a backblocks shearer, mate, as easily can be seen,

Shore in most of the shearin‚Äô sheds on the plains of the Riverine,

I shore in most of the famous sheds, and I‚Äôve seen big tallies done,

But somehow or other, I don‚Äôt know why, I never became a gun.

Chorus (after each verse):

But hurrah, my boys, my shears are set, and I feel both fit and well,

Tomorrow you‚Äôll find me at my pen when the gaffer rings the bell.

With Hayden‚Äôs famous thumb-guards fixed and both o‚Äô my blades pulled back,

Tomorrow I‚Äôll go with a sliding blow, for the century or the sack.

I‚Äôve opened up the wind-pipe straight, I‚Äôve opened behind the ear,

I shore in every possible style in which a man could shear.

I studied all the cuts and drives of the famous men I‚Äôve met,

But I never managed to plaster up those three little figures yet.

Well, the boss came up this mornin‚Äô, and I saw him stare at me,

I‚Äôd mastered Morgan‚Äôs shoulder-cut, as he could plainly see.

But I‚Äôve another surprise for him, ‚Äôt will give his nerves a shock,

Tomorrow he‚Äôll see that I‚Äôve mastered Pierce‚Äôs rang-tang block!

And if I succeed as I hope to do, then I intend to shear

At the Wagga demonstration that‚Äôs held there every year,

And there I‚Äôll lower the colours, my boys, the colours of Mitchell & Co.,

And instead of Deeming you shall hear of Widgegoera Joe!

items.title: The Beggar / Jolly Good Ale and Old
items.notes:
[

; Master title: The Beggar

; Ballad Index ShH78

; VWML CJS2/9/1304

, RoudFS/S137451

; Bodleian

; DT BACK&SID

, BCK&SID2

; Mudcat 41977

; William Stevenson, c.1550-1575]

Maud Karpeles:

Cecil Sharp‚Äôs Collection of English Folk Songs
items.lyrics:
I cannot eat but little meat,

My stomach is not good;

But I do think that I can drink

With him that wears the hood.

Though I go bare, take ye no care

I am never cold;

I stuff my skin so full within

Of jolly good ale and old.

Chorus (after each verse):

Let back and sides go bare,

Both hand and foot go cold,

But belly, God send thee good ale enough,

Whether it be new or old!

I love no roast but a nut-brown toast,

And a crab laid in the fire.

My belly I stuff with the good brown ale,

Much bread I never desire.

No frost, nor snow nor wind, I trow

Can hurt me if it would,

While I am wrapped and thoroughly lapped

In jolly good ale and old.

I care right nought, I take no thought

For clothes to keep me warm;

Have I good drink, I surely think

That none can do me harm.

For truly then I fear no man,

Though they be never so bold,

While I am armed and thoroughly warmed

With jolly good ale and old.

So now let them drink till they nod and wink

Even as good fellows should do,

They shall not miss to have all the bliss

Good ale doth bring them to.

And all good souls that scour black bowls

And them hath lustily trolled

God save the lives of them and their wives

Whether they be young or old!

versions.title: Steeleye Span sing The Beggar
versions.lyrics:
I‚Äôd rather be a beggar than a king

Tell you the reason why:

A king can‚Äôt swagger, nor drink like a beggar,

Nor be half so happy as I.

Chorus (repeated after every other verse):

Let the back and side go bare

Let the hands and feet go cold

Give to the belly beer enough

Whether it be new or old

Sometimes we lie like hogs in a sty

In a flock of straw on the ground

Sometimes eat a crust that‚Äôs rolled in the dust

And are thankful it can be found.

Sometimes we call at a rich man‚Äôs hall

To beg for bread and beer.

Sometimes we‚Äôre lame, sometimes we‚Äôre blind,

Sometimes too deaf to hear.

From the hag and hungry goblin

That into rags would rend you

And the spirits that stand by the naked man

In the book of moons defend you

That of your five sound senses

You never be forsaken

Nor travel from yourselves with me

Abroad to beg your bacon

versions.title: Ninebarrow sing Back and Sides
versions.lyrics:
I would sooner be a beggar than a king

And I‚Äôll tell you the reason why

A king cannot swagger

Or walk like a beggar

Or be half as happy as I

Chorus (after each verse):

Let your back and sides go bare my boys

Feet and your hands go cold

But give to your belly, boys

Beer enough

Whether it be new or old

Oh, there‚Äôs a sixpence in my pocket lads

And I worked very hard for that

Oh there ain‚Äôt no Turk

That‚Äôs gonna make me work

When the beggin‚Äô is as good as that

Sometimes we call at a nobleman‚Äôs hall

To beg for bread and beer

Sometimes we are lame

Sometimes we are blind

Sometimes too deaf to hear

Sometimes we lie like hogs in a sty

Frost and snow on the ground

Sometimes eat a crust

That‚Äôs rolled in the dust

And be thankful for what we‚Äôve found!

items.title: The Bells of Rhymney
items.notes:
The Bells of Rhymney

[words Idris Davies, music Pete Seeger]

Pete Seeger sang Idris Davies‚Äô poem The Bells of Rhymney ,

set to his own music,

at a Ballads and Blues concert at St. Pancras Town Hall Theatre on

4 October 1959 that was released by Folklore Records in 1963 on the album

Pete Seeger in Concert .

The Ian Campbell Folk Group sang The Bells of Rhymney

in 1963 on their eponymous Transatlantic album,

This is the Ian Campbell Folk Group .

Canterbury Fair sang The Bells of Rhymney

on their eponymous 1977 album
items.lyrics:
Oh, what will you give me?

Say the sad bells of Rhymney.

Is there hope for the future?

Cry the brown bells of Merthyr.

Who made the mine owner?

Say the black bells of Rhondda.

And who robbed the miner?

Cry the grim bells of Blaina.

They will plunder willy-nilly,

Say the bells of Caerphilly.

They have fangs, they have teeth,

Shout the loud bells of Neath.

Even God is uneasy,

Say the moist bells of Swansea.

Oh, what will you give me?

Say the sad bells of Rhymney.

Throw the vandals in court,

Cry the bells of Newport.

All would be well if, if, if,

Say the green bells of Cardiff.

Why so worried, sisters, why?

Sing the silver bells of Wye.

Oh, what will you give me?

Say the sad bells of Rhymney?

items.title: The Black Ball Line
items.notes:
The Black Ball Line

[

; Ballad Index LxA489

; DT BLAKBALL

; Mudcat 40463

; trad.]

Joanna C. Colcord:

Songs of American Sailormen

W.B. Whall:

Sea Songs and Shanties

Ewan MacColl and chorus sang The Black Ball Line

on his and A.L. Lloyd‚Äôs Topic albums
items.lyrics:
I served my time in the Black Ball Line,

To me way hay ho row yah!

In the Black Ball Line I served my time,

Hoorah for the Black Ball Line!

The Black Ball ships they make good time,

With long clean runs and entrance fine.

Oh, that‚Äôs the line where you can shine,

That‚Äôs the line where I spent my prime.

Just take the trip to Liverpool,

To Liverpool that packet school.

Them Yankee sailors you‚Äôll see there,

With their red-top boots and short-cut hair.

Oh, around Cape Horn in the month of May,

Around Cape Horn is a bloody long way.

Around Cape Horn with the skys‚Äôls set,

Around Cape Horn and we‚Äôre wringin‚Äô wet.

Well, if we drown while we are young,

It‚Äôs better to be drowned than hung.

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings The Black Ball Line
versions.lyrics:
I served me time in the Black Ball Line,

Timme, way, ay, ay hoorah oh,

In the Black Ball line I wasted me prime,

Hoorah for the Black Ball Line.

Just take a trip to Liverpool,

To Liverpool that packet school.

The Yankee sailors ye‚Äôll see there

Wiv red-topped boots and short cut hair.

There‚Äôs Liverpool Pat wiv his tarpaulin hat

And Paddy McGee that packet rat.

Oh once there was a Black Ball ship,

That fourteen knots along could clip.

Around Cape Stiff wiv the mains‚Äôls set

Around Cape Stiff all wringing wet.

They‚Äôll carry yer through the frost and snow,

They‚Äôll take yer where the winds don‚Äôt blow.

When a Black Ball liner‚Äôs ready fer sea,

The sights on the fo‚Äôc‚Äôsls are funny to see.

There‚Äôs tinkers, fakirs, soljers all

On a Black Ball ship they‚Äôre sailors all.

So drink a health to the Black Ball Line,

Their ships are stout and their men are fine.

And when we git to New York Town,

We‚Äôll meet old Pat and drink till we drown.

items.title: The Bloody Red Hand
items.notes:
The Bloody Red Hand

[

; Mudcat 15638

; Randy Percy]

Danny Spooner sang Irishman Randy Percy‚Äôs song

The Bloody Red Hand

on his 1986 album

I Got This One From‚Ä¶ .

He noted:

Randy Percy is well known to folkies in Australia as the lad who ran the

Dan O‚ÄôConnell for years and a great job he did too. But, he also has a talent

with words, as can be heard in The Bloody Red Hand which he wrote in
items.lyrics:
Oh the lion of England is fierce I hear say

And the Yankee‚Äôs bald eagle makes a handsome display

But of all your fine symbols of any great land

You‚Äôll find none so awesome as the bloody red hand

In the days that‚Äôs gone by in this land of the saints

There is many a picture of greatness I paint

Where learning‚Äôs bright light like a beacon shone grand

But that light has been dimmed by the bloody red hand

Now fear stalks the streets there by day and by night

And children taught hatred soon learn how to fight

Before they are thirteen as bigots they‚Äôre damned

They too have been touched by the bloody red hand

There‚Äôs many brave deeds that by young men‚Äôs been done

And there‚Äôs many a mother who weeps for her son

For they answered the call and they‚Äôve taken their stand

For Ireland, for Ulster, for the bloody red hand

Oh this country of mine has no future but hate

So go down on your knees now before it‚Äôs too late

And pray for forgiveness of your fellow man

And in peace we‚Äôll live free of the bloody red hand

items.title: The Catalpa
items.notes:
The Catalpa

[

;

AFS 21

; Ballad Index FaE056

; trad.]

Danny Spooner sang

The Catalpa

on his 2004 CD of Australian songs of toil and reward,

‚ÄôArd Tack .

He noted:

This song refers to the escape of six Irish prisoners in 1876, assisted by
items.lyrics:
A noble whale ship and commander

Was called the Catalpa, they say,

Came out to Western Australia

And took six poor Fenians away.

Chorus (after each verse):

So come all you screw warders and jailers,

Remember Perth regatta day:

Take care of the rest of your Fenians

Or the Yankees will steal them away.

For seven long years they had served here

And seven long more had to stay,

For defending their country, Old Ireland,

For that they were banished away.

Well, you kept them in Western Australia

Till their hair it begun to turn grey,

When a Yank from the States of America

Came out here and stole them away.

And all the Perth boats were a-racing

And making short tacks for the spot,

But the Yank tacked into Fremantle

And he took the best prize of the lot.

The Georgette, armed with bold warriors,

Went out the poor Yanks to arrest.

But she hoisted her star-spangled banner,

Saying, ‚ÄúYou will not board me, I guess.‚ÄĚ

So remember those six Fenians colonial

And sing o‚Äôer these few verses with skill

And remember the Yankee that stole them

And the home that they left on the hill

And now they‚Äôre safe in Americay

And there they are able to cry,

‚ÄúHoist up the green flag and the shamrock,

Hurrah for old Ireland we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll die!‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Culler‚Äôs Lament
items.notes:
The Culler‚Äôs Lament

[lyrics Peter Cape, music Don Toms, c. 1962]

Martyn Wyndham-Read sang The Culler‚Äôs Lament

on his 1984 Greenwich Village album

A Rose From the Bush .

He commented in his sleeve notes:

This comes from the singing of Danny Spooner, another strong singer from

Australia, who in turn learnt it from the writer Peter Cape. It tells once

again of the disruptions to family life caused by men leaving their women at

home. But in this song the woman strikes back. The ‚Äėculler‚Äô

referred to is the man who goes to kill the deer. A ‚ÄėBlack Matai‚Äô

is not a rugby player, but a tree native to New Zealand.
items.lyrics:
What are you singing, black matai , black matai?

Snow‚Äôs on the tops and the fire‚Äôs burning down.

What are you singing, east wind in the matai?

Your love‚Äôs left the station , she‚Äôs gone to the town.

What are you chattering, tall mountain birches?

wind‚Äôs in the west, and the rain‚Äôs pelting down.

Flash floods are coming, I‚Äôm bound to keep moving,

But she‚Äôs gone from the station, she‚Äôs gone to the town.

The smell of these deer-skins, the weight of my rifle,

This eighty-pound pack that keeps dragging me down:

I‚Äôll get out of the mountains, get back to the sheep-yards,

But she‚Äôs gone from the station, she‚Äôs gone to the town.

Wind on the high tops, what are you calling?

There‚Äôs deer in the valley a thousand feet down.

You cry on the cols, and you shout on the ridges

That my love‚Äôs left the station and gone to the town.

What are you whispering, wind in the snow-grass?

Combing the tussocks, and smoothing them down?

My love‚Äôs hair was golden as snow-grass in summer,

But she‚Äôs gone from the station, she‚Äôs gone to the town.

items.title: The Diamantina Drover
items.notes:
The Diamantina Drover

[Hugh McDonald]

Redgum sang The Diamantina Drover ,

written by their singer and fiddle player Hugh McDonald,

in 1983 on their album

Caught in the Act .

It was produced by Trevor Lucas.

Another recording of this song was included in 2004 on their anthology

Against the Grain .

Steve Turner sang The Diamantina Drover

in 1984 on his Fellside album

Eclogue .
items.lyrics:
The faces in the photograph are fading

I can‚Äôt believe he looks so much like me,

It‚Äôs been ten long years today

That I left for old Cork station

Saying, ‚ÄúI won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt be back when the droving√Ę¬Ä¬ôs done.‚ÄĚ

For the rain never falls on the dusty Diamantina.

The drover finds it hard to change his mind.

For the years have slowly gone,

Like the drays from old Cork Station,

And I won‚Äôt be back when the droving‚Äôs done.

Well, it seems like the sun comes up each morning,

It sets it up, then takes it all away.

For the dreaming by the light

Of the campfire at evening

Ends with the burning light of day.

Sometimes I think I‚Äôll settle back in Sydney,

But it‚Äôs been so long, it‚Äôs hard to change your mind.

For the cattle trains go on and on,

the fences run forever‚ÄĒ

And I won‚Äôt be back when the droving‚Äôs done.

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings The Diamantina Drover
versions.lyrics:
Oh, the faces in the photograph have faded

And I can‚Äôt believe he looks so much like me,

For it‚Äôs been ten years today

Since I left for old Cork station

Saying, ‚ÄúI won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt be back √Ę¬Ä¬ôtill the droving√Ę¬Ä¬ôs done.‚ÄĚ

For the rain never falls on the dusty Diamantina.

For the years have surely gone,

Well, it seems like the sun gets up each morning,

Sometimes I think I‚Äôll settle back in Sydney,

And I won‚Äôt be back till the droving‚Äôs done.

items.title: The Flighty Tailor
items.notes:
The Flighty Tailor

[trad.]

Danny Spooner sang

The Flighty Tailor

on his 1987 album

When a Man‚Äôs in Love .

This track was also included in 2007 on his anthology

Years of Spooner .

He noted:

Translated from the Irish, this song evokes a multitude of responses to

betrayal. Pride is certainly hurt, but the loss of his love, his reason for

living, also diminishes the blacksmith‚Äôs skills. It should be remembered that
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Ding dong de dilly um, blower and nailer, (√ó3)

My wife‚Äôs gone away with a tailor.

I can‚Äôt mend a slan and I can‚Äôt mend a spade,

items.title: The Gift of Years
items.notes:
The Gift of Years

[Eric Bogle]

Danny Spooner sang Eric Bogle‚Äôs song The Gift of Years

on his 2017 final CD,

Home .

He noted:

Written by Eric Bogle in 1990 for the seventy fifth anniversary of the

landing at Gallipoli on 25 April 1915. The last remaining veterans were

invited to return to the battlefield. The title was prompted by a letter

which Eric had read from a Second World War veteran to his children.

He thanked his friends, comrades & family.
items.lyrics:
Please find the lyrics for this song at
Eric Bogle‚Äôs website (PDF document).

Sorry, I can't show the lyrics here as I don't have the copyright
owners' permission to publish them.
But please feel free and ask me to send you the song's lyrics.

items.title: The ‚ÄėJamestown‚Äô Homeward Bound
items.notes:
The ‚ÄėJamestown‚Äô Homeward Bound

[

; Ballad Index Colc133

; trad.]

Ye Mariners All (John Roberts, John Rockwell and Larry Young)

sang The ‚ÄėJamestown‚Äô Homeward Bound on their 2003 CD

The farmer‚Äôs heart with joy is filled

For three long years have passed away

Our long-felt wish has come at last

ūĚĄÜ And we√Ę¬Ä¬ôre homeward bound once more. ūĚĄá

To where the sky is clear as the maiden‚Äôs eye

ūĚĄÜ To your homes and liberty. ūĚĄá

items.title: The Loss of (Bob) Mahoney
items.notes:
The Loss of (Bob) Mahoney

[

; trad.]

Louis Killen sang

Lord Franklin

in 2003 on the Fellside anthology

items.lyrics:
Far outward bound one night on the deep

Off yon green Island, out far from here,

Where we lost Mahoney and his boat‚Äôs gear.

Here‚Äôs Cap‚Äôn Kennedy of Hobart Town,

And Cap‚Äôn Reynolds of high renown

And Cap‚Äôn Robertson and many, many more,

They‚Äôve long been cruising Macquarie‚Äôs shore.

They cruised east and they cruised west,

Round the South West Cape where they thought best,

But no tide nor tale could they see or hear,

Concerning Mahoney or his boat‚Äôs gear.

In Recherche Bay where the Black Whale blow,

The fate of Mahoney they all do know,

They say he‚Äôs gone like many, many more,

He left his home to return no more.

As we drew nigh unto Hobart‚Äôs shore

She was sobbing, sighing, saying, ‚ÄúPity Me,

I‚Äôve lost my brother young Bob Mahoney;

I‚Äôve lost my brother no more I‚Äôll see,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve lost my brother young Bob Mahoney.‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Lothian Hairst (Harvest)
items.notes:
[

; G/D 3:404

; Ballad Index Ord264

; trad.]

Rob Watt sang The Lothian Hairst

on the 1968 Topic album of songs and ballads from the Lowland East of Scotland,

Peter Hall commented in the album‚Äôs sleeve notes:

Before the days on mechanisation, workers would travel round the country,

hiring themselves out as harvesters, moving on to another farm as each job was

finished. Aberdeenshire workers are known to have gone as far afield as East

Anglia.
items.lyrics:
On August twelfth frae Aberdeen

We sailed in the Prince,

And safe arrived on Shawfield‚Äôs shore,

The harvest tae commence.

For six lang weeks the country roon

Frae toon tae toon we went,

And took richt weel wi‚Äô the Lothian chiels

And were aye richt weel content.

Oor gaffer, Willie Mathieson,

Frae the sweet Deeside he came;

The foreman was frae that same place,

And Logan was his name.

Well, we followed Logan on the point,

Sae weel he layed it doon,

And sae nimble he was what he lead

Owre mony‚Äôs thristle croon.

But my mate and me had little chance

For Logan‚Äôs watchful eye,

My mate and me had little chance

For Logan was sae sly.

He cleared the bothy every nicht

Before he went tae sleep,

And never one of them did leave

Sae well his rules did keep.

But farewell MacKenzie, Reid and Rose

And the rest o‚Äô the merry crew,

There‚Äôs Chalmers, Shepherd, Logan, Jock

And the Royal Stewarts too.

And as for me, a Hielan‚Äô chiel,

I would find nae better cheer

Than a Lothian lass and a weel-made bed

And a nicht‚Äôs as lang‚Äôs a year.

But come fill our glasses tae the brim

Before the boat does start;

And may we safely reach the shore

And all in friendship part.

versions.title: Geordie Murison sings The Lothian Hairst
versions.lyrics:
August twelfth fae Aiberdeen

We sailed upon the Prince,

Landed safe on Clifford‚Äôs fields,

The hairstin tae commence.

Six lang wiks the country roon,

Fae toon tae toon we went,

We teen richt weel tae the Lothian fare

And wis richt weel content.

Oor gaffer, Willie Mathieson,

Fae sweet Deeside he came;

Oor foreman cam frae that same airt,

An Logan wis es name.

I followed Logan on the pint,

Sae weel he layed it doon,

Ably he led oor squad

Ower monys a thrissle toon.

My mate and I we got nae chance

For Logan‚Äôs watchful eye,

And wi the lads we got nae sport

For Logan wis sae fly.

He cleared the bothy ivery nicht

Afore we gid tae sleep,

Niver did he een bit leave

Sae strict his rules did keep.

Fin he gets back tae Aiberdeen

He‚Äôll weel deserve a spree

For the herdin o us a sae weel

Fae the Lothian lads win free.

Noo the corn is cut

An we are on the pier,

Fareweel ye Lothian feather beds

An a the Lothian cheer.

Farewell MacKenzie, Reid an Rose,

And a the jovial crew,

Oor chaumer shepherd Lothian Jock,

And the Royal Stewart too.

O a the lads that‚Äôs in oor squad,

Tae tak them een bi een,

Commend me tae the Deeside lads,

Twis them that led the van.

I, masel a Deeside lass,

Wid sic nae better cheer

Than a Deeside lad in a Lothian bed

And a nicht as langs a year.

items.title: The Maid of Culmore
items.notes:
[

; Henry H687

; Ballad Index HHH687

; Mudcat 19653

; trad.]

Sam Henry‚Äôs Songs of the People

Sheila Stewart of Blairgowrie, Perthshire, learned

The Maid of Culmore

from her uncle, Donald MacGregor, and sang it

in September 1954 to Maurice Fleming.

This recording was included in 2011 on the Greentrax anthology

Songs and Ballads From Perthshire Field Recordings of the 1950s
items.lyrics:
From sweet Londonderry to fair London town

There‚Äôs no better harbour on earth can be found,

Where the children each evening do play around the shore

And the joy bells were ringing for the maid of Culmore.

Noo the first time I met her, oh she passed me by,

And the next time I met her she bid me goodbye.

But the last time I met her she grieved my heart sore

Sayin, ‚ÄúAdieu Londonderry and away from Culmore.‚ÄĚ

Noo if I had the power a storm I‚Äôd rise,

Fro the wind tae blow high and tae darken the skies.

Fro the wind tae blow high and the salt seas for tae roar

On the day that my love sailed away from Culmore.

Tae the north of America my love I‚Äôll go tae see,

‚ÄôTis there I know no-one and no-one knows me.

But if I do not find her I‚Äôll return home no more,

Like an eagle I‚Äôll wander for the maid of Culmore.

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings The Maid of C√ļil M√≥r
versions.lyrics:
From sweet Londonderry to fair London town

There is no finer fairground on the earth to be found,

Where people each evening there‚Äôs a playground and shore

To join in the dancing with the maids of C√ļil M√≥r.

Oh, the first time I saw her she passed me by,

And the next time I met her she bade me goodbye.

And the last time I saw her it grieved my heart sore

For she sailed down Lough Foyle and away from C√ļil M√≥r.

Oh if I had the power the storms for to arise,

I would blow the wind high and darken the skies.

I would blow the wind higher, make the waves for to roar

For taking my darling away from C√ļil M√≥r.

But now I‚Äôm bound to North Americay my love for to see,

And it‚Äôs there I‚Äôll know no-one and no-one knows me.

But should I not find her I will return home no more,

But like an pigrim I‚Äôll wander for the maid of C√ļil M√≥r.

versions.title: Kevin Dempsey sings The Maid from Coolmore
versions.lyrics:
From sweet Londonderry to fair London town

There‚Äôs no nicer harbour anywhere to be found,

Where the children each evening disappear on the shore

And they join in the dancing the maid from Coolmore.

The first time I saw her she passed me by,

And the next time I saw her she bid me goodbye.

And the last time I saw her she grieved my heart sore

As she sailed down Lough Foyle away from Coolmore.

I‚Äôll follow my love to North America

For it‚Äôs there I‚Äôll know no-one and no-one knows her.

Like a pigrim I‚Äôll follow the maid from Coolmore

And if I do not find her I‚Äôll go home no more.

If I had the power, storms I would rise,

I would blow the winds higher and darken the skies,

I would blow the winds stronger, cause the salt seas to roar,

On the day that my love sailed away from Coolmore.

(repeat first verse)

items.title: Miner‚Äôs Washing
items.notes:
Miner‚Äôs Washing

[John Warner]

Margaret Walters sang John Warner‚Äôs song Miner‚Äôs Washing

in 1992 on John Warner‚Äôs album

The Sea and the Soil

and in 1993 on their album

Pithead in the Fern .

Cloudstreet sang Miner‚Äôs Washing

on their 2006 CD

Dance up the Sun .

Danny Spooner sang The Miner‚Äôs Washing

in 2017 on his final CD
items.lyrics:
She came from Durham in 1899,

Married a laddie from the Coal Creek mine,

The finest lad that a girl could ever know,

Till he brought her his washing from the pit below.

Chorus (after each verse):

Scrubbing the miner‚Äôs clothes,

Scrubbing the miner‚Äôs clothes,

All piled up in a ghastly stack,

Heavy as lead, and smelly and black,

And oh the pain in her aching back,

Scrubbing the miner‚Äôs clothes.

Now yer Korumburra miner is a grimy sort of bloke,

So she slings in his duds for an all-night soak.

Takes a bar o‚Äô soap and grates it like a cheese,

And she chucks that in the bucket with his grubby dungarees.

She‚Äôs up in the morning at the break of light,

Copper for to fill and the fire to light,

Gives her man his crib while the water‚Äôs on the boil,

Then girds up her muscles for a day‚Äôs hard toil.

She drags ‚Äôem from the copper to the rinsing tub,

Pound ‚Äôem with the dolly, then it‚Äôs scrub, scrub, scrub.

Throw away the mucky water, do it all again,

Haul ‚Äôem through the wringer, and pray it doesn‚Äôt rain.

Beyond Kardella, the weather√Ę¬Ä¬ôs looking fine‚ÄĒ

She humps out the washing to the long clothes line,

You can bet when it‚Äôs pegged out and she‚Äôs heaved up the prop,

The rain starts falling and the wind‚Äôll drop.

Now all you lassies that to marriage are inclined,

Never wed a laddie from the Coal Creek mine,

A squatter might be surly, a merchant might be mean,

A banker might be boring‚ÄĒbut they√Ę¬Ä¬ôre easier to clean.

items.title: The Mines of Australia / Leaving Australia
items.notes:
[trad.]

Kate Burke and Ruth Hazleton sang The Mines of Australia ,

‚Äúcollected by Warren Fahey from Cyril Duncan, 1973, Queensland,‚ÄĚ

on their 2002 album

Swapping Seasons .

Danny Spooner sang The Mines of Australia

on his 2004 CD of Australian songs of toil and reward,

‚ÄôArd Tack .

He noted:

This music hall melodrama I got from Gordon McIntyre in the 1970s.

He believed that my cockney accent would suit it better than his Scots one.

I thanked him at the time and still do, it‚Äôs a poignant song.
items.lyrics:
I sailed to the west with a schoolmate of mine

And together we shared the hard toil.

It was hard times at home that caused us to roam,

No work for the sons of the soil.

So I bade me old father and mother goodbye,

And I said I‚Äôll not be long away;

For ten years have passed, fortune‚Äôs favoured at last

And I‚Äôm leaving Australia today.

I sailed to the west with a dear pal of mine,

Each having a share in one claim

And taking bad luck as it came with the rest,

And working on just the same

Til a cowardly blow struck my poor pal low,

Who struck him I never could tell;

But the share of his gold placed close to my heart

For mother and dear sister, Nell.

Well I‚Äôm going back to my dear old home

That‚Äôs far away over the sea

Right back to the scenes of my childhood

Where there‚Äôll be a welcome for me

Well many‚Äôs the year it has passed away

Since I left old England‚Äôs shore,

And may God speed the vessel that carries me back

To my dear old home once more.

(repeat first verse)

items.title: The Newgate Stone
items.notes:
The Newgate Stone

[

; Bodleian

; Mudcat 161683

; trad.]

Danny Spooner, accompanied by Mick Farrell, sang

The Newgate Stone

in 1978 on their album

Limbo .

He noted:

During one of his many visits to the Melbourne State Library in the

mid-1960s, George Black (a great singer and guitarist) found these words in
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

With your chisel so fine, tra la, tra la,

With your chisel so fine, tra la

When Claude Duval were in Newgate thrown,

He carved his name on the dungeon stone;

Quoth a dubsman who gazed at the shattered wall,

‚ÄúYou have carved your epitath, Claude Duval.‚ÄĚ

Duval were hanged, and the next who came

On the self-same stone inscribed his name;

Then quoth the dubsman with devilish glee,

‚ÄúTom Waters, your doom is the triple tree.‚ÄĚ

All in the dungeon lay Captain Bew,

Rumbold and Whitney, a jovial crew,

Each carved their name on the stone, and all

Suffered the fate of the bold Duval.

Over twenty highwaymen blithe and bold

Rattled their chains in that dungeon old;

Of all the number there escaped not one

Who carved his name on the Newgate stone.

items.title: The New Road
items.notes:
The New Road

[John Beavis]

Danny Spooner sang John Beavis‚Äô song The New Road

on his 2007 CD of fairly contemporary Australian songs,

Emerging Tradition .

He noted:

This is the work of Victorian singer and songwriter John Beavis. His

wonderfully crafted and succinct retelling of the life of Christ is carried

on a melody very reminiscent of J.S. Bach, and I reckon that the old

‚Äúkapellmeister‚ÄĚ would approve.
items.lyrics:
You who puzzle on the Saviour‚Äôs deeds,

Will you stop and listen where the New Road leads;

First born child of the refugees,

He was raised in Nazareth, schooled in charity,

And found salvation on His knees.

Manhood brought Him to the Jordan shore,

Where the Baptist shivered in the rags he wore;

Plunged his cousin in the pilgrim stream,

And the Dove descended and the Old Road ended

And the New Road wakened from a dream.

Red sun sinking over Galilee

Saw the stranger walking by an inland sea;

Four young fishermen around entwine,

For the New Road heading to a Canaan wedding

Where He turned the water into wine.

Thousands listened on the mountain slope,

As they dined on miracles and leaved in Hope;

Blind men followed with the light restored,

For the sightless Pharisees condemned as heresies

The wide-eyed workings of the Lord.

Alleluja, how the people cheer,

The palm leaves rustle as the King draws near;

Woe, Jerusalem, the truth you shun,

And your sins ensuing are your own undoing

Till your stones lie broken in the sun.

Thirteen gathered in an upstairs room,

As the high priest plotted at the Saviour‚Äôs doom;

Blood and body in the wine and bread,

Then He kissed His enemy in sweet Gethsemane;

Twelve hours later He was dead.

Mary wondered at the stone flung wide,

And the tomb rang hollow as she stepped inside;

Angels seated where the Christ was lain,

Bid her quit the prison for the Son had risen

And would speak in Galilee again.

Show by living what the Lord had done,

In the selfless giving of his only son;

Chart this passage to the last Amen.

For the climb is steady if the pilgrim‚Äôs ready,

The New Road reaches out again.

items.title: The Old Moke Picking on a Banjo
items.notes:
The Old Moke Picking on a Banjo

[

; Ballad Index Be022

; VWML CJS2/10/2960

; trad.]

Danny Spooner sang The Old Moke Picking on a Banjo

in 2009 on his album

Bold Reilly Gone Away .

He noted:

The Old Moke Picking on a Banjo probably originates from Negro

and Irish navvies working in the railway gangs in America. How it got to sea

is anyone‚Äôs guess. This version is for the capstans either raising the anchor
items.lyrics:
He bang, she bang, daddy shot a bear,

Shot him in the arse my boys and never turned a hair.

Chorus (after each verse):

We‚Äôre all from the railroad too-ra-loo

And the Old Moke‚Äôs pickin‚Äô on the banjo.

Hooraw! What the Hell‚Äôs the row,

We‚Äôre all from the railroad too-ra-loo,

We‚Äôre all from the railroad too-ra-loo

And the Old Moke‚Äôs pickin‚Äô on the banjo.

Pat get back, take in yer slack, heave away my boys,

Heave away my bully boys, why don‚Äôt yer make some noise.

Rock her boys, roll her boys, give her flamin‚Äô gyp,

Git the anchor out the mud and let the bastard rip.

Whiskey Oh! Johnny Oh! The mud hook is in sight,

‚ÄôTis a hell of a way to the gals that wait and the old Nantucket Light.

Out chocks, two blocks, heave the capstan round,

Fish that flamin‚Äô anchor up for we are outward bound.

Rock her boys, roll her boys, git that mains‚Äôl tout,

Watch her dip her scroll my boys as we roll out of port.

Fare-thee well you tow-rope girls we‚Äôll see you in a while,

If you give us an inch my gal, well we will take a mile.

The anchor it is catted home and we are bound away,

Bound away across the seas where all them fishes play.

items.title: The Pommy‚Äôs Lament
items.notes:
The Pommy‚Äôs Lament

[

; trad.]

Danny Spooner sang

The Pommy‚Äôs Lament

on his and Martyn Wyndham-Read‚Äôs 1989 Sandstock album

All Around Down Under .

Gael Shannon noted:

Another from Ron Edwards‚Äô collection

[ Great Australian Folksongs ].

Ron got it from a neighbour, ‚ÄėTiger‚Äô O√Ę¬Ä¬ôShane, who learned the

first bit when he was working on immigrant ships in the 1920s. He recited it
items.lyrics:
Hoorah for good old England, we‚Äôll soon be going there

This awful life and hungry strife we can no longer bear

We were pitched into the back blocks, we might just as well‚Äôve been dead

A chaff bag for our pillow and the ground our featherbed

We lived on tea and damper and sometimes kangaroo

Daytimes we were scorched to death, the night times frozen blue

But now at last we see a chance at getting from this hole

Hoorah for good old England where they‚Äôre dishing out the dough

You whinging lot of bastards, no bloody guts you got

I hope the bloody boat goes down and drowns the bloody lot

Since you‚Äôve been out in Aussie you‚Äôve done nothing else but moan

You‚Äôre just a whinging lot of bastards and you should have stayed at home

You thought you‚Äôd pick up nuggets in our bloody creeks

You thought you‚Äôd pick up sovereigns in our bloody streets

You thought you‚Äôd make your fortune after being out here a day

You‚Äôre just a whinging lot of bastards and you should have stayed away

Oh! I say, steady on, old chap!

items.title: The Rambler From Clare
items.notes:
The Rambler From Clare

[

; Ballad Index Wa059

; MusTrad MT150

; DT RMBLCLAR

; Mudcat 144088

; trad.]

Colm O Lochlainn:

More Irish Street Ballads

Sarah Anne O‚ÄôNeill sang The Rambler From Clare

on her and her brother George Hanna‚Äôs 1978 Topic album

On the Shores of Lough Neagh .
items.lyrics:
Well the first of my fortunes that ever was known,

I straight took my way to the County Tyrone

Where among the pretty fair maids they used me well there,

And they called me the stranger and the Rambler from Clare.

That‚Äôs when I enlisted in the town of Fermoy,

But with so many masters I could not comply;

I deserted next morning, the truth I declare,

And for Limerick Town went the Rambler from Clare.

I then took my way to the town of Tralee

Where I fell to courting sweet Sally McGee;

First I won her favour, then I left her there.

Now they too are searching for the Rambler from Clare.

But like a deserter my case I bewail.

I was captured and taken in the town of Rathkail,

Then to army headquarters I had to repair,

And in the black-hole lay the Rambler from Clare.

Well, I took off my cap and I made a low bow

In hopes that my colonel would pardon me now.

But the pardon he gave me was harsh and severe,

√Ę¬Ä¬ôTwas, ‚ÄúBind him, confine him, he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the Rambler from Clare.‚ÄĚ

Oh, my poor aged mother the tears filled her eyes

And likewise my brothers, their shouts reached the skies;

But ‚ÄúBrave boys,‚ÄĚ said my father, ‚ÄúYour arms now prepare

And bring home my darlin√Ę¬Ä¬ô the Rambler from Clare.‚ÄĚ

They assembled together in a harmonious throng

With their guns on their shoulders determined and strong.

Then their firing began, and I vow and declare,

They burst the jail doors and freed the Rambler from Clare.

We then marched along to the Barony of Forth

Where some of our heroes had camped long before;

We prepared to do battle, and the truth I declare,

Their chief commander was the Rambler from Clare.

But now I‚Äôve the title of a United Man

So I cannot stay here in my own native land;

So off to Americay I must repair

And leave all my friends in the sweet County Clare.

Farewell to my comrades wherever ye be,

Likewise to my darlin‚Äô sweet Sally McGee;

Our ship it is ready, the wind it blows fair.

‚ÄúOh, he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs gone, joy be with him, he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the Rambler from Clare.‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Rambling Beauty
items.notes:
The Rambling Beauty

[

; Master title: The Rambling Beauty

; Laws P12

; G/D 6:1213

; Ballad Index LP12

; VWML RoudFS/S159036

; Bodleian

; trad.]

Danny Spooner sang

The Rambling Beauty

on his 1978 album
items.lyrics:
Oh once I courted a fair young woman,

I courted her to be my own;

But she proved cruel unto her jewel,

The truth to you it shall be known.

A chain of gold I gave unto her,

Likewise a pretty wedding ring;

The chain of gold she did accept it,

But would not after wedding ring.

The chain of gold she hung around her neck

And ev‚Äôry morning were in my view;

She said, ‚ÄúBegone, you tarry sailor,

For I can get better lads than you.‚ÄĚ

I went unto her own dear mother,

A-thinking she would prove my friend.

But she proved cruel just like her daughter

And for the officer did send.

She cried, ‚ÄúYou dog, you have ruined my daughter,

So surely punished you shall be!‚ÄĚ

Six months and more on bread and water

In prison strong they confinded me.

My sentence past, I went to London city,

And my false love I chanced to meet;

I saw her down in some door a-begging,

And I myself now in high estate.

And when I spoke to this pretty woman,

She on her bended knees did fall,

Saying, ‚ÄúKind sir, I ask your pardon,

My cruel mother was my downfall.‚ÄĚ

I put my hand into my pocket,

And I pulled out one half-a-crown.

She says, ‚ÄúKind sir, that is better to me

Than unto you five-hundred pound.‚ÄĚ

Now with their tears this couple parted

And she lies now in Bedlam strong,

Confessing she is well rewarded

For doing this poor sailor wrong.

versions.title: Steve Turner sings The Rambling Beauty
versions.lyrics:
All you that love any rambling beauty,

I pray you by me a warning take.

I loved one more than was my duty

That made me suffer for her sake.

There was a farmer‚Äôs daughter Nancy,

And she was dressed in silk so fine,

For her I had the greatest fancy

I ever had for womankind.

On day I went to this rambling beauty

And offered me a wedding ring.

So scornfully she did refuse it‚ÄĒ

From me she would have no such thing.

‚ÄúBegone,‚ÄĚ said she, ‚Äúyou prating fellow!

How dare you come to trouble me!

Go wear a bunch of the cold green willow;

A wife for you I will never be.‚ÄĚ

Then she was wed to a handsome young man,

The greatest rover in all the town.

But soon he began to disregard her

And let her many‚Äôs the day alone.

Then after years I returned from foreign lands

And chanced this beauty to meet one day.

She seemed to be in a mean condition,

Where I was blessed in a thriving way.

I put my hand all in my pocket,

And gave her guineas two and three.

And said, ‚ÄúTake this from a former lover‚ÄĒ

You surely must remember me.‚ÄĚ

This set her sorrowful heart a-bleeding

But all of her sorrows were now too late.

She said, ‚ÄúLet all maids wherever dwelling

From me a sad, sad warning take:

‚ÄúCome all of you fair maids in every station.

A warning take by me I pray:

There‚Äôs many a dark and cloudy morning

Turns out to be a sunny day.

All of you fair maids wherever dwelling,

Be constant aye to a lover kind.

For blessed and happy is every maiden

That to her lover has a constant mind.‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Red Fox (Men of the Bush)
items.notes:
The Red Fox (Men of the Bush)

[Lynne Muir]

Danny Spooner sang Lynne Muir‚Äôs song

The Red Fox

on his 2007 CD of fairly contemporary Australian songs,

Emerging Tradition

and on his 2011 CD

The Fox, The Hare and the Poacher‚Äôs Fate .

He commented in his first album‚Äôs liner notes:

Lynne Muir wrote this great hunting song about her grandfather in 1986.

Geoff Muir had spent most of his life in the shadow of Hanging Rock in Victoria

and like most country-bred men he knew the best places in the area to fish,
items.lyrics:
Where the red fox runs we will hunt him down

We will chase him o‚Äôer the mountains ‚Äôtill the sun goes down,

Poor old fox, we mean no harm

But the fire‚Äôs in our blood and so we must follow far from the lights of home.

Chorus (after each verse):

For we‚Äôre men of the bush

And we‚Äôre part of the land

And we do not kill for pleasure

That we‚Äôd have you understand.

With the sun on our brow or the moonlight on our path,

We will follow the tracks of our fathers gone before.

We roam the plains and we‚Äôll set a rout

Be it fair or stormy weather we will seek and hunt him out,

Where the rabbit runs we will set our snare

But you must not think us heartless men or men who do not care.

For we do not thrill to the blood or the kill

But we live from the land and so we will eat from it when we can,

We‚Äôre tired old men on a worn-out trail

When the tables are turned, maybe the fox will be hunting for the man.

items.title: The Red-Haired Man‚Äôs Wife
items.notes:
The Red-Haired Man‚Äôs Wife

[

; Ballad Index OLoc097

; DT REDWIFE

, WIFERED2

; Mudcat 3439

; trad.]

Colm O Lochlainn:

Irish Street Ballads

Ray Fisher sang

The Red-Haired Man‚Äôs Wife

in 1982 on her Folk-Legacy album
items.lyrics:
O ye muses divine, combine and lend me your aid

To pen these few lines, for I find that my heart is betrayed

By a virgin most pure who is dearer to me than my life.

But from me she is flown and is known as the red-haired man‚Äôs wife.

O a letter I‚Äôll send with a friend down to the seashore

To let her understand I‚Äôm the man that does her adore.

And if she would but leave that slave I will forfeit my life;

She‚Äôd live like a lady and ne‚Äôer be the red-haired man‚Äôs wife.

I offered a favour and sealed it with my own hand.

She thus answered and said, ‚ÄúWould you lead me to break the command?

O take it easy, since nature can cause so much strive.

I was given away and will stay as the red-haired man√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wife.‚ÄĚ

May my life never end nor my yearning for passion abate

Till me and my darling lie as one ‚Äôneath the pleasant tree‚Äôs shade.

With no one to be near us, Save the black bird in the green leaves alone

And the red-haired man in his grave With his head ‚Äôneath a stone.

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings The Red-Haired Man‚Äôs Wife
versions.lyrics:
‚ÄôTis what they say, thy little heel fits in a shoe,

‚ÄôTis what they say thy little mouth kisses well too;

‚ÄôTis what they say, a thousand loves you‚Äôve left me to rue,

That the tailor went the way, that the wife of the red-haired man knew.

Many months did I spend in a prison closed and tightly bound,

Chains on my arms, and a thousand locks all around.

But I would leap as the leap of the lonely swan,

To be lying down beside the wife of the red-haired man.

Many times have I thought of one house between thee love and me,

And ofttimes have I thought of my child all there on your knee;

But a curse from the High One on him now let it be,

And on all the band of boys that put silence love, twixt thee and me.

In the garden grows a tree and her branches tremble and shake,

Place my hand on the bark and I feel my poor heart will break;

Just one wish alone in my soul it has run,

Just one little kiss, from the wife of the red-haired man.

But a day of judgement it will come, and the seas and mountains will be rent,

And a mist shall fall from the thunder-clouds so newly sent;

And the seas will run dry and the earth under mourning shall ban,

And beloved she will cry, the wife of the red-haired man.

versions.title: Kevin Mitchell sings The Red-Haired Man‚Äôs Wife
versions.lyrics:
Ye muses divine, combine and lend me your aid

To pen these few lines for I find that my heart is betrayed

By a virgin so pure who is dearer to me than my life

And from me she is flown and is known as the red haired man‚Äôs wife.

Remember the day when you gave unto me your true heart;

You solemnly swore that never no more we should part,

But your mind‚Äôs like the ocean, each motion has taken her flight,

And left me bewailing the tale of the red haired man‚Äôs wife.

A letter I‚Äôll send with a friend down to the sea-shore

To let her understand I‚Äôm the man that does her adore

And if she‚Äôd but leave that slave, I‚Äôd forfeit my life.

She‚Äôd live like a lady and ne‚Äôer be the red haired man‚Äôs wife.

I offered a favour and sealed it with my own hand.

She thus answered and said, ‚ÄúWould you lead me to break the command?

So take it easy, since nature has caused so much strife.

I was once given away and will stay as the red haired man√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wife.‚ÄĚ

May my life never end or my yearning for passion abate

Till I and my darling recline neath the pleasant trees‚Äô shade;

No one to be near us but the blackbird in the green leaves alone

And the red headed man in his grave with his head neath a stone.

My darling sweet Phoenix, if now you would be my own!

For the patriarch David had a number of wives, it‚Äôs well known.

So yield to my embraces and straight put an end to all strife

Or else I‚Äôll run crazy, or gain the red haired man‚Äôs wife.

items.title: The Sailor‚Äôs Alphabet
items.notes:
The Sailor‚Äôs Alphabet

[

; Ballad Index RcTSAlp

; MusTrad MT230

; Folkinfo 139

; DT SAILALPH

; Mudcat 57772

; trad.]

Captain Manus J. Bonner of Charlevoix, Quebec, sang

The Sailor‚Äôs Alphabet

to Ivan H. Walton in 1932; he said that he learned it

‚Äúwhen a hand before the mast in the 1870s‚ÄĚ.
items.lyrics:
A is the anchor of our gallant ship,

B is the bowsprit that in the seas dip,

C is the capstan so merrily goes ‚Äôround, and

D is the davits to which our boat‚Äôs bound.

Chorus (after each verse):

So merry, so merry, so merry are we,

No mortals on earth are as happy as we;

Hi derry, ho derry, hi derry down,

Give sailor boys rum and there‚Äôs nothing goes wrong!

E is the ensign at our masthead,

F is the fo‚Äôc‚Äôsle where is our bed,

G is the gun‚Äôl, against it seas splash,

H is the hawser that holds the ship fast.

I is the iron , without it we‚Äôre lost,

J is the jolly boat that rows us acrost

K is the keelson as I have been told, and

L is the lany‚Äôrd that keeps a good hold.

M is the mainmast so stout and so tall,

N is the nettings that hangs our hammocks all.

O is the oars we often do row, and

P is the pennant so lightly does flow.

Q is the quarterdeck on which our good captain stood,

R is the riggin‚Äô so stout and so good.

S is the steward that weighs our beef, and

T is the tops‚Äôls we oft have to reef.

U is the union to which our troubles pass,

V is the vang that holds steady the gaff.

W is the wheel by which we do steer, and

X, Y and Z are the rest of the gear.

versions.title: Sam Larner sings A Sailor‚Äôs Alphabet
versions.lyrics:
A stand for Anchor we carry on our bow

B stand for Bowsprit to bowl her along.

C for the Capstan which we heave around, and

D for our Duty, to which we are bound.

Chorus (after each verse):

Merry, cheerily, so merrily are we,

No mortal on earth like a sailor at sea.

Heave away haul away, hi-do-a-down,

Give a sailor his grog and there‚Äôs nothing go wrong.

E stand for Ensign, we fly at our Main,

F stand for Fo‚Äôcastle, belong to the man.

G stand for Galley, that Cook, he belong, and

H for our Halliards, so long and so strong.

I stand for Iron to which we are bound,

J stand for Jolly-boat, so safe and so sound.

K stand for Keelson, the keel which is from, and

L are the Lanyards so long and so strong.

M stand for Mainmast so long and so strong,

N stand for Northpoint which never point wrong

O stand for Owners that boats they belong, and

P stand for Pumps‚ÄĒwe all jog on.

Q stand for Quadrant which we take the sun,

R stand for Rudder the keel it ship on.

S stand for Standpost the rudder ship on, and

T for the Topsails, to pull her along.

U stand for Union, to which we do fly,

V stand for Venus, which we now pass by.

W for Wheel, in which we take time,

And the other three letters, they won‚Äôt come in rhyme.

versions.title: Johnny Doughty sings The Sailor‚Äôs Alphabet
versions.lyrics:
A‚Äôs for the anchor that swings at our bow,

B for the bowsprit through the wild seas do plough.

C for the capstan we merrily around,

D are the davits we lower our boats down.

Chorus (after each verse):

Sing high, sing low, wherever you go,

Give a sailor his tot and there‚Äôs nothing goes wrong.

Now E for the ensign that flies at our peak,

F is for the fo‚Äôc‚Äôsle where the good sailors sleep.

G for the galley where the cooks hop around,

H are the halyards we haul up and down.

Now I is the iron the ship is made of,

J for the jib which moves her along.

K is the keel at the boom of the ship,

L is the lanyards that never do slip.

Now M is the mainmast so neat and so strong,

N for the needles which never go wrong.

O for the oars we row our boats out,

P for the pumps that we keep her afloat.

Q for the quarterdeck Where officers do stand,

R is the rudder that steers us to land.

S for the sailors which move her along,

T for the topsails we pull up and down.

U for the union which flies from our peak,

V for the viles which the sailors do eat.

W for wheel where we all take our turn,

X, Y, Z is the name on our stern.

versions.title: Harold Smy sings The Bargeman‚Äôs Alphabet
versions.lyrics:
Now A is for the anchor that hangs on our bow

And B is for the bowsprit which we ships in our bow

C is for the galley where the cook runs around

And D are for the davits which we merrily run round.

Chorus (after each verse):

So merrily, so merrily so merrily are we

No mortal on earth like a bargemen at sea

Blow high or blow low as we‚Äôre sailing along

Give a bargemen his beer and there‚Äôs nothing goes wrong.

E is for the ensign that fly at our peak

And F is for the fo‚Äôc‚Äôsle where the sailors do sleep

G is for the galley where the cook runs around

And H are the halyards all numbered and told.

I is for the irons all numbered and told

And J is the for the jib that we often let rip

K is for the cask at the bottom of our hold

And L is for the lamps all numbered and told.

M is for the mast, so stout and so strong

N is for the needle that never point wrong.

O is for the oars we all pull in each boat

And P is for the pumps that keep us afloat.

Q is for the quarter-deck where the captain do stand

And R is for the rudder that steer us along

And S is for the sails that run at our fore

T is for the topsail that pull at our fore.

U is for the Union Jack that fly at our door

And F is the baying at our fore

And W is the wheel, we all take our turn

And X,Y and Z is the name on our stern.

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings The Sailor‚Äôs Alphabet
versions.lyrics:
A for the anchor we carry at our bow,

B for the bowsprit to bowl her along,

C for the capstan, the which we walk round,

D for the davits to lower our boats down.

Chorus (after each verse):

Merrily, cheerily, so merrily are we,

No mortal on earth like a sailor at sea.

Heave away, haul away, the ship rolls along,

Give a sailor his grog and there‚Äôs nothing goes wrong.

E‚Äôs for the ensign that flies at our peak,

F for the fo‚Äôc‚Äôsle belongs to the men,

G for the galley, the salt-horse smells strong,

H for the halyards that we hauls with a song.

I is for Imez to which we are bound,

J for the jolly-boat to run us around,

K for the keelson so stout and so strong,

And L is for lanyard that allus holds long.

M for the mainmast so stout and so strong,

N for the needle that never points wrong,

O for the oars in our jolly-boat,

P for the pinnace that always will float.

Q for the quarter, the capstan we box,

R for the rudder keeps us from the rocks,

S for the stuns‚Äôls to make up some time,

T for the tops‚Äôl where the topmen they climb.

U is for uniform, only wore aft,

V for the vangs running from the main gaff,

W for the wheel at which we spend time,

The last three letters they won‚Äôt come in rhyme.

items.title: The Sailor‚Äôs Hymn
items.notes:
The Sailor‚Äôs Hymn

[

RoudBS B97181

; William Whiting]

Danny Spooner sang The Sailor‚Äôs Hymn

in 2014 on his CD

Sailor‚Äôs Consolation .

He noted:

Written by William Whiting (1825-78), this hymn is always sung at the

blessing of the fishing fleets in Britain. It is always a very moving

ceremony.
items.lyrics:
Eternal Father, strong to save,

Whose hand does bind the restless wave,

Who bidst the mighty ocean deep

Its own appointed limits keep;

Oh, hear us when we cry to Thee,

For those in peril on the sea!

Oh savior whose almighty word

The winds and waves submissive heard,

Who walkedst on the foaming deep,

And calm amidst its rage did sleep;

Oh, hear us when we cry to Thee,

For those in peril on the sea!

Oh Sacred Spirit who didst brood

Upon the chaos dark and rude,

Who badst the angry tumult cease,

And gavest light and life and peace;

Oh, hear us when we cry to Thee,

For those in peril on the sea!

Oh Trinity of love and power!

Our brethren shield in danger‚Äôs hour;

From rock and tempest, fire and foe,

Protect them wheresoe‚Äôer they go;

And ever led there rise to thee

Glad hymns of praise from land and sea.

items.title: The Song of the Sheetmetal Worker
items.notes:
The Song of the Sheetmetal Worker

[John Dengate]

Danny Spooner sang John Dengate‚Äôs Song of the Sheetmetal Worker

on his 2007 CD of fairly contemporary Australian songs,

Emerging Tradition .

He noted:

John Dengate‚Äôs songs run the gamut of human experiences; often humorous and

biting he has long been a chronicler of the Australian political and social

experience. Proud of his Irish and working class background, John has combined

the two in this tribute to his father, written in 1974. John‚Äôs haunting words

are carried on the Irish Air for the Valley of Knockanure .
items.lyrics:
Oh, when I was a boy in Carlingford

All sixty years ago,

The eucalypts grew straight and tall

And the creeks did sweetly flow.

But times was hard when the old man died

And the orchard would not pay,

So I left the land for the fact‚Äôry bench

And I‚Äôm working there still today.

I‚Äôve earned my bread in the metal shops

For forty years or more,

My hands are hard and acid-scarred

As the boards on the workshop-floor,

My soul is sheathed in Kembla steel

And my eyelids have turned to brass.

And the orchard‚Äôs gone, and the apple trees

Where the wind whispered through the grass.

The workbench is my altar

Where I come to take the host,

Copper, brass and fine sheet steel‚ÄĒ

Father, Son and Holy Ghost.

The sacramental wine of work

Grows sour upon my tongue.

Oh, the fruit was sweet on the apple trees

When my brothers and I were young.

items.title: The Tailor‚Äôs Breeches / The Beggar
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS958

; Master title: The Tailor‚Äôs Breeches

; TYG 49

; Ballad Index PuMB087

; DT TAILRBR

; Mudcat 72395

; trad.]

Steve Gardham:

A Yorkshire Songster

Mary and Nigel Hudleston:

Songs of the Ridings: The Yorkshire Musical Museum
items.lyrics:
Come ladies and gentlemen if you listen unto me

I‚Äôll sing you a song of the north country.

In a village near to Whitby town a tailor once did dwell,

and women, wine and company he loved them right well.

A dance on New Year‚Äôs evening the Tailor did attend,

I‚Äôm sure that he would ne‚Äôer of gone, if he could have seen the end.

Ah, the poor little tailor, he will ne‚Äôer forget that night,

For never yet was tailor seen in such a sorry plight.

O he danced and he sang, he got whiskey many a tot.

Your jolly little tailor was the merriest of the lot,

The lady he was dancing with, the tailor he declared,

‚ÄúIf you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll lend to me your petticoats, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll dance like a maid.‚ÄĚ

O his britches he put off and her petticoat put on,

The maid the tailor‚Äôs britches she quickly did adorn,

O, the fiddler did play to them a merry, merry tune,

She danced his money, watch and britches clean out of the room.

‚ÄúO bring me my britches back‚ÄĚ, the tailor loudly called,

‚ÄúO, bring me my britches back, me money, watch and all.‚ÄĚ

O, the company there assembled with laughter they did roar.

When the little tailor‚Äôs petticoats fell down upon the floor.

‚ÄúO Lord‚ÄĚ, said the tailor, ‚Äúwherever thou may be,

O Lord‚ÄĚ, said the tailor, ‚Äúhave pity on me!‚ÄĚ

What to do the little tailor he really did not know,

O his little shirt was far too short to cover all below.

‚ÄėTwas then the fiddler played a tune for all that he was worth,

The tune he played the tailor was the famous Cock of the North

All the ladies were delighted and they loudly shouted ‚ÄúNo!‚ÄĚ

When the tailor took his trilby hat to cover Uncle Joe.

When at last the little tailor got out into the street,

A bevy of fair damsels he chanced for to meet,

All the ladies screamed with laughter when the tailor did appear,

They wished him a merry Christmas and a happy new year.

The poor little tailor those ladies did address.

Says he, ‚ÄúIt is not ladylike to laugh at a man√Ę¬Ä¬ôs distress.‚ÄĚ

Says the ladies to the tailor, ‚ÄúGive us no more of that,

If you call yourself a gentleman why don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you raise your hat?‚ÄĚ

In that village near to Whitby town there‚Äôs old men living yet,

They‚Äôll tell you of that famous dance they never will forget,

Old ladies too will tell to you the dance they loved the best,

Was the dance the little tailor showed his cuckoo‚Äôs nest.

Now that jolly little tailor from that day to this,

0 women, wine and company he gave them all a miss,

At a dance the little tailor they never more did catch,

Since the ladies pinched his breeches, his money and his watch.

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings The Tailor‚Äôs Breeches
versions.lyrics:
Well, it‚Äôs of a brisk young tailor and a story I‚Äôll relate.

He lived at an inn called The Ram and the Gate

The Ram and the Gate were the place where he did dwell

And wine and women‚Äôs company he loved exceeding well.

Oh well, oh well, oh well my boys, oh well,

And wine and women‚Äôs company he loved exceeding well.

Well, this tailor had been drinking a glass or two of wine;

Not being used to drinking, it caused his face to shine.

It caused his face to shine like the rising of the sun,

He swore he‚Äôd have a pretty maid before the night were done.

Were done, were done, etc.

Well, he got himself a pretty maid, he called her his dear honey;

But while they were talking, she was fingering of his money.

Fingering of his money, when the tailor laughed and said

‚ÄúIf you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll lend me your petticoats, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll dance like a maid.‚ÄĚ

A maid, a maid etc.

So the tailor took his breeches off and the petticoats put on,

The tailor danced a dance and the maid she sang a song;

The tailor danced a dance and they played a pretty tune

They danced the tailor‚Äôs breeches right out of the room.

The room, the room, etc.

‚ÄúWell, were ever a poor tailor been done as I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been done?

My gold watch and my money and my breeches are all gone

And if ever I go out like this, they√Ę¬Ä¬ôll call me ‚Äėgarden flower‚Äô,

And if ever I get my breeches back I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never dance no more.‚ÄĚ

No more, no more, etc.

versions.title: John Roberts and Debra Cowan sing The Tailor‚Äôs Breeches
versions.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of a brisk young tailor, a story I‚Äôll relate.

He lived at an inn called The Ram and the Gate

The Ram and the Gate was the place where he did dwell

And wine and women‚Äôs company he loved exceeding well

So well, so well, so well my boys, so well.

And wine and women‚Äôs company he loved exceeding well.

One evening this young tailor had been drinking a jug of wine

Not being used to so much drink, it caused his face to shine

It caused his nose to shine like the rising sun

And he swore he‚Äôd have a bonny lass before the night was done

Was done, was done, etc.

So he took her in his arms and he called her his dear honey

While they was a-talking, she was fingering of his money.

Fingering of his money, when the tailor smiled and said

‚ÄúIf you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll lend me your petticoats, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll dance like a maid.‚ÄĚ

A maid, a maid etc.

So the tailor took his breeches off and the petticoats put on

The tailor danced a dance while the lady sang a song;

The tailor danced a dance while she played a pretty tune

She waltzed the tailor‚Äôs breeches right out of the room.

The room, the room, etc.

‚ÄúOh, was ever a poor tailor undone as I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm undone?

My watch and my wallet and my breeches are all gone

If ever I dare go out, they√Ę¬Ä¬ôll call me ‚Äėgarden flower‚Äô

And if ever I get my breeches back I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll never dance no more.‚ÄĚ

No more, no more, etc.

Your jolly little tailor was the merriest of the lot.

To the lady he was dancing with, the tailor he declared,

His britches he put off and her petticoat put on.

‚ÄúBring me my britches back‚ÄĚ, the tailor loudly called,

‚ÄúLord‚ÄĚ, said the tailor ‚Äúwherever thou may be‚ÄĚ,

‚ÄôTwas then the fiddler played a tune for all that he was worth,

The tune he played the tailor was the famed ‚ÄėCock of the North‚Äô

The ladies were delighted and they loudly shouted ‚ÄúNo!‚ÄĚ,

When the tailor took his trilby hat to cover Uncle Joe.

When at last the tailor got out into the street,

A bevy of fair damsels he chanced for to meet.

That poor little tailor those ladies did address,

Says he, ‚ÄúIt is not ladylike to laugh at a man√Ę¬Ä¬ôs distress.‚ÄĚ

Says the ladies to the tailor, ‚ÄúGive us no more of that,

If you call yourself a gentleman, why don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you raise your hat?‚ÄĚ

In that village near to Whitby town there‚Äôs old men living yet,
