items.title: The Wallaby Track
items.notes:
The Wallaby Track

[

; Ballad Index MA186A

; trad.]

According to Gwenda Beed Davey and Graham Seal,

A Guide to Australian Folklore , Kangaroo Press, 2003, p. 264,

‚ÄúTo ‚Äėgo on the wallaby‚Äô or ‚Äėon the wallaby track‚Äô

was a colonial phrase for taking to the road in the manner of a swagman,

usually in search of work.‚ÄĚ

Dave de Hugard sang The Wallaby Track

in 1986 on his Larrikin album

The Magpie in the Wattle .
items.lyrics:
One morning I rolled up the few things I‚Äôd got,

Strapped on my saddle my quart and pint pot.

Told my old boss that I might not be back,

I was off for a trip on the wallaby track.

Well, the morning was fine, though it blew rather cold

And the sun was just topping the mountains with gold.

And my favourite old dog, running close to my back,

He knew we were off on the wallaby track.

Chorus (after each verse):

With the tooraleye, ooraleye, ooraleye ay,

Whack fol the diddle ay, tooraleye day

We‚Äôd a fair way to go to an old camping place

So we rattled along at a pretty smart pace.

There were friends we would meet if the tucker got slack,

They all live quite handy to the wallaby track.

But we hadn‚Äôt gone far, a few mile I suppose,

When I met a young woman and she said, ‚ÄúG√Ę¬Ä¬ôday Joe.‚ÄĚ

I said, ‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre mistaken, my name it is Jack,

And I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm off for a trip on the wallaby track.‚ÄĚ

She said, ‚ÄúGet down off your horse and rest yourself now.

Have you seen on your travels my old Poley cow?

She was the one that we used to call Black,

But I think she√Ę¬Ä¬ôs gone off on the wallaby track.‚ÄĚ

So I got off of my horse and I patted my dog

And together we sat on the stringy-bark log.

I lit the fire and I ratted the pack,

And we all had a feast on the wallaby track.

She was a fine looking girl, pretty clever and neat,

And a thump in my chest, well, fair had me beat.

She said, ‚ÄúWhere d√Ę¬Ä¬ôyou come from? When will you be back?

And why are you off on the wallaby track?‚ÄĚ

Well I told this young woman I was searching a wife,

And how could she fancy a partner for life?

She gimme a wink and she said, ‚ÄúThat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a whack!‚ÄĚ

Put the end of my trip on the wallaby track.

items.title: The Waterwitch
items.notes:
The Waterwitch

[trad.]

Danny Spooner sang The Waterwitch

on his 2006 CD of songs of the whaling industry,

The Great Leviathan .

He noted:

From the singing of a Mr Jack Davies of Hobart, this is similar to

The Coast of Peru

and

New Zealand Whales .

It recalls the days when Tasmanian whalers hunted the Southern Right Whale

from the Derwent across the Tasman sea. I got the words originally from
items.lyrics:
A neat little packet from Hobart set sail

To cruise the wide oceans for the monster sperm whale;

To cruise to the wide oceans where the stormy winds blow,

Bound away in the Waterwitch to the west‚Äôard we‚Äôll go.

Chorus (after each verse):

Bound away, bound away, where the stormy winds blow

Bound away in the Waterwitch to the west‚Äôard we‚Äôll go.

Oh ‚Äôtwas early one morning just as the sun rose

The man from her mast head cries out, ‚ÄúThere she blows!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhere away?‚ÄĚ cries our skipper and springing aloft,

‚ÄúThree points off yer lee bow and scarce three miles off.‚ÄĚ

We sailed off the west wind and came up a pace,

The whale boats was lowered and set for the chase,

Get yer lines in the boats see yer box line is clear

And lower her down boys and after him steer.

We fought him alongside, the harpoon thrust in,

In just over an hour he rolled out his fin.

The whale was cut in boys, tried out and stowed down,

He‚Äôs worth more to us, boys, than five hundred pound.

When the ship she gets full boys to Hobart we‚Äôll steer

Where there‚Äôs plenty of pretty girls and plenty good beer,

We‚Äôll spend our money freely with the girls on the shore,

And when it‚Äôs all gone go a-whaling for more.

items.title: The Wounded Whale
items.notes:
The Wounded Whale

[

; Ballad Index SWMS023

; Mudcat 99752

; trad. / Archie Fisher]

Joanna C. Colcord:

Songs of American Sailormen

Archie Fisher sang

The Wounded Whale

in 1976 on his Folk-Legacy album

The Man With a Rhyme .

He noted:
items.lyrics:
Lo, as the sun from her ocean bed was rising,

Broad on the water her glittering light throws,

Hark, from the masthead our lookouts are crying:

‚Äú√Ę¬Ä¬ôTis hard on your lee beam, a whale, thar she blows‚ÄĚ

Call up your sleepers, your larboard and starboardmen;

Main yard aback and your boats clear away,

For hard on our lee beam see the white water gleam,

Glittering and foaming in glorious array.

See the Leviathan in vastness is lying,

Making the ocean her sumptuous bed,

While high overhead the sea birds are flying,

Combing the billows that break o‚Äôer her head.

High, wide and swimming, her dark flukes are flying;

Stately, but slowly, she sinks in the main.

Peak all your oars awhile, rest from your weary toil,

Watching and waiting her rising again.

Row, hearties, row for the pride of your nation;

Spring to your oars, let the reeking sweat flow!

Now for the blood, let it have circulation,

Forward on your thwarts, give away all you know.

See how the boats advance, gaily, as to a dance,

Floating like feathers over the dark blue sea.

Stand up and give her some, send both your irons ho me.

Stern off and trim your boat; we are all clear.

Wounded and sore, fins and flukes in commotion,

Blackskin and oars contending the spray,

While loud, long and shrill blows the horn of the ocean.

Fretting and lost, she brings to in dismay.

Haul line, every man, and gather in all you can.

Lances and spades from your thwarts clear away.

Now peak your oars again while fast each boat remains,

For, safely and surely, we hold her at bay.

Surrounded by foes, with strength undiminished,

Heed how she flashes her dark flukes in the air!

A lance in the life, and the struggle is finished;

See how she sinks with her chimney on fire.

While so loud and shrill are the cries from our seamen,

Mocking the whale in her terrible hour.

Watch her as she dies; see, the blue signal flies.

Here she goes ‚Äúfin out‚ÄĚ. The contest is o√Ę¬Ä¬ôer.

versions.title: Danny Spooner sings The Wounded Whale
versions.lyrics:
Low as the sun from the ocean bed is rising,

Clear on the waters a glimmering light throws;

Now from the masthead the lookouts are crying,

‚ÄúClear on yer lee-beam, a whale, thar she blows.‚ÄĚ

Wake all your sleepers, your larboard and your starboard men,

Main-yard a-back, and yer boats clear away,

Hard on our lee-beam, see the wide waters gleam,

Foaming, and billowing, in glorious array.

Now see old leviathan in vastness a-lying

Making of the ocean a sumptuous bed,

While high above him the seabirds are flying

Combing each billow that he breaks with his head.

High, wide and swimming, great flukes gently driving,

Stately and slowly he sinks in the main.

Now peak your oars awhile, rest from your weary toil,

Watching and waiting his rise once again.

Now row hearties row for the pride of your nation,

Lean on yer oars and let the reeking sweat flow.

Now for the blood let it have circulation,

Forward, on your thwarts, give way all ye know.

Now see how our boats advance, bravely and dancing

Flying like feathers over the dark blue main,

Harpooner, stand, give him some, send both your irons home.

Stern all, trim the boat, let the line run free.

Gallied and sore, fins and flukes in commotion,

Black skin and boats contending the spray,

While loud and shrill screams the horn of the ocean,

Wounded and lost, he brings to in dismay.

Now, haul line every man, gather in all you can,

Lances and spades from the thwarts clear away;

Now peak your oars again while fast each boat remains,

Safely and surely, we‚Äôll hold him at bay.

Wounded and lost but with strength undiminished,

See how he lashes his great flukes in the air,

But a lance in the life and the struggle is finished,

See now he sinks, with his chimney on fire.

Now loud and shrill hear the cries of our sailors,

Mocking the whale in his terrible hour,

Watch him as he dies, see the blue signal flies.

Here he goes, ‚Äúfin out,‚ÄĚ the contest is o√Ę¬Ä¬ôer.

items.title: Thirty Shillings a Month
items.notes:
Thirty Shillings a Month

[Martin McKenna]

Danny Spooner sang Martin McKenna‚Äôs song Thirty Shillings a Month

in 2017 on his final CD,

Home .

He noted:

Martin McKenna is interested in colonial history and makes up his songs

singing aloud on the farm. His song was the winner of the

Monster Meeting Song Award. The brain child of The Chewton Domain Society

and Jan Wositzki in 2012, it produced a great two-CD album and a show performed

by the song-writers at the Castlemaine Theatre Royal. The Monster Meeting was

called in December 1851 to protest against the proposed increase to the Miners‚Äô
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Thirty shillings a month to dig for gold,

In the dust and the heat and the rain and the cold.

While the squatter pays, I understand,

Around the same for a swag of lamb.

For ten quid a year they give him the vote‚ÄĒ

He gets a say in what we have to pay;

Then they lock up the land from folks such as us:

Time to stand up for what‚Äôs right and what‚Äôs just.

Some diggers strike riches but most do it tough

Now the government claims that we don‚Äôt pay enough.

So they‚Äôll double the fee, it‚Äôs easy you see,

To keep us in chains while the squatter rides free.

When we came to this land we hoped we‚Äôd find

The old Master and Servants Act left far behind.

But the gentry and crown they were eager to see

Us kept in our place, so they‚Äôll double the fee.

So come gather, boys, at Forest Creek,

Time to be bold, there‚Äôs no place for the meek.

We‚Äôll rally for justice and what is our right:

Victory is ours if the diggers unite.

items.title: Thirty-Ton Line
items.notes:
Thirty-Ton Line

[Don Henderson]

Danny Spooner sang Don Henderson‚Äôs Thirty-Ton Line

on his 2007 CD of fairly contemporary Australian songs,

Emerging Tradition .

He noted:

When Don Henderson died on 20 August 1991, Australia lost one of its most

prolific song makers. He was committed to the betterment of working conditions

and a more peaceful world, and this song, written in 1979, reminds us that the

lives of workers are constantly put at risk by employers seeking the cheaper

option. As an ex-tug skipper the song evokes many memories for me.
items.lyrics:
Purpose-built tugs with line boats attending

Berth big, bulk coal-carriers in open seas,

To fulfil that function, the union contended

Required four deckhands, Fenwick‚Äôs said three.

Three deckhands and a motorman just couldn‚Äôt handle

Sixteen inch polyprop double dead-eyes.

When the tow-hook was blacked, the company gambled

On a tension winch, ten inch calm sea compromise.

Chorus (after each verse):

Broadsound, Belyando, Nebo, Serina

The sea snaps your hawsers like thin strands of twine.

Broadsound, Belyando, Nebo, Serina

Hundred-tons bollard-pull, thirty-ton line.

At two in the morning they‚Äôve made fast the Martha,

At nine the Academy Star had been berthed;

Then all tugs and line-boats returned to the harbour

Their work being finished, the crews they dispersed.

But at five the same evening storm warnings were sounding

Cyclone approaching, no time for delay,

At their berths the big bulkies were taking a pounding,

Broadsound and Belyando must get them away.

With all engines driving the two tugs went pounding,

Got lines in the Martha on wharf number two,

Though twelve foot green water on their decks was crashing

The order ‚ÄúUse maximum power‚ÄĚ had come through.

With the whole hull vibrating and the tension winch screaming

Then came the moment that all tug-men dread,

The sudden lurch forward and the broken line whipping

The thoughts of old ship mates, the injured, the dead.

The Martha was cleared just as our line had parted,

The Academy Star was on wharf number one,

Though the help we could offer was nought but a token

In her state, that help would be better than none.

But time and again we tried to position

To get her away with all possible speed,

But with jury-rigged lines and in such bad conditions,

Three deckhands and a motorman couldn‚Äôt succeed.

Only part loaded, riding high in the water,

The Academy Star could not be controlled,

For the strong on-shore wind had her hard on the quarter,

She slammed at the pylons ‚Äôtill her hull had holed.

But still Utah‚Äôs bosses and those who do their will,

Put tugs to sea short on gear, undermanned;

One million dollars will be the repair bill,

They‚Äôd pay that in preference to one more deckhand.

items.title: Three Jolly Huntsmen / Three Jolly Sportsmen
items.notes:
[

/ Song Subject MAS130

; Master title: Three Jolly Huntsmen

items.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of three jolly huntsmen went out to hunt for fox

But where shall we find him amongst the hills and rocks?

Chorus:

With my hip, hip, hip and my holloa

And away went the merry, merry band.

With my ran tan tan and my chivvy, chivvy chan

All over the merry, merry strand.

With my ugle, ugle, ugle, went the bugle horn,

Fal le ral, fal le ral, fal le ral le dee.

Through the woods we‚Äôll go, brave boys,

And through the woods we‚Äôll go.

The first we met was a fair maid a-combing out her locks,

She swore she saw bold Reynolds amongst the farmer‚Äôs ducks.

The next we met was a farmer a-ploughing of his land,

He swore he saw bold Reynolds amongst the ewes and lambs.

And the next we met was a miller a-working of his mill,

He swore he saw bold Reynolds run over yonder hill.

And the next we met was a blind man, as blind as blind could be,

He swore he saw bold Reynolds run up a hollow tree.

And the next we met was a parson, and he was dressed in black,

He swore he saw bold Reynolds upon the huntsman‚Äôs back.

Tally-Ho, Tally-Ho pursue him!

A-Hi-Li, A-Hi-Lo

And a down the woods we glide.

The first we met was a blacksmith

And he was very black,

He said he saw poor Reynard

A-crossin‚Äôthe railroad track.

The next we met was a miller

A-grindin‚Äô of his corn,

He said he saw poor Reynard

A-blowin‚Äô a shepherds horn.

versions.title: Judy Cook sings Bowena

Come hic, come hic, come high-low, along the merry stream,

And through the woods we‚Äôll run, brave boys,

And through the woods we‚Äôll run.

Come all you gallant sportsmen who love to hunt the fox

The first we saw was a blind man as blind as he could be

So come all you gallant sportsmen who love to hunt the fox

items.title: Trademan‚Äôs Toast
items.notes:
Trademan‚Äôs Toast

[

; Mudcat 100120

; John Warner]

John Warner sang his own song All Trades (Tradesmen‚Äôs Toast)

on his 1992 CD

The Sea and the Soil .

According to Mudcat he wrote it in 1985.

Danny Spooner and Duncan Brown sang Trademan‚Äôs Toast

in 2016 on their CD of songs of the working life,

Labour and Toil .

They commented:
items.lyrics:
Here‚Äôs to the smith, with his sets and hammer

Fuller and hardie, swage and tongs;

Beating his will on the blazing steel,

To the forge‚Äôs roar and the anvil‚Äôs song.

Chorus (after each verse):

All trades, their gear and tackle,

Fittings and fettlings, tools and blades,

Subtle hands working ageless patterns‚ÄĒ

Here√Ę¬Ä¬ôs to the people of‚ÄĒall trades!

Here‚Äôs to the weaver‚Äôs loom and shuttle,

Heddle and beater, warp and weft;

Tweed and gabardine, twill and tartan,

Shuttles shooting both right and left.

Here‚Äôs to the mariner‚Äôs sheets and halyards,

Fairleads, fiddle blocks, anchor and oars,

Here‚Äôs to the shanty at the weather main brace

When the tide has turned and the wind‚Äôs off-shore.

Here‚Äôs to the cooks, their leeks and onions,

Rosemary, basil, garlic and chives,

Cleavers, skillets, pans and ladles,

Cauldrons, kettles and carving knives.

Here‚Äôs to the cooper‚Äôs croze and cressett,

In-shave, flagging iron, trusses and adze,

Driving the hoops on the smoking barrels

To hold good ale for the lasses and lads.

Here‚Äôs to the shipwright‚Äôs planks and gunwales,

Stem post, stern post, scantling and beams

Trenails, inwales, iron and mallet,

Tar and oakum to caulk the seams.

Here‚Äôs to the wheel of your home-spun spinner,

Bobbin whirling on the Mother of All.

Here‚Äôs to her carders, combs and treadle

As she plies and spins the greasy wool.

Here‚Äôs to the hands of men and women,

Here‚Äôs to the fine-wrought goods they‚Äôve made.

Here‚Äôs to the tools, the words, the secrets,

The marks of humanity on every trade.

items.title: Union Boy
items.notes:
Union Boy

[

;

AFS 90

; Ballad Index MA256

; trad.]

Danny Spooner sang

Union Boy

in 2017 on his final CD,

Home .

He noted:

When John Meredith first visited Gulgong NSW he recorded this song from
items.lyrics:
When I first arrived in Quirindi those girls all jumped for joy

Saying one unto the other, ‚ÄúHere comes a union boy.

We‚Äôll treat him to a bottle, likewise to a dram,

And with all our hearts we welcome him, the loyal union man.‚ÄĚ

Well, I hadn‚Äôt been in Quirindi about two days or three

When an handsome pretty fair young maid she fell in love with me,

Introduced me to her mother as a loyal union man,

Saying, ‚ÄúMother, dearest mother, he wants to join our gang.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh daughter, dearest daughter, now this can never be,

For just four years he scabbed at Forquadee.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBut mother, dearest mother, the truth to you I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll tell‚ÄĒ

Since then he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs joined the union and the country knows it well.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell Fred, if you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve joined the union, you must stick to it like glue

And all the scabs that were upon your back will soon be very few;

But if you go a-scabbing or blacklegging likewise,

It‚Äôs with my long, long fingernails I‚Äôll scratch out both your eyes.

I‚Äôll put you to every cruelty, I‚Äôll squeeze you in a vice,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll chop you up in a hay machine and sell you for Chinese rice.‚ÄĚ

Come all you honest workers wherever that you be,

Rise up the flag, our union flag, the flag of unity.

Then scabbing in this country will all be at an end

And I pray that every worker here will be loyal union friends.

items.title: Weevils in the Flour
items.notes:
>

Martyn Wyndham-Read >

Songs >

Weevils in the Flour

Weevils in the Flour

[Dorothy Hewett, Mike Leyden]

The Bushwackers sang Dorothy Hewett‚Äôs poem Weevils in the Flour

in 1981 on their album produced by Trevor Lucas,

Faces in the Street .

Roy Bailey sang Weevils in the Flour ,

accompanied by Martin Simpson on guitar,

on his 2005 CD
items.lyrics:
On an island in a river,

How that bitter river ran,

We lived on scraps of charity

In the best way that you can.

On an island in a river,

Where I grew to be a man.

And just across the river

Stood the mighty BHP,

Poured pollution on the water,

Poured the lead of misery,

And its smoke was black as Hades

Rolling hungry to the sea.

Chorus (after each verse):

For dole bread is bitter bread,

Bitter bread and sour.

There‚Äôs grief in the taste of it

There‚Äôs weevils in the flour,

There‚Äôs weevils in the flour.

In those humpies by the river

Well, we lived on dole and stew,

While just across the river

Those greedy smokestacks grew,

And the hunger of the many

Filled the bellies of the few.

On an island in a river,

How that bitter river ran,

It broke the banks of charity

But it baked the bread of man.

On an island in a river

Where I grew to be a man.

Final Chorus:

For dole bread is bitter bread,

There‚Äôs weevils in the flour,

But man grows strong as iron on

Black bread and sour,

Black bread and sour.

items.title: Wish I Could Write a Lovesong
items.notes:
Wish I Could Write a Lovesong

[Chas Hodges, Dave Peacock]

Danny Spooner sang Wish I Could Write a Lovesong

on his 1987 album

When a Man‚Äôs in Love .

This track was also included in 2007 on his anthology

Years of Spooner .

He noted:

I‚Äôm sure that many men (including me) could associate with this brilliant

song by Chas Hodges and Dave Peacock, better known as the cockney duo

Chas and Dave. This song says it all by exposing our vulnerability.

Real love makes us awkward, clumsy and very human. Dennis Mitchell‚Äôs piano
items.lyrics:
I wish that I could write a love-song, to show the way I feel

But perhaps I just ain‚Äôt got it, perhaps I never will

Wish that I could write down words, that I want you to see,

Wish I could write a love-song, just to you from me.

I write the words down, then change ‚Äôem all around, ‚Äôcos I ain‚Äôt to sure,

It always comes out wrong, it‚Äôs just another song,

And it‚Äôs all bin done before and it‚Äôs gotta mean much more.

Wish that I could write a love-song, to show the way I feel,

But perhaps I just ain‚Äôt got it, perhaps I never will.

Rock and roll so easy, rock and roll songs, dead easy.

Is it simply that I love you more than words can say?

Is that I feel silly when I let my feelings show

Or am I afraid to say too much, in case some day you go;

If I give in to my emotions, then I might get hurt,

If some day you run away, with my uncle Bert.

But I‚Äôm making fun again, trust me to make a joke, out of anything

Wish I could make up lines wish I could make up rhymes,

Ain‚Äôt good for anything good to sing.

Wish I could write a love-song, to show the way I feel,

But I guess I just ain‚Äôt got it, guess I never will.

Rock and roll songs so easy, rock and roll songs, dead easy.

Is it simply that I love you, more than words can say?

Think I‚Äôll write a love-song, just for you today.

items.title: Yangtze River Shanty
items.notes:
Yangtze River Shanty

[Hamish McLaren]

Ye Mariners All (John Roberts, John Rockwell and Larry Young)

sang Yangtse River Chantey on their 2003 CD

Songs of the Sea

and, also in 2003, at the

24 th Annual Sea Music Festival at Mystic Seaport .

John Roberts noted:

Hamish MacLaren included the Yangtse River Chantey in his book of

verse, Sailor with Banjo . Charlie Ipcar of Portland, Maine is

responsible for the musical setting, and also did a nice job of making the

text more singable. I learned it from our good friend Barry Finn
items.lyrics:
My lotus lady I‚Äôll see no more,

Away, boys, away-o

Since I left her on the China shore.

Away, boys, lift and walk away.

Chorus (after each verse):

Away, boys, away-o

Blow me down that Yangtse River

Away, boys, lift and walk away.

When we first met she was like a queen,

Prettiest little thing that I‚Äôd ever seen.

She‚Äôd flashing eyes and long black hair,

All I could do was stand and stare.

The sweetest flower, my Yangtse honey,

My own good gal she spent my money.

I bought her silks and a golden comb,

Trouble‚Äôs over now the anchor‚Äôs home.

I blowed my silver for to win her,

Now there‚Äôs nothing left but pork for dinner.

We‚Äôre outward bound, cookie‚Äôs in the galley,

Farewell, young Moon of the Yangtse Valley.

For an old man come courting me, hi down dearly day,

For an old man come courting me, hi dearly day;

items.title: Ashen Bowl
items.notes:
Ashen Bowl

[

; VWML RoudFS/S317620

; trad. English c. 1700s]

Eliza Carthy and Jon Boden sang

Ashen Bowl

in 2023 on their Hudson album

Glad Christmas Comes .

Eliza Carthy noted:

I came across this in Heywood Sumner‚Äôs beautiful 1888 collection

The Besom Maker

[where it has the title The Wassail Song ].
items.lyrics:
A wassail, a wassail goes all round our town

Our cup it is white and our ale it is brown

Our bowl is made of a good ashen tree

And oh my kind fellow we‚Äôll drink unto thee

We are in the old time, the new time comes fast

The new time comes fast and the old time is past

So I wish you all a happy new year

Your pockets full of money

And your barrels full of beer

Our bowl it is made of the good ashen tree

And so my good fellow we‚Äôll drink unto thee

We‚Äôll drink master‚Äôs health and our mistress beside

And all the pretty family around the fire side

And all that he has got well I know he does not mind

We‚Äôll drink master‚Äôs health in water or in wine

Our bowl it is made of the good ashen tree

And so my good fellow we‚Äôll drink unto thee

We‚Äôll drink master‚Äôs health with the star on his breast

And when that he is dead we hope his soul will rest

So I wish you all a happy new year

Your pockets full of money

And your barrels full of beer

Our bowl it is made of the good ashen tree

And so my good fellow we‚Äôll drink unto thee

items.title: Benjamin Bowmaneer
items.notes:
Benjamin Bowmaneer

[

/ Song Subject MAS601

; Master title: Benjamin Bowmaneer

; Ballad Index VWL020

; DT BENBOWMR

; Mudcat 17062

, 52686

; trad.]

Benjamin Bowmaneer is a song from

The Penguin Book of English Folk Songs ,

collected by Mary E. Spence from Sarah Foster of Sedbergh, Yorkshire.
items.lyrics:
Have you heard how the wars began,

Benjamin Bowmaneer?

Have you heard how the wars began?

Castors away!

Have you heard how the wars began

When England fought to a man?

And the proud tailor rode prancing away

Of his shear board he made a horse,

Benjamin Bowmaneer

Of his shear board he made a horse,

Castors away!

Of his shear board he made a horse

For him to ride across.

And the proud tailor rode prancing away

Of his scissors made bridle bits,

Benjamin Bowmaneer

Of his scissors made bridle bits,

Castors away!

Of his scissors made bridle bits

To keep the horse in his wits.

And the proud tailor rode prancing away

As the tailor rode o‚Äôer the lea,

Benjamin Bowmaneer

As the tailor rode o‚Äôer the lea,

Castors away!

As the tailor rode o‚Äôer the lea,

He spied a flea on his knee.

And the proud tailor rode prancing away

Of his needle he made a spear,

Benjamin Bowmaneer

Of his needle he made a spear,

Castors away!

Of his needle he made a spear

To prick that flea through his ear.

And the proud tailor rode prancing away

Of his thimble he made a bell,

Benjamin Bowmaneer

Of his thimble he made a bell,

Castors away!

Of his thimble he made a bell

To ring that flea‚Äôs funeral knell.

And the proud tailor rode prancing away

Was this how the wars began,

Benjamin Bowmaneer?

Was this how the wars began?

Castors away!

Was this how the wars began

When England fought to a man?

And the proud tailor rode prancing away

items.title: Blind Fiddler
items.notes:
Blind Fiddler

[

; Ballad Index FSWB055

; trad.]

Eliza Carthy played and sang the Blind Fiddler

in 1996 on her first solo album,

Heat Light & Sound .

This track was also included on the anthology

New Electric Muse .

She noted:

An American song, learned from Pete Seeger via my Dad and a banjo.

It was collected from Emma Dewsbury, who sold it in return for a cow.
items.lyrics:
I lost my eyes in the blacksmith‚Äôs shop in the year of ‚Äô56

While cleaning out a T-flange that was out of fix

It bounded up from out of the tongs and there concealed my doom

I‚Äôm a blind fiddler and far from my home

I‚Äôve been to San Francisco, I‚Äôve been to Dr Lane

He operated on one of my eyes, nothing could he gain

He says I will be blind for life, there‚Äôs no use to mourn

I‚Äôm a blind fiddler and far from my home

I have a wife and three little babes depending now on me

They go through all my trials, wherever they may be

I hope that they‚Äôll be happy now while I am forced to roam

I‚Äôm a blind fiddler and far from my home

items.title: Bonaparte‚Äôs Retreat
items.notes:
Bonaparte‚Äôs Retreat

[trad. arr. Kings of Calicutt]

Sung and played by Eliza Carthy and The Kings of Calicutt

(Andi Wells, Barnaby Stradling, Saul Rose and Maclaine Colston)

on their eponymous album

Eliza Carthy and The Kings of Calicutt .

The album sleeve notes say:

Segments of two songs in this, an old Kay Starr hit (thanks Liza‚Äôs mum)

and a bawdy Morris number. The intro/outro bass line is pinched from

Tortoise.

Martin Carthy used the tune Bonaparte‚Äôs Retreat

for his rendering of
items.lyrics:
Met the girl I love

In a town way down in Dixie

Neath the stars up above

She was the sweetest little girl I ever did see

Then I held her in my arms

And told her of her many charms

And kissed her while the fiddles played

The Bonaparte‚Äôs Retreat

O dear mother what a fool I‚Äôve been

Six young fellows* come a-courting me

Five were blind and the other couldn‚Äôt see

Mother what a fool I‚Äôve been

Note: * ‚ÄúFellows‚ÄĚ is just a guess at what Eliza sings.

items.title: By Then
items.notes:
By Then

[Eliza Carthy]

Eliza Carthy recorded her own song by then

in 1996 for her first solo album,

Heat Light & Sound .

She commented in the record‚Äôs sleeve notes:

This song is about wanting what the other person doesn‚Äôt want.

You know it will be a mess in the end but you don‚Äôt care.
items.lyrics:
I see my pain before it is near to me

I shut it out before it is a problem

I have my eyes I have my mind

But still I believe that all is fine and still within my reach

I met my pain a day out on the road

I took a look and saw it as a thing I‚Äôd know

I have my eyes I have my mind

But still I believe that it will pass and I won‚Äôt look behind

So see my pain as it takes hold my senses

Of all the sugared kisses these are sweetest

I have my eyes I have my mind

But still I believe the weals they leave will not begin to show

items.notes:

items.lyrics:
O the bonny fisher boy

O the bonny fisher boy

items.title: Glad Christmas Comes
items.notes:
Glad Christmas Comes

[words John Clare c. 1827, last verse and music Eliza Carthy, Jon Boden]

Eliza Carthy and Jon Boden sang

Glad Christmas Comes

in 2023 on their Hudson album

Glad Christmas Comes .

Jon Boden noted:

We were excited to discover a John Clare poem that seemed eminently singable

and that might therefore (when furnished with a new tune) be a wholly new

addition to the canon. Sadly whilst working through the many many verses in

Eliza√Ę¬Ä¬ôs kitchen we came upon the ‚ÄúFor Pence and Spicy Ale‚ÄĚ verse.

‚ÄúYup‚ÄĚ said Eliza√Ę¬Ä¬ôs dad‚Ä¶ ‚Äúthat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs where we got it from.‚ÄĚ
items.lyrics:
Glad Christmas comes, and every hearth

Makes room to give him welcome now,

E‚Äôen want will dry its tears in mirth,

And crown him with a holly bough;

Though tramping ‚Äôneath a winter sky,

O‚Äôer snowy paths and rimy stiles,

The housewife sets her spinning by

To bid him welcome with her smiles.

Each house is swept the day before,

And windows stuck with ever-greens,

The snow is besom‚Äôd from the door,

And comfort crowns the cottage scenes.

Gilt holly, with its thorny pricks,

And yew and box, with berries small,

These deck the unused candlesticks,

And pictures hanging by the wall.

Neighbours resume their annual cheer,

Wishing, with smiles and spirits high,

Glad Christmas and a happy year,

To every morning passer-by;

Milkmaids their Christmas journeys go,

Accompanied with favour‚Äôd swain;

And children pace the crumping snow,

To taste their granny‚Äôs cake again.

The shepherd, now no more afraid,

Since custom doth the chance bestow,

Starts up to kiss the giggling maid

Beneath the branch of mistletoe

That ‚Äôneath each cottage beam is seen,

With pearl-like berries shining gay;

The shadow still of what hath been,

Which fashion yearly fades away.

The singing wates, a merry throng,

At early morn, with simple skill,

Yet imitate the angels song,

And chant their Christmas ditty still;

And, ‚Äômid the storm that dies and swells

By fits‚ÄĒin hummings softly steals

The music of the village bells,

Ringing round their merry peals.

When this is past, a merry crew,

Bedeck‚Äôd in masks and ribbons gay,

The ‚ÄúMorris-dance‚ÄĚ, their sports renew,

And act their winter evening play.

The clown turn‚Äôd king, for penny-praise,

Storms with the actor‚Äôs strut and swell;

And Harlequin, a laugh to raise,

Wears his hunch-back and tinkling bell.

And oft for pence and spicy ale,

With winter nosegays pinn‚Äôd before,

The wassail-singer tells her tale,

And drawls her Christmas carols o‚Äôer.

While ‚Äôprentice boy, with ruddy face,

And rime-bepowder‚Äôd, dancing locks,

From door to door with happy pace,

Runs round to claim his ‚ÄúChristmas box.‚ÄĚ

The block upon the fire is put,

To sanction custom‚Äôs old desires;

And many a fagot‚Äôs bands are cut,

For the old farmers‚Äô Christmas fires;

Where loud-tongued Gladness joins the throng,

And Winter meets the warmth of May,

Till feeling soon the heat too strong,

He rubs his shins, and draws away.

While snows the window-panes bedim,

The fire curls up a sunny charm,

Where, creaming o‚Äôer the pitcher‚Äôs rim,

The flowering ale is set to warm;

Mirth, full of joy as summer bees,

Sits there, its pleasures to impart,

And children, ‚Äôtween their parent‚Äôs knees,

Sing scraps of carols o‚Äôer by heart.

And some, to view the winter weathers,

Climb up the window-seat with glee,

Likening the snow to falling feathers,

In Fancy‚Äôs infant ecstasy;

Laughing, with superstitious love,

O‚Äôer visions wild that youth supplies,

Of people pulling geese above,

And keeping Christmas in the skies.

As tho‚Äô the homestead trees were drest,

In lieu of snow, with dancing leaves;

As tho‚Äô the sun-dried martin‚Äôs nest,

Instead of i‚Äôcles hung the eaves;

The children hail the happyday ‚ÄĒ

As if the snow were April‚Äôs grass,

And pleas‚Äôd, as ‚Äôneath the warmth of May,

Sport o‚Äôer the water froze to glass.

Thou day of happy sound and mirth,

That long with childish memory stays,

How blest around the cottage hearth

I met thee in my younger days!

Harping, with rapture‚Äôs dreaming joys,

On presents which thy coming found,

The welcome sight of little toys,

The Christmas gift of cousins round.

The wooden horse with arching head,

Drawn upon wheels around the room;

The gilded coach of gingerbread,

And many-colour‚Äôd sugar plum;

Gilt cover‚Äôd books for pictures sought,

Or stories childhood loves to tell,

With many an urgent promise bought,

To get to-morrow‚Äôs lesson well.

And many a thing, a minute‚Äôs sport,

Left broken on the sanded floor,

When we would leave our play, and court

Our parents‚Äô promises for more.

Tho‚Äô manhood bids such raptures die,

And throws such toys aside as vain,

Yet memory loves to turn her eye,

And count past pleasures o‚Äôer again.

Around the glowing hearth at night,

The harmless laugh and winter tale

Go round, while parting friends delight

To toast each other o‚Äôer their ale;

The cotter oft with quiet zeal

Will musing o‚Äôer his Bible lean;

While in the dark the lovers steal

To kiss and toy behind the screen.

Old customs! Oh! I love the sound,

However simple they may be:

Whate‚Äôer with time hath sanction found,

Is welcome, and is dear to me.

Pride grows above simplicity,

And spurns them from her haughty mind,

And soon the poet‚Äôs song will be

The only refuge they can find.

O‚Äôer snowy paths and rimy stiles,

The housewife sets her spinning by

To bid him welcome with her smiles.

The singing wates, a merry throng,

At early morn, with simple skill,

Yet imitate the angel‚Äôs song,

And chant their Christmas ditty still;

And, ‚Äômid the storm that dies and swells

By fits‚ÄĒin hummings softly steals

The music of the village bells,

Ringing round their merry peals.

When this is past, a merry crew,

Bedeck‚Äôd in masks and ribbons gay,

The ‚ÄúMorris-dance‚ÄĚ, their sports renew,

And act their winter evening play.

The clown turn‚Äôd king, for penny-praise,

Storms with the actor‚Äôs strut and swell;

And Harlequin, a laugh to raise,

Wears his hunch-back and tinkling bell.

Each house is swept the day before,

And windows stuck with ever-greens,

The snow is besom‚Äôd from the door,

And comfort crowns each cottage scenes.

And oft for pence and spicy ale,

With winter nosegays pinn‚Äôd before,

The wassail-singer tells her tale,

And drawls her Christmas carols o‚Äôer.

Thou happy day of sound and mirth,

That long with childish memory stays,

How blest around the cottage hearth

I met thee in my younger days!

Around the glowing hearth at night,

The harmless laugh and winter tale

Go round, while parting friends delight

To toast each other o‚Äôer their ale.

Glad Christmas comes, now every heart

Make room to give him welcome now!

Unite in fellowship and mirth

And crown him with a holly bough.

Now tramping ‚Äôneath a winter sky

To bid him welcome and rejoice.

items.title: Good Morning, Mr Walker
items.notes:
Good Morning, Mr Walker

[

; Mudcat 66627

; Slinger Francisco]

Slinger Francisco, also known as The Mighty Sparrow, wrote and recorded

Good Morning, Mr Walker in 1968.

Joseph Spence sang it in 1972 as the title track of his album

and Niles Hokkanen sang it in 1994 on the Hokey Pokey charity CD

Out on the Rolling Sea: A tribute to the music of Joseph Spence & the Pinder Family .

Eliza Carthy and The Kings of Calicutt

(Andi Wells, Barnaby Stradling, Saul Rose and Maclaine Colston)
items.lyrics:
She‚Äôs ugly, yes, but wearing that expensive dress.

The people say she‚Äôs ugly but her father‚Äôs full of money.

Oh, Lord, Mama, why, why?

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Good morning Mr Walker

I‚Äôve come to see your daughter,

Oh Mr Walker

I‚Äôve come to see your daughter.

Sweet Rosemary, she promised she would marry me

And now I‚Äôm tired of waiting,

I‚Äôve come to fix the wedding.

I fixed the wedding day. I don‚Äôt care what nobody say.

Every time I take a good look at her face, I see a bankbook.

Oh, Lord, Mama, why, why?

All I know‚Äôs I don‚Äôt intend to let her go,

For if she was a beauty, someone like me couldn‚Äôt get she.

Oh, Lord, Mama, why, why?

Apart from that, they say how she‚Äôs so big and fat.

When she dresses, they tantalise. She saying monkey wearing a mini.

Oh, Lord, Mama, why, why?

items.title: Happiness
items.notes:
>

Martin Carthy >

Songs >

Happiness

Happiness

[Molly Drake]

Eliza Carthy sang Molly Drake‚Äôs song

Happiness

in 2014 on her and Martin Carthy‚Äôs duo album,

The Moral of the Elephant .

She noted:

The wistful quality in this song is present on the whole of the
items.lyrics:
Happiness is like a bird with twenty wings,

Try to catch him as he flies.

Happiness is like a bird that only sings

When his head is in the skies.

You can try to make him walk beside you,

You can say the door is open wide.

If you grab at him, woe betide you,

I know because I‚Äôve tried.

Like a butterfly upon an April morning

Very quickly taking fright,

Happiness is come and gone without a warning,

Jack-O-Lantern in the night.

I will follow him across the meadow,

I will follow him across the hills,

And if I can catch him I will try to bring you,

Oh yes, happiness.

And if I can catch him I will try to bring you

All my love and happiness.

But if my love leaves me, what will I do?

If my love knew I could wash and wring

If my love knew I could weave and spin

I‚Äôd make a jacket of the finest kind

But for want of money he‚Äôs left me behind

There is a dance hall in Maradyke

There my love goes almost every night

He takes a strange girl upon his knee

Now don‚Äôt you think that it vexes me

I know my love is an errant rover

I know my love sails the whole world over

In foreign lands he is sure to tarry

And some foreign girl he is sure to marry

items.title: In London So Fair
items.notes:
In London So Fair

[

; G/D 1:178

; Henry H203

; Ballad Index HHH203

; Mudcat 37314

; trad.]

Gale Huntington:

My Ship Shall Sail the Ocean

Elizabeth Stewart:

Up Yon Wide and Lonely Glen

Mary Ann Carolan sang
items.lyrics:
In London so fair, a lady she lived there,

A lady of great beauty and great might,

And unto this lady fair I became a servant there,

And in me she took great delight.

This lady had a son of mirth and beauty born,

And he became a sailor on the sea.

And he courted a fair maid till he had her heart betrayed,

And then he was bound for the sea.

It happened one day in the cabin where he lay,

And the tears from her eyes they did flow,

Saying, ‚ÄúYoung man, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre going away so far from me to stray,

And to leave me on the shores for to mourn.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAh, no, no,‚ÄĚ said he, ‚Äúsuch things can never be,

For as long as I‚Äôm a sailor on the sea,

The ship that I command may she never reach the land.

The day that I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd be false, love, unto thee.‚ÄĚ

She cut off her yellow hair that hung down her back so fair,

For fear anybody would know,

And in sailor‚Äôs dress that very night she went

And straight to the captain she did go.

She being neat and slim, complete in every limb,

And the clothes fitted tightly to her knee,

While the crew did un-guess, the captain to her says,

‚ÄúYoung man, were you ever on the sea?√Ę¬Ä¬Ě

‚ÄúAh, no, no,‚ÄĚ said she, ‚ÄúI was never on the sea,

But ship me as a young sailor bold,

For I now do choose to go where the stormy winds that blow

To purchase some silver and some gold.‚ÄĚ

It happened one day in the cabin where he lay,

And the tears from his eyes they did flow,

Saying, ‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre like a lover of mine I think on many a time

I left on the shores for to mourn.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAh, no, no,‚ÄĚ said she, ‚Äúsuch things can never be,

For as long as I‚Äôm a sailor on the sea,

The ship that I command may she never reach the land

The day that I be false, love, unto thee.‚ÄĚ

His own words he knew, she spoke beneath the crew,

While into her arms he flew like a dove,

Saying, ‚ÄúDo not shed a tear; you followed me, my dear,

So it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs married, it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs married we will be.‚ÄĚ

They sent for a clerk; they sent for a maid;

They sent for a clergyman likewise.

So this couple they were wed and together they do bed

And now they‚Äôre living happy on the sea.

items.title: I Wish That the Wars Were All Over
items.notes:
I Wish That the Wars Were All Over

[

/ Song Subject MAS61

Roy Palmer:

items.lyrics:
In the meadow one morning when pearly with dew

A fair pretty maiden plucked violets so blue,

I heard a sweet voice making all the woods ring -

Oh my love is in Flanders to fight for the king

And I would that the wars were all over

Oh I would that the wars were all done.

I‚Äôll pluck the red robin so jaunty and gay,

Yet I have my Robin but he‚Äôs far away.

His jacket is red and his cheeks as the rose

And he sings of his Nell as to battle he goes.

And I would that the wars were all over

Oh I would that the wars were all done.

Ten thousands of bluebells now welcome the spring

Oh when will the church bells the victory ring

And the soldiers return and all England rejoice?

Oh then I‚Äôll be wed to the lad of my choice.

And I would that the wars were all over

Oh I would that the wars were all done.

(repeat first verse)

versions.title: Tim Eriksen sings I Wish the Wars Were All Over
versions.lyrics:
Down by deep water where sweet linden stand

I saw pretty Polly wringing her hands.

The song that she sung made the whole grove to ring,

My Billy has left me to fight for a king

And I wish the wars were all over.

I stood in amaze to hear what she said,

I‚Äôll follow my Billy whether living or dead.

The notes that she sung were the nightingale‚Äôs notes,

How the lark and the linnet they warble their throats,

And I wish the wars were all over.

I said my dear Polly if you‚Äôll stay with me

I‚Äôll make you as happy as happy can be.

No then she said that never can be,

I‚Äôll never be happy til my Billy I see

And I wish the wars were all over.

Now from my parents I‚Äôll haste and away

I‚Äôll dress myself out in a young man‚Äôs array.

And every company that I pass by

I‚Äôll ask for my Billy or where he does lie

And I wish the wars were all over.

versions.title: Eliza Carthy sings I Wish That the Wars Were All Over
versions.lyrics:
In the meadow one morning when pearly with dew

A fair pretty maiden she plucked violets blue,

And I heard a clear voice making all the woods ring -

My love he‚Äôs in Flanders to fight for the king.

How I wish that the wars were all over

I wish that the wars were all done.

I‚Äôll pluck the red robin so jaunty and gay.

Well I had my robin but he flew far away.

His little jacket was red and his cheeks as the rose

How he sings of his girl as to battle he goes.

How I wish that the wars were all over

I wish that the wars were all done.

Ten thousand of bluebells now welcome the spring

Oh when will the church bells of this victory ring

When do our soldiers return, when do we rejoice

And when do I wed to the love of my choice

How I wish that the wars were all over

I wish that the wars were all done.

items.title: King James Version
items.notes:
King James Version

[Billy Bragg]

Billy Bragg sang his own song King James Version

in September 1996 on his CD

William Bloke

Eliza Carthy and the Ratcatchers

sang King James Version

in 2005 on her CD

Rough Music .

She commented in the CD booklet:

Greg Stephens is one of those musicians that makes other people‚Äôs lives

fuller. I have admired him for a long time. He gave this 3/2 tune to my
items.lyrics:
He was trapped in a haircut he no longer believed in.

She says, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm a teacher here, I teach the children.‚ÄĚ

And he wondered to himself then of all the things he could learn from her.

A great mighty wonder.

I think of the names you once called me in anger,

I remember the sadness in Florence Ballard‚Äôs eyes.

Imagine all the melancholy you could find in the arms of a stranger.

Come, come all ye faithful.

Seems like nothing goes right

For the world that we were born in,

But the horizon is bright

Because yonder comes the morning.

Upstairs they‚Äôre buying a stairway to heaven

And down here in the garden we‚Äôre changing sticks into snakes.

And the jangle of religious medals would put the fear of God into angels,

Bread, bread of heaven.

It looks like a shift to the right

For the world that we were born in,

Oh but the horizon is bright

Because yonder comes the morning.

items.title: King of the Birds
items.notes:
King of the Birds

[Eliza Carthy]

Eliza Carthy and Jon Boden sang Eliza‚Äôs song

King of the Birds

in 2023 on their Hudson album

Glad Christmas Comes .

Eliza Carthy noted:

I was commissioned to write this, or more accurately put it together from

various floating verses, by the venerable Taffy Thomas, Storyteller of my

childhood, and his wife Chrissy. I grew up with them both and with their three

children on the folk scene in the UK and cherish their kinship. The tune is

Tuesday Morning ,
items.lyrics:
The wren, the wren, the king of the birds

On St Stephen‚Äôs Day he was caught in the furze

Though he is little his family is great

Rise up, Mrs Thomas, and give us a treat

Chorus (after each verse):

Hello, hello, hello boys

Hello, hello hello

Between the finger and the thumb

Oh there was a blister as big as a plum

As big as an apple, as plum as a cherry

Oh give us all something to make us all merry

Mr Thomas, a worthy old man

Oh he‚Äôs come on home and he‚Äôs brought us a wren

Brought us a wren, lads, to sing us a tune

So we can be happy on Old Christmas Morn

items.notes:

items.lyrics:
As I was a-walking, one morning in spring

I heard a fair damsel, so sweetly did sing.

As she was a-milking, when this she did sing,

‚ÄúI √Ę¬Ä¬ôm going to get married next Monday morning.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO where is your dwelling, fair maid,‚ÄĚ I recall.

‚ÄúI dwell in yon house, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm the fairest of all.

I dwell in yon cot at the foot of yon hill

And I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm going to get married next Monday morning.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO fifteen years old is to young for to marry,

A year or two longer, I‚Äôll have you now tarry.

For young men are false, their vows to fulfil

So put off your wedding next Monday morning.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO you talk like a man without sense, without skill!

Three years now I‚Äôve tarried against my own will,

I have made a vow that I mean to fulfil

I‚Äôm going to get married next Monday morning.

‚ÄúSo next Sunday night, I mean to prepare

To comb out my locks and to curl up my hair

And six pretty fair maids, so neat and so trim

Shall dance at my wedding next Monday morning.

‚ÄúSo next Monday night when I go to my bed

So close to my true-love I will lay my head.

If a maid I remain when I rise again

I shall wish I had never a-seen Monday morning.

‚ÄúSo next Monday morn when I put on my rings

Now my husband he gave me two far finer things.

Two precious jewels he gave my adorning,

So I be his bride next Monday morning.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúPray tell me your name and where do you belong?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI belong to the sign of the Bonny Bluebell,

My age is sixteen and you know very well.‚ÄĚ

I said, ‚ÄúSixteen years is too young to marry,

‚ÄúWell, you talk like a man without any skill,

‚ÄúOn Monday night, it is all my care

Ewan MacColl, Peggy Seeger:
items.lyrics:
‚ÄúO mother, go and make my bed,

Spread me the milk white sheets,

That I might go and lay down so low

For to see whether I could sleep.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMother, go and tell now,

Go tell your brother‚Äôs son,

Your true love‚Äôs sick and she‚Äôs going to die.

She will die before you can come.‚ÄĚ

The first few miles this little boy walked

And the next few miles he ran.

When he came to the broad water‚Äôs side

He bent his back and he swam.

When he came to the high front door

They were all sat down to meat.

‚ÄúWell if you knew why I have come to you

Not a single bite more could you eat.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYour high castle walls they√Ę¬Ä¬ôre not falling down,

Nor are they overthrown.

Your true love‚Äôs sick and she‚Äôs going to die,

She will die before you can come.‚ÄĚ

Then he said to the little boy,

‚ÄúBring me the milk white steed,

That I might go kiss her cherry cherry cheeks

That once they were so sweet.‚ÄĚ

The rose and the briar they grew all together

Till they could get no higher.

They grew and they tied a true lover‚Äôs knot

And the rose wrapped around the sweet briar.

versions.title: Anna Baldwin sings Lady Maisry
versions.lyrics:
‚ÄúO mother, o mother, go and make my bed,

And lay out the milk white sheets,

That I may go and lay between the cold clothes

And see if I can sleep.

‚ÄúGo and fetch to me some little boy,

My own dear sisters son,

That he may go and tell my true lover

That I am going to die.‚ÄĚ

This little boy walked the first two miles,

The next to two miles he ran.

He run until he came to the broad water side

When he fell on his breast and swum.

He swum till he got to some high park gates

Where they sat down to meet.

‚ÄúAnd if you knew the news, the news that I bring,

Scarce a bit more bread could you eat.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the news, what√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the news?‚ÄĚ our captain he cried,

‚ÄúO tell me the news, my son!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYour lover she is going, she is going for to die,

She will die before you come.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúGo saddle for me my milk-white steed,

That I might ride, ride away.

That I may go and kiss her cherry, cheery lips

Before they are turned into clay.‚ÄĚ

Three times he walked round the rim of her hat

And three times the sole of her shoe.

Three times he‚Äôd kissed her cherry blooming lips

As she lay on the ground fast asleep.

The lady she died on the Saturday

At twelve o‚Äôclock at noon,

The lord he died on the Sunday following

Before evening prayer was done.

The lady was buried in the high chancel

And the lord he was buried in the choir.

And out of the lady there grew a damask rose

And out of the lord a sweet briar.

These two flowers grew proper and tall,

They grew till they couldn‚Äôt grow no higher.

They grew and they grew into a lover‚Äôs knot,

The rose wrapped round the sweet briar.

items.title: Nelly Was a Lady
items.notes:
Nelly Was a Lady

[

; Ballad Index Dean122

; Mudcat 152927

; Stephen Collins Foster]

According to Sigmund Spaeth,

A History of Popular Music in America , p. 106,

Nelly Was a Lady

was ‚Äú[Stephen C.] Foster√Ę¬Ä¬ôs hit in 1849, now chiefly known as a

barber-shop favorite.‚ÄĚ

Steve Turner sang Nelly Was a Lady

in 1987 on his Fellside album
items.lyrics:
Down on de Mississippi floating,

Long time I trabble on de way,

All night de cottonwood a-toting,

Sing for my true lub all de day.

Chorus (after each verse):

Nelly was a lady,

Last night she died,

Toll de bell for lubly Nell,

My dark Virginny bride.

Now I‚Äôm unhappy, and I‚Äôm weeping,

Can‚Äôt tote de cottonwood no more;

Last night, while Nelly was a-sleeping,

Death came a-knockin‚Äô at de door.

When I saw my Nelly in de morning,

Smile till she open‚Äôd up her eyes,

Seem‚Äôd like de light ob day a-dawning,

Jist ‚Äôfore de sun begin to rise.

Close by de margin ob de water,

Whar de lone weeping willow grows,

Dar lib‚Äôd Virginny‚Äôs lubly daughter;

Dar she in death may find repose.

Down in de meadow, ‚Äômong de clober,

Walk wid my Nelly by my side;

Now all dem happy days am ober,

Farewell, my dark Virginny bride.

Now all those happy days are over,

Farewell, my dark Virginny bride.

items.title: Peggy
items.notes:
Peggy

[Eliza Carthy]

Eliza Carthy recorded her own song Peggy

in 1996 for her first solo album,

Heat Light & Sound .

She commented in the record‚Äôs sleeve notes:

A song about being a performer and a responsible lover at the same time,

these can clash!
items.lyrics:
I think Peggy‚Äôs my love and my life as she stands just so

And all I can say is today, there she is, here am I

Peggy is Peggy, she scares the wits from me when I think she knows

When I think I want to be alone and want her goodbye

Oh oh, Peggy oh, it‚Äôs not for the time I have in my mind

Peggy oh Peggy, the way that I‚Äôm wanted I see it

And it puts you behind in my mind if you had been there at all

Look at me, look at me, celebrate, this is the way that I feed it

And I‚Äôm half a man and you know I‚Äôll not stay beyond nightfall

Oh oh, Peggy oh, it‚Äôs not for the man I know that I am

It‚Äôs not for the man in my mind, not for my Peggy-o

Not for the thought of always being alone

Not for you and not for me, not that I‚Äôm not able to see

I‚Äôll take myself down to the sea

Peggy‚Äôs my love and my life as she turns to go

And all I can see is my road and such a heavy load

Since I am free, easily I will take the blow

And pity the poor guilty head that lies on my pillow

She doesn‚Äôt want my name, Peggy oh Peggy-o

She knows it would be the same if we ever met again

As for you and as for me, as for them that so easily flee

Come with me down to the sea oh

items.title: In Sheffield Park
items.notes:
,

/ Song Subject MAS149

items.lyrics:
In Sheffield Park where I did dwell

A brisk young lad I loved him well.

He courted me my heart to gain;

He‚Äôs gone and left me full of pain.

I went upstairs to make the bed,

And laid me down and nothing said,

My mistress came and to me said,

‚ÄúWhat is the matter with you, my maid?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO little dove, what does you know

What pain and sorrow I undergo,

Just lay your hand on my left breast,

My fainting heart can take no rest.‚ÄĚ

My mistress turned away with speed;

‚ÄúSome help, some help is what you need.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNo help, no help, no help, I say

My William stole my heart away.‚ÄĚ

We‚Äôll gather green grass all for her bed

And a flowery pillow for her head,

And the leaves that‚Äôs blowing from tree to tree

Shall be the coverlet over she.

versions.title: Frank Hinchliffe sings In Sheffield Park

Down Sheffield Park a maid did dwell,

Her mistress came, unto her did say:

‚ÄúWhat is the matter with you today?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh Mistress, oh Mistress, you little do know,

Place your right hand upon my left breast,

My fainting heart, it knows no rest.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThen write him a letter, and write it with speed,

And send it to him, if he can read.

And bring me an answer without delay,

For young Colin has stolen your heart away.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThen gather leaves to make my bed,

A feathery pillow for my weary head.

And the leaves they‚Äôll flutter from tree to lea,

Will make a covering o‚Äôer me.

‚ÄúThere is a flower that bloom-eth in May,

That‚Äôs seldom seen by night or day.

And the leaves they flutter from tree to lea,

Will make a covering o√Ę¬Ä¬ôer me.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Eliza Carthy sings Sheffield Park

In Sheffield Park oh there did dwell

In Sheffield Park oh there did dwell

items.title: Sweet if You Like
items.notes:
Sweet if You Like

[words from a 17 th century poem by Francis Davidson, music Jools Holland]

Eliza Carthy sang Francis Davidson‚Äôs poem

in a recording made on 9 June 2003 at Helicon Mountain Studios

for Jools Holland & his Rhythm & Orchestra‚Äôs album

Jack o the Green .
items.lyrics:
Sweet if you like and love me still

And yield me love for my goodwill.

And do not from your promise start

When your fair hand gave me your heart.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

If dear to you I‚Äôll be

As you are dear to me

Then yours I am

And forever will be

Then yours I am and will be ever,

No time nor place shall my love sever.

Like constant, constant marble stone

I‚Äôm loving you and you alone.

I know that you love to range

And how you often chop and change.

If you get some new fangled mate

My doting love might turn to hate.

‚ÄúWhen you met with me at first

‚ÄúAnd when you meet a pretty maid

‚ÄúThere is a cock in my father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs yard,

She said, ‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre like an oul dog my father had,

He had a bushy tail,

He‚Äôd lay his head right in in the sack

But he wouldn‚Äôt eat the meal

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre like an oul cock my father had,

He couldn‚Äôt tread the hens;

Sure I think you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre one of them.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Cyril Barber sings Hail the Dewy Morning
versions.lyrics:
I went out one May morning to see what I could shoot,

I there a-spied a fair pretty maid a-rowing in a boat.

Chorus (after each verse):

Singing hail the dewy morning,

Blow the winds high-ho,

Clear away the morning dew,

How sweet the winds do blow.

We both strolled on together ‚Äôtil we came to some cocks of hay,

I said, ‚ÄúYoung lady it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a very nice place for you and I to play.‚ÄĚ

I put my arms around her and tried to lay her down,

She said, ‚ÄúYoung man this dewy grass will spoil my new silk gown.

‚ÄúIf you come down to my father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs house there you may lay me down,

And take away my maidenhead, likewise ten thousand pounds.‚ÄĚ

We strolled down to her father‚Äôs house, where she quickly locked me out,

She said, ‚ÄúYoung man I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm a maid within, and you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre a fool without!‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Eliza Carthy sings Blow the Winds
versions.lyrics:
There was a shepherd‚Äôs son,

He kept sheep on the hill.

He laid his pipe and his crook aside

And there he slept his fill.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

And blow the winds high-o, high-o

Sing blow the winds high-o

Well he looked east and he looked west,

He took another look

And there he saw a lady gay

Was dipping in a brook.

She said: ‚ÄúSir, don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt touch my mantle,

Come let my clothes alone.

I will give you as much bright money

As you can carry home.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI will not touch your mantle,

I‚Äôll let your clothes alone,

But I‚Äôll take you out of the water clear

My dear to be my own.‚ÄĚ

He mounted her on a milk white steed,

Himself upon another,

And there they rode along the road

Like sister and like brother.

And as they rode along the road

He spied some cocks of hay,

‚ÄúOh look!‚ÄĚ he says, ‚Äúthere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a lovely place

For men and maids to play.‚ÄĚ

And when they came to her father‚Äôs house

They rang long at the ring,

And who is there but her brother

To let the young girl in.

When the gates were opened

This young girl she jumped in,

‚ÄúOh, look!‚ÄĚ she says, ‚Äúyou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre a fool without

And I‚Äôm a maid within!

‚ÄúThere is a horse in my father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs stable,

He stands behind the thorn,

He shakes himself above the trough

But dares not pry the corn.

‚ÄúThere is cock in my father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs yard,
A double comb he wears,

He shakes his wings and he crows full loud

But a capon‚Äôs crest he bears.

‚ÄúAnd there is a flower in my father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs garden,

It‚Äôs called the marigold,

The fool that will not when he can,

He shall not when he would.‚ÄĚ

Says the shepherd‚Äôs son as he doffed his shoes,

‚ÄúMy feet they shall run bare

And if I ever meet another girl

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll have that girl, beware.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Karine Polwart sings Shepherd Lad
versions.lyrics:
Once there was a shepherd lad kept sheep upon the hill

An he‚Äôs laid his pipe and crook aside and there he‚Äôs slept his fill.

He woke up on a riverbank on a fine May mornin,

And there he spied a lady swimming in the clothes that she was born in.

So he raised his head from his green bed and he approached the maid.

‚ÄúPut on yer claithes, my dear,‚ÄĚ he says, ‚Äúand do not be afraid.

It‚Äôs fitter for a lady fair to sew a silken seam

Than to rise on a fine May morning and swim against the stream.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell, if you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll not touch my mantle and you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll leave my claithes alane,

Then I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll give you all the money, sir, that you can carry hame.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll not touch your mantle and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll leave yer claithes alane,

But I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll tak you out of the clear water, my dear, to be my ane.‚ÄĚ

So he‚Äôs taen her oot o the clear water and he‚Äôs rowed her in his arms.

‚ÄúPut on yer claithes, my dear,‚ÄĚ he says, ‚Äúand hide your bounteous charms.‚ÄĚ

He put her on a milk white steed and himself upon another,

And it‚Äôs all along the way they rode like sister and like brother.

They rode intae her faither‚Äôs gate and she‚Äôs tirled at the pin,

And ready stood a porter there to let the fair maid in.

When the gates were opened, it‚Äôs so nimbly she stepped in.

She said, ‚ÄúKind sir, you are a fool without and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm a maid within.

‚ÄúSo fare thee weel, my modest boy. I thank you for your care,

But if you had done as you desired, I‚Äôd never have left you there.

I will sew no silken seam on a fine May morning.

You can bide your time till your time runs out, so take this as fair warning.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Lisa Knapp sings Dew Is on the Grass
versions.lyrics:
As I walked out one midsummer‚Äôs morn

Chorus (after each verse):

Fol de lie de lay

I boldly stepped up to her

‚ÄúO but if you come to my father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs house

You can have my maidenhead

But when we got to her father‚Äôs house,

It was walled in all around, sir.

And she ran in and shut the gate,

‚ÄúO when you met with me at first

‚ÄúAnd if you meet a pretty girl

‚ÄúThere is a flower in my father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs garden

And if you will not when you may

versions.title: Rachael McShane sings Shepherd Lad

‚ÄúPut on your clothes my dear,‚ÄĚ he said,

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs fitter for a lady fair

‚ÄúIf you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll not touch my mantle

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll not touch your mantle

versions.title: David Stacey sings Hail the Dewy Morning
versions.lyrics:
Now as I went walking around yonder fields

Well, I met a girl in brown, Sir,

She said, ‚ÄúKind sir what have you got,

What is it do possess you?

Blow the misty morning, blow the wind hi ho

How sweet the winds do blow.

‚ÄúFor if you come to my father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs house

Well it‚Äôs neatly built all round, Sir

But when they came to her father‚Äôs house

items.lyrics:
O don‚Äôt sing love songs, you‚Äôll wake my mother,

She‚Äôs sleeping here, right by my side.

And in her right hand, a silver dagger,

She says that I can‚Äôt be your bride.

All men are false, says my mother,

They tell you wicked, loving lies.

And in the next breath they‚Äôll court another,

They‚Äôll leave you ere the sun does rise.

My daddy is a handsome devil,

He has a chain five miles long.

On every link a heart does dangle

Of another maid he‚Äôs loved and wronged.

O then go home to your own garden,

O then go home, return no more.

No other man may come a-courting,

No other lover cross my door.

(repeat first verse)

versions.title: Judy Cook sings Drowsy Sleeper
versions.lyrics:
Wake, O wake you drowsy sleeper

Wake, O wake, it is almost day

How can you sleep, you cruel creature

When you have stolen my heart away

Hush, hush, hush, don‚Äôt you wake my mother

No songs of love will she let me hear

If you sing songs, go court some other

And whisper lowly in my ear.

My father he lies in the next room, simple

A-taking of his natural rest

And in his hand he holds a weapon

He swears he‚Äôll kill the one that I love best

Come go with me, you will stop my pining

Sit you down by the side of me

And lay your loving arms around me

And say you‚Äôll love no one but me

I‚Äôll go with you to some lonesome valley

And spend the rest of my days with you

Although my meat may be pretty berries

And the water that I drink be the morning dew

Pretty flowers was made to bloom, love,

Pretty stars was made to shine

And pretty girls was made for boys, love,

And maybe you was made to be mine.

versions.title: The Devil‚Äôs Interval sing Silver Dagger
versions.lyrics:
Don‚Äôt sing love songs, you‚Äôll wake my mother,

She‚Äôs sleeping here, right by my side.

In her right hand is a silver dagger,

She says that I can‚Äôt be your bride.

Young men are false, so says my mother,

They tell you wicked, loving lies.

And then they‚Äôll go and court some other,

Leave you alone to pine and cry.

My daddy is a handsome devil,

He‚Äôs got a chain that‚Äôs five miles long.

On every link a heart does dangle

Of another maid he‚Äôs loved and wronged.

Go court another tender maiden

In hopes that she might be your wife.

For I‚Äôve been warned, so I‚Äôve decided

To sleep alone all of my life.

(repeat first verse)

versions.title: Bryony Griffith sings Arise, Arise
versions.lyrics:
‚ÄúArise, arise, my drowsy sleeper,

Awake, awake, it‚Äôs almost day.

O come unto your bedroom window

And hear what your true love does say.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBegone, begone, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll awake my mother,

My father too will quickly hear;

Go tell your tale unto another

And whisper softly all in her ear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO then, o then, go ask your father,

If he‚Äôll consent you my bride to be.

If he denies you, come and tell me

And it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the last time I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll visit thee.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs in his chamber, writing,

And noting down on his merchandise,

And in his hand he holds a letter

And it speaks much of your dispraise.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúTo my dispraise, love, to my dispraise, love,

To my dispraise, love, how can it be?

I have never slighted, nor yet denied you

Until this night you‚Äôve denied of me.

‚ÄúO then, o then, go ask your mother

If she‚Äôll consent you my bride to be.

If she denies you, come and tell me

And it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the last time I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll visit thee.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy mother√Ę¬Ä¬ôs in her chamber sleeping,

And words of love she will not hear,

Go tell your tale unto another

And whisper softly all in her ear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe fish shall fly, the sea run dry, love,

The rocks shall melt all in the sun;

And the labouring man shall forget his labour,

Before that I shall return again.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: The Full English sing Awake, Awake
versions.lyrics:
Awake, awake, you drowsy sleeper,

Awake, awake, it is dawn of day.

How can you lie there so long in sleeping

Since you have stolen my heart away?

Awake, awake, it is dawn of day.

How can you lie there so long in sleeping

Since you have stolen my heart away?

Be gone, be gone, you will wake my father,

My mother she will you quickly hear.

Go tell your tales to some other lover

And whisper softly all in her ear.

Awake, awake, it is dawn of day.

Go tell your tales to some other lover

And whisper softly all in her ear.

I won‚Äôt be gone for I have no other,

You are the girl I do much admire.

‚ÄôTis I my dear who do love you deeply

And thoughts of love that have brought me here.

Awake, awake, it is dawn of day.

‚ÄôTis I my dear who do love you deeply

And thoughts of love that have brought me here.

The old man heard the young couple talking,

So nimble he stepped out from his bed.

He put his head to the bedroom window,

Young Johnny dear, he has turned and fled.

Awake, awake, it is dawn of day.

He put his head to the bedroom window,

Young Johnny dear, he has turned and fled.

So much for love and the valiant hearted,

So much for faithful and strong young men.

I‚Äôll turn my bed on the morrow morning

And call young Johnny return again.

Awake, awake, it is dawn of day.

I‚Äôll turn my bed on the morrow morning

And call young Johnny return again.

versions.title: Eliza Carthy sings Waking Dreams
versions.lyrics:
Awake, awake, you drowsy traitor,

Awake, arise, it‚Äôs almost day.

How can you sleep such treacherous slumbers

When you have all my heart away?

You learned my secrets, warmed in comfort

But left me cold now so it seems.

You left me lying, prone to crying

And beset my waking dreams.

Farewell my friends, farewell my family,

Now I am bound while he goes free.

I will go down to those far white mountains

Where the air is thin and you cannot see.

I thought I heard your voice a-shouting,

I thought I heard you loud and clear.

But it was just a faithless echo

And you were nothing that I could call dear.

I loved his face, I loved his body,

I loved his voice when he sang with me.

And every day brought port and brandy

To toast his name in a glass or three.

Now take my heart, I do not need it.

I‚Äôm through with love, I‚Äôm safe alone.

And I ‚Äôll sit here on my white mountain

And watch your beauty turn to stone.

items.lyrics:
In comes I old Father Christmas

Step in brave George my noble son

Show your face all in this place now Georgie

Here before the company

In comes I the loathsome dragon

Snapdragon is my name

Lords and champions quail before me

In comes I Prince George of Britain

I will face that dragon bold

I will fight him, I will smite him bravely

And I‚Äôll win three crowns of gold

In comes I the old quack doctor

I‚Äôll drive out lumbago, gout and palsy

Now our sport is almost ended

And Christmas comes but once a year

We hope there‚Äôs none here we‚Äôve offended

We hope we‚Äôve brought you all good cheer

Here‚Äôs hoping that we‚Äôve pleased the company

And we hope you‚Äôll treat us right

Pass the cup and fill it up with money

Then we‚Äôll bid you all good night

items.lyrics:
A brisk you lass so brisk and gay

‚ÄúThere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a peck of corn all for to grind;

versions.title: Eliza Carthy sings Miller and the Lass

items.title: The Wrong Favour
items.notes:
The Wrong Favour

[Eliza Carthy]

Eliza Carthy sang her own song The Wrong Favour

in 1993 on her and Nancy Kerr‚Äôs eponymous album

Eliza Carthy & Nancy Kerr .

She commented in the record‚Äôs sleeve notes:

The Wrong Favour is a spleen-venting song. I wrote it in

June 1983.
items.lyrics:
You never went to her when she was sleeping

Never went to her when she was alone

You never went to her when the rain dripped down outside

And she was alone

She knows the sweetest words are spoken one time

And the quickest tongue can ache for only one to say

There was nothing more to say

New words don‚Äôt add to what we know today

Don‚Äôt tell me anything any more

I don‚Äôt want to know

And I don‚Äôt know who you are

‚ÄôCept a thorn in my side

And a stain on my sense of being

She wished that there was something she could say to you

Wished that there were thoughts inside your mind of hers

She wished that you would go away far far away

And leave her to her

She looked away from [me?] and what she saw there

Was pairs of mocking eyes in what she thought was her home

She pulled out her hair she tore at her skin

And cried Leave me alone

Don‚Äôt show me anything any more

I don‚Äôt want to know

And I don‚Äôt know who you are

‚ÄôCept an ache in my soul

And a blight on my self-composure

She said she‚Äôd never felt much like your lover

Never had the peace of mind to be a friend

Still she hung around looking for a chance to be

A friend to any friend

She turned back to the arms of someone waiting

Said Look at me closely Am I allowed to be

You know he came to me You know he ran from me

But he never spoke to me

So don‚Äôt let him tell you anything any time

You don‚Äôt want to know

And don‚Äôt find out who he is

He‚Äôll be a thorn in your side

And he‚Äôll take all your laughter from you

A gentleman being passing by

He stood a while and he gazed upon her

Saying, fair young lady will you marry me

I‚Äôm no lady but a poor girl

And a poor man‚Äôs daughter of low degree

So now, young man, choose another sweetheart

For I‚Äôm not fitting your maid to be

I have houses and I have lands

And I have money to set you free

I‚Äôll make you a nice young lady

And you‚Äôll have servants to wait on thee

It‚Äôs seven years since I had a sweetheart

And seven more since i did him see

Seven more I will wait upon him

If he‚Äôs alive he‚Äôll come home to me

It‚Äôs seven years since you had a sweetheart

And seven more since you did him see

Seven more you will wait upon him

Perhaps that young man you‚Äôll never see

If he‚Äôs sick I wish him better

And if he‚Äôs dead I wish him rest

If he‚Äôs alive he will come home to me

He‚Äôs the fairest man I do love best

He put his hand into his pocket

His gentle fingers they were thin and small

And out between them he drew a gold ring

And when she saw it down she did fall

He took her up all in his arms

And gave her kisses most tenderly

Saying, I‚Äôm your true love, a single sailor

Who came home from sea, to wed with thee

If you‚Äôre my true love, a single sailor

Your face and features are strange to me

But seven years make great alterations

On the raging seas between you and me

items.lyrics:
About my time with the son, my Cordelia, I won‚Äôt tell you,

When there‚Äôs a footprint of fire on my face,

So lets us all be what we have to be,

With a cool, cool breeze on my face

Wouldn‚Äôt it be better if we three stood up to be counted?

items.lyrics:
Oh no, here comes that sun again,

That means another day without you my friend.

And it hurts me to look into the mirror at myself

And it hurts even more to have to be with somebody else.

Chorus:

And it‚Äôs so hard to do and so easy to say

But sometimes, sometimes you just have to walk away.

I‚Äôve so many people to love in my life,

Why should I worry about one?

But you put the ‚Äúhappy‚ÄĚ into my happiness

And you put the good times into my fun.

Chorus

We‚Äôve tried the goodbye so, so many days,

We walked in the same direction so that we could never stray.

They say, if you love somebody you have got to set them free

But I would rather be locked to you than live in this pain and misery.

They say, time will make this go away.

But it‚Äôs time that has taken my tomorrows and turned them into yesterdays.

So once again that rising sun is droppin‚Äô back down,

Once again you, my friend, are nowhere to be found.

Chorus

My love is but a sailor bold, sailor bold,

Chorus (after each verse):

So bring me back that one I love

And bring and give him back to me

If I only had that one I love

How happy, happy should I be.

I wish my heart was made of glass

So he might stand and view me through

And read the secrets of my heart

For if you love one you can‚Äôt love two.

O think of me and bear in mind, in mind

That I‚Äôm the girl that you passed by

And when you find one fond and true, fond and true

Never change that old love for the new.

My love is like a little bird

That flies across from tree to tree

And when he gets so far away, far away,

I know he thinks no more of me.

Now think of me and bear in mind, in mind

A constant heart is hard to find

And when you find one fond and true, fond and true,

Never change that old love for the new.

I wish to God my baby was born

Sat smiling on his dadda‚Äôs knee

But maid again I never shall be

I wish to God my baby was born

items.title: Auld Matrons
items.notes:
