Auld Matrons

[

; Child 249

; Ballad Index C249

; trad.]

Ewan MacColl sang

Auld Matrons

in 1982 on his and Peggy Seeger‚Äôs album
items.lyrics:
My love she is a gentlewoman,

Has her living by the seam;

I ken nae how she is provided

This nicht for me and my foot-groom.

Willie‚Äôs gane tae Annie‚Äôs bower-door

And tirled gently at the pin;

‚ÄúSleep ye or wake ye, my dear Annie?

Open the door and let me in.‚ÄĚ

Wi‚Äô her white fingers lang and sma‚Äô,

She‚Äôs lifted gently at the pin;

Flang her airms a‚Äô aboot him,

Kindly welcomed Willie in.

‚ÄúO, will ye gang tae cairds or dice,

Willie, will ye gang tae play?

Or will ye gang tae a weel-made bed

And lie and sleep awhile till day?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy love Annie, my dear Annie,

I would be at your desire,

If it wasnae for yon Auld Matrons

Sitting by the kitchen fire.‚Äú

‚ÄúWillie dear, keep up your hairt,

Keep up your hairt and dinnae fear;

For seiven year and mair hae passed

Since last her feet did file the flee .

They hadnae kissed nor gi‚Äôen love‚Äôs handsel

The way of lovers when they meet;

When up arose yon Auld Matrons,

And sae weel she spread her feet.

O wae befa‚Äô yon Auld Matrons

Sae clever as she took the gate,

And she‚Äôs gane up yon high high hill

And chappit at the sheriff‚Äôs yett .

‚ÄúSleep ye or wake ye, my good lord,

And are ye no‚Äô your bower within?

There‚Äôs a knicht in bed wi‚Äô your dochter

And I fear she√Ę¬Ä¬ôs gotten wrang.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYe√Ę¬Ä¬ôll gae doon through Kelso toon,

And wauken a‚Äô my merry men;

And when ye hae this wark weel done

Then I will come and tak√Ę¬Ä¬ô command.‚ÄĚ

She‚Äôs done her doon through Kelso toon

And waukened a‚Äô his merry men;

And when she had this wark weel done

Then he has come and ta‚Äôen command.

He has his horse wi‚Äô corn foddered,

A‚Äô his men were armed in mail;

He‚Äôs gi‚Äôen Auld Matrons half a mark

Tae lead his men oot ower the hill.

Willie slept but Annie waukened

When she heard their bridles ring;

She shook her Willie by the shouther ,

‚ÄúRise, my love, ye sleep ower lang.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO, gi√Ę¬Ä¬ôe nae sign, my ain dear lassie

Till I‚Äôve pit on my shooting gear;

Then I wadna fear the King himsel‚Äô

Though he and a√Ę¬Ä¬ô his men were here.‚ÄĚ

They shot their arrows through the window.

Ane o‚Äô them grazed Willie‚Äôs broo ,

The lassie grat and tore her hair,

‚ÄúThis nicht we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll a√Ę¬Ä¬ô hae cause to rue.‚ÄĚ

Willie shot his arrows oot

Until the bow brunt in his hand;

And aye he kissed her rosy lips,

Says, ‚ÄúSee they fa√Ę¬Ä¬ô on ilka hand!‚ÄĚ

He set his horn intil his mouth

And blawn a blast sae lood and shrill;

It was heard by Jock, his brither,

In Ringlewood whaur he lay still.

The firsten shot his brither lowsed

It stretched twa men oot on the green;

The neisten shot his brother lowsed

Then it‚Äôs ca‚Äôd oot the sheriff‚Äôs e‚Äôen .

‚ÄúWha will be my e√Ę¬Ä¬ôen?‚ÄĚ he cried,

‚ÄúLend me an airm or mebbe twa!

And they that come for strife this nicht

Tak‚Äô horse! Tak‚Äô horse and ride awa‚Äô!

‚ÄúBut wae befa√Ę¬Ä¬ô ye, Auld Matrons,

And an ill deith may ye dee!

I‚Äôll burn ye on yon high hill-heid

And blaw your ashes in the sea.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Steeleye Span sing Old Matron
versions.lyrics:
My love she is a gentlewoman,

Has her living by the seam.

I know how she has provided

This night for me and my foot-groom.

He is gone to Annie‚Äôs door,

Gently tirled at the pin.

‚ÄúYou sleep, you wake, my true love Annie?

Rise up and let your love in!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy love Annie, my dear Annie,

I would be at your desire.

But what about the old matron

As she sits by the fire?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWilliam, keep your heart,‚ÄĚ she said,

‚ÄúKeep up your heart and do not fear.

It‚Äôs seven years or even more

Since her feet did touch the floor.‚ÄĚ

They had not kissed nor sworn devotion

As lovers when they meet,

When up rose the old matron

And well she‚Äôs spread her feet.

O woe be for the old matron,

So clever she has jumped the gate.

And she‚Äôs gone o‚Äôer the long, long hill,

Knocked at the sheriff‚Äôs gate.

‚ÄúYou sleep, you wake, my lord?‚ÄĚ she said,

‚ÄúAre you at your bower within?

There‚Äôs a man lies with your daughter

I fear she has gone wrong‚ÄĚ

He had his horse with corn well foddered,

His men all armed in mail.

He gave the matron half a mile

To show them o‚Äôer the hill.

William slept, but Annie wakened

When she heard their bridles ring.

She tapped on her true lover‚Äôs shoulder

And said, ‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôve slept too long!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh save me, save me, blessed lady

‚ÄôTil I‚Äôve got on my shooting gear.

I do not fear the sheriff himself

Though all his men were here.‚ÄĚ

Then they shot in and William out,

The arrow grazed his brow.

The maid she wept and tore her hair,

Says, ‚ÄúThis can never do,‚ÄĚ

They shot in and he shot out,

The bow burnt William‚Äôs hand.

But then he kissed her ruby lips

And says, ‚ÄúMy dear, we will stand!‚ÄĚ

He set his horn unto his mouth

And has blown both loud and shrill,

And he has called his brother John

In the woods where he lay still.

The first round that Lord John he.shot

He wounded fifty and fifteen,

The next shot that he did let fly

Put out the sheriff‚Äôs eye.

‚ÄúOh, some of you lend him an arm,‚ÄĚ

Says William to the sheriff‚Äôs men,

‚ÄúAnd you that came for strife this day

Take horse and ride fast home.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBut woe be to you old matron,

An ill death may you see.

I‚Äôll burn you on yon high hill-head,

Blow your ashes in the sea.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Ballad of Accounting
items.notes:
Ballad of Accounting

[

; DT BALACCNT

; Ewan MacColl]

Enoch Kent sang Ewan MacColl‚Äôs sang The Ballad of Accounting

in 1966 on The Exiles‚Äô Topic album

Freedom, Come All Ye .

Ewan MacColl wrote this song as a link between the six episodes of a series

of television documentaries called Landmarks , broadcast by the

BBC in 1965.

Ewan MacColl and Peggy Seeger sang Ballad of Accounting

in a 1972 recording on MacColl‚Äôs 1990 Cooking Vinyl anthology
items.lyrics:
In the morning we built the city

In the afternoon walked through its streets

Evening saw us leaving

We wandered through our days as if they would never end

All of us imagined we had endless time to spend

We hardly saw the crossroads

And small attention gave

To landmarks on the journey from the cradle to the grave,

cradle to the grave, cradle to the grave

Did you learn to dream in the morning?

Abandon dreams in the afternoon?

Wait without hope in the evening?

Did you stand there in the traces and let them feed you lies?

Did you trail along behind them wearing blinkers on your eyes?

Did you kiss the foot that kicked you?

Did you thank them for their scorn?

Did you ask for their forgiveness for the act of being born,

act of being born, act of being born?

Did you alter the face of the city?

Did you make any change in the world you found?

Or did you observe all the warnings?

Did you read the trespass notices, did you keep off the grass?

Did you shuffle off the pavement just to let your betters pass?

Did you learn to keep your mouth shut,

Were you seen and never heard?

Did you learn to be obedient and jump to at a word,

jump to at a word, jump to at a word?

Did you ever demand any answers?

The who, the what or the reason why?

Did you ever question the setup?

Did you stand aside and let them choose while you took second best?

Did you let them skim the cream off and then give to you the rest?

Did you settle for the shoddy?

Did you think it right

To let them rob you right and left and never make a fight,

never make a fight, never make a fight?

What did you learn in the morning?

How much did you know in the afternoon?

Were you content in the evening?

Did they teach you how to question when you were at the school?

Did the factory help you grow, were you the maker or the tool?

Did the place where you were living

Enrich your life and then

Did you reach some understanding of all your fellow men,

all your fellow men, all your fellow men?

items.title: Black and White
items.notes:
Black and White

[Ewan MacColl]

Peggy Seeger and Ewan MacColl sang MacColl‚Äôs song

Black and White

in 1973 on their Folkways album

Folkways Record of Contemporary Songs .

Peggy Seeger sang the first two lines and Ewan MacCool sang the last two lines

of each verse, except for the last verse which they sang together.

They noted:

Written at the time of

Sharpeville Massacre ,

this song still speaks for the plight of African Blacks in their own countries.
items.lyrics:
The apples ripe upon the bow

The orange on the tree

The hands were black that picked the fruit

For you, but not for me

Diamonds shining in the rock

Gleaming white and blue

Ten hours a day in the diamond mines

For me, but not for you

A sea of grass and mountain ranges

Beckoning the free

A place to walk with head held high

For you, but not for me

The burning sun of Africa

The sky that‚Äôs always blue

Apartheid and the pass laws

For me, but not for you

A big land, a rich land

Stretched from sea to sea

And all the riches of the earth

For you, but not for me

A big land, a gracious land

A land where old meets new

The bullets fired at Sharpville were

For me, but not for you

You‚Äôve planted seeds of fear and hate

In all who would be free

At harvest time the fruit will be

For you, and not for me

items.title: Captain Ward and the Rainbow / Ward the Pirate
items.notes:
[

; Master title: Captain Ward and the Rainbow

items.lyrics:
Come all ye jolly mariners,

That love tae tak‚Äô a dram,

I‚Äôll tell ye o‚Äô a robber

That o‚Äôer the seas did come.

He wrote a letter to his King

On the eleventh o‚Äô July,

To see if he wad accept o‚Äô him

For his jovial company.

‚ÄúO na, o na,‚ÄĚ says the King,

‚ÄúSuch things they cannae be.

They tell me ye are a robber,

A robber on the sea,‚ÄĚ

He has built a bonnie ship,

And sent her tae the sea,

Wi‚Äô fower and twenty mariners,

Tae guard his bonnie ship wi‚Äô.

They sailed up, and they sailed doon,

Sae stately, blythe and free,

Till they spied the King‚Äôs high Reindeer

Like a leviathan on the sea.

‚ÄúWhy lie ye here, ye tinker ,

Ye silly, coordly thief?

Why lie ye here, ye tinker,

And hold oor King in grief?‚ÄĚ

They fought frae yen in the morning

Till it was six at night,

Until the King‚Äôs high Reindeer

Was forced tae tak‚Äô her flight.

‚Äú Gang hame , gang hame, ye tinkers,

Tell your king fae me,

Though he reign the King upon guid dry land,

I will reign king on the sea.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Cyril Poacher sings Captain Ward and the Rainbow
versions.lyrics:
Her name it is gallant Rainbow ,

You might have heard her name.

With four and twenty of the king‚Äôs fine ships

To drive her back again.

‚ÄúGo ye home, go ye home,‚ÄĚ cried Captain Ward,

‚ÄúYour galliant Rainbow sail.

With four and twenty of the king‚Äôs fine men

To drive her back again.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Peter Bellamy sings Ward the Pirate
versions.lyrics:
Come all you gallant seamen bold, all you that march to drum,

Let‚Äôs go and look for Captain Ward, far out on the sea he roams.

For he is the biggest robber that ever you did hear;

And there‚Äôs not been such a robber found in above this hundred year.

Now a ship it was sailing from the east and going to the west,

Loaded with silks and satins and velvets of the best.

But in meeting there with Captain Ward, it was a sad meeting:

For he robbed them of their wealth and their store and bid them tell their king.

So it‚Äôs then our king he builded a ship of noted fame,

She‚Äôs call‚Äôd the Royal Rainbow if you would know her name.

And she was as well provided for as any ship can be,

With thirteen hundred men on board to bear her company.

And at eight o‚Äôclock in the morning when they did begin to fight;

And so did they continue there till nine o‚Äôclock at night.

‚ÄúFight on, fight on!‚ÄĚ cried Captain Ward, ‚ÄúFor this sport pleases me;

But though you fight a month or more, your master still I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be!‚ÄĚ

So it‚Äôs then the Royal Rainbow she fired but she fired all in vain,

Till six and sixty of her men all on the decks lie slain.

‚ÄúGo home, go home!‚ÄĚ cried Captain Ward, ‚ÄúTell your king from me:

Though he reigns king on all dry land I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll reign king on the sea!‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Jon Boden sings Captain Ward
versions.lyrics:
Come all you gallant sailors bold, all you who march to drum,

Let‚Äôs go and look for Captain Ward, far out on the seas he roams.

For he is the biggest robber that ever you did hear;

And there has not been such a robber found in above this hundred year.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

The wind blows high and the wind blows low

All on the raging sea

A ship was sailing from the east and going to the west,

All loaded with silks and satins fine and velvets of the best.

But in meeting there with Captain Ward, it was a sad meeting:

For he robbed us of our wealth and their store and bid us tell our king.

So our king then he has built him a ship of noted fame,

She‚Äôs called the Royal Rainbow if you would know her name.

She was as well provided for as any ship can be,

With thirteen hundred sailors bold to bear her company.

And at six o‚Äôclock in the morning how they did begin to fight;

And so they did continue there till eight o‚Äôclock at night.

‚ÄúFight on, fight on!‚ÄĚ says Captain Ward, ‚ÄúFor this sport pleases me;

Although you fight a month or more, your master still I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be!‚ÄĚ

And then the Royal Rainbow she fired but she fired all in vain,

Until three hundred sailors bold all on the ship lay slain.

‚ÄúGo home, go home!‚ÄĚ says Captain Ward, ‚ÄúAnd tell your king from me:

Although he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs king of all dry land yet I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm king of the sea!‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Rachael McShane sings Captain Ward
versions.lyrics:
Come all you jolly mariners that love to take a dram,

Well, I‚Äôll tell you of a robber who o‚Äôer the seas did come.

And he sent a letter to his king on the eleventh of July,

To see if he would accept of him for his jovial company.

‚ÄúOh no, oh no,‚ÄĚ the king he said, "such things can never be,

For they tell me you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre a robber, a robber on the sea.‚ÄĚ

So he has built a bonny ship and sent her to the sea,

With four and twenty mariners to guard his bonny ship wi‚Äô.

They sailed up and they sailed down so stately, blithe, and free,

Till they spied the king‚Äôs high Reindeer a-sailing on the sea.

‚ÄúWhy lie you here, you tinkers, you silly cowardly thieves?

Why lie you here, you tinkers, an√Ę¬Ä¬ô hold our king in grief?‚ÄĚ

They fought from one in the morning till it was six at night,

Until the king‚Äôs high Reindeer was forced to take her flight.

‚ÄúGo home, go home, you tinkers, and tell your king from me,

Though he reign king upon good dry land, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll reign king upon sea.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Come All Ye Fisher Lassies / The Fish Gutters‚Äô Song
items.notes:

The Fish Gutters‚Äô Song

was written for the third of the eight

BBC radio ballads by Ewan MacColl, Charles Parker and Peggy Seeger,

(first broadcast on 16 August 1960, released on an Argo LP in 1966

and now available on a Topic CD).

It was sung by sisters Elizabeth and Jane Stewart from Fetterangus.

Peggy Seeger noted in
items.lyrics:
Come a‚Äô ye fisher lassies, ay, it‚Äôs come awa‚Äô wi‚Äô me,

Fae Cairnbulg and Gamrie and fae Inverallochie;

Fae Buckie and fae Aberdeen an‚Äô a‚Äô the country roond,

We‚Äôre awa‚Äô tae gut the herrin‚Äô, we‚Äôre awa‚Äô tae Yarmouth Toon.

Rise up in the morning wi‚Äô your bundles in your hand,

Be at the station early or you‚Äôll surely hae to stand;

Tak‚Äô plenty to eat and a kettle for your tea,

Or you‚Äôll mebbe die of hunger on the wa‚Äô to Yarmouth quay.

The journey it‚Äôs a lang yen and it tak‚Äôs a day or twa ,

And when you reach your lodgin‚Äôs sure it‚Äôs soond asleep you fall;

But ye rise at five wi‚Äô the sleep still in your e‚Äôe ,

You‚Äôre awa‚Äô tae find the gutting yards alang the Yarmouth quay.

It‚Äôs early in the morning and it‚Äôs late into the nicht ,

Your hands a‚Äô cut and chappit and they look an unco‚Äô sicht ;

And you greet like a wean when you put them in the bree

And you wish you were a thoosand mile awa‚Äô frae Yarmouth quay.

There‚Äôs coopers there and curers there and buyers, canny chiels ,

And lassies at the pickling and others at the creels .

And you‚Äôll wish the fish had been a‚Äô left in the sea

By the time you finish gutting herring on the Yarmouth quay.

We‚Äôve gutted fish in Lerwick and in Stornoway and Shields;

Warked along the Humber ‚Äômongst the barrels and the creels;

Whitby, Grimsby, we‚Äôve traivelled up and doon,

But the place to see the herrin‚Äô is the quay at Yarmouth Toon.

items.title: Dirty Old Town
items.notes:
Dirty Old Town

[

; DT DIRTTOWN

; Mudcat 30172

, 48907

; Ewan MacColl]

Ewan MacColl wrote Dirty Old Town

about Salford, Lancashire, England, the city where he was born and brought up.

It was originally composed for an interlude to cover an awkward scene change in

his 1949 play Landscape With Chimneys , set in a North of England

industrial town, but many people now erroneously regard it as a

traditional song.
items.lyrics:
I found my love on the gasworks croft,

Dreamed a dream by the old canal;

Kissed my girl by the factory wall,

Dirty old town, dirty old town.

Heard a siren from the docks,

Saw a train set the night on fire;

Smelt the spring on the smoky wind,

Dirty old town, dirty old town.

Clouds a-rolling across the sky,

Cats a-prowling upon their beat;

Spring‚Äôs a girl in the street at night,

Dirty old town, dirty old town.

I‚Äôm going to make a good sharp axe,

Shining steel tempered in the fire;

We‚Äôll chop you down like an old dead tree,

Dirty old town, dirty old town.

versions.title:

items.lyrics:
A‚Äô the week your man‚Äôs awa‚Äô

And a‚Äô the week you bide your lane;

A‚Äô the time you‚Äôre waiting for

The minute that he‚Äôs comin‚Äô hame.

Ye ken whit wha‚Äô he has tae wark,

Ye ken the hours he has tae keep;

And yet it mak‚Äôs ye angry when

Ye see him just come hame tae sleep.

Through the months and through the years

While you‚Äôre bringing up the bairns,

Your man‚Äôs awa‚Äô tae here and there

A-followin‚Äô the shoals o‚Äô herrin‚Äôs.

And when he‚Äôs back there‚Äôs nets tae mend

You‚Äôve maybe got a score or twa;

And when they‚Äôre done he‚Äôll rise and say,

‚ÄúWife it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs time I was awa√Ę¬Ä¬ô.‚ÄĚ

Work and wait and dree your weird,

And pin yer faith in herrin‚Äô sales;

And ofttimes lie awake at nicht

In fear and dread of winter gales.

But men maun work tae earn their breid

And men maun sweat to gain their fee,

And fishermen will aye gang oot

As long as fish swim in the sea.

versions.lyrics:
Aw‚Äôm a poor cotton wayver as many a one knows.

Aw‚Äôve nowt t‚Äôeat in the house, an‚Äô aw‚Äôve wore out me clo‚Äôes.

Me clogs are both brokken an‚Äô stockins aw‚Äôve none.

Yo‚Äôd hardly gie tuppence for all aw‚Äôve got on.

Yo‚Äôd think it were hard to be sent in the world

To clem an‚Äô do best ‚Äôat yo can.

Our parish church parson kept tellin us long

Aw‚Äôve howden me tongue till aw‚Äôve near lost me breath,

An‚Äô aw think in me heart he means to starve us to death.

Aw know he lives weel wi‚Äô backbitin the Devil,

But he never picked o‚Äôer in his life.

We held on six weeks thinkin each day were t‚Äô last.

We tarried an‚Äô shifted till now we‚Äôre quite fast.

We lived upon nettles while nettles was good,

An‚Äô Wayterloo porritch were t‚Äô best of us food.

Aw‚Äôm tellin ye true, aw can find folk enoo

That are livin no better nor me.

Now, owd Bill o‚Äô Dans sent bailiffs one day

For t‚Äô shop score he‚Äôs owed that aw couldn‚Äôt pay.

But he‚Äôs just too late, for owd Billy o‚Äô Bent

Had sent round his cart an‚Äô ta‚Äôen goods for rent.

They left nowt but a stool that‚Äôre seats for two

An‚Äô on it sat Margit an‚Äô me.

My piece were cheont off, an‚Äô aw took it him back.

I hardly durst speak, m aster lookit so black.

He said: ‚ÄúYou were o√Ę¬Ä¬ôerpaid last time you come.‚ÄĚ

Aw said: ‚ÄúIf aw were, √Ę¬Ä¬ôtwere wi√Ę¬Ä¬ô weavin baht loom.‚ÄĚ

An‚Äô in t‚Äômind that aw‚Äôm in, aw‚Äôd ne‚Äôer pick o‚Äôer again,

For aw‚Äôve woven meself to t‚Äôfur end.

So aw come out the warehouse an‚Äô left him chew that.

When aw thought things o‚Äôer, aw were so vexed till aw swat,

All the days o‚Äô me life, an‚Äô then die in their debt.

But aw‚Äôll give ower this trade and work wi‚Äô a spade

Our Margit declares if hoo ‚Äôd clothes to put on

items.title: Jamie Foyers
items.notes:
Jamie Foyers

[

; G/D 1:106

; Ballad Index McCST084

; Bodleian

; DT JAMFOYE2

, JAMFOYE3

; Mudcat 3009

; trad. / Ewan MacColl]

Norman Buchan:

101 Scottish Songs

Karl Dallas:
items.lyrics:
Far distant, far distant, lies Scotia the brave,

No tombstone memorial shall hallow his grave

His bones they are scattered on the rude soil of Spain

For young Jamie Foyers in battle was slain.

From the Perthshire Militia to serve in the line,

The brave Forty-second we sailed for to join;

To Wellington‚Äôs army we did volunteer,

Along with young Foyers, that brave halberdier.

The night that we landed the bugle did sound,

The general gave orders to form on the ground,

To storm Burgos castle before break of day,

And young Jamie Foyers to lead on the way.

But mounting the ladder for scaling the wall,

By a shot from a French gun young Foyers did fall;

He leaned his right arm upon his left breast

And young Jamie Foyers his comrades addressed.

‚ÄúFor you, Robert Percy, that stands a campaign,

If goodness should send to old Scotland again,

You will tell my old father, if yet his heart warms,

That young Jamie Foyers expired in your arms.

‚ÄúBut if a few moments in Campsie I were,

My mother and sisters my sorrow would share;

Now, alas, my old mother, long may she mourn,

But young Jamie Foyers will never return.

‚ÄúOh, if I had a drink of Baker Brown√Ę¬Ä¬ôs well,

My thirst it would quench and my fever would quell.‚ÄĚ

But his very live-blood was ebbing so fast,

And young Jamie Foyers soon breathed his last.

They took for his winding sheet his ain Tartan plaid,

And in the cold ground his body was laid;

With hearts full of sorrow they covered his clay,

And saying, ‚ÄúPoor Foyers!‚ÄĚ marched slowly away.

His father and mother and sisters will mourn,

But Foyers, the brave hero, will never return;

His friends and his comrades lament for the brave

Since young Jamie Foyers is laid in his grave.

The bugle may sound and war drum may rattle,

No more will they raise this young hero to battle;

He fell from the ladder like a hero so brave,

And rare Jamie Foyers is lying in his grave.

versions.title: Ewan MacColl sings Jamie Foyers
versions.lyrics:
Far distant, far distant, lies Foyers the brave,

No tombstone memorial shall hallow his grave

He‚Äôs gane frae the shipyard that stands on the Clyde;

To fecht by the side o‚Äô the people of Spain.

There wasna his equal at work or at play,

O, young Jamie Foyers was the floo‚Äôer o‚Äô them a‚Äô.

He came frae the shipyaird, took aff his working claes,

O, I mind that time weel in the lang simmer days;

He said, ‚ÄúFare ye well, lassie, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll come back again.‚ÄĚ

But young Jamie Foyers in battle was slain.

In the ficht for Belchite he was aye to the fore,

He focht at Gandesa till he couldna fecht more;

He lay owre his machine-gun wi‚Äô a bullet in his brain

And young Jamie Foyers in battle was slain.

He lies by the Ebro in far away Spain,

He died so that freedom and justice might reign;

Remember young Foyers and others of worth

And don‚Äôt let one fascist be left on this earth.

items.title: Just a Note (The Exile Song)
items.notes:
[Ewan MacColl]

Ewan MacColl wrote

Just a Note

for the BBC Home Service Radio Ballad

Song of a Road ,

first broadcast 5 November 1959. It wasn‚Äôt released as an Argo record

as most of the Radio Ballads were, but had to wait until 1999 when Topic

released it on CD.

Peter Bellamy sang

in 1985 on his EFDSS album

Second Wind .
items.lyrics:
Just a note for time is short dear.

Hard the work and long the day,

But my heart is with you, Mary,

Though I‚Äôm many a mile away.

Kiss the children for me Mary,

Do not let them pine or grieve.

Tell them how I‚Äôm working for them,

Why our home I had to leave.

Building dams, airfields and factories,

Moving concrete by the load ‚ÄĒ

But I‚Äôll be with you in November

When I‚Äôm finished on the road.

There was a lady all skin and bone,

And she walked up and she walked down,

And from his nose unto his chin,

‚ÄúShall I be so when I am dead?‚ÄĚ

And the sexton to the lady said,

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be the same when you are dead.‚ÄĚ

versions.lyrics:

And such a lady was never known;

It happened on a holiday,

The lady went to the church to pray.

And when she came unto the stile,

She tarried there a little while;

And when she came unto the door,

She tarried there a little more.

And when she came unto the aisle,

She had a sad and a woeful smile;

She had come a long and a weary mile,

Her sin and sorrow to beguile.

Well, she‚Äôs walked up and she‚Äôs walked down,

And she saw a dead man all on the ground;

And from his nose unto his chin,

The worms crept out and the worms crept in.

And as the worms through him did creep,

Then asked the lady while sore she did weep,

‚ÄúWill I be this way oh when I die?‚ÄĚ

And the dead man turned his head and he answered, ‚ÄúAye!‚ÄĚ

items.title: Lang Johnny More / Long John, Old John and Jackie North
items.notes:
[

; Child 251

; G/D 2:246

; Ballad Index C251

; DT LANGJMOR

, LANGJMO2

; Mudcat 24474

; trad.]

Alexander Keith:

Last Leaves of Traditional Ballads and Ballad Airs

Katherine Campbell:

Songs From North-East Scotland
items.lyrics:
There lives a man in Rynie‚Äôs land

And anither in Auchindore,

But the bravest lad among them a‚Äô

Was lang Johnnie More.

Now, Johnnie, he‚Äôs a growin‚Äô lad

Fu‚Äô sturdy, stout and strang,

And the sword that hung by Johnnie‚Äôs side

Was fully ten feet lang.

Now, Johnnie was a clever youth

A sturdy lad o‚Äô might,

He was full three yards aboot the waist

And fourteen feet in height.

Johnnie‚Äôs gane to London toon

To see what he could see;

And the fairest lass in a‚Äô the lands

Fell in love wi‚Äô young Johnnie.

The news has gane through London toon

Till it cam‚Äô to the king,

That a muckle Scot has won the hairt

O‚Äô his dochter, Lady Jean.

Now, when the king got word o‚Äô that,

A muckle oath swore he.

That Johnnie More should stretch a rope

And be hangit on a tree.

Now, Johnnie heard the sentence passed,

A muckle laugh gied he;

While I hae strength to life my sword

They‚Äôll no‚Äô be hangin‚Äô me.

But the English dogs are cunning rogues

And around him they did creep;

And they gied him drops o‚Äô laudamy

While he lay fast asleep.

When Johnnie waukened frae his sleep,

A sorry hairt had he,

Wi‚Äô jaws and hands in iron bands

And his feet in fetters three.

‚ÄúO, whaur will I get a bonnie boy

That w ill win meat and fee?

That will rin to my uncle‚Äôs house

At the foot o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Benachie?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO, here am I, a bonnie boy

Will wark for meat and fee;

And I‚Äôll rin to your uncle‚Äôs hoose

At the foot o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Benachie.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhen ye come to Benachie,

You‚Äôll neither chap nor ca‚Äô;

But you‚Äôll ken auld Johnnie stannin‚Äô there

Three foot abune them a√Ę¬Ä¬ô.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYe√Ę¬Ä¬ôll gie to him this lang letter

Sealed wi‚Äô my faith and troth;

And ye‚Äôll bid him bring alang wi‚Äô him

The lad ca√Ę¬Ä¬ôd Jock o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Noth.‚ÄĚ

And when he cam‚Äô to Benachie

He would neither chap nor ca‚Äô;

But he kent auld Johnnie stannin‚Äô there

Three feet abune them a‚Äô.

‚ÄúWhit news, whit news, my little wee boy,

You were never here before.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNae news, nae news, but a letter

Your nephew, Johnnie More.

‚ÄúHe sends to you this lang letter

Sealed wi‚Äô his faith and troth;

And ye‚Äôre bidden bring alang wi‚Äô ye

The lad ca√Ę¬Ä¬ôd Jock o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Noth.‚ÄĚ

Noo, Benachie lies unco‚Äô low

And the tap o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Noth lies high‚ÄĒ

But for a‚Äô the distance ‚Äôtween them twa

They heard auld Johnny cry.

And when these twa auld champions cam‚Äô

A-rinnin‚Äô side by side.

There were three feet between their brows,

And their shouthers three yards wide.

They rin ower hills, they rin ower dales,

They ran ower m ountains high ,

Until they cam‚Äô to London toon

At the dawn o‚Äô the third day.

And when they cam‚Äô to London‚Äôs gates

Bound wi‚Äô an iron band;

Wha should they see but a trumpeter

Wi‚Äô a trumpet in his hands.

‚ÄúO, what√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the matter, ye keepers all,

And what‚Äôs the matter within,

That the drums do beat and the bells do ring

And raak√Ę¬Ä¬ô sic a doleful din=‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs naithin√Ę¬Ä¬ô the matter,‚ÄĚ the keeper said,

‚ÄúThere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs naithin√Ę¬Ä¬ô that matters to thee.

But a muckle Scot‚Äôs to stretch a rope

And this mom he maun dee .

‚ÄúThen open the gates, ye keepers proud,

Open without delay!‚ÄĚ

But the keeper winked his e‚Äôe and said,

‚ÄúI hanna got the key.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOpen the gates, ye keepers proud,

Open without delay!

For here‚Äôs a laddie at my back

Frae Scotland√Ę¬Ä¬ôs brocht the key.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYe√Ę¬Ä¬ôll open the gates,‚ÄĚ says Jock o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Noth

‚ÄúYe√Ę¬Ä¬ôll open them when I ca√Ę¬Ä¬ô!‚ÄĚ

And wi‚Äô his foot he has drove in

Three yards‚Äô breadth o‚Äô the wa‚Äô.

They gaed in by Drury Lane

And doon by the toon‚Äô ha‚Äô,

And there they saw young Johnnie More

Stand on the English wall.

‚ÄúYe√Ę¬Ä¬ôre welcome here, my uncle dear,

Ye‚Äôre welcome here to me;

Come loose the knot and slack the rope

And set me frae the tree.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO is√Ę¬Ä¬ôt for murder or for theft

Or is‚Äôt for robbery?

If ye‚Äôve injured ony wee folk here

There√Ę¬Ä¬ôs nae remeid for thee.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs no√Ę¬Ä¬ô for murder nor for theft,

It‚Äôs no‚Äô for robbery;

It‚Äôs a‚Äô for lovin‚Äô a bonnie lass

They mean to gar me dee.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThen whaur√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the lady?‚ÄĚ says Jock o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Noth,

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs fain I wad her see.‚ÄĚ

She‚Äôs lockit in her ain chamber

And the king he keeps the key.

So they hae gane before the king

Wi‚Äô courage bold and free,

And their armour bricht cast sic a licht

It almost blint his e‚Äôe .

‚ÄúO whaur√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the lass?‚ÄĚ says Jock o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Noth,

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs fain we wad her see.

We‚Äôve come to see her bedded

Fae the foot o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Benachie.‚ÄĚ

‚Äú O tak√Ę¬Ä¬ô the lady,‚ÄĚ says the king,

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôre welcome a√Ę¬Ä¬ô for me.

But I never thought to see sic lads

Fae the foot o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Bennachie.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIf I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd hae kent ,‚ÄĚ says Jock o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Noth,

‚ÄúYe√Ę¬Ä¬ôd hae been surprised at me,

I‚Äôd wad hae brocht my brither,

He‚Äôs three times as big as me.

‚ÄúLikewise, if I had thocht that I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd

Been sic a fright tae thee,

I‚Äôd hae brocht young Jock o‚Äô Erskine Park

He√Ę¬Ä¬ôs thirty foot and three.‚ÄĚ

‚Äú Wae be to the bonnie boy,‚ÄĚ said the king,

‚ÄúThat brocht the news to thee.

Let a‚Äô England say what it will,

High hangit he shall be.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO, if ye hang that bonnie boy

That brocht the news to me,

W e‚Äôll come to the funeral

And we will bury thee.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO tak√Ę¬Ä¬ô the lady,‚ÄĚ says the king,

And let the bonnie boy be.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA priest, a priest!‚ÄĚ young Johnnie cried,

‚ÄúTo join my love and me.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA clerk, a clerk!‚ÄĚ the king he cried,

‚ÄúTo seal her tocher wi√Ę¬Ä¬ô.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWe need nae clerk,‚ÄĚ auld Johnnie said,

‚ÄúWe need nae gear fae thee.

‚ÄúFor I hae cows and ewes enough,

And fifty plows and three,

And a hunder horse to pu‚Äô them wi‚Äô

At the foot o‚Äô Benachie.

‚ÄúHae ye ony masons at your court

Or ony at your ca‚Äô?

Ye‚Äôd better noo send some o‚Äô them

To mend your English wa√Ę¬Ä¬ô.‚ÄĚ

So auld Johnnie More and young Johnnie More

And Jock o‚Äô Noth, a‚Äô three,

And the English lady and the little wee boy

Went back to Benachie.

versions.title: Martin Carthy sings Long John, Old John and Jackie North
versions.lyrics:
Now Long John‚Äôs from the mountain gone, he‚Äôs to London town,

And the king‚Äôs daughter in fair London she fell in love with him.

Now Long John was a giant born, he was fourteen feet in height,

And the king‚Äôs daughter she wept for him as she laid alone at night.

And when the king he heard of this an angry man was he,

Says, ‚ÄúThis mighty man shall stretch the rope that hangs on the gallows tree!‚ÄĚ

So he sent men and cunning men and around him they did creep

And they fed him drops of laudanum and they laid him fast asleep.

So that when he‚Äôs awaked out of his sleep a sorry man was he,

With his jaws and hands in iron bands and his feet in fetters three.

So he‚Äôs bribed him a servant, Long John he‚Äôs given him meat and fee

To run to his uncle Old John to come and rescue he.

And the first few miles the little boy walked and the next few miles he ran

And he run till he come to the broad water where he lay down and swam.

And when he come to the mountain high he cried out aloud

For there he spied him Jackie North with Old John by his side.

And there as these two giants stood a grisly sight to see

For they were tall as the eagles call and broad as the oaken tree.

‚ÄúOh rise rise, Old John, Jackie North, come thee!

For Long John√Ę¬Ä¬ôs in prison strong and hanged he must be.‚ÄĚ

So they ran over hill and they ran over dale and they ran over mountain high

Till they come down to London town at the dawning of the day.

They cried upon up your city gates, ‚ÄúOpen at my call!‚ÄĚ

Then they up with their feet and they kicked a hole straight in through London Wall.

And they trampled down by Drury Lane, the crowd before them ran,

Till there they spied him, Long John stood under the gallows pin.

They said, ‚ÄúIs it for murder? Is it for rape? Is it for robbery?

For if it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs any heinous crime we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll stand and watch you die.‚ÄĚ

He says, ‚ÄúNot for murder, not for rape, not for robbery.

But it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs all for the love of a gay lady they are here to see me die.‚ÄĚ

So they took him from the gallows pin, before the king went they

Their armour bright cast such a light it fair dazzled his eye.

‚ÄúGood day to you,‚ÄĚ cries Jackie North, ‚ÄúGood day to you,‚ÄĚ cries he,

‚ÄúFor we have come for your daughter√Ę¬Ä¬ôs wedding all down from the mountains high.‚ÄĚ

When the king he seen them come an angry man was he,

Cries, ‚ÄúOne of you is tall enough what shall I do with three?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh cursed be that serving boy the tidings bore away.

For I do vow and I do swear high hanged he shall be.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh if you hang this little boy the tidings brought to me

We three shall come to his burial and paid you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll surely be.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúA priest, a priest,‚ÄĚ Long John he cries, ‚Äúto join my love and me,

A priest, a priest,‚ÄĚ Long John he cries, ‚Äúfor married we will be.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh take my daughter, Long John, my curse upon you fall.

And take my serving boy also lest all my city fall.‚ÄĚ

They‚Äôve taken the lady by the hand, set her prison free

And the drums did beat and the fifes did play, they spent the night with glee.

And then Long John and Old John, Jackie North all three,

A freed bride and a serving boy went back to the mountains high.

items.title: Moses of the Mail
items.notes:
Moses of the Mail

[

; Mudcat 128515

; Ewan MacColl]

Karl Dallas:

One Hundred Songs of Toil

Ewan MacColl:

The Shuttle and Cage

Ewan MacColl recorded his own song

Moses of the Mail

in 1951 for a 78rpm record (Topic TRC51).

He published this song in 1954 in his book
items.lyrics:
It was a dark and stormy night and snow was falling fast,

I stood on Thorpbridge Junction where the reckless Moses passed.

His hair was widely waving as through the air he sped;

He‚Äôd never had such doings since he startet at the shed.

The signals on at Newton Heath, the shed was close at hand;

He sent his mate for some more oil and a couple of bags of sand.

At Moston√Ę¬Ä¬ôs dreary cutting the struggle was extreme ‚Äď

Both front sanders failed to work and the engine wouldn‚Äôt steam.

On passing Hopwood Cabin he heard the engine growl,

And reaching for the tallow pot he broke his collarbone.

When Castleton appeared in view he shook his weary head

And stepping over to his mate this is what he said:

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôve worked upon the L&Y for forty years or more

But such a wretched night as this I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve never had before.‚ÄĚ

At Hebden Bridge they stopped the train some wagons to reload

And Moses shouted to his mate, ‚ÄúWe√Ę¬Ä¬ôre off the blooming road!‚ÄĚ

Up came old Moses, stick in hand, his head hung down with grief;

He viewed the scene contemptously, then wired for relief.

‚ÄúPray don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt lie iron hands on me,‚ÄĚ poor Valentine did exclaim,

‚ÄúI know you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve done your very best, I know you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre not to blame.‚ÄĚ

Now flowers may bud and bloom in spring and memories fade away,

But they will not forget that night until their dying day.

But when I‚Äôm dead and laid to rest place on my grave sweet roses,

These were, I‚Äôm told, the very last pathetic words of Moses.

items.title: Moving On Song
items.notes:
Moving On Song

[

; Ballad Index RcMovOn

; DT MOVEON

; Mudcat 116059

; Ewan MacColl]

Ewan MacColl‚Äôs

Moving On Song

is a song from the

BBC radio ballad

The Travelling People ,

first broadcast on 17 April 1964.
items.lyrics:
Born in the middle of the afternoon

In a horse-drawn wagon on the old A5;

The big twelve-wheeler shook me bed,

‚ÄúYou can√Ę¬Ä¬ôt stop here,‚ÄĚ the policeman said,

Chorus (after each verse):

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôd better get born in some place else,

So move along, get along!

Move along, get along!

Go! Move! Shift!‚ÄĚ

Born in the tattie-lifting time

In an old bow-tent near a tattie field;

The feirmer said, ‚ÄúThe wark√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a√Ę¬Ä¬ô done,

It√Ę¬Ä¬ôs time that you was moving on,‚ÄĚ

Born on a common near a building site,

Where the ground is rutted by the trailers‚Äô wheels;

The local people said to me,

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôll lower the price of property,‚ÄĚ

Wagon, tent or trailer born,

Last week, last year or in far-off days;

Born here or a thousand miles away,

There‚Äôs always men nearby who say,

Born at the back of a blackthorn hedge,

When the white hoar frost lay all around;

No Eastern kings came bearing gifts,

Instead the order came to shift.

The winter sky was hung with stars

And one shone brighter than the rest;

The wise men came, so stern and strict

And brought the order to evict.

items.title: North Sea Holes
items.notes:
North Sea Holes

[Ewan MacColl]

North Sea Holes was written for the third of the eight

BBC radio ballads by Ewan MacColl, Charles Parker and Peggy Seeger,

Singing the Fishing

(first broadcast on 16 August 1960, released on an Argo LP in 1966

and now available on a Topic CD).

It celebrates the herring fishermen of the steam-drifter era.

Ewan MacColl and chorus sang North Sea Holes

in 1971 on The Critics Group‚Äôs Argo album

As We Were A-Sailing .

Danny Spooner and Gordon McIntyre sang North Sea Holes
items.lyrics:
Come all you gallant fishermen

That plough the stormy sea

The whole year round

On the fishing grounds

Of the Northern Minch and the Norway Deeps,

On the banks and knolls of the North Sea holes,

Where the herring shoals are found.

It‚Äôs there you‚Äôll find the Norfolk boys

And lads from Peterhead,

There‚Äôs Buckie chiels and men from Shields

On the Northern Minch and the Norway Deeps,

On the banks and knolls of the North Sea Holes,

Where the herring shoals are found.

From Fraserburgh and Aberdeen,

From Whitby, Yarmouth Town,

The fleet‚Äôs away at the break of day

To the Northern Minch and the Norway Deeps,

To the banks and knolls of the North Sea Holes,

Where the herring shoals are found.

It‚Äôs off with a boiler full of steam and your engine spic and span

To fish the grounds the North Sea round.

And fish and knolls and the North Sea Holes,

Try your luck at the North Shields Gut,

Wi a catch of a hundred cran.

No need to wait for the wind and tide, you‚Äôre the master of the sea,

Come calm or squall, just shoot and haul,

And fill the hold with the fish to be sold,

And steam ahead for the curing shed.

And the buyers on the Yarmouth quay.

items.title: Song of the Iron Road
items.notes:
>

Song of the Iron Road

Song of the Iron Road

[

; DT IRONROAD

; Mudcat 77136

; Ewan MacColl]

Ewan MacColl wrote

Song of the Iron Road

for the 1958 Radio Ballad
items.lyrics:
The iron road‚Äôs a hard road and the work is never ending

Working night and day on the iron way

We‚Äôre the boys who keep the engines rolling

You sigh on at the local shed, they put you to the cleaning

In your dungarees, cleaning super dees

You‚Äôre a sweeper-upper, brewer-upper, shovel slinger,

spanner bringer Steam raiser, fire dropper, general cook and bottle washer

Learning how to keep ‚Äôem rolling

When you‚Äôve done your time at the loco shed and had your share of trouble

On the old footplate, you‚Äôre the driver‚Äôs mate

And you‚Äôre married to a lousy shovel

It‚Äôs check the water, check the tools and chuck the blooming coal in

Give the gauge a wipe, check injector pipe, now it‚Äôs

Swing your shovel at the double, give her rock watch the clock

Steam rising, sweat running, back aching, bone shaking

Fireman, fireman, keep her rolling

When you‚Äôve shoveled a million tons of coal some ten or twelve years later

And your only dream is of raising steam

Then they hand to you your drivers papers

The iron road is a hard road and the work is never ending

Working night and day on the iron way, we‚Äôre the

Local drivers, early riser, lodging turners, mile burners

Eleven quid a week earners

We‚Äôre the boys that keep ‚Äôem rolling

items.title: Sweet Thames Flow Softly
items.notes:
Sweet Thames Flow Softly

[

; DT SWTHAMES

; Mudcat 702

; Ewan MacColl]

Ewan MacColl wrote

Sweet Thames Flow Softly

for an experimental production by the Critics Group,

based on Romeo and Juliet ,

which was broadcast to schools in May 1966.

It was published in 1967 as title track of the Critics Group‚Äôs Argo album

Sweet Thames Flow Softly ,
items.lyrics:
I met my girl at Woolwich Pier beneath the big crane standing

And all the love I felt for her it passed all understanding

Took her sailing on the river, flow sweet river flow

London town was mine to give her, sweet Thames flow softly

Made the Thames into a crown, flow sweet river flow

Made a brooch of Silvertown , sweet Thames flow softly

At London Yard I held her hand, at Blackwell Point I faced her

At the Isle of Dogs I kissed her mouth and tenderly embraced her

Heard the bells of Greenwich ringing, flow sweet river flow

All the time my heart was singing, sweet Thames flow softly

Limehouse Reach I gave her there, flow sweet river flow

As a ribbon for her hair, sweet Thames flow softly

From Shadwell Dock to Nine Elms Reach we cheek to cheek were dancing

A necklace made of London Bridge her beauty was enhancing

Kissed her once again at Wapping, flow sweet river flow

After that there was no stopping, sweet Thames flow softly

Richmond Park it was a ring, flow sweet river flow

I‚Äôd have given her anything, sweet Thames flow softly

From Rotherhithe to Putney Bridge my love I was declaring

And she from Kew to Isleworth her love for me was swearing

Love it set my heart a-burning, flow sweet river flow

Never saw the tide was turning, sweet Thames flow softly

Gave her Hampton Court to twist, flow sweet river flow

Into a bracelet for her wrist, sweet Thames flow softly

But now, alas, the tide has changed, my love she has gone from me

And winter‚Äôs frost has touched my heart and put a blight upon me

Creeping fog is on the river, flow sweet river flow

Sun and moon and stars gone with her, sweet Thames flow softly

Swift the Thames runs to the sea, flow sweet river flow

Bearing ships and part of me, sweet Thames flow softly

items.title: The Ballad of Springhill / Springhill Disaster
items.notes:
[

; Ballad Index FSWB124A

; DT SPRINGHI

; Mudcat 1714

, 115567

; Maurice Ruddick; Peggy Seeger, Ewan MacColl; Chris Stuart]

There are several different songs about the 23 October 1958

‚Äúbump‚ÄĚ collapse at No. 2 Colliery of the Cumberland mines in

Springhill, Nova Scotia .

Maurice Ruddick, a 46 year-old African Canadian, was one of the seven last

miners to be found alive after the disaster. The poem he wrote about it

was expanded by Bill Clifton with local friends and musicians, Paul Clayton
items.lyrics:
The twenty-third of October, we‚Äôll remember that day;

Down the shaft underground in our usual way.

In the Cumberland pit how the rafters crashed down

And the black hell closed ‚Äôround us ‚Äôway down in the ground.

Now when the news reached our good neighbors nearby,

The rescue work started; our hopes were still high.

But the last bit of hope like our lamps soon burned dim;

In the three-foot high dungeon we joined in a hymn

In that dark, black hole in the ground.

Only God will ever know all that happened down there.

How we watched Percy Rector die gasping a prayer,

And young Clarke had his birthday, he thought, in his grave;

After days of cruel torture we‚Äôd no hopes to be saved.

We sang altogether, though racked through with pain.

When they broke through we knew that our prayers weren‚Äôt in vain.

I crawled through the tunnel, they helped me along.

I said: ‚ÄúGive me some water and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll sing you a song

Of that dark, black hole in the ground!‚ÄĚ

I‚Äôll sing you a song of the bravest of men,

Of those who remained to go digging again

To bring the coal up from ten thousand feet deep,

And the others who stayed there forever to sleep.

Oh, be thankful you fellows brought back from the dead,

And pray for your friends who have gone on ahead.

And you boys up in heaven as you look on down,

Don‚Äôt forget to remember Springhill mining town

And that dark, black hole in the ground!

versions.title: Martin Carthy sings Springhill Mine Disaster
versions.lyrics:
In the town of Springhill, Nova Scotia,

Deep in the heart of the Cumberland Mine;

There‚Äôs blood on the coal and the miners lie

In roads that never saw sun nor sky,

Roads that never saw sun nor sky.

In the town of Springhill, you don‚Äôt sleep easy,

Often the earth would tremble and roll;

When the earth is restless, miners die,

Bone and blood is the price of coal,

Bone and blood is the price of coal.

In the town of Springhill, Nova Scotia,

Late in the year of fifty-eight,

The day still comes and the sun still shines

But it‚Äôs dark as the grave in the Cumberland mine,

Dark as the grave in the Cumberland mine.

Down at the coal face, miners working,

Rattle of the belts and the cutter blades;

Then a rumble of rock and the walls close round

Living and the dead men two miles down,

Living and the dead men two miles down.

Twelve men lay two miles from the pitshaft,

Twelve men lay in the dark and sang;

Long hot days in the miner‚Äôs tomb,

It was three foot wide by a hundred long,

Three foot wide by a hundred long.

Three days passed and the lights gave out

When the leading man got up and said,

‚ÄúThere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs no more water nor light nor bread,

So we‚Äôll live on songs of hope instead,

Live on songs of hope instead.‚ÄĚ

Listen for the shouts of the bare-face miners,

Listen through the rubble for the rescue team;

Six hundred feet of coal and slag,

Hope imprisoned in a three-foot seam,

Hope imprisoned in a three-foot seam.

Eight days passed and some were rescued,

Leaving the rest to die alone;

Through all their lives they dug a grave,

Two miles of earth for a marking stone,

Two miles of earth for a marking stone.

items.title: The Card Song / Tom Brown
items.notes:

Katherine Campbell:

Frank Kidson:
items.lyrics:
Oh! the king will take the queen; but the queen will take the knave:

And since we‚Äôre all together, boys, we‚Äôll have a merry stave.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Here‚Äôs to you Tom Brown,

Here‚Äôs to you, with all my heart!

We‚Äôll have another glass, my boys,

At least, before we part:

Here‚Äôs to you Tom Brown,

Here‚Äôs to you Tom Brown

The queen will take the knave, but the knave will take the ten;

And since we‚Äôre all together, boys, we‚Äôll keep it up like men.

The knave will take the ten, but the ten will take the nine;

And since we‚Äôre all together, boys, we‚Äôll drink the best of wine.

As the verse-making is entirely dependent upon the singer,

the reader will be able to readily support the missing links.
It concludes‚ÄĒ

The four will take the trey, but the trey will take the deuce;

And since we‚Äôre all together, boys, we‚Äôll never cry a truce.

The tray will take the deuce, but the ace will take them all;

And since we‚Äôre all together, boys, we won‚Äôt go home at all.

the trey will take the deuce, the ace will take them all

items.title: The Coachman
items.notes:
The Coachman

[

/ Song Subject MAS334

; Ballad Index K172

; Bodleian

; Mudcat 170649

; trad.]

Sam Larner sang The Jolly Young Coachman

to Ewan MacColl and Peggy Seeger in 1961. This recording was included

in 2014 on his Musical Traditions anthology

Cruising Round Yarmouth .

Rod Stradling noted:
items.lyrics:
I once was a jolly young coachman,

But my wages I tried to advance;

I once took a trip up to London

And then I went over to France.

I‚Äôve learn√©d all sorts of driving,

I‚Äôll drive them in the fashion so gay.

If there‚Äôs any young lady want riding,

I‚Äôll ride her the new-fashioned way.

The first one I met was a young lady;

She was dressed in her finest, so gay.

She had a nice little white pony,

And likewise a neat carriage.

‚ÄúThree guineas a week I will pay you,

And I‚Äôll dress you the finest, so gay.

But remember, young man, if I hire you,

You must ride me the new-fashioned way.‚ÄĚ

Righ fal-the-ral-ural-I-ady

Righ fal-the-ral-ural-I-day

‚ÄúRemember, young man, if I hire you,

You must ride me the new-fashioned way.‚ÄĚ

She took me down in her cellar

And she gave me the liquor so quick

We hadn‚Äôt been long in the cellar,

When she wanted to look at my whip.

She took it, she mauled it all over,

And laying it down with a smile,

She said, ‚ÄúTo look at your whip, Sir,

I think you can drive me a while.‚ÄĚ

Righ fal-the-ral-ural-I-ady

Righ fal-the-ral-ural-I-day

‚ÄúI think to look at your whip, Sir,

I think you can drive me a while.‚ÄĚ

Three times I entered her coach-box,

And the wheel went spinning around,

One sudden crack of the whip, Sir,

She lost the main stay of her gown.

Righ fal-the-ral-ural-I-ady

Righ fal-the-ral-ural-I-day

One sudden crack of the whip, Sir,

She lost the main stay of her gay.

Spoken: Now that ‚Ä¶ I knew that when I was a little boy,
when I first went to sea, they would sing that in sail ‚Ä¶

versions.title: John Roberts and Tony Barrand sing The Coachman
versions.lyrics:
I once took a job as a coachman

My money was paid in advance

So I traveled first down to Dover

And then I crossed over to France

I was met by a charming young lady

Who clothed me in breeches so tight

Said, I see I have got a fine coachman

To drive me by day and by night:

Chorus (after each verse):

She was such a charming young lady

All in the height of her bloom

And me being a dashing young coachman

I drove her ten times ‚Äôround the room

She first took me down to the cellar

And filled me with liquor so quick

She told me to drink in a hurry

Then she asked for a look at my whip

She held it, viewed it a moment

And then laid it down with a smile

Said, I can see by the length and the look of your slash

You can drive in the old-fashioned style:

She bade me get into position

So I climbed right in to the seat

Three swishes I gave with my cracker

And drove her right down the High Street

I handled my whip with good judgment

Until I was sure of her ways

But the very first tug that I gave on the brakes

I broke the main spring of her stays:

When my mistress grew tired or grew weary

And wanted to take a short rest

She‚Äôd call for her servant maid, Sally

She‚Äôd say, Sally, we‚Äôve got a fine coachman

He understands driving in style

While the spring on my chassis‚Äôs being strengthened again

I‚Äôll let him drive you for a while:

items.title: The First Time Ever I Saw Your Face
items.notes:
The First Time Ever I Saw Your Face

[

; DT FIRSTIME

; Mudcat 143527

; Ewan MacColl]

Ewan MacColl wrote The First Time Ever I Saw Your Face

in 1957 for his then lover‚ÄĒand later wife‚ÄĒPeggy Seeger.

It is said that he wrote and taught it to her over the telephone

when she needed a song for a play she was cast for.

Peggy Seeger sang and recorded this song lots of time but I know of

no recording of Ewan MacColl‚Äôs. The version on the MacColl anthology

Black and White
items.lyrics:
The first time ever I saw your face

I thought the sun rose in your eyes,

And the moon and the stars were the gifts you gave

To the dark and the empty skies, my love,

To the dark and the empty skies.

The first time ever I kissed your mouth

I felt the earth turn in my hand,

Like the trembling heart of a captive bird

That was there at my command, my love,

That was there at my command.

The first time ever I lay with you

And felt your heart beat close to mine,

I thought our joy would fill the earth

And would last till the end of time, my love,

And would last till the end of time.

items.title: The Gresford Disaster
items.notes:
>

The Albion Band >

Songs >

Gresford Disaster

The Gresford Disaster

[

; Ballad Index PBB116

; Folkinfo 866

; DT GRESFORD

; Mudcat 73638

; trad.]

Karl Dallas:
items.lyrics:
You‚Äôve heard of the Gresford disaster,

And the terrible price that was paid.

Two hundred and forty-two colliers were lost,

And three men of a rescue brigade.

It occurred in the month of September,

At three in the morning, that pit

Was racked by a violent explosion

In the Dennis where gas lay so thick.

The gas in the Dennis deep section

Was packed there like snow in a drift,

And many a man had to leave the coal-face

Before he had worked out his shift.

A fortnight before the explosion.

To the shotfirer, Tomlinson cried:

‚ÄúIf you fire that shot we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be all blown to hell!‚ÄĚ

And no one can say that he lied.

The fireman‚Äôs reports they are missing.

The records of forty-two days;

The colliery manager had them destroyed

To cover his criminal ways.

Down there in the dark they are lying,

They died for nine shillings a day.

They have worked out their shift and now they must

In the darkness until Judgement Day.

The Lord Mayor of London‚Äôs collecting

To help both our children and wives.

The owners have sent some white lilies

To pay for the poor colliers‚Äô lives.

Farewell, our dear wives and our children,

Farewell, our old comrades as well.

Don‚Äôt send your sons down the dark dreary pit;

They‚Äôll be damned like the sinners in hell.

‚ÄúNae mair I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll gang a-rovin√Ę¬Ä¬ô,

Though the moon shines e√Ę¬Ä¬ôer sae bright.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúFor if ye had been a decent lass,
as I took you to be,

I would a‚Äô made you the queen
of a√Ę¬Ä¬ô the counterie.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO tak you this, my bonny lass,
for to pay the nurse√Ę¬Ä¬ôs fee.‚ÄĚ

Nae mair I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll gang a-rovin√Ę¬Ä¬ô ‚Ä¶

versions.title: Charlotte Renals sings The Beggar
versions.lyrics:
There was a poor old beggar man,
come tripling over the plain;

He called into a farmhouse,
some lodgings for to gain.

Chorus (after each verse):

Rove along, rove a-lee,

Rove a-rangle rover thee.

The old farmer he came out,
and he searched the beggar around;

He said you are a dirty beggar man,
and no lodgings can be found.

Then the daughter she came out,
and she searched the beggar around;

She says he‚Äôs not a dirty beggar,
and so lodgings can be found.

She took him into the barn,
and she made him a bed with hay;

She made it soft and easy,
and along with him she lay.

She rose early next morning,
before the break of day;

She took him into her father‚Äôs house,
to make him a cup of tea.

The old farmer he came down,
he began to curse and swear;

He picked hold of the beggar‚Äôs bag,
and he dashed it against the wall;

May the Devil have the beggar‚Äôs bag,
and make them fair and all.

‚ÄúOh hooley, hooley wi me sir, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll waken oor guid man.‚ÄĚ

The beggar being a cunning loon, it‚Äôs ne‚Äôer a word he spak,

Til he had had his will o her then he began to craic.

‚ÄúHave ye onie dogs aboot the hoose, or ony cats ava?

For I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm feart they√Ę¬Ä¬ôll reive my mealy pyokes afore I gang away.‚ÄĚ

She‚Äôs ta‚Äôen the meal pyokes in her hand, she‚Äôs thrown them o‚Äôer the wa‚Äô,

‚ÄúThe de√Ę¬Ä¬ôil gang wi yer mealy pyokes, my maidenhead√Ę¬Ä¬ôs awa.‚ÄĚ

He‚Äôs ta‚Äôen a trumpet frae his side, he‚Äôs blown baith loud and shrill,

And four and twenty belted knights cam riding o‚Äôer the hill.

He‚Äôs ta‚Äôen the penknife frae his pooch, let a‚Äô his duddies fa‚Äô,

And he was the brawest belted knight that wis among them a‚Äô.

‚ÄúIf ye had been a decent lass, as I ta√Ę¬Ä¬ôen ye tae be,

Then I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd hae made o ye the Queen o√Ę¬Ä¬ôer a√Ę¬Ä¬ô the hail countrie.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Fiona Ross sings Davy Faa
versions.lyrics:
There was a wealthy farmer lived in the north country

He had a lovely dochter, she was always frank and free

And it‚Äôs day by day and night by night she was always in my e‚Äôe

There cam a rovin tinker lad tae this farm hoose

Have ye ony pots or pans or candlesticks to mend

Or have ye ony lodgins for me, a single man?

Noo the farmer he thocht it nae hairm the tinker for tae keep

The lassie she thocht it nae hairm the tinker‚Äôs bed tae mak

But the bonnie laddie followed her and he did bar the door

He‚Äôs catched her by the middle sma and laid her on the floor

He‚Äôs catched her by the middle sma and up against the wa

And it‚Äôs there he‚Äôs ta‚Äôen the wills o her afore she‚Äôs won awa

And it‚Äôs oh the bonnie lassie blushed, and oh but she thocht shame

Since ye‚Äôve ta‚Äôen the wills o me, come tell tae me your name

He‚Äôs whispered in the lassie‚Äôs ear, they call me Davy Faa

Ye‚Äôll aye mind upon the happy nicht, amang the pease straw

Six weeks has passed and gane, this maid grew white and pale

Nine months and better brocht her forth a bonnie son

Since the bairnie‚Äôs born said she, we‚Äôll call him Davy Faa

We‚Äôll aye mind upon the happy nicht amang the pease straw

Ony man that weds my girl, I‚Äôll gie him gold quite free

Ony man that weds my girl, I‚Äôll gie him farms three

For although she‚Äôs tint her maidenhead, what the waur is she?

items.title: The Jolly Highwayman / (Alan) Tyne of Harrow / Valentine O‚ÄôHara
items.notes:

/ Song Subject MAS952

The Constant Lovers

The Bodleian Libraries have broadsides of the then still unnamed

Daring Highwayman

dated between either 1819 and 1844 or 1828 and 1829.
items.lyrics:
I am a daring highwayman, likewise a gallant rover,

From London town I surely came since I became a rover,

For a maid who proved with child for England I sailed over,

I left my parents almost wild since I became a rover.

I am a daring highwayman likewise a gallant rover,

From London Town I surely came since I became a rover,

For a maid who proved with child for England I sailed over,

I left my parents almost wild since I became a rover.

How many battles I‚Ä≤ve been in thro‚Äô France and Spain and Flanders,

And always fought with courage bold under my brave commanders,

But thro‚Ä≤ the usage I received no one shall ever control me,

Resolved for my liberty strong walls never should hold me.

Back to England‚Äôs shores I came as fast as wind could blow me,

Resolved for my liberty, no one should e‚Äôer control me,

Back to Englands‚Äôs shores I came and found my name deserted

My parent‚Äôs heart was almost broke and I was much more frightened.

For want of money and a friend then poverty come on me,

For want of money and a friend it brought destruction on me,

The very first man I ever robbed it was a lord of honour

The nobleman I did insult all in a roguish manner.

Give me your gold my lord and make no more denial,

If you resist it is my design with powder and ball to fire,

I put my pistol to his breast which made him for to shiver

Two hundred pounds in brightest gold to me he did deliver.

Besides a gold repeating watch to me he did surrender,

I thought I had a noble prize to be thus befriended,

With a hundred guineas in bright gold I bought a famous gelding

He could jump over the turnpike gate I bought him off Jem Sheldon.

Now mounted on my gallant steed I looked bold and daring

Resolved on the road to go no man I ever did fear him,

The very next man I robbed it was in Covent Garden,

And in two hours after in Newgate I was fasten‚Äôd.

I have robbed both lords and dukes of silver plate and money,

All for to maintain myself and for my dearest Polly,

But now in Newgate cells I lie until I am convicted,

For my folly I now mercy crave for I am sore afflicted.

versions.title: Ewan MacColl sings Tyne o‚Äô Harrow
versions.lyrics:
I am a daring highwayman; my name is Tyne o‚Äô Harrow.

I come of poor but honest folks nigh tae the hills o‚Äô Yarrow.

For getting o‚Äô a maid with child, for England I sailed over.

I left my parents and became a wild and daring rover.

Straight to London I did go where I became a soldier,

Resolved to fight Britannia‚Äôs foes; no sergeant-at-arms was bolder.

They sent me tae a foreign land whaur cannons loud did rattle.

Believe me, lads, I do not boast how I behaved in battle.

Many‚Äôs the battle I‚Äôve been in, in Holland and French Flanders;

I always fought with courage keen, led on by brave commanders.

But a cruel ensign called me out and I was flogged and carted;

Cruel the usage they gave me, and so I soon deserted.

Straight for England I set sail as fast as wind could heave me,

Resolved that of my liberty there should no man deprive me.

I slipped intae the fields by night, by all my friends forsaken,

I could not walk the road by day for fear I should be taken.

Being of a courage keen and likewise able bodied,

I robbed Lord Lowndes on the King‚Äôs highway with my pistols heavy loaded.

I clapped my pistol tae his breast which set him all a-quiver,

Five hundred pounds in ready gold to me he did deliver.

With part of my new store of gold I bought a famous gelding

That could jump over a five-barred gate; I bought him from Ned Fielding.

Lord Arkinstone intae his coach I robbed near Covent Garden,

And two hours later that same night I robbed the Earl of Warren.

One night at Turnham Green I robbed a revenue collector,

And what I took from him I gave to a widow to protect her.

I always robbed the rich and great, to rob the poor I scorn-ed,

But now in iron chains I‚Äôm bound, in doom I now lie born-ed.

For straight in Newgate I‚Äôm confined and by the law convicted.

To hang on Tyburn tree‚Äôs my fate, of which I‚Äôm much affrighted.

Fareweel, my friends and countrymen and my native hills of Yarrow.

Kind providence may test the soul of Alan Tyne o Harrow.

versions.title: Peter Bellamy sings Tyne of Harrow
versions.lyrics:
I am a gallant highwayman; my name is Tyne of Harrow.

‚ÄôTwas for getting of a maid with child, to England I came over.

I left my parents and became a wild and daring rover.

Resolved to fight Britannia‚Äôs foes; no champion could be bolder.

They sailed me to a foreign land where the cannon loud did rattle.

And believe me, lads, I do not boast how I behaved in battle.

For many‚Äôs the battle I was in, in Holland and French Flanders;

I always fought with a courage keen, led on by brave commanders.

But a cruel ensign called me out and I was flogged and carted;

Cruel the usage they gave me, and so I soon deserted.

And straight for England I set sail as fast as wind could heave me,

I slept by night in auburn fields, by all old friends forsaken,

But being of a courage keen and likewise able bodied,

Well, I robbed Lord Lowndes on the King‚Äôs highway with my pistols heavy loaded.

I clapped my pistols to his breast which caused him for to quiver,

With part of my new store of gold I bought a famous gelding

That could jump o‚Äôer a five-bar gate; I bought it from Ned Fielding.

Lord Arkinstone in his fine coach I robbed at Covent Garden,

And two hours later the same night I robbed the Earl of Warren.

And one night by Turnham Green I robbed a revenue collector,

And what I took form him I gave to a widow to protect her.

For I always robbed the rich and great, for to rob the poor I scorn-ed,

But now they leave me to my fate, in iron chains adorn-ed.

Yes, it‚Äôs straight in Newgate I am bound and by the laws convicted.

For to hang on Tyburn tree‚Äôs my fate, of which I‚Äôm much affrighted.

Farewell, my friends and countrymen and my native hills of Yarrow.

Kind providence will test the soul of Alan Tyne of Harrow.

versions.title: Tim Radford recitates The Jolly Highwayman
versions.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of a Jolly Highwayman, likewise a noted rover

I drove my parents almost wild when I first went a roving.

I rob‚Äôed lords, I rob‚Äôed dukes in a very rakish manner

Not only to maintain myself likewise my aged mother.

The very first man that I did rob, it being a lord of honour

I was resolved the roads to go, not one could come a-near me.

‚ÄúDeliver your money my lord,‚ÄĚ said I, ‚Äúwithout any more desire,

For if you don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs my delight some powder and shot to fire.‚ÄĚ

I put a pistol to his breast, which made him for to shiver

Ten hundred guineas all in bright gold to me he did deliver.

Besides a gold repeater watch to me he did surrender

I thought I had a noble prize to me he did deliver.

The very next man that I did rob, was down in Commons garden,

versions.title: Daoir√≠ Farrell sings Valentine O‚ÄôHara
versions.lyrics:
I am a gallant highwayman called Valentine O‚ÄôHara,

And I come from poor but decent folk nigh to the Hill of Tara.

By the getting of a maid with child to England I went over;

I left my parents and I became a wild and daring rover.

Well it‚Äôs straight to England I did go where I became a soldier,

Resolved to fight Britannia‚Äôs foe, not Hector Great was bolder.

I fought all in some foreign shores where cannons loud did rattle,

Believe me boys I do not boast how I behaved in battle.

Well it‚Äôs many‚Äôs the battle I fought in, in Holland and French Flanders;

But I always fought with a courage keen led on by great commanders.

Until a cruel ensign found me out and I was flogged and carted,

Oh, cruel usage they gave me and so I soon departed.

Well it‚Äôs straight back to England I did go as fast as winds would drive me,

Resolved that of my liberty no man could e‚Äôer deny me.

But I slept out in the fields at night by all my friends forsaken,

I dare not walk the road by day for fear I might be taken.

But I being of a courage keen and likewise able bodied,

I robbed Lord Nounce on the King‚Äôs highway with pistols heavy loaded.

I placed my pistols to his breast which caused his heart to quiver,

Five hundred pounds in ready gold to me he did deliver.

Well with part of my new store of gold I bought a famous gelding

That could jump over a five bar gate and I bought him from Ned Fielding.

Lord Arkenstone all in his coach I robbed near Covent Garden

And two hours later that same night I robbed the Earl of Warren.

In Turnham Green I next did rob the revenue collector

And what I took from him I gave to a widow to protect her.

I always robbed the rich and great for to rob the poor I scorned,

And now in iron chains I‚Äôm bound and doomed I now lie burning.

And it‚Äôs straight to Newgate I do go all by the law convicted,

To hang all on the Tyburn tree of which I‚Äôm much affrighted.

Farewell my friends and neighbours, all likewise my native Tara,

Kind providence may test the soul of Valentine O‚ÄôHara.

items.title: The Joy of Living
items.notes:
The Joy of Living

[

; DT JOYLIVE

; Mudcat 42500

;

new words and trad. arr. Ewan MacColl, tune trad. Sicilian]

Ewan MacColl wrote

The Joy of Living

in 1986. He and Peggy Seeger sang it in the same year on their Blackthorne

album

Items of News .

He alsio sang it in 1990 on his Cooking Vinyl album
items.lyrics:
Farewell, you northern hills, you mountains all, goodbye.

Moorland and stony ridges, crags and peaks, goodbye.

Glyder Fach , farewell, Cui Beig , Scafell , cloud-bearing Suilven .

Sun-warmed rock and the cold of Bleaklow ‚Äôs frozen sea,

The snow and the wind and the rain of hills and mountains.

Days in the sun and the tempered wind and the air like wine‚ÄĒ

And you drink and you drink till you‚Äôre drunk on the joy of living.

Farewell to you, my love, my time is almost done.

Lie in my arms once more until the darkness comes.

You filled all my days, held the night at bay, dearest companion.

Years pass by and are gone with the speed of birds in flight,

Our life like the verse of a song heard in the mountains.

Give me your hand then, love, and join your voice with mine‚ÄĒ

We‚Äôll sing of the hurt and the pain and the joy of living.

Farewell to you, my chicks, soon you must fly alone.

Flesh of my flesh, my future life, bone of my bone.

May your wings be strong, may your days be long, safe be your journey.

Each of you bears inside of you the gift of love‚ÄĒ

May it bring you light and warmth and the pleasure of giving.

Eagerly savour each new day and the taste of its mouth‚ÄĒ

Never lose sight of the thrill and the joy of living.

Take me to some high place of heather, rock and ling .

Scatter my dust and ashes, feed me to the wind.

So that I will be part of all you see, the air you are breathing.

I‚Äôll be part of the curlew‚Äôs cry and the soaring hawk,

The blue milkwort and the sundew hung with diamonds.

I‚Äôll be riding the gentle wind that blows through your hair.

Reminding you how we shared in the joy of living.

items.lyrics:
It‚Äôs coming down to Manchester to gain my liberty,

I met a pretty young doxy and she seemed full of glee.

Yes, I met a pretty young doxy, the prettiest ever I see.

At the Angel Inn in Manchester, there is the girl for me.

Then early next morning, just at the break of day,

I went to my love‚Äôs bedside, my morning vows to pay.

I hugged her, I cuddled her, I bade her to lie warm;

And she said: ‚ÄúMy jolly soldier, do you mean me any harm?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúTo mean you any harm, my love, is a thing that I would scorn.

If I stopped along with you all night, I‚Äôd marry you in the morn.

Before my lawful officer, my vows I will fulfil.‚ÄĚ

Then she said, ‚ÄúMy jolly soldier, you may lie as long as you will.‚ÄĚ

Our rout came on the Thursday, on the Monday we marched away.

The drums and fifes and bugles so sweetily did play.

Some hearts they were merry, but mine was full of woe.

She says: ‚ÄúMay I go along with you?‚ÄĚ‚ÄĒ ‚ÄúOh no, my love, oh no.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIf you should stand a sentry go, on a cold and bitter day,

Your colours they would go, love, and your beauty would decay

If I saw you handle a musket, love, it would fill my heart with woe

So stay at home, dear Nancy.‚ÄĚ But still she answered, ‚ÄúNo!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go down to your officer, and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll buy your discharge,

Ten guineas I‚Äôll surrender if they‚Äôll set you at large.

And if that will not do, my love, along with you I‚Äôll go,

So will you take me with you now?‚ÄĚ And still I answered, ‚ÄúNo.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go down in some nunnery and there I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll end my life.

I‚Äôll never have no lover now, nor yet become a wife.

But constant and true-hearted, love, for ever I‚Äôll remain,

And I never will get married till my soldier comes again!‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Manchester Rambler
items.notes:
The Manchester Rambler

[

; DT MNCHRAMB

; Mudcat 59616

, 116644

; Ewan MacColl, John Tams]

Ewan MacColl and Peggy Seeger sang The Manchester Rambler

The World of Ewan MacColl and Peggy Seeger .

This track was also included in 1973 on the Argo anthology

The World of Folk Vol. 2

items.lyrics:
I‚Äôve been over Snowdon, I‚Äôve slept upon Crowden

I‚Äôve camped by the Wainstones as well

I‚Äôve sunbathed on Kinder, been burned to a cinder

And many more things I can tell

My rucksack has oft been my pillow

The heather has oft been my bed

And sooner than part from the mountains

I think I would rather be dead

Chorus:

I‚Äôm a rambler, I‚Äôm a rambler from Manchester way

I get all my pleasure the hard moorland way

I may be a wage slave on Monday

But I am a free man on Sunday

The day was just ending and I was descending

Down Grindsbrook, just by Upper Tor

When a voice cried ‚ÄúEh you‚ÄĚ in the way keepers do

He‚Äôd the worst face that ever I saw

The things that he said were unpleasant

In the teeth of his fury I said

‚ÄúSooner than part from the mountains

I think I would rather be dead‚ÄĚ

He called me a louse and said ‚ÄúThink of the grouse‚ÄĚ

Well I thought, but I still couldn‚Äôt see

Why all Kinder Scout and the moors roundabout

Couldn‚Äôt take both the poor grouse and me

He said ‚ÄúAll this land is my master√Ę¬Ä¬ôs‚ÄĚ

At that I stood shaking my head

No man has the right to own mountains

Any more than the deep ocean bed

I once loved a maid, a spot-welder by trade

She was fair as the Rowan in bloom

And the blue of her eye matched the June moorland sky

And I wooed her from April to June

On the day that we should have been married

I went for a ramble instead

For sooner than part from the mountains

I think I would rather be dead

So I‚Äôll walk where I will over mountain and hill

And I‚Äôll lie where the bracken is deep

I belong to the mountains, the clear running fountains

Where the grey rocks lie rugged and steep

I‚Äôve seen the white hare in the gullys

And the curlew fly high overhead

And sooner than part from the mountains

I think I would rather be dead.

versions.title: Patterson Jordan Dipper sing The Manchester Rambler
versions.lyrics:
I‚Äôve camped out on Crowden, rambled on Snowdon

Slept by the Wainstones as well.

I‚Äôve sunbathed on Kinder, been burnt to a cinder

And many‚Äôs the tale I can tell.

My rucksack has oft been my pillow

Heather has oft been my bed.

But, sooner than part from these mountains I love,

Well I think I would rather be dead.

There‚Äôs pleasure in dragging the peat bogs, and bragging,

Of all the fine walks that you know.

There‚Äôs even a measure of some kind of pleasure,

In wading through ten feet of snow.

Well I‚Äôve seen the white hare on the heather,

The curlew fly high overhead.

But, sooner than part from these mountains I love,

Well, I think I would rather be dead.

Chorus:

Nothing changes, it all stays the same,

They‚Äôre selling the moorland for profit and gain.

They‚Äôve sold all the rivers, bought all the rain,

And you can‚Äôt go up there there, you‚Äôre disturbing the game.

Cods roe and caviar, milk stout and champagne,

Gold cards and dole cards, but, never the twain,

That√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the game, that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs their game ‚Ä¶

Nothing changes, it all stays the same.

So, I‚Äôll go where I will over mountain and hill,

And I‚Äôll lie where the bracken lies deep.

I belong to these mountains, these clear crystal fountains,

Where the rocks they stand rugged and steep.

Well, I‚Äôve stood on the edge of the downfall,

Seen all the valleys outspread.

No man has the right to own these mountains I love,

Anymore than the wide ocean bed.

items.lyrics:
[EM, track 1]
With our nets and gear we‚Äôre faring

On the wild and wasteful ocean.

It‚Äôs there on the deep that we harvest and reap our bread

As we hunt the bonny shoals of herring.

[ALL, track 4]
Oh, it was a fine and a pleasant day,

Out of Yarmouth harbour I was faring

As a cabin boy on a sailing lugger,

For to go and hunt the shoals of herring.

[ALL, track 4]
Oh, the work was hard and the hours were long

And the treatment surely took some bearing.

There was little kindness and the kicks were many

As we hunted for the shoals of herring.

[ALL, track 4]
Oh, we fished the Swarte and the Broken Bank;

I was cook and I‚Äôd a quarter-sharing.

And I used to sleep standing on my feet

And I‚Äôd dream about the shoals of herring.

[ALL, track 4]
Oh, we left the homegrounds in the month of June,

And to canny Shields we soon was bearing,

With a hundred cran of the silver darlings

That we‚Äôd taken from the shoals of herring.

[EM, track 4]
Now you‚Äôre up on deck, you‚Äôre a fisherman.

You can swear and show a manly bearing.

Take your turn on watch with the other fellows

While you‚Äôre searching for the shoals of herring.

[ALL, track 5]
Oh, I earned my keep and I paid my way,

And I earned the gear that I was wearing,

Sailed a million miles, caught ten million fishes,

We were sailing after shoals of herring.

[EM, track 15]
Wi‚Äô our nets and gear we‚Äôre faring

On the wild and wasteful ocean.

It‚Äôs there on the deep that we harvest and reap our bread

As we hunt the bonny shoals of herring.

[ALL, track 15]
Night and day the sea we‚Äôre daring,

Come wind or come winter gale, sweating or cold,

Growing up or growing old or dying,

While we‚Äôre hunting for the shoals of herring.

(EM = verse sung by Ewan MacColl, ALL = sung by A.L. Lloyd;
the track numbers refer to the Topic CD reissue of

versions.title: Ewan MacColl on Black and White
versions.lyrics:
With the nets and gear we‚Äôre faring

On the wild and wasteful ocean.

It‚Äôs there on the deep that we harvest and reap our bread

As we hunt the bonny shoals of herring.

Oh, it was a fine and a pleasant day,

Out of Yarmouth harbour I was faring

As a cabin boy on a sailing lugger,

For to go and hunt the shoals of herring.

Oh, the work was hard and the hours were long

And the treatment, sure it took some bearing.

There was little kindness and the kicks were many

As we hunted for the shoals of herring.

Oh, we fished the Swarte and the Broken Bank;

I was cook and I‚Äôd a quarter-sharing.

And I used to sleep standing on my feet

And I‚Äôd dream about the shoals of herring.

Well, we left the homegrounds in the month of June,

And to canny Shields we soon was bearing,

With a hundred cran of the silver darlings

That we‚Äôd taken from the shoals of herring.

Now you‚Äôre up on deck, you‚Äôre a fisherman.

You can swear and show a manly bearing.

Take your turn on watch with the other fellows

While you‚Äôre following the shoals of herring.

In the stormy seas and the living gales

Just to earn your daily bread you‚Äôre daring

From the Dover Straits to the Faeroe Islands

While you‚Äôre following the shoals of herring.

Well, I earned my keep and I paid my way,

And I earned the gear that I was wearing,

Sailed a million miles, caught ten million fishes,

We was following the shoals of herring.

items.title: The Space Girl‚Äôs Song
items.notes:
>

Songs >

Space Girl

>
