Songs >

Space Girl

The Space Girl‚Äôs Song

[Peggy Seeger, Ewan MacColl]

Peggy Seeger and Ewan MacColl wrote

The Space Girl‚Äôs Song
items.lyrics:
My mama told me I should never venture into space,

But I did, I did, I did;

She said no Terran girl could trust the Martian race,

But I did, I did, I did.

A rocket pilot asked me on a voyage to go

And I was so romantic that I couldn‚Äôt say no,

That he was just a servo-robot, how was I to know?

So I did, I did, I did.

My papa warned me never trust a space engineer

But I did, I did, I did;

He said free-fall and super-drive would surely cost me dear,

And they did, they did, they did.

I‚Äôve been as far in hyper-space as anybody can,

I‚Äôve travelled through the time-warp on the psycho-plan,

They say a gal must travel for to find her superman

So I did, I did, I did.

They warned me not to go around among the asteroids,

But I did, I did, I did;

They said I‚Äôd make those class-three mutants dreadfully annoyed,

And I did, I did, I did.

They said that Saturn was too hot and Venus not much fun,

And bug-eyed monsters tended to be just a trifle dumb,

They said I‚Äôd need a blaster and a needle-freezer gun,

And I did, I did, I did.

They said to find a man out there and try to settle down,

So I did, I did, I did.

They said my kids might grow up one-eyed, green or bald or round,

And they did, they did, they did.

My cosmic husband died of mumps a hundred years ago,

My daughter‚Äôs in the Milky Way a-tracking down a beau,

And I‚Äôm so old and doddering I‚Äôve nothing for to show

What I did, I did, I did.

items.title: The Terror Time
items.notes:
The Terror Time

[

; DT TERRTIME

; Mudcat 12449

; Ewan MacColl]

The Terror Time

is a song from Ewan MacColl‚Äôs 1964 radio ballad

The Travelling People .

The Terror Time is one of Ewan MacColl‚Äôs stark song vignettes
items.lyrics:
The heather will fade and the bracken will die

Stream will run cold and clear.

And the small birds will be going,

And it‚Äôs then you will be knowing

That the Terror Time is near.

Whaur will ye turn noo, whaur will ye bide

Now that the wark‚Äôs a‚Äô done?

For the fairmer doesna need ye

And the Council winna heed ye,

And the Terror Time has come.

The woods give no shelter, the trees they are bare

Snow falling all around.

And the children they are crying

And the bed in which they‚Äôre lying

Is frozen to the ground.

The snow winna lift and the stove winna draw,

There‚Äôs ice in the water churn,

In the mud and snaw you‚Äôre sloshing

Trying to dae your bit o‚Äô washing

And the kindling winna burn.

Needing the warming of your own human kind,

You move near a town, but then

Well, the sight of you‚Äôs offending,

And the police they soon are sending‚Ä¶

And you‚Äôre on the road again.

items.title: The Thirty-Foot Trailer
items.notes:
>

The Thirty-Foot Trailer

The Thirty-Foot Trailer

[

; DT TRAIL30

; Mudcat 117039

; Ewan MacColl]

The Thirty-Foot Trailer is a song from Ewan MacColl‚Äôs 1964

radio ballad

The Travelling People .
items.lyrics:
The old ways are changing, you cannot deny,

The day of the traveller is over;

There‚Äôs nowhere to go and there‚Äôs nowhere to bide,

So farewell to the life of the rover.

Chorus (after each verse):

Farewell to the tent and the old caravan,

To the Tinker, the Gypsy, the Travelling Man

And farewell to the thirty-foot trailer.

Farewell to the cant and the travelling tongue,

Farewell to the Romany talking,

The buying and selling, the old fortune telling,

The knock on the door and the hawking.

You‚Äôve got to move fast to keep up with the times

For these days a man cannot dander;

It‚Äôs a bylaw to say you must be on your way

And another to say you can‚Äôt wander.

Farewell to the besoms of heather and broom,

Farewell to the creel and the basket,

For the folks of today they would far sooner pay

For a thing that‚Äôs been made out of plastic.

Farewell to the pony, the cob, and the mare

Where the reins and the harness are idle;

You don‚Äôt need a strap when you‚Äôre breaking up scrap

So farewell to the bit and the bridle.

Farewell to the fields where we‚Äôve sweated and toiled

At pulling and shoving and lifting,

They‚Äôll soon have machines and the Travelling Queens

And their menfolk had better be shifting.

items.title: The Tunnel Tigers
items.notes:
The Tunnel Tigers

[

; Mudcat 3094

; Ewan MacColl]

Ewan MacColl sang his own song The Tunnel Tigers

in 1972 on his Argo album

Solo Flight .

The liner notes laconically stated:

Written by Ewan MacColl in 1966 for a BBC documentary film dealing with the

building of the Victoria Underground Line in London.

Canterbury Fair sang

The Tunnel Tigers
items.lyrics:
Hares run free on the Wicklow mountains,

Wild geese fly and foxes play;

Sporting Wicklow boys are working,

Driving a tunnel through the London clay.

Chorus (after each verse):

Up with the shield and jack it! Ram it!

Driving a tunnel through the London clay.

Below Armagh the wild ducks breeding,

Wild fowl gather on Lough Rea,

The sporting boys of Longford County

Driving a tunnel through the London clay.

The Lough Derg trout grow fat and lazy,

Salmon sport in Cushla bay;

Fishermen of Connemara

Driving a tunnel through the London clay.

The curragh rots on Achill Island,

Tourists walk on the Newport quay;

Mayo boys have all gone roving,

Driving a tunnel through the London clay.

The Carlow girls are fine and handsome,

All decked out so neat and gay;

Carlow boys don‚Äôt come to court ‚Äôem

Driving a tunnel through the London clay.

Down in the dark are the tunnel tigers,

Far from the sun and the light of day;

Down in the land that the sea once buried,

Driving a tunnel through the London clay.

items.lyrics:
Three Eynsham chaps went out one day,

To Lord Abingdon‚Äôs manor they made their way;

They took their dogs to catch some game

And soon to Wytham Woods they came.

Chorus (after each verse):

Laddie i-o, laddie i-o, fol-de-rol over a laddie i-o

We had not long been beating there

Before our spaniel put up a hare;

Up she jumped, away she ran,

At the very same time a pheasant sprang.

We had not beat the woods all through

When Barrett, the keeper, he came in view;

And when we saw the old bugger look

When we got there ‚Äôtwas full to the brim

And you‚Äôd have laughed to see us swim,

Ten feet of water, if not more;

When we got out our dogs came o‚Äôer.

Over hedges, ditches, gates and rails,

Our dogs followed after behind our heels;

And you can all say what you will

We‚Äôll have our hares and pheasants still.

items.title: Red & Gold
items.lyrics:
Chorus:

Red and Gold are royal colours

Peasant colours are green and brown

Green is the corn in the brown earth when it‚Äôs growing

Red and gold when the harvest is cut down.

Through Cropredy in Oxfordshire the Cherwell takes its course

And the willows weep into its waters clear

My name it is Will Tims and it‚Äôs here that I was born

And raised in faith my King and God to fear.

In 1644 the King in Oxford Town did dwell

Though we‚Äôd heard that Cromwell‚Äôs army was nearby

It did not occur to me that little Cropredy

Could be witness to the meeting of both sides

On June the 29 th that year I was about my work

Cutting hedges in the meadow by the stream

My blade slipped, I cut my hand and my own dear blood did flow

Upon the brown earth and the corn still green

Now it did distress me so to watch my own blood flow

And quickly soak into the greedy ground

In red and gold my colours swam and sweat broke on my brow

And faint I knew that I must lay me down

(Chorus)

At first I thought the thundering was just inside my head

So I raised myself above the hedge to see

And I watched as in a dream as the armies fought downstream

The Battle for the Bridge at Cropredy

Now the King‚Äôs men fought in red and gold though Cromwell‚Äôs men were plainer

The blood they spilled was coloured just the same

Through the hedgerow‚Äôs fragile cover I saw brother killing brother

And all of this was done in Jesus‚Äô name

(Chorus)

All that day and all the next the battle it was raging

Though when darkness came I slipped away

But the crying of the dying kept me wakeful and just lying

In my bed until the dawning of the day

And the dreams I had were red and gold

And the little stream became a flood

From all my brothers killing one another

Till waking I realised it was all my own dear blood

Some were buried in the church and some just where they fell

With no markers to declare their place of rest

But the poppies they do grow where they were never sown

And to my mind they do declare it best

And each year when the green corn once again turns into gold

And the poppies in the field again remind me

Like the scar upon my hand and the blood spilled on this land

And the hungry earth so eager to confine me

For read and gold they are the colours

One is blood and one is power

Though I may find my rest in Cropredy Church

In golden fields forever will spring the poppy flower

By Cropredy the Cherwell is still bidden to keep flowing

And the willows by its side still gently weep

But still in restless dreams by this most peaceful stream

The poppies wake me from my rightful sleep

And the dreams I have are red and gold

And the little stream becomes a flood

From all my brothers killing one another

Till waking I realise it‚Äôs all my own dear blood

items.title: Reno, Nevada
items.notes:
Reno, Nevada

[Ian Matthews]

Reno, Nevada

was a central part of the early Fairport Convention repertoire but was

never recorded for one of their albums‚ÄĒthis was done in 1971 by Ian

(or Iain, as he later became known) Matthews on his 1971 album.

If You Saw Thro‚Äô My Eyes .

Even before

A Sailor‚Äôs Life ,

this is one of the great Fairport examples of brilliant instrumental

improvisation.

However, Fairport performed Reno, Nevada in several TV and radio
items.lyrics:
It‚Äôs a long long way down to Reno Nevada

It‚Äôs a long long way to your home

And the change in your pocket it‚Äôs beginning to crumble

And you reap just about what you sow

You can walk down the street

Pass your face in a window

You can go on foolin‚Äô around

You can work night and day take a chance on promotion

You can fall through a hole in the ground

Well there ain‚Äôt no game like the game that you‚Äôre playing

When you‚Äôve got a little something to lose

And there ain‚Äôt no time like the time that you‚Äôre wasting

And you waste just about what you choose

There‚Äôs a man at the table and you know he‚Äôs been able

To return all the odds that you lay

And you can‚Äôt feed your hunger

And you ain‚Äôt getting younger

And your tongue it‚Äôs got nothing to say

It‚Äôs a long long way down to Reno Nevada

It‚Äôs a long long way to your home

And the ground underneath you it‚Äôs beginning to crumble

And the sky up above you has grown

There‚Äôs a time to be grievin‚Äô and a time to be screamin‚Äô

And a time just to scroll on the wall

And you ain‚Äôt got the double

And it ain‚Äôt worth the trouble

You‚Äôre feeling you‚Äôre going nowhere at all

items.title: Shattering Live Experience
items.notes:
Shattering Live Experience

[Simon Nicol]

Fairport Convention recorded Simon Nicol‚Äôs song

Shattering Live Experience

on 4 February 1969 in BBC Studio 1, 201 Piccadilly, for

the BBC radio show ‚ÄúSymonds on Sunday‚ÄĚ.

It was broadcast on 9 February 1969.

This recording was included on their 1976 privately issued cassette

that was officially released in 1987 on Hannibal Records,

items.lyrics:
Evening‚Äôs dawning, missed the morning,

Slept the afternoon.

Half the day‚Äôs gone, still I laze on,

Got no silver spoon.

But there‚Äôs someone out there somewhere,

Some girl with my other side.

She won‚Äôt know me, but she‚Äôll show me

How to treat another heart.

Life is bedlam if you let them

Kick and put you down.

They will race you, beat, disgrace you,

Push your face into the ground.

But you can get out of there somehow,

She will show you how it‚Äôs done.

She‚Äôs no lady, but she made me

Feel that I need a crown.

I‚Äôll repay her and convey her

My realities.

She‚Äôll respond with, condescend with

Trivialities.

For she doesn‚Äôt have to think of

For too long to read my mind.

She‚Äôs a winner, no beginner,

She‚Äôs a friend of mine.

items.title: A Carol for Twelfth Day / Kilmore Carol
items.notes:
A Carol for Twelfth Day / Kilmore Carol

[

; trad.]

The Voice Squad sang the

Kilmore Carol

on their 1987 album

Many‚Äôs the Foolish Youth .

Se√°n Corcoran noted:

In 1684 while in exile in Ghent, Luke Wadding, Bishop of Ferns (which

includes Wexford) published a ‚ÄėSmale Garland‚Äô of carols.

After his death these became immensely popular in Ireland (in fact, an edition

was printed for a Drogheda bookseller, James Connor, in 1728) and are sung
items.lyrics:
Now to conclude our Christmas mirth, with news of our redemption,

We will end our songs on our Saviour‚Äôs birth with one that deserves attention.

Three great wonders fell on this day, a star led Kings where the Infant lay,

Water made wine in Galilee and Christ baptised in Jordan.

Those Kings might have known what Balaam of old said of a star that would arise,

In Jacob‚Äôs land where he foretold the coming of the Messiah.

Jasper, Melchior and Balthazar set out when they saw the new bright star,

Leaving their eastern kingdoms far, to find out the new-born Jesus.

Amazed to see the cottage poor, the stall where He was born in,

They left their retinue at the door, though great, they entered without scorn.

The Blessed Babe and mother found, leaving their crowns and sceptres down.

Adored Him prostrate on the ground and might have spoke as follows:

Oh King of Kings here in disguise whom stars obey and angels serve.

Though wealth and grandeur You despise, You have given us more than we deserve.

Our beds are gold and ivory, our garments rich with broidery.

Beset with pearls and pagentry, whilst You lie in a stable.

What else might have passed, you may conceive in this fond conversation.

They bade farewell, taking their leave, home to their habitation.

Farewell good Christians, fare you well too, many a happy Christmas we wish you,

With a blessed end for to ensue, through the merits of Sweet Jesus.

items.title: A Collier Lad
items.notes:
A Collier Lad

[

; trad.]

A Collier Lad is a short jingle of a song found in the book of

mining songs from the North of England, Doon the Wagon Way ,

edited by Micheal Dawney. The song was collected in 1966 from a miner

called Peter Wright of Methley, Castleford.

The song was popular in the West Riding of Yorkshire and is unique with its

accelerated, almost chant-like end. It is related to the North-East song

The Bonny Pit Laddie (Roud 3487).

Bryony Griffith and Alice Jones sang A Collier Lad

in 2022 on their album of Yorkshire songs,
items.lyrics:
A collier lad, a collier lad, a collier lad for me.

‚ÄôE works in ‚Äôis ‚Äôoil , as black as the coil , an‚Äô gives all ‚Äôis money to me.

‚ÄôE says sithee mi lass, a‚Äôll gi‚Äôthee mi brass , a‚Äôll not come ‚Äôome late anymoor .

Mi shoes ah gonna be mended, mi stockins ah gonna dye green,

An‚Äô a‚Äôll be ready for Sunday, an‚Äô a‚Äôll be fit for a Queen.

‚ÄôOw d‚Äôye like mi collar mi cuff, ‚Äôow do ye like mi tie?

‚ÄôOw d‚Äôye like mi walkin‚Äô stick, mi glass upom mi eye?

‚ÄôOw de ye like mi tailer, who med me such a toff ?

‚ÄôE‚Äôs doin‚Äô is reg‚Äôlar business down the old pop shop.

items.title: Across the Blue Mountain
items.notes:
Across the Blue Mountain

[

(formerly Roud 25278)

; Ballad Index AF014

; DT BLUEMNTN

; Mudcat 65759

; trad.]

Sandy and Caroline Paton sang

Across the Blue Mountain

in 1966 on their Folk-Legacy album

Folksongs and Ballads .

Sandy Paton noted:
items.lyrics:
One morning, one morning, one morning in May,

I overheard a married man to a young girl say,

‚ÄúArise you up, pretty Katie, and come along with me,

Across the blue mountain to the Allegheny.

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll buy you a horse, love, and a saddle to ride;

I‚Äôll buy me another‚Äôn to ride by your side.

We‚Äôll stop at every tavern and drink when we‚Äôre dry,

Across the blue mountain goes Katie and I.‚ÄĚ

Well, up stepped her mother, in anger was she then.

‚ÄúDaughter, dear Daughter, he is a married man.

Besides, there‚Äôs young men a-plenty is handsomer than he.

Let him take his own wife to the Allegheny.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh, Mother, dear Mother, he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the man of my heart.

Wouldn‚Äôt it be an awful shame for me and my love to part?

I‚Äôd envy all of the women that ever I did see,

Across the blue mountain to the Allegheny.‚ÄĚ

Well, the last time I seen him, he was saddled to ride.

Katie, his darling, was there by his side,

A-laughing and a-singing, and happy to be free,

Across the blue mountain to the Allegheny.

versions.title: Niamh Parsons sings Across the Blue Mountain
versions.lyrics:
One morning, one morning, one morning in May,

I overheard a married man to a young girl say,

‚ÄúGo dress you up, pretty Katie, and come along with me,

Across the blue mountain to the Allegheny.

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll buy you a horse love, and a saddle to ride,

I‚Äôll buy myself another to ride by your side,

We‚Äôll stop at every tavern, we‚Äôll drink when we‚Äôre dry,

Across the blue mountain goes my Katie and I.‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke the mother, and angry she was then,

‚ÄúOh daughter, oh daughter, he is a married man,

And there‚Äôs plenty of young men more handsome than he,

So let him take his own wife to the Allegheny.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh mother, oh mother, he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs the man of my heart,

And wouldn‚Äôt it be a dreadful thing if we were to part?

I‚Äôd envy every woman that I‚Äôd ever see

Go across the blue mountain to the Allegheny.‚ÄĚ

We left before daybreak on a buckskin and roan,

Past tall shimmering pines where mockingbirds moan,

Past dark cabin windows where eyes never see,

Across the blue mountains to the Allegheny.

Past dark cabin windows where eyes never see,

Across the blue mountains to the Allegheny.

items.title: Adieu to Bogieside
items.notes:
Adieu to Bogieside

[

/ Song Subject MAS196

; G/D 8:1517

; Ballad Index FCS265

; John Riddell]

Katherine Campbell:

Songs From North-East Scotland

Alison McMorland / Elizabeth Stewart:

Up Yon Wide and Lonely Glen

John Ord:

Bothy Songs and Ballads
items.lyrics:
Assist me, all ye Muses,
your downcast spirits raise,

And join me in full chorus
to sing brave Huntly‚Äôs praise,

For the girl I left behind me
whose charms were all my pride,

When I said fareweel tae Huntly Toun1
on bonny Bogieside.

For it‚Äôs doon the road tae Huntly Lodge
wi pleasant steps I‚Äôve rode,

Almost inspired with rapture
the sweet girl that I loved,

Who join‚Äôt me in my rambles
and choosed me for her guide

To walk upon the Deveron‚Äôs banks,
on bonny Bogieside

Fareweel, ye lads o Huntly Toun,
tae you I‚Äôll bid adieu;

The pleasures of a even‚Äôs walk
I‚Äôll share nae mair wi you;

The sky was clear an bonny
when on an eventide

I‚Äôll lay me doon and rest awhile
upon that Deveronside.

May the powers above protect this girl,
so young and fair and fine,

And save her from all dangers
who has this heart of mine,

Until ma hairt2 forgets to beat
and aifters3 us divide.

For I‚Äôll return tae Huntly Toun
on bonny Bogieside.

versions.title: Shona Donaldson sings Adieu tae Bogieside
versions.lyrics:
Assist me all you muses, my downcast spirits raise,

And join me in full chorus to sing dear Huntly‚Äôs praise.

For I leave a girl behind me whose joy was all my pride,

And I‚Äôll bid fareweel tae Huntly toon, and adieu tae Bogieside.

Companions of my youth fareweel, I‚Äôll bid ye a‚Äô adieu;

The pleasures of an evening‚Äôs walk I‚Äôll no more share wi‚Äô you.

Where e‚Äôer I chance tae wander in regions far and wide

My hairt will be in Huntly toon and on sweet Bogieside.

How heartsome I have wandered to see the gowans spring,

All in the merry month of May and hear the linties sing.

When wearied with my fishing rod and when at even tide

I‚Äôll sit me doon tae rest a while, upon sweet Bogieside.

Farewell ye lovely meadows of you I‚Äôll often talk,

Likewise the hawthorn bushes that grace the gravel walk.

The pleasures I‚Äôve enjoyed wi‚Äô you they in my hairt will bide

When I am far fae Huntly toon and from sweet Bogieside.

Providence protect the girl to whom I‚Äôve sent these lines

And keep her free from danger who has this heart of mine.

Bless her with contentment and keep her free from pride

Till I return tae Huntly toon and to sweet Bogieside.

items.title: Adieu to Bon County / Farewell to Yorkshire
items.notes:
[

; Ballad Index LxA178

; DT ADIEUBON

; trad.]

Gavin Davenport sang Farewell to Yorkshire

in 2013 on his Haystack album

The Bone Orchard .

He noted:

This began as a couple of versions of Adieu to Bon County

and moved a bit closer to home, taking in parts of

Spencer the Rover

en route.
items.lyrics:
In Yorkshire near Rotherham I lived in contentment,

Loved a young girl and I told her my mind.

My tender lover she being some rover,

In anger my parents to her proved unkind.

Chorus (after each verse):

Eat, drink and be jolly and care not for folly,

Drown away sorrows in bottles of wine.

Pass it to the boys in those full flowing bumpers

And play on thy fiddle to pass away time.

It‚Äôs a great separation my parents have caused me

By baring my spite so their favour was won.

It‚Äôs a great separation, likewise a vexation,

And they‚Äôll be sorry for what they have done.

And adieu to Bon County I‚Äôm bound for to leave you

To seek my heart‚Äôs fortune in some foreign land.

Happy we may be at our next meeting

And upon that day we may join heart and hand.

Farewell to my friends and my dear old companions,

Likewise the girl that I‚Äôll not see again.

This world is wide and I‚Äôll range it in pleasure

And care not for those who‚Äôve laid scorn out so plain.

My portion is small and I now do confess it,

What little I have it is all of my own.

I might have had longer to spend it in pleasure

If my cruel parents had left me alone.

versions.title: Faustus sing Adieu to Bon County
versions.lyrics:
lt‚Äôs a great separation my friends they have caused me,

By hearing their spite that my favour was won.

lt‚Äôs a great separation, likewise a vexation,

And they shall be sorry for what they have done.

Chorus (after each verse):

Eat, drink and be jolly and care not for folly,

And drown away sorrow in a bottle of wine.

Pass it to the boys in full-flowing bumpers,

And play on the fiddle to pass away time.

Farewell to Bon County, I‚Äôm bound for to leave you,

And seek my heart‚Äôs fortune in some foreign land.

Where bottles and glasses are my greatest comfort,

And when we do meet we will join heart and hand.

Farewell to my friends and my good old neighbours,

Likewise to the girl I will never see more.

This world it is wide and I‚Äôll spend it in pleasures,

I care for no one that won‚Äôt care for me.

My fortune is small so freely I own it,

What little I have it is all of my own.

I might have lived longer to enjoy it with pleasure,

lf my poor friends had have let me alone.

I‚Äôve money aplenty to bear my expenses,

And when it‚Äôs all gone I‚Äôll chop wood and get more.

When death it comes on me I‚Äôll freely go with it,

Pay up my last dues and go with it home.

items.title: Adieu to Old England
items.notes:
Adieu to Old England

[

; Mudcat 7339

; words Gregg Butler, music Chris Pollington]

Strawhead‚Äôs Gregg Butler wrote

Adieu to Old England

in 1983 and Chris Pollington set it to his tune.

They sang it on their 1984 Traditional Sound Recordings album of

‚Äútales of the Elizabethan adventures‚ÄĚ,

Gentlemen of Fortune .
items.lyrics:
So merrily we landed upon this alien strand

So steadfastly we set our new homes in this new found land

So resolutely gathered summers fruit braved winters chill

But now as spring fast fades our hearts with apprehension fill

Chorus (after each verse):

To our dear England we bade adieu

Sailed westward bravely we gallant few

Now here abandoned on a foreign shore

Our hope dies never we‚Äôll see thee more

For varied wants we sent the word back to old England‚Äôs might

To better till the soil and plant and thus maintain our right

Full ten long months ago our ships sails left our longing view

Our anxious watch now hopeless fades our fate in dread we rue

Oh England how could you abandon thus your own true sons

Your husbands wives and daughters all we thought your chosen ones

Chosen to plant your steadfast seed deep in Virginia‚Äôs soil

But now by negligence betrayed with quaking hearts we toil

Alas too few too ill equipped without support to thrive

Our parlous state we plainly see and think not to survive

Another year oh where‚Äôs the ship they promised soon to send

And failing by men‚Äôs greed its lack has brought us to our end

Goodbye forever England that we left with hopes so brave

Goodbye to you that scorned us left us helpless ‚Äôcross the wave

We die unsung neglected in this thrice-accursed land

We die reproachful and denied a parent‚Äôs helping hand

items.title: Adown Winding Nith
items.notes:
Adown Winding Nith

[

; Robert Burns]

Chorus (after each verse):

items.title: Ae Fond Kiss
items.notes:
Ae Fond Kiss

[

; DT FONDKISS

; Mudcat 32222

; Robert Burns]

Norman Buchan:

101 Scottish Songs

Nigel Denver sang Ae Fond Kiss

in 1974 on their Folk Heritage album
items.lyrics:
Ae fond kiss, and then we sever!

Ae fareweel, and then for ever!

Deep in heart-wrung tears I‚Äôll pledge thee

Warring sighs and groans I‚Äôll wage thee.

Who shall say that Fortune grieves him,

While the star of hope she leaves him?

Me, nae cheerful twinkle lights me,

Dark despair around benights me.

I‚Äôll ne‚Äôer blame my partial fancy:

Naething could resist my Nancy!

But to see her, was tae love her,

Love but her, and love for ever.

Had we never loved sae kindly,

Had we never loved sae blindly,

Never met‚ÄĒor never pairted‚ÄĒ

We had ne‚Äôer been broken-hearted.

Fare thee weel, thou first and fairest!

Fare thee weel, thou best and dearest!

Thine be ilka joy and treasure,

Peace, enjoyment, love and pleasure!

Ae fond kiss and then we sever!

Ae fareweel, alas for ever!

Deep in heart-wrung tears I‚Äôll pledge thee

Warring sighs and groans I‚Äôll wage thee!

items.title: After Aughrim‚Äôs Great Disaster
items.notes:
After Aughrim‚Äôs Great Disaster

[

; Ballad Index PGa017

; Mudcat 7866

; Patrick Augustine Sheehan (1852-1913)]

John Lyons sang After Aughrim

in 1974 on his Topic album

The May Morning Dew

and in 2012 on his Veteran CD with his brother Tim Lyons,

Easy and Bold .

A.L. Lloyd and Sandra Kerr noted:

Catholic James II was backed by Louis XIV of France and the bulk of
items.lyrics:
After Aughrim‚Äôs great disaster when our foe in sooth was master,

It was you who first plunged in and swam the Shannon‚Äôs boiling flood.

And through Slieve Bloom‚Äôs dark passes you led our Gallowglasses,

Although the hungry Saxon wolves were howling for our blood.

And as we crossed Tipperary we broke the clan O‚ÄôLeary,

And drove a creacht before us as our horsemen onward came.

With our swords and spears we gored them and through flood and night we bore them,

Still Se√°n √ď Duibhir a Ghleanna we were worsted in the game.

Long long we held the hillside, our couch it was the rillside

The sturdy oaken boughs our curtain overhead.

The summer blaze we laughed at, the winter snows we scoffed at,

And trusted in our long broad swords to win our daily bread.

Then the Dutchmen‚Äôs troops they found us in fire and steel they bound us.

They blazed the woods and mountains till the very sky was flamed.

Yet our sharpened swords cut through them, through their coats of mail we hewed them,

But Se√°n √ď Duibhir a Ghleanna we were worsted in the game.

‚ÄôTwas all night in woeful sorrow we waited for the morrow,

Alone upon the mountain top we lingered for the dawn.

To see beneath us, spreading, field and farm and steading,

Hill and dale and valley, meadow, moor and bog.

Our good ship then we sought her, where rose the deep Blackwater.

The river‚Äôs bright and pungent to swell her bosom came.

From all the farewell taking, from sad hearts sore and aching,

Ah, but Se√°n √ď Duibhir a Ghleanna we were worsted in the game.

Here‚Äôs a health to your and my king, the sovereign of our liking.

And to Sarsfield underneath whose flag we‚Äôll cast once more a chance.

For the morning‚Äôs dawn will bring us across the sea and wing us,

To take a stand and wield a brand among the sons of France.

And though we part in sorrow, still Se√°n √ď Duibhir a chara,

Our prayer is, ‚ÄúGod save Ireland and pour blessings on her name,‚ÄĚ

May her sons be true when needed, may they never feel as we did,

For Se√°n √ď Duibhir a Ghleanna we were worsted in the game.

items.title: Agamemnon
items.notes:

In fact having been recently promoted commodore, Nelson was flying his

single-handed half a Spanish armada. But it was, I believe, even then as the

captain of the Agamemnon that he was best known, and, anyhow,

I am certainly not going to wreck the old sailors‚Äô chorus for a trifling

error like that.

Sink him in his hammock, boys, he‚Äôs gone far away!

Oak and iron and blood of her, his fair one, his darling,

Where are all the shipwrights now that built the Agamemnon ?

Let them lie at rest, boys, under the green grass.

Saw and swinging riveters we‚Äôll never more be hearing

On Beaulieu‚Äôs woodland waters where the Agamemnon was.

Where is Captain Nelson now, that sailed the Agamemnon ?

Fought and beat the Spanish crews in Cape St. Vincent Bay ‚ÄĒ

Oak and iron blood of her, his fine one, his darling ‚ÄĒ

Sink him in his hammock, boys, he‚Äôs gone far away!

‚ÄúO cobbler, o cobbler, √Ę¬Ä¬ôtis soon we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be wed

And nestling together in a fine feather bed.

‚ÄúO Father, o Father, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve got me a man

And he is the one I would wed if I can.

As handsome as ever in leather did stand

The father was thinking and thinking again

Come out, ‚Äôtis now September, the hunter‚Äôs moon‚Äôs begun,

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

items.title: All You That Are to Mirth Inclined / The Sinner‚Äôs Redemption
items.notes:

All you that are unto mirth inclined,

Consider well and do bear in mind

What our great God for us hath done

In sending His beloved Son.

Let all your songs and your praises be

Unto His heavenly Majesty;

And evermore, amongst your mirth.

Remember Christ our Saviour‚Äôs birth.

The five-and-twentieth of December

Great cause we have to remember;

In Bethlehem, upon that morn,

There was our bless‚Äôd Messiah born.

Near Bethlehem shepherds they did keep

Their herds and flocks, a-feeding sheep,

To whom God‚Äôs Angel did soon appear

Which put the shepherds in great fear.

Prepare and go, the Angel said,

To Bethlehem, be ye not afraid;

There shall ye see, this blessed morn,

The heavenly Babe, sweet Jesus, born.

With thankful heart and with joyful mind

The shepherds went for this Babe to find;

And, as the heavenly Angel told,

They did our Saviour Christ behold.

Within a manger the Babe was laid;

The Virgin Mary beside Him stayed,

Attending on the Lord of Life,

Being both Mother, Maid and Wife.

If choirs of angels they did rejoice,

Well may mankind, both with heart and voice,

Sing praises to the God of heaven,

Who unto us His Son hath given.

in 1972 in her home in Witney, Oxfordshire, to Mike Yates.

This recording was included in 1975 on the Topic anthology of countryside

songs from Southern England collected by Mike Yates,

When Sheepshearing‚Äôs Done ,

in 2001 on the Musical Traditions anthology of songs and music from the
items.lyrics:
As I was a-walking one morning in May,

I met a fair damsel to sigh and to say,

‚ÄúMy love he√Ę¬Ä¬ôs gone from me and showed me false play,

It was down in the meadows among the green hay.‚ÄĚ

The very next time that I did him see,

He vowed and declared he‚Äôd be constant to me.

I asked him his name and he made this reply,

‚ÄúIt is T-I-M-O-T-H-Y.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy father√Ę¬Ä¬ôs possessed with ten thousand or more,

And I am his daughter and his only heir.

Not one penny in portion, he vows and declares

If I marries to Y-O-U my dear.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh, that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs for your fortune, love, you never mind,

I‚Äôll make you a husband both loving and kind.

So off to the church, love, come let us prepare,

Never mind your F-A-T-H-E-R.‚ÄĚ

So off to the church the very next day,

And home to her father without more delay.

‚ÄúDear hon-or-ed father I tell unto you,

We are M-A-R-R-I-E-D.‚ÄĚ

And then the old man he began for to swear,

‚ÄúYou√Ę¬Ä¬ôve married my daughter, and my only heir.

But since it is so, I have a new son,

You are W-E-L-C-O-M-E.‚ÄĚ

items.lyrics:
In the Mill o‚Äô Tifty lived a man,

In the neighbourhood of Fyvie,

He had a lovely daughter fair,

Whose name was bonnie Annie.

Lord Fyvie had a trumpeter

Whose name was Andrew Lammie

Who had the art to gain the heart

Of Mill o‚Äô Tifty‚Äôs Annie.

Her mother called her to the door,

Come here to me, my Annie,

Did ever you see such a prettier man

Then the trumpeter o‚Äô Fyvie.

O nothing she said but thinking sore,

Alas for bonnie Annie,

She durst not own her heart was won

By the trumpeter o‚Äô Fyvie.

Her father came to hear o‚Äô this,

And a letter wrote to Fyvie,

To tell his daughter had been bewitched

By his servant Andrew Lammie.

Her father locked the door at night

Laid by the keys full canny,

An‚Äô when he heard the trumpet sound,

He said, Your coo is lowin‚Äô, Annie.

O father dear, I pray forbear,

Reproach not your Annie;

I would rather hear that cow in low

Than a‚Äô yer kye in Fyvie.

But if ye strike me I will cry

An‚Äô gentlemen will hear me,

Lord Fyvie he‚Äôll come ridin‚Äô by

An‚Äô he‚Äôll come in an see me.

Her father struck her wondrous sore,

And also did her mother,

Her sisters also did her scorn

Ah, but woe to be her brother.

Her brother struck her wondrous sore

Wi‚Äô cruel strokes, aye, an‚Äô many,

He broke her back in yon ha‚Äô door

For lovin‚Äô Andrew Lammie.

At that same time the Lord comes in,

He said, What ails thee, Annie?

It‚Äôs all for love that I must die

For lovin‚Äô Andrew Lammie.

O mother dear, make me my bed

An‚Äô lay my face til Fyvie,

It‚Äôs there I‚Äôll lie till I will die

For lovin‚Äô Andrew Lammie.

Her mother then she made her bed,

An‚Äô turn‚Äôt her face to Fyvie;

Her tender hairt then soon did brake

And she ne‚Äôer see‚Äôd Andrew Lammie.

Now people hear, both far and near,

All pity Tifty‚Äôs Annie,

Who died for love of one poor lad

For bonnie Andrew Lammie.

versions.title: Martin Simpson sings Andrew Lammie
versions.lyrics:
At Mill o‚Äô Tifty there lived a man

In the neighbourhood of Fyvie

And he had a fine lovely daughter dear

Whose name was Bonnie Annie

Her bloom was like the springing flower

That hails the rosy morning

With innocence and grace likewise

Her lovely face adorning

Now Fyvie had a trumpeter

Whose name was Andrew Lammie

And he had the art for to gain the heart

Of Mill o‚Äô Tifty‚Äôs Annie

Proper he was, both young and gay

His like was not in Fyvie

Nor was one there that could compare

With this same Andrew Lammie

Lord Fyvie he rode by the door

Where lived this Tifty‚Äôs Annie

And his trumpeter rode him before

Even this same Andrew Lammie

Her mother called her to the door

Saying, ‚ÄúCome here to me, my Annie

Did you ever see a prettier man

Than the trumpeter o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Fyvie?‚ÄĚ

Nothing she said, but sighing sore

Alas for Bonnie Annie

For she dared not own that her heart was won

By the trumpeter o‚Äô Fyvie

At night when all went to their bed

All slept full soon but Annie

Love so oppressed her tender breast

Thinking on Andrew Lammie

‚ÄúOh love comes in at my bedside

And love lies down beside me

Love has possessed my tender breast

And love will waste my body

‚ÄúThe first time me and my love met

Twas in the woods o‚Äô Fyvie

And his lovely form and his speech so soft

Soon won the heart of Annie‚ÄĚ

He called me ‚ÄúMistress‚ÄĚ, I said ‚ÄúNo

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm Tifty√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Bonnie Annie‚ÄĚ

And with apples sweet he did me treat

But now alas her father heard

That the trumpeter o‚Äô Fyvie

Had had the urge to gain the heart

O‚Äô Mill o‚Äô Tifty‚Äôs Annie

And her father soon a letter wrote

And he sent it on to Fyvie

To say his daughter was bewitched

By the servant Andrew Lammie

Then up the stairs his trumpeter

He called full soon and shortly

‚ÄúPray tell me now what is this you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve done

To Mill o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Tifty√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Annie?‚ÄĚ

In wicked art I had no part

Nor therein am I canny

It was love alone the heart has won

Of Tifty‚Äôs Bonnie Annie

Woe be to Mill o‚Äô Tifty‚Äôs bride

For it has ruined many

To the trumpeter o‚Äô Fyvie

‚ÄúOh Tifty, Tifty give consent

It would be to one of a higher degree

Than the servant Andrew Lammie

‚ÄúHad she been born as rich in kin

And made her queen of Fyvie‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh, Fyvie√Ę¬Ä¬ôs lands are far and wide

At this her father did her scorn

And likewise did her mother

But woe be to her brother

For her brother struck her wondrous sore

‚ÄúOh mother, father, sisters dear

My heart was broken first by love

‚ÄúOh mother, mother oh make my bed

And turn my face to Fyvie

And it‚Äôs there I‚Äôll lie and there I‚Äôll die

For the servant Andrew Lammie‚ÄĚ

versions.title: The Spiers Family sing Tifty‚Äôs Annie
versions.lyrics:
At Mill o Tifty lived a man

In the neighbourhood o Fyvie,

He hid a lovely dauchter fair

And they ca‚Äôed her bonny Annie.

Lord Fyvie he rade by the door

Faar dwelt sweet Tifty‚Äôs Annie.

His trumpeter rode him afore,

And his name Andrew Lammie.

Her mither ca‚Äôed her tae the door,

‚ÄúCome here tae me, my Annie,

Did ye iver see a bonnier man

Than the trumpeter o Fyvie?‚ÄĚ

That nicht fan they gaed tae their beds

They aa slept soond but Annie,

For love sair oppresst her tender breast

Wi her thouchts o Andrew Lammie.

The first time that this couple met

It was in the wids o Fyvie.

And his handsome face and flatterin tongue

Seen won the hairt o Annie.

Then It‚Äôs up and doon in Tifty‚Äôs den

Faar the burn rins clear and bonny,

She his often gane tae meet her love,

Her handsome Andrew Lammie.

But faan her faither got tae ken

That the trumpeter o Fyvie

Had used his airt tae win the hairt

Oh his dauchter bonny Annie.

A letter he did quickly write

And sent it tae Lord Fyvie,

It said that Annie wis bewitched

By his servant, Andrew Lammie.

Faan Fyvie hid the letter read

He ca‚Äôed for Andrew Lammie.

‚Äú Pray tell me fit is this ye√Ę¬Ä¬ôve daen

Tae Tifty√Ę¬Ä¬ôs bonny Annie.‚ÄĚ

O waes be tae auld Tifty‚Äôs pride

In wicked airt I‚Äôve played nae pairt

Nor thoucht tae injure ony.

It‚Äôs honest love that won the hairt

Of Tifty‚Äôs bonnie Annie.

But tae Edinburgh he wis sent

For they thoucht that she wid seen forget

The next time that Lord Fyvie passed

An said, ‚ÄúIf ye came o higher kin

Say√Ę¬Ä¬ôs she, ‚ÄúYer lands are far and wide

‚ÄúO mither will ye maak my bed

For it‚Äôs there I‚Äôll lie until I die

For the love o Andrew Lammie.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAlas for Tifty√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Annie,

The fairest flower‚Äôs cut doon by love

items.title: Andro and His Cutty Gun / Blythe, Blythe and Merry Was She
items.notes:
Andro and His Cutty Gun / Blythe, Blythe and Merry Was She

[

; Ballad Index MPCAACG

; DT CUTTYGUN

; trad.]

Ewan MacColl:

Scotland Sings

Folk Songs and Ballads of Scotland

Ewan MacColl sang Andro and His Cutty Gun

in 1956 on his Riverside album

Scots Drinking Songs .

and in 1962 on his Dionysos album
items.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Blythe, blythe and merry was she,

Blythe was she but and ben.

Weel she loo‚Äôed a Hawick gill ,

And leuch tae see a tappit hen .

She took me in, she set me doon

And hecht tae keep me lawin free,

But cunnin‚Äô carlin that she was

She gart me birle my bawbee .

The landluck brocht her kebbucks ben,

Wi‚Äô girdle cakes weel toasted broon

Weel does the canny kimmer ken

They gar the swats gae glibber doon .

We loo‚Äôed the liquor weel eneuch

But wae‚Äôs my hert my cash was done,

Afore that I had quenched my drooth,

And laith was I tae pawn my shoon.

When we had three times toomed the stoop ,

And the neist chappin new begun

In started tae heeze up oor hopes,

Young Andro wi‚Äô his cutty gun .

We ca‚Äôd the bicker aft aboot,

Till dawnin‚Äô we ne‚Äôer lee‚Äôed oor bun

And aye the cleanest drinker oot

Was Andro wi‚Äô his cutty gun.

I ha‚Äôe been east, I ha‚Äôe been west,

I ha‚Äôe been far ayont the sun,

But the blythest lad that e‚Äôer I saw

Was Andro wi‚Äô his cutty gun.

items.title: Angelina Baker
items.notes:
Angelina Baker

[

, 17854

; Ballad Index BWV4047

; Bodleian

; AngelineBaker at Old Songs

; DT ANGELINE

; Mudcat 6169

; Stephen Collins Foster, 1850]

Angelina Baker

is a song written by Stephen Collins Foster for the

Christy Minstrels, and published in 1850. It laments the loss of a woman
items.lyrics:
Way down on de old plantation
dah‚Äôs where I was born,

I used to beat de whole creation
hoein in de corn;

Oh! den I work and den I sing
so happy all de day,

Till Angelina Baker came
and stole my heart away.

Chorus:

Angelina Baker! Angelina Baker‚Äôs gone

She left me here to weep a tear

And beat on de old jawbone.

I‚Äôve seen my Angelina
in de spring-time and de fall,

I‚Äôve seen her in de corn-field
and I‚Äôve seen her at de ball;

And ebry time I met her
she was smiling like de sun,

But now I‚Äôm left to weep a tear
cayse Angelina‚Äôs gone.

Angelina am so tall
she nebber sees de ground,

She hab to take a wellumscope
to look down on de town

Angelina likes de boys
as far as she can see dem,

She used to run old Massa round
to ax him for to free dem.

Early in de morning
ob a lubly summer day

I ax for Angelina,
and dey say ‚Äúshe√Ę¬Ä¬ôs gone away‚ÄĚ

I don‚Äôt know wha to find her,
cayse I don‚Äôt know wha she‚Äôs gone,

She left me here to weep a tear
and beat on de old jawbone.

versions.title: The Norfolk Broads sing Angelina Baker
versions.lyrics:
I used to work, I used to sing so happy all the day

Till Angelina Baker came and stole my heart away.

Angelina Baker, she‚Äôs the girl I love so true,

Oh, Angelina Baker, I would give my heart to you.

Chorus (after every other verse):

Angelina Baker, age of forty-three,

Fed her sugar candy but she wouldn‚Äôt marry me.

Angelina Baker, Angeline I know,

Should have married Angeline twenty years ago.

Angelina Baker lives on the village green,

The way I always loved her beats all you‚Äôve ever seen.

Angeline is handsome, Angeline is tall,

She broke her little ankle dancing in the hall.

Angelina liked the boys as far as she could see,

I waited by her window but she never looked at me.

I bought her pounds of cherries and a ribbon for her hair,

But when I went to find her Angelina wasn‚Äôt there.

I‚Äôve seen my Angelina in the springtime and the fall,

I‚Äôve seen her in the cornfield and I‚Äôve seen her at the ball.

And every time I met her she was smiling like the sun

But now I‚Äô left to weep a tear ‚Äôcause Angelina‚Äôs gone.

Early in the morning on a lovely summer‚Äôs day

I asked for Angelina and they said she‚Äôd gone away.

I don‚Äôt know where to find her cause I don‚Äôt know where she‚Äôs gone

She‚Äôs left me here to weep a tear and lay on the old jawbone.

Bought Angeline a brand new dress, neither black nor brown,

The colour of the stormy sky before the rain came down.

Sixteen horses in my team, the leader he was blind.

I dreamed that I was dying, I saw my Angeline.

Maxwelton Braes are bonnie where early fa‚Äôs the dew,

And it‚Äôs there that Annie Laurie gi‚Äôed me her promise true.

Gi‚Äôed me her promise true as ne‚Äôer forgot will be,

Aye for bonnie Annie Laurie I‚Äôd lay me doon and dee.

As e‚Äôer the sun shone on and dark blue is her e‚Äôe,

items.title: An Old Man‚Äôs Advice
items.notes:
An Old Man‚Äôs Advice

[

; Ballad Index PECS036

; trad.]

Roy Palmer:

Everyman‚Äôs Book of English Country Songs

Walter Pardon sang

An Old Man‚Äôs Advice

in a recording made by Mike Yates at home in Knapton, Norfolk on

5 December 1978. This recording was included in 1982 on his Topic album

A Country Life .

and on his 2000 Musical Traditions anthology
items.lyrics:
My grandfather worked when he was very young

And his parents felt grieved that he should.

To be forced in the fields to scare away crows

To earn himself a bit of food.

The days they were long and his wages were but small,

And to do his best he always tried.

But times are better for us all

Since the old man died.

Chorus (after each verse):

For the union is started, unite, unite.

Cheer up faint-hearted, unite, unite.

The work‚Äôs begun, never to stop again,

Since the old man died.

My grandfather said in the noontide of life

Poverty was a grief and a curse.

For it brought to his home sorrow, discord and strife,

And kept him poor with empty purse.

So he took a bold stand and joined the union band,

To help his fellow men he tried.

A union man he vow‚Äôd he‚Äôd stand

‚ÄėTil the day he died.

My grandfather‚Äôs dead. As we gathered round his bed

These last words to us he did say.

‚ÄúDon√Ę¬Ä¬ôt let your union drop, nor the education stop,

Or else you will soon rue the day.

Get united to a man, for it is your only plan;

Make the union your care and your pride.

Help on reforming every way you can.‚ÄĚ

Then the old man died.

items.title: Ar Fa La La Lo
items.notes:

Hugh S. Roberton printed Air Falalalo in his 1950 songbook

Songs of the Isles (p.32).

It is credited to words by Hugh S. Roberton,

traditional Gaelic tune arranged by Hugh S. Roberton.

Gordon McIntyre sang

items.lyrics:
There‚Äôs a lilt in the song I sing, there‚Äôs laughter and love,

There‚Äôs a tang from the sea and blue from heaven above.

Of reason there‚Äôs none and why should there be forby?

But the fire in the blood and toes and the light in the eye.

Chorus (after each verse):

Er fa la la lo horo er fa la la lay.

Er fa la la lo horo er fa la la lay.

Er fa la la lo horo er fa la la lay.

Fa li fa lo horo er fa la la lay.

The heather‚Äôs ablaze wi‚Äô bloom, the myrtle is sweets,.

There‚Äôs a song in the air, the road‚Äôs a song at our feet.

So step it along as light as the bird on the wing

And stepping along let‚Äôs join our voices and sing.

And whether the blood be Highland, Lowland or no,

And whether the skin be black or white as the snow.

Of kith and of kin we are one be it right be it wrong

As long as our hearts beat true to the lilt of the song.

O fair-haired cow-herd, if it is your desire to be first to speak:

My hand is yours if you come to me with joy.

It is sad that being together is not our destiny,

On an isle with no shore, without boat or rudder or oar.

If you go to sea, my dear, there will be a guinea in your pocket,

And drink to my health each place that you sit at the table.

With your pure, glad and joyful heart,

I desire the mouth from which comes forth the music.

I so like the mouth, the tooth that would not with rancor speak,

Lying beside me, my darling, if only you‚Äôd come:

Were not the gossipers there my dear I‚Äôd give thanks,

With order of clergy our binding together forever.

Would that you and I, on mountain, on moor or on heath,

Or on the white beach in a place where no one e‚Äôer was

Seven nights, seven days, without respite, without sleep without food

But just you and I my dear, and your arm‚Äôs protection around me.

items.title: A Rose in April
items.notes:
A Rose in April

[Kate Rusby]

Kate Rusby sang her own song A Rose in April

in 1997 on her album

Hourglass .

She commented in her liner notes:

This is a song written by me on a common theme in traditional music.

The parents of a courting couple try to separate them with tragic

repercussions.

Jon Boden sang A Rose in April

as the 2 April 2011 entry of his project

A Folk Song a Day .
items.lyrics:
‚ÄúOh can I go to my love√Ę¬Ä¬ôs side, oh let me go this day,

Please let me go to my love‚Äôs side, oh mother, don‚Äôt make me stay.

I was a rose in April and still a rose in June

I fear that come the winter, I shall no longer bloom.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh daughter, daughter you cannot go, oh you cannot go this day.

Your father, he forbade you, and at home you must remain.

You were a rose in April, and still a rose in June,

But God can send five winters and I know my rose will bloom.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMother, mother, I must away unto my true love√Ę¬Ä¬ôs side.

Bring to me my white horse, and away to him I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll ride.‚ÄĚ

She‚Äôs rode through fields of barley, and she‚Äôs rode through fields of corn,

She‚Äôs come unto her true love one hour before the dawn.

Her father, being awakened, heard of his daughter‚Äôs flight.

He‚Äôs taken up his dagger and he‚Äôs rode into the night.

He‚Äôs rode through fields of barley and he‚Äôs sped through fields of corn.

He‚Äôs come unto her true love‚Äôs house just before the dawn.

He‚Äôs found his daughter sleeping in her true lover‚Äôs arms.

He‚Äôs taken up his dagger and it‚Äôs deep into their hearts.

‚ÄúOh father, cruel father, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôve killed my love, killed me,

But now I‚Äôll rest beside him, locked in his arms I‚Äôll be,

Locked in his arms I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be.‚ÄĚ

items.title: The Hunting of Arscott of Tetcott
items.notes:
The Hunting of Arscott of Tetcott

[

; Ballad Index BGFS002

; VWML SBG/2/1/6

, SBG/3/2/37

; Mudcat 30066

; trad.]

Jim Causley sang

in 2005 on his WildGoose CD

Fruits of the Earth .

He noted:
items.lyrics:
In the month of November, in the year fifty-two,

Three jolly foxhunters, all Sons of the Blue,

Came o‚Äôer from Pencarrow, not fearing a wet coat,

To have some diversion with Arscott of Tetcott.

Chorus (after each verse, and repeat last two lines ov verse):

Sing fol-de-rol, lol-de-rol, lol-de-rol lol!

Sing fol-de-rol, lol-de-rol, lol-de-rol lol!

The daylight was dawning, right radiant the morn,

When Arscott of Tetcott he winded his horn;

And he blew such a flourish, so loud in the hall

That the rafters resounded, and danced to the call.

In the kitchen the servants, in kennels the hounds,

In stables the horses, were roused by the sounds,

On Black-cap in saddle sat Arscott,

‚ÄúToday√Ę¬Ä¬®I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll show you good sport, lads, Hark! follow, away!‚ÄĚ

They tried in the coppice, from Becket to Thorn,

There were Ringwood and Rally, and Princess and Scorn;

When out bounded Reynard, away they all went,

With the wind in their tails, on a beautiful scent.

‚ÄúHark, Vulcan!‚ÄĚ said Arscott, ‚ÄúThe best of good hounds!

Hey Venus!‚ÄĚ he shouted, ‚ÄúHow nimbly she bounds!

And nothing re-echoes so sweet in the valley,

As the music of Rattler, of Fillpot, and Rally.‚ÄĚ

They hunted o‚Äôer fallow, o‚Äôer field and on moor,

And never a hound, man or horse would give o‚Äôer.

Sly Reynard kept distance for many‚Äôs the mile,

And no one dismounted for gate or for stile.

‚ÄúHow far do you make it?‚ÄĚ said Simon, the Son,

‚ÄúThe day that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs declining will shortly be done.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWe√Ę¬Ä¬ôll follow till Doomsday,‚ÄĚ said Arscott, before

They heard the Atlantic with menacing roar.

Through Whitstone and Poundstock, St. Gennys they run,

As a fireball, red, in the sea set the sun.

When out on Penkenner a leap, and they go,

Full five hundred feet to the ocean below.

When the tempest is howling, his horn you may hear,

And the bay of his hounds in their headlong career;

For John Arscott of Tetcott loves hunting so well,

That he breaks for the pastime from Heaven or Hell.

When the full moon is shining as clear as the day,

John Arscott still hunts round the country, they say;

You may see him on Black-Cap, and hear, in full cry,

The pack from Pencarrow to Dazard go by.

items.title: Arthur O‚ÄôBradley‚Äôs Wedding
items.notes:
[

; Master title: Arthur O‚ÄôBradley‚Äôs Wedding

; Ballad Index ReSh005

; VWML HHA/32/1

; Bodleian

; GlosTrad

; Wiltshire

65

, 1097

; trad.]

Fay Hield and Nancy Kerr sang

Arthur O‚ÄôBradley
items.lyrics:
Come neighbours and listen a while

If ever you wish for to smile

Or hear a true story of old,

Attend to what I do unfold.

A fella whose fame did resound

Through every village and town,

For fun, for frolic and whim,

None ever was equal to him.

Arthur being stout and bold,

Near upon thirty years old.

He needs a-wooing must go

To get him a lady, you know.

So getting young Dolly‚Äôs consent,

Away to be married they went.

To make himself noble appear

He mounted the old padded mare.

Chorus:

Come neighbours and listen a while

If ever you wish for to smile

Or hear a true story of old,

Attend to what I do unfold.

Then he packed up his drudgery horse

And put on his holiday clothes.

His coat it was scarlet so fine,

Full trimmed with buttons behind.

Two sleeves it had, it is true,

One yellow, the other was blue.

The cuffs and capes of green,

The longest that ever were seen.

His hat, though greasy and tore,

Cocked up with a feather before.

Under his chin it was tied

With a strip from an old cow‚Äôs hide.

His breeches three times had been turned

And two holes in the left side were burned.

This was a misfortune, you‚Äôll say,

But still he looked gallant and gay.

Thus kitted away he did ride,

Whilst Dolly did trudge by his side,

Till coming up to the church door

Amid five thousand or more.

Then from the old mare he did ‚Äôlight

Which put the poor clerk in a fright.

And the parson so dreadfully shook

That he presently dropped down his book.

Which Arthur soon picked up again

And swore that he must now begin,

Crying, ‚ÄúDolly, my dearie, come hither

And let us be married together.‚ÄĚ

Chorus

The parson, his duty discharged

Without any fee or reward,

He swore no money he‚Äôd have

And poor Arthur had none him to give.

To make him a little amends

He invited him home with his friends,

Take a sweet kiss on the bride

And eat a good dinner beside.

The dishes though few were good,

Sweetest of all living food.

There was roast guinea pig and a bantam,

Sheep‚Äôs head stewed in a lanthorn.

Two calves feet and a bull‚Äôs trotter,

The fore and hind legs of an otter,

Lampfish, lippets and dabs,

Crayfish, cockles and crabs.

Red herrings and sprats by the dozen

To feast all his uncles and cousins

Who were so well pleased with the treat,

Heartily they did all eat.

Chorus

The guests being well satisfied,

The dishes were laid on one side.

And Arthur, to make their hearts merry,

Brought pale ale, perkin and perry.

‚ÄúCome, give us a dance,‚ÄĚ quoth Doll,

‚ÄúCome, Geoffrey, and play us Mad Moll.

‚ÄôTis time to be merry and frisky,

But first let us have some more whisky.

‚ÄúMy lily, my lark, my love,

My daffy down dilly, my dove,

My everything and my wife,

I ne√Ę¬Ä¬ôer was so pleased in my life.‚ÄĚ

The pipers then screwed up their bags

And the girls began shaking their rags.

Whilst some only one leg had gotten

And that which they had it was rotten.

The parson let off at the top;

Some danced, while the others did hop.

There was lead up and down, figure in,

Cross hands and then back again.

Dancing they spent the whole night

Till Phoebus appeared in their sight,

When each took a kiss of the bride

And hopped to his own fireside.

Chorus

items.title: A Sailor in the North Country
items.notes:
A Sailor in the North Country

[

; Master title: A Sailor in the North Country

; Ballad Index VWL093

; VWML RVW2/2/94

; GlosTrad

; Mudcat 22869

; trad.]

Mrs Verrall of Monksgate, near Horsham, Sussex, sang

A Sailor in the North Country

to Ralph Vaughan Williams in 1904

[VWML RVW2/2/94 ]
items.lyrics:
A sailor in the north country,

He had a most beautiful wife.

Her courage was so great and her temper more than sweet,

And the sailor he loved her as his life.

As they were walking out one day,

They met a noble captain on the way,

Kind obedience to the maid! But she bowed and nothing said,

‚ÄôTwas her beauty did the captain‚Äôs heart betray.

The captain to his house then he goes,

And sent for the sailor straight away,

‚ÄúMy business runs so: to the West Indies you must go,

In the morning, or by the break of day.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúTo obey the noble Master I will go,

On the sea, to venture my life.‚ÄĚ

But little did he dream the captain‚Äôs heart was so inflamed,

On the charms of his most beautiful wife.

The sailor to his wife then he goes,

And kissed her and called her his dear.

‚ÄúBad news I have to tell You, I must bid you farewell,

In the morning when daylight does appear.‚ÄĚ

As soon as she beard him say so,

She wrung her hands and bitterly did cry,

She kissed him and said: ‚ÄúMy dear Jimmy I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm afraid

You√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be drowned in the raging ocean wide.‚ÄĚ

The hour and the moment did come,

The poor sailor no longer could stay

To bear his wife lament till his heart was discontent.

He kissed her and went weeping away.

He had only been gone two days or three

On the seas for to venture his life,

Before the captain came with his heart in great flame,

To seize on the poor sailor‚Äôs wife.

‚ÄúYour pardon, dear lady,‚ÄĚ he cried,

‚ÄúYour pardon, dear lady, if you please,

Your pardon if you please, for ‚Äôthis you can give me ease

One night to enjoy your sweet charms.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh, are you any lord, duke, or king,

Or are you any ruler of the land?

The King shall lose his crown before at my feet you shall lie down,

Or before I will be at your command.

‚Äú√Ę¬Ä¬ôTwas only one twelve-month ago

That I was made your man Jimmy‚Äôs bride,

It‚Äôs pleasing to my lot, the best husband I have got,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be constant unto him for life.‚ÄĚ

items.title: As I Came in by Yon Castle Wall
items.notes:
As I Came in by Yon Castle Wall

[

; trad.]

Ellen: I learned this from Don Martin, a founder member of the folk group

Clutha.

The song is now generally attributed to Burns, but it doesn‚Äôt bear his name

on its first known printing in the
items.lyrics:
As I came in by yon castle wa

And doon by yon garden green,

It was there I spied a bonny bonny lass

But the flower borders were us between.

And such a bonny lassie she was

As ever my eyes had seen.

‚ÄúFive hundred pounds would I give

Tae have such a bonny bride as ye.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh tae have such a bonny bride as me,

Young man you‚Äôre sairly mista‚Äôen.

Gin you were the king o‚Äô all Scotland

I would disdain to be your queen.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh talk not so very high bonny lass,

Oh talk not so very, very high,

For the man at the fair that would sell,

He mun learn frae the man that would buy.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAh but I mean to climb a far higher tree

And herry a far richer nest.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAch tak this advice frae me my bonny lass

Humility would serve ye best.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh to have such a bonny bride as me,

Young man you‚Äôre sorely mista‚Äôen.

If you were the king o‚Äô all Scotland

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd disdain to be your queen.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh talk not so very high, bonny lass,

‚ÄúAh but I mean to climb a far higher tree

‚ÄúOh take this advice from me my bonny lass

Humility would serve you best.‚ÄĚ

items.title: As I Was Going to Banbury
items.notes:
As I Was Going to Banbury

[

; Ballad Index Pea014

, CJS2/10/2095

Maud Karpeles:

items.lyrics:
As I was a-going to Banbury

Ri fol latitee O

As I was a-going to Banbury

I saw a fine coddlin apple tree

With a ri fol latitee O.

And when the coddlins began to fall

I saw five hundred men in all

One of the men I saw was dead

I sent for a hatchet to open his head

Out of his head there ran a spring

And seven young salmon a-learning to sing

Now one of the salmon was big as I

And do you not think I am telling a lie?

One of the salmon was big as an elf

If you want any more you can sing it yourself

items.title: As Shepherds Watched Their Fleecy Care
items.notes:
As Shepherds Watched Their Fleecy Care

[

; Ballad Index PECS142

; Joseph Key]

Roy Palmer:

Everyman‚Äôs Book of English Country Songs

As Shepherds Watched Their Fleecy Care

is a song written by Joseph Key and first published in 1785 in

Five Anthems, Four Collects, Twenty Psalm Tunes .

Roy Palmer had a nearly identical version of The Fleecy Care ,

but without the chorus, in his 1979

Everyman‚Äôs Book of English Country Songs .
items.lyrics:
As shepherds watched their fleecy care,

An heav‚Äônly angel did appear:

‚ÄúShepherds, attend; to you I bring

Glad tidings of a new-born king.‚ÄĚ

Chorus (after each verse):

Loud hosannas send on high,

Raise your voices to the sky:

Hymns of praises ever sing,

To this new-born heav‚Äônly King.

In Bethle‚Äôm town this blessed morn,

The Saviour of mankind is born;

Born of a spotless virgin pure,

Free from all sin, from guilt secure.

Arise, your tender care forsake,

With hasty steps your journey take,

To David‚Äôs city; there you‚Äôll find

The great redeemer of mankind.

In swaddling clothes the babe behold,

No costly garb his limbs unfold,

Laid in a manger there you‚Äôll see

The pattern of humility.

To save us from eternal death,

The great Messiah came on earth;

Then let us with united voice

In hallelujahs all rejoice.

items.title: Atholl Gathering / Gallant Murray
items.notes:
Atholl Gathering / Gallant Murray

, 20473

He noted:

A 1745 song. The Murrary referred to is Lord George Murray,
items.lyrics:
Wha will ride wi‚Äô gallant Murray?

Wha will ride wi‚Äô Georgie‚Äôs sel?

He‚Äôs the flower o‚Äô a‚Äô Glenisla,

And the darling o‚Äô Dunkel‚Äô.

See the white rose in his bonnets!

See his banner o‚Äôer the Tayl

His gude sword he now has drawn it,

And has flung the sheath away.

Menzies he‚Äôs our friend and brother,

Gask and Strowan are nae slack;

Noble Perth has taen the field,

And a‚Äô the Drummonds at his back.

Let us ride wi‚Äô gallant Murray,

Let us fight for Charlie‚Äôs crown;

From the right we‚Äôll never sinder,

Till we bring the tyrant down.

versions.title: Andy M. Stewart sings Gallant Murray (Gathering of Athole)
versions.lyrics:
Chorus (after each verse):

Wha will ride wi‚Äô gallant Murray?

Wha will ride wi‚Äô Geordie‚Äôs sei‚Äô?

He‚Äôs the floow‚Äôr o‚Äô a‚Äô Glenisla

And the darlin‚Äô o‚Äô Dunkel‚Äô

See the white rose in his bonnet

See his banner o‚Äôer the Tay

His gude sword‚Äô he now has drawn it

And he‚Äôs flung the sheath away

Every faithful Murray follows

First of heroes! Best of men

Every true and trusty Stewart

Blythely leaves his native glen

Athole lads are lads of honour

Westland rogues are rebels a‚Äô

When we come within their border

We may gar the Campbells claw

Menzies, he‚Äôs our friend and brother

Gask and Strowan are nae slack

Noble Perth has ta‚Äôen the field

And a‚Äô the Drummonds at his back

Let us ride wi‚Äô gallant Murray

From the right we‚Äôll never sinder

Till we bring the tyrants down

Mackintosh, the gallant soldier

Wi‚Äô the Grahams and Gordons gay

Spite of all their chiefs could say

Point the musket bend the rapier

Shift the brog for Lowland shoe

Scour the durk, and face the danger

items.title: Auld Lang Syne
items.notes:
Auld Lang Syne

; Ballad Index FSWB381B

, AULDLNG2

, 53209

; trad.]

Norman Buchan:

Ralph Dunstan:
items.lyrics:
We twa hae rin aboot the braes

And pu‚Äôd the gowans fine

We‚Äôve wandered many‚Äôs the weary step

Since auld lang syne

We twa he paidled in the burn

Frae morning sun till dine

Since auld lang syne

Aye and surely you‚Äôll be your pint stoup

And surely I‚Äôll be mine

We‚Äôll tak a cup o‚Äô kindness yet

For auld lang syne

So here‚Äôs a hand my trusty frien‚Äô

And here‚Äôs a haud o‚Äô thine

We‚Äôll drink a right guid wullie waught

For the sake o‚Äô auld lang syne

For auld lang syne my dear

For auld lang syne

We‚Äôll tak‚Äô a cup o‚Äô kindness yet

For the sake o‚Äô auld lang syne

versions.title: Mairi Campbell sings Auld Lang Syne
versions.lyrics:
Should auld acquaintance be forgot,

And never brought to mind?

Should auld acquaintance be forgot,

And auld lang syne?

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

For auld lang syne, my jo,

For auld lang syne,

We‚Äôll tak a cup o kindness yet,

For auld lang syne.

And surely ye‚Äôll be your pint-stowp!

And surely I‚Äôll be mine!

And we‚Äôll tak a cup o kindness yet,

For auld lang syne.

We twa hae run about the braes,

And pow‚Äôd the gowans fine;

But we‚Äôve wander‚Äôd mony a weary fitt,

Sin auld lang syne.

We twa hae paidl‚Äôd in the burn,

Frae morning sun till dine;

But seas between us braid hae roar‚Äôd

And there‚Äôs a hand, my trusty fiere!

And gie‚Äôs a hand o thine!

And we‚Äôll tak a right gude-willie-waught,

Should old acquaintance be forgot,

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

For old times,

We‚Äôll take a cup of kindness yet,

items.title: Auld Robin Gray
items.notes:
Auld Robin Gray

[

; G/D 7:1364

; Ballad Index Pea482

; Mudcat 152830

; trad.]

David Herd:

Ancient and Modern Scottish Songs, Heroic Ballads, etc., Second Volume

Tom Hughes played the song air Auld Robin Gray

Tom Hughes and His Border Fiddle

and on the 2015 Springthyme anthology
items.lyrics:
When the Sheep are in the Fauld, and the Kye at hame,

And a‚Äô the warl‚Äôd to Sleep are gane,

The Waes o‚Äô my heart fa‚Äô in Show‚Äôrs frae my ee

When my Gudeman lyes sound by me.

Young Jamie lo‚Äôed me well and he sought me for his bride;

But saving a crown he had naething beside,

To make that crown a pound my Jamie gade to Sea;

And the crown and the pound were baith for me.

He had nae been awa a week but only twa,

When my mother she fell sick and the Cow was stown awa,

My father brake his arm and my Jamie at the sea.

And Auld Robin Gray came a-courting me.

My father couldna work and my mother couldna spin;

I toil‚Äôd day and night but their bread I couldna win;

Auld Rob maintain‚Äôd them baith and wi‚Äô tears in his ee

Said Jenny for their saks O marry me.

My heart it said nay I look‚Äôd for Jamie back,

But the wind it blew high and the Ship it was a wrack,

The Ship it was a wrack why didna Jenny dee?

Or why do I live to say waes me.

Auld Robin argued sair tho‚Äô my mother didna speak,

She look‚Äôd in my face till my heart was like to break.

So they gied him my hand tho‚Äô my heart was in the sea,

And Auld Robin Gray is Gudeman to me.

I hadna been a wife a week but only four,

When sitting sae mournfuly at the door,

I Saw my Jamies wraith for I couldna think it he,

Till he said I‚Äôm come back for to marry thee.

O sair did we greet, and mickle did we say,

We took but ae kiss and we tore ourselves away.

I wish that I were dead but I‚Äôm no like to dee,

And why do I live to say waes me.

I gang like a Ghaist, and I carenae to spin,

I darena think on Jamie for that wad be a sin.

But I‚Äôll do my best a Gudewife to be,

For Auld Robin Gray is kind to me.

versions.title: Lainey Dempsey sings Auld Robin Gray
versions.lyrics:
When the sheep are in the fauld, and the kye at hame

And all the world tae sleep has gane

The waes o‚Äô my heart fall like showers fae my e‚Äôe

When my gid man lies sound by me

May Jamie loved me well and he sought me for his bride

But saving for a pound he had naething else beside

Tae make that crown a pound, my Jamie gaed tae sea

And the crown and the pound were baith fer me

He hadne been gane fer 12 months and a day

When ma faither broke his leg and oor kye wis stown away

Ma mither she fell sick, and ma Jamie at the sea

And auld Robin Gray came a courtin‚Äô me

Ma faither couldne work, ma mither couldne spin

I toiled nicht and day but their bread I couldne win

Auld Rab maintained them baith and wi‚Äô tears in his e‚Äôe

Said Jeanie fer their sake, O marry me?

My heart it said nae, I wanted Jamie back

But the wind it had blown strong and his ship it was a wreck

His ship it was a wreck o why didne Jamie Dee?

And why dae I live to say wae is me?

Ma faither pressed me sair, ma mither didne speak

But she looked intae ma face and my heart wis fit tae break

They gied him my hand, though ma heart wis at the sea

And Auld Robin Gray is guid man tae me

I hudne been a wife a week but only four

When sitting sae mournfully aw nicht at the door

I saw ma Jamie‚Äôs ghaist, for I couldne think it he

Till he said I‚Äôve come hame for tae marry thee

Well sair did we greet and meikle did we say

We took but ae kiss, and we tore oorselves away

I wish that I were died but I‚Äôm no like to dee

Oh why do I live to say ‚ÄúWoe is me‚ÄĚ?

I care nae tae work, I care nae tae spin

I darenae think o Jamie, for that would be a sin

But I‚Äôll dae ma best, a guid wife tae be,

For Auld Robin Gray, he is kind tae me

items.title: Avington Pond
items.notes:
Avington Pond

items.lyrics:
Come, gentlemen all, and I‚Äôll sing you a song

About the mudplumpers of Avington Pond.

They are hearty good fellows, I‚Äôll give them their due,

Their delight is in drinking Miss Munday‚Äôs strong brew.

Chorus (after each verse):

Derry down, down, down, derry down

The tenth of December this job was begun,

How the wheelbarrows did rattle and run;

With three or four hundred of wheels to spin round,

There‚Äôs no better hundred, I‚Äôll bet fifty pound.

There‚Äôs Davis and Morris and Will Mason too,

Hearty good fellows, I‚Äôll give them their due;

One day the strong beer got in Davis‚Äôs crown,

And into the water he came tumbling down.

Here‚Äôs Archer and Churcher and Joe, Jim and Dick,

For swiftness of run as close we could get;

Here‚Äôs Andrew‚Äôs gang, we will push them about,

We‚Äôll all work like Turks and we‚Äôll soon work it out.

So now this job it is finished and done,

Get over to Itchen to get your due fund;

We‚Äôll dance and be merry, we‚Äôll drink and we‚Äôll sing,

We‚Äôll make every room in the Plough for to ring.

items.title: Awake, Arise Good Christians
items.notes:
Awake, Arise Good Christians

[

; trad.]

is a carol from the Sheffield village carols tradition.

items.lyrics:
Awake, arise good Christians, let nothing you dismay,

When a holy band of angels came down from God on high!

Chorus (after each verse):

Hosannah! Hosannah! To Jesus we‚Äôll sing,

Hosannah! Hosannah! Our Saviour, our King.

‚ÄúFear not, we bring glad tidings, for on this happy morn,

The promised One, the Saviour, in Bethlehem√Ę¬Ä¬ôs town was born!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThen let us go in haste,‚ÄĚ they say, ‚ÄúThis Holy Child to find.‚ÄĚ

And like unto the shepherds, we wander far and near,

And bid you wake, good Christians, the joyful news to hear;

items.title: Awa Wi Ma Laddie

items.lyrics:

items.title: Away My Brave Boys
items.notes:
Away My Brave Boys

[

; trad.]

Eliza Carthy sang

Away My Brave Boys

on her 2023 album

Conversations We‚Äôve Had Before .

She noted:

From [a Victorian Songster given to me by our friend Slim],

a nice bit of jingoism. Made me think of this though, apparently the first

anti-war song ever recorded:

I didn‚Äôt raise my boy to be a soldier
items.lyrics:
Away, my brave boys, hoist the flags, beat the drums,

Let the streamers wave over the main:

When old England she calls us, we merrily come,

She won‚Äôt call a sailor in vain;

Already we seem an armada to chase,

Already behold the galleons;

Undaunted, unconquer‚Äôd, look Death in the face,

And return with a load of doubloons.

Then farewell, for a time, lovely sweethearts, dear wives!

Nancy, fear not the death of Old Blue;

Though we leave you, and merrily venture our lives,

To our loved ones we‚Äôll ever be true;

With spirit we go an armada to chase,

With rapture behold the galleons,

Undaunted, unconquer‚Äôd, look Death in the face,

And return with a load of doubloons.

items.title: A Week‚Äôs Work Well Done / On Monday Morning (I Married a Wife)
items.notes:
[

; Master title: A Week‚Äôs Work Well Done

; Laws Q6

; Ballad Index LQ06

; VWML GG/1/17/1073

, SBG/3/14/23A

, RoudFS/S269594

; Wiltshire

515

; DT HOLLYTWG

; Mudcat 19427

; trad.]
items.lyrics:
On Monday morning I married a wife,

Thinking to live a sober life;

But as she turned out, I‚Äôd better be dead,

The remarkable day that I was wed,

To me rite fol-lol-liddle-lol-le-day.

On Tuesday morning I goes to the wood,

I cut a stick both fine and good.

The finest stick that ever you did see,

I cut him out of a holly, holly tree,

To me rite fol-lol-liddle-lol-le-day.

On Wednesday morning then home I goes,

Thinking a battle I must try.

I beat her about her back and her wig

Until I‚Äôd a-broke my holly, holly twig,

To me rite fol-lol-liddle-lol-le-day.

O Thursday morning my poor wife,

She was sick and like to die.

If she isn‚Äôt better tomorrow to be

The devil may have her tomorrow for me,

To me rite fol-lol-liddle-lol-le-day.

On Friday morning the sun did shine

And I walked out in the midst of my prime.

O the devil he‚Äôs come in, in the midst of the game

And he took her away, both blind and lame,

To me rite fol-lol-liddle-lol-le-day.

On Saturday morning it‚Äôs five days past,

My poor wife is dead at last.

The big bell shall ring and the little one shall toll

Ad I‚Äôll go home as a jolly old soul,

To me rite fol-lol-liddle-lol-le-day.

On Sunday morning I dined without,

I had ne‚Äôer a wife to scold me about.

Here‚Äôs good luck to my pipe, to my bottle and my friend,

And here‚Äôs good luck to a week‚Äôs work end,

To me rite fol-lol-liddle-lol-le-day.

versions.title: Texas Gladden sings The Scolding Wife

I cut me some switches so sharp and keen,

Thursday morning I waled her well,

And if she is not better tomorrow,

I ‚Äôll wale her some more to her great sorrow.

On Friday morning, just before day,

And carried her off in a dust of wind.

On Saturday morning, all alone,

I had no wife nor scolding drone,

And my week‚Äôs work was at an end,

And my whiskey bottle was my best friend.

Than ever I heard in my life before.

On Wednesday morning I went to the wood,

I cuts me a twig of the holly green,

I trust the toughest I‚Äôd ever seen.

I hung the stick up well to dry,

I thought on Thursday it to try,

I laid it about her head and her back,

Before my twig began to crack.

On Friday morning to my surprise,

A little before the sun did rise,

She rattled her clapper in scolding tone,

I turn‚Äôd my back and I left her alone.

On Saturday morning, as I may say,

As she on her pillow consulting lay,

A Bogie arrived in fume and flame

And carried her off both blind and lame.

On Sunday, neighbours, I dines without

A scolding wife and a brawling rout;

Enjoys my bottle, and my best friend,

Surely this is a brave week‚Äôs end.

items.title: Babylon

Babylon is Fallen is a church hymn of North American origin,

printed in Sacred Harp , No. 117.

G.W. William‚Äôs article

‚ÄúBabylon is Fallen: The Story of a North American Hymn‚ÄĚ in

The Hymn , Volume 44, April 1993, pp. 31-35, said:
items.lyrics:
Hail the day so long expected!

Zion‚Äôs walls are now erected,

And her watchmen publish peace:

From the distant coasts of Shinar,

The shrill trumpet loudly roars

Chorus (twice after each verse):

Babylon is fallen! is fallen! is fallen!

Babylon is fallen to rise no more.

Hark, and hear her people crying,

‚ÄúSee the city disappear!

Trade and traffic all are dying!

Lo, we sink and perish here!‚ÄĚ

Sailors who have bought her traffic,

Crying from her distant shore,

All her merchants cry with wonder,

‚ÄúWhat is this that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs come to pass?‚ÄĚ

Murm‚Äôring like the distant thunder

Crying out, ‚ÄúAlas! Alas!‚ÄĚ

Swell the sound, ye kings and nobles!

Priests and people, rich and poor!

Lo, the captives are returning!

Up to Zion see them fly!

While the smoke of Babel‚Äôs burning

Rolls across the darken‚Äôd sky!

Days of mourning now are ended,

Years of bondage now are o‚Äôer,

Zion‚Äôs children raise your voices,

And the joyful news proclaim!

How the heavenly host rejoices!

Shout and echo back the same!

See the ancients of the city,

Terrify‚Äôd at the uproar!

Tune your harps, ye heavenly choir!

Shout, ye foll‚Äôwers of the Lamb!

See the city all on fire!

Clap your hands and blow the flame!

Now‚Äôs the day of compensation

On the scarlet colour‚Äôd whore;
