items.title: Baker‚Äôs Oven
items.notes:
Baker‚Äôs Oven

[

; VWML AW/2/25

; Wiltshire

67

; trad.]

Rosie Hood sang

Baker‚Äôs Oven

on her 2017 RootBeat CD,

The Beautiful & the Actual .

She noted:

An unusual old song with a number of bread based puns collected from
items.lyrics:
Job Jenkins was a baker, a very honest elf,

And by baking crust and crumb he made a tidy crust himself.

But Job he lived in better days when bills were freely paid

And bakers were thought honest men for bread was never weighed.

Success creates ambition in this world between the poles,

Job thirsted after office even though a master of the rolls.

Job‚Äôs patience it did not tire out as quickly did appear;

They very soon appointed him as Parish Overseer.

At length the tallow chandler the debt of nature paid,

And in his place, without delay, Job the churchwarden was made.

He soon declared that to his house a man must be a sinner

To toil for parish work and go without his parish dinner.

While strolling through the churchyard he saw some old tombstones

That long had marked the resting place of some poor old neighbour‚Äôs bones;

‚ÄúThese bodies have long gone to dust, the stone√Ę¬Ä¬ôs no use,‚ÄĚ he said,

‚ÄúThey√Ę¬Ä¬ôll mend the bottom to my oven and improve my bread.‚ÄĚ

Tom Snooks, the parish mason, a very sporting blade,

Who in racehorses and the dead he had done a decent trade;

To him Job gave the order, regardless of amount,

And charged it to the parish in his next half year‚Äôs account.

The job was done, the bread was baked, Job in his highest glee

Goes up at early morning so that he might the improvement see;

But soon as drawn he dropped the peal with horror on his looks,

And roared out like a madman and knocked down Tommy Snooks.

‚ÄúGet up, you wretch! and come and see the blunders you have made!

Your tombstone bottoms sure will prove a deathblow to my trade.‚ÄĚ

He took him to the bakehouse where a curious sight was seen,

The words on every loaf were marked that on the tombstone been.

One quarter had, ‚ÄúIn memory of‚ÄĚ, another, ‚ÄúHere to pine‚ÄĚ,

A third, ‚ÄúDeparted from this life at the age of ninety-nine‚ÄĚ.

A batch of rolls when they were done said this, ‚ÄúOur time is past,

Thus day by day we pine away and come to this at last.‚ÄĚ

Next came the cottage loaves, and there, upon the bottoms plain,

‚ÄúWe trust in Him that made us and we hope to rise again.‚ÄĚ

On every loaf that they drew out all from that oven door

There on the bread each one could read the letters on the floor.

Now, Snooks he turned away his head, his laughter to conceal,

He said he thought it was a nobby way of making a bread seal;

Says Job, ‚ÄúThy seal has sealed my fate, how can I sell my bread

To feed the living when it bears the memory of the dead?‚ÄĚ

items.title: Balaclava / The Light Brigade
items.notes:
Balaclava / The Light Brigade

[

; Master title: Balaclava

; Ballad Index Doe276

; trad.]

The Cruels Wars

The Rambling Soldier

Walter Pardon sang

Balaclava

at home in his cottage in Knapton, Norfolk, on 12 February 1977.

This recording was released in the same year on his Leader album

Our Side of the Baulk .
items.lyrics:
Six hundred stalwart warriors, of England‚Äôs pride the best,

Did grasp the lance and sabre on Balaclava‚Äôs crest;

And with their trusty leader, Earl Cardigan the brave,

Dashed through the Russian valley to glory or a grave.

Their foemen stood in thousands, a dark and awful mass,

Beneath their famous strongholds resolved to guard the pass;

Their guns with fierce defiance belched thunders up the vale,

Where sat our English horsemen firm beneath their iron gale.

O ‚Äôtis a famous story, proclaim it far and wide,

And let your children‚Äôs children re-echo it with pride.

How Cardigan the fearless, his name immortal made,

When he crossed that Russian Valley with his famous Light Brigade.

When Nolan brought the order, ‚ÄúGreat God, can it be true?‚ÄĚ

Cried Cardigan the fearless, ‚ÄúAnd my Brigade so few;

To take these awful cannon from yonder teeming mass;

Tis madness, sir, where shall we charge? What guns bring from the pass?‚ÄĚ

The messenger with hauteur looked once at the brave Earl,

Then pointing to the enemy, his lip began to curl.

‚ÄúThere, there, my lord, there are your guns and there your foemen too‚ÄĚ,

Then turned his charger‚Äôs head away, and bade the Earl adieu.

And they were but six hundred, ‚Äôgainst two score thousand foes,

Hemmed in with furious cannon, and crushed with savage blows;

Yet fought they there like heroes, for our noble England‚Äôs fame;

O glorious charge, heroic deed! what glory crowns thy name.

Four hundred of those soldiers fell, fighting where they stood.

And thus that fatal death vale they enriched with English blood;

Four hundred of those soldiers bequeathed their lives away,

To the England they had fought for on that wild October day.

versions.title: Roy Last sings Balaclava
versions.lyrics:

When he crossed the Russian valley with his gallant Light Brigade.

Horty Nolan brought the order: ‚ÄúGood God can it be true?‚ÄĚ

Cried Cardigan the fearless, ‚ÄúAnd my brigade so few!

To face those deadly cannon from yonder teeming mass.

Why it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs madness sir: where shall we charge, what gun bring up the pass?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄĚThere, there my lord, there are the guns, there are your foemen too.‚ÄĚ

He turned his horse‚Äôs head away and he bid the Earl adieu.

Did grasp the lance and sabre on Balaclava‚Äôs crest.

And with their trusty leader, Earl Cardigan the brave,

They crossed that Russian valley, to glory and the grave.

items.lyrics:

items.title: Baltimore
items.notes:
Baltimore

[

; Ballad Index Hugi418

; trad.]

Stan Hill noted in his book

Shanties From the Seven Seas

on the saucy Baltimore :

Next we have a version of a once well-known shore-song called

Baltimore . It was a shanty very popular in German sailing ships,

usually sung at the capstan when making sail by leading the halyard to the

capstan instead of hauling on the fall. I have taken part in the singing

of it myself aboard a German barque, and it was sung as late as 1951 aboard
items.lyrics:
And he kissed her on the cheek and the crew begin to roar,

Oh, oh and up she goes, we‚Äôre bound for Baltimore.

And he kissed her on the face and the crew begin to roar,

Oh, oh and up she goes, we‚Äôre bound for Baltimore.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

No more, no more, no more, we‚Äôre going to sea no more.

As soon as we reach port tonight we‚Äôre heading for the shore.

And then he kissed her on the neck [etc.]

And he kissed her on the face [etc.]

And then he kissed her on the lips [etc.]

And then he kissed her on the arms [etc.]

And he kissed her on the legs [etc.]

items.title: Bampton Fair
items.notes:
Bampton Fair

[

; Mudcat 111289

; trad.]

Jim Causley sang

Bampton Fair ,

which seems to be a Devon variant of Patrick Christopher Farrell‚Äôs

Galway song

Ballinasloe Fair ,

on his 2021 album

Devonshire Roses .

He noted:
items.lyrics:
When I was a young maid my daddy said to me,

Daughter, come along to the horse fair with me,

Up in the morning, away we will go

And we‚Äôll take the long road to the Fair at Bampton Hall.

We started off at daybreak in the cold October air,

We saw the Travelling People all going to the fair.

And as we went through Witheridge, it wasn‚Äôt far to go

To reach the town of Tiverton that made history long ago.

And as we jogged along to the outskirts of the fair

We then went down to Brook Street and to the Market Square.

‚ÄôTwas there we saw the horse dealers busy at their trade,

They‚Äôd horses by the hundred, by every hue and shape.

Chorus (after each verse):

There was draught horses, race horses, cobs and hunters too,

And little Exmoor ponies, but them were quite a few.

We heard the ballad singers singing songs of long ago

Everyone was gathered for the Fair at Bampton Hall.

And as we went along the road we saw the famous ring,

‚ÄôTwas here they were performing the crowning of the King.

We heard him make his speeches and he seems so very proud

As he jumped unto the platform, at the cheering of the crowd.

But times have changed a bit today, a new system now exists,

They‚Äôre chosen by the ballots instead of by the fist.

The (fathers?) they would practice in the days of long ago

To fight the whole in battle for the King of Bampton Hall.

And then to Maggie May‚Äôs we went for a jar or two of cider,

We heard the music playing and the dancing getting wilder.

The women in their home-made shawls, the men in hobnail boots

The atmosphere was jolly with the fiddles and the flutes.

And then my father says to me, he‚Äôd had enough to drink,

So hop into the horse and cart, we hit the midnight trip.

We started on the road again, it was a fair a way‚Äôs to go,

I never shall forget about the Fair at Bampton Hall.

items.title: Banks of Marble
items.notes:
Banks of Marble

[

; DT BNKMRBLE

A studio recording was released in 2009 on their CD

Ewan McLennan sang
items.lyrics:
I‚Äôve travelled round this country

From shore to shining shore

It really made me wonder

At the things I heard and saw.

I saw the weary farmer

Ploughing sod and loam

l heard the auction hammer

Just a‚Äô knocking down his door

But the banks are made of marble

With a guard at every door

And the vaults are stuffed with silver

That the farmer sweated for

I saw the seaman standing

Idly by the shore

And I heard the bosses saying

Got no work for you no more

But the banks are made of marble

With a guard at every door

And the vaults are stuffed with silver

That the seaman sweated for

I saw the weary miner

Scrubbing coal dust from his back

I heard his children crying

Got no coal to heat the shack

But the banks are made of marble

With a guard at every door

And the vaults are stuffed with silver

That the miner sweated for

I‚Äôve seen the weary old folks

Without a nickel left to give

And the hovels they exist in

Ain‚Äôt fit for rats to live

But the banks are made of marble

With a guard at every door

And the vaults are stuffed with silver

That the old folks sweated for

I‚Äôve seen my people working

Throughout this mighty land

And I‚Äôve prayed we get together

And together make a stand

Then we‚Äôd own those banks of marble

With a guard at every door

And we‚Äôd share those vaults of silver

That we‚Äôve all sweated for

items.title: Banks of the Clyde
items.notes:
Banks of the Clyde

[

; Henry H812

; Ballad Index HHH812

; Bodleian

; Mudcat 83006

; trad.]

Niamh Parsons sang

Banks of the Clyde

in 2002 on her Green Linnet album

Heart‚Äôs Desire .

She noted:
items.lyrics:
As I went out a-walking one pleasant summer‚Äôs evening

Down by the banks of a dear winding stream,

In ambush I lay as two lovers were talking

And clearly the streams they did gently play.

A young sailor standing by he appeared as a stranger

And he said, ‚ÄúGentle fair one have come from afar,

So do not be offended nor consider it a danger

For to walk by the side of a jolly young tar.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell indeed then kind sir, √Ę¬Ä¬ôtis the truth I will tell you,

For the clothes that you wear they are dear to my heart,

They are like my young William‚Äôs whom I love so dearly

Who‚Äôs now crossing die ocean to some foreign part.

And what if your William whom you love so dearly

Is now joined in wedlock in some foreign part?

Well if that be the case then you have lost him forever

So give to me your hand, love, and say you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be mine.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI pray you√Ę¬Ä¬ôll excuse me for I must retire

And in this green shade I will bid you adieu.

‚ÄôTis a humble request that you seem to desire

But my favour, kind sir, to another is due.

Should flowers decay in the cold stormy weather

Yet spring‚Äôs gentle breezes they will then renew.

This heart from my true love no other will sever,

So fare thee well young man I‚Äôll hid you adieu.

‚ÄúAnd since that sad fortune has now separated

Right constant I‚Äôll prove should I ne‚Äôer see him more.

Whilst yonder green hills are with flowers decorated,

For my own dearest William I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll always adore.‚ÄĚ

Well he paused and he gazed with his eyes full of pleasure,

In his former disguise he could no longer hide.

‚ÄôTil at length he explained with full joy being the measure,

‚ÄúIs this my love Jane I have left on the tide?

‚ÄúWell √Ę¬Ä¬ôtis Jane, look at me for you seem not to know me.‚ÄĚ

When out of his pocket a small bill he drew,

Saying, ‚ÄúHere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs fifty pounds as a token I give you,

Since you in my absence have proved loyal and true.

And since that kind fortune has brought us together

‚ÄôTis tomorrow darling I will make you my bride.

And you and I true love will never be parted;

I will wed my Jane on the Banks of the Clyde.‚ÄĚ

to Sam Steele in ca. 1959. This recording was included in 2005 on the
items.lyrics:
On the banks of the Clyde
stood a lad and lassie,

The lad‚Äôs name was Georgie,
the lass‚Äôs was Belle.

She flung her arms round him
and cried ‚ÄúDo not leave me,‚ÄĚ

For Georgie was going
to fight for his queen.

Chorus (after each verse):

So we‚Äôll beat the big drum
and we‚Äôll play the pipe merrily.

We‚Äôll play the dead march
as we carry him along.

We‚Äôll take him to the churchyard
and fire three volleys o‚Äôer him,

He‚Äôs a bonny young soldier
cut down in his prime.

But some years later,
when I met young Georgie,

Dark was the night
and cold was the day.

He asked for a flannel
to bind his poor head with,

He was wropped in a blanket
and colder than clay.

His aged mother
his grey-haired old father

Oft times had told him
about his past life;

Never go courting
the flash girls of the city,

In the flash girls of the city
he took his delight.

Now on his tombstone
you will find it was written:

‚ÄúAll your jolly fellows
take warning from me,

And never go courting
the flash girls of the city,

For the flash girls of the city
were the ruin of me.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: George Belton sings The Lad in the Scots Brigade
versions.lyrics:
On the banks of the Clyde stood a lad and a lassie,

The lad‚Äôs name was Geordie, the lass‚Äôs was Jean.

She threw her arms round him and cried ‚ÄúDo not leave me‚ÄĚ,

For Geordie was going to fight for his Queen.

She gave him a lock of her bright auburn tresses,

He kissed her and pressed her once more to his heart,

‚ÄėTil his eyes spoke the words that his lips would not utter.

Together they weep, and they kiss, and they part.

Chorus (eafter each other verse):

Over the burning plains of Egypt,

Under the scorching sun,

He thought of the stories he‚Äôd have to tell

His love when the fight was won.

He treasured with care that dear lock of hair,

For his own darling Jeannie he prayed,

But his prayer was in vain

For she‚Äôll ne‚Äôer see again

Her lad in the Scots brigade

Though an ocean divided the lad from the lassie

And Geordie was sent far away on the foam;

His roof was the sky, and his bed was the desert

His heart with his Jeannie was always at home.

On the morning that dawned on that vain day of battle

Found Geordie enacting a true hero‚Äôs part

√Ę¬Ä¬ėTil an enemy√Ę¬Ä¬ôs bullet brought with it his ‚Ä¶

And buried that that dear lock of hair in his heart.

On the banks of the Clyde stood a heart-broken mother,

They told her of how the great victory was won.

But the glory to England to her was no comfort,

The glory to her meant the loss of her son.

But Jeannie is with her to comfort and cheer her

Together they weep and together they pray

And Jeannie her daughter will be while she‚Äôs living

For the sake of the laddie that died far away.

versions.title: Viv Legg sings The Banks of the Clyde
versions.lyrics:
On the banks of the Clyde stood a lad and a lassie -

The lad‚Äôs name was Georgie, the lassie‚Äôs was Jean.

She threw her arms round him and cried, ‚ÄúDear don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt leave me!‚ÄĚ

For Georgie was going to fight for his Queen.

She gave him a lock of her bright golden tresses,

And he kissed her and pressed her once more to his heart.

Their eyes spoke of love their lips would not utter.

The last words were spoken, they‚Äôd kiss and they‚Äôd part.

Chorus (after each verse):

Over the burning plains of Eygpt,

Under the scorching sun,

He thought of the stories he‚Äôd have to tell,

His love when the fight was won.

He cherished with care that dear lock of hair,

And his own darling Jeannie she prayed.

But the prayers were in vain,

She‚Äôd never see again,

Her lad from the Scots brigade.

The ocean divided the lad from the lassie,

As Georgie was forced over the foam.

His roof was the sky, his bed was the desert,

But his heart with his Jeannie was always at home.

That morning at dawn on that famed day of battle,

Found Georgie partaking a true hero√Ę¬Ä¬ôs part‚ÄĒ

When an enemy bullet flew straight to its billet,

And buried the dear lock of hair in his heart.

On the banks of the Clyde stands a heartbroken mother,

When they told her how brave that great victory was won.

That victory to her brings her no comfort,

That victory to her means the loss of her son.

items.title: Banks of the Ohio
items.notes:
Banks of the Ohio

[

; Laws F5

Banks of the Ohio is a grizly ballad of a cruel murder by

a jealous lover, similar to

The Prentice Boy

and

Cruel Knife .
items.lyrics:
I asked my love to take a walk,

To take a walk, just a little walk

Down beside where the waters flow

Down by the banks of the Ohio.

Chorus (after each verse):

And only say that you‚Äôll be mine

And in no other‚Äôs arms entwine,

Down beside where the waters flow,

Down by the banks of the Ohio

I held a knife against her breast

As close into my arms she pressed,

She cried, ‚ÄúOh Willie, don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt you murder me!

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm not prepared for eternity!‚ÄĚ

I took her by the lily white hand

And led her down by the water‚Äôs strand,

I picked her up and pitched her in

And watched her body floating by.

I wandered home ‚Äôtwixt twelve and one,

I cried, ‚Äú My God, what have I done?

I‚Äôve killed the only woman I loved,

Because she would not be my bride.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Bantry Girls‚Äô Lament
items.notes:
Bantry Girls‚Äô Lament

[

; Ballad Index OLoc077

; DT BANTRYGL

, BANTRYG2

; Mudcat 83746

; trad.]

Colm O Lochlainn:

Irish Street Ballads

Colm O Lochlainn printed The Bantry Girls‚Äô Lament

in his 1939 book

Irish Street Ballads .
items.lyrics:
Who will plough the fields now who will sow the corn?

Who will mind the sheep now and keep them neatly shorn?

And the stack that‚Äôs in the haggard, un-threshed it will remain

Since Johnny went a threshing all in the wars of Spain.

And the girls from the B√°n√≥g in sorrow may retire,

And the piper and his bellows go home and blow his fire.

For Johnny, lovely Johnny, is sailing o‚Äôer the main,

Away with other patriots to fight the king of Spain.

The boys they‚Äôll surely miss him when moneymore comes around

And they grieve their brave captain is nowhere to be found.

The peelers must stand idle, against their will and grain

Since the gallant lad that gave them work now peels the king of Spain.

At wakes and hurling matches your likes we‚Äôll seldom see

‚ÄôTil you return back home again a st√≥r√≠n gheal mo chro√≠.

Won‚Äôt you trouble those buckeens who show us great disdain

Because our eyes are not as bright as those you‚Äôll see in Spain.

If cruel faith does not permit our Johnny to return

This heavy loss we Bantry girls will never cease to mourn.

We‚Äôll resign ourselves to our sad loss and die in grief and pain

Since Johnny died for freedom‚Äôs sake, in the foreign land of Spain.

Who will plough the fields now, who will sow the corn?

Who will mind the sheep now and keep them neatly shorn?

And the stack that‚Äôs in the haggard, un-threshed it will remain

Since Johnny went a threshing, all in the wars of Spain.

Johnny died for freedom‚Äôs sake, in the foreign land of Spain.

items.title: Battle of Waterloo
items.notes:
Battle of Waterloo

[Jim Malcolm]

Jim Malcolm sang his own song

Battle of Waterloo

in 1998 on his Greentrax album

Rohallion .

He set it to music adapted from a traditional pipe tune.

This track was also included in the same year on the Greentrax anthology

of traditional music from a new generation,

Folkal Point: Edinburgh .

A live recording from Glenfarg Village Hall in November 2003

was released in 2004 on his album
items.lyrics:
Spring comes to Kirrie, all the world‚Äôs in bloom,

Winter is forgiven now, fooled by April‚Äôs broom.

Kirrie, oh Kirrie, you were aye my hame

Till Napoleon‚Äôs bloody cannon hit their aim.

Jeanie, oh Jeanie, I am surely done,

Stricken down in battle at the mooth o‚Äô Boney‚Äôs guns.

Jeanie, oh Jeanie, aye sae dear tae me,

Let me hold you in my mind afore I dee.

Chorus (after each verse):

For the cold returns in autumn when the wind rakes the trees,

And the summer lies forgotten in a cold bed of leaves.

As winter begins aye mind Boney, it wasn‚Äôt only you

Who was broken on the field of Waterloo.

Surgeon, oh surgeon, leave me wi‚Äô my pain,

Save your knife for others who will surely rise again.

Surgeon, oh surgeon, leave my blood to pour,

Let it drain into the bitter clay once more.

Daughter, oh daughter, listen dear tae me,

Never wed a sodger, or a widow you will be.

Daughter, oh daughter, curse your lad to die

‚ÄôEre he catches the recruitin‚Äô sergeant‚Äôs eye.

Boney, oh Boney, war was aye your game,

Bloody field your table, cannon yours to aim.

Boney, oh Boney, we aye lived the same,

Drillin‚Äô laddies not to fear the muskets‚Äô flame.

items.title: Beautiful Nancy
items.notes:
Beautiful Nancy

[

; Master title: Beautiful Nancy

Maud Karpeles:
items.lyrics:
As beautiful Nancy was a-walking one day,

She met a young sailor upon the highway.

Said he, ‚ÄúPretty fair maid, where are you a going,

Where are you a going, tell me pretty maid?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI am searching for Jemmy, young Jemmy my dear,

I have not a-seen him for more than nine year,

But while he is absent, a maid I will live and die.‚ÄĚ

But little did she think it was Jemmy so nigh.

Young Jemmy he stood watching for as long as he could forbear

And straightway he made himself be known to his dear.

She denied lords and squires for so many a long year,

For her long absent Jemmy, her fond sailor dear.

‚ÄúI was captured by pirates, I could not get away.

My thoughts have been with you love, by night and by day,

And now I‚Äôve gained my freedom, and I‚Äôm safe at home with thee,

No more will I venture all on the salt sea.‚ÄĚ

He flew into her arms and around her neck did twine,

Saying, ‚ÄúBeautiful Nancy forever I will be thine.

Come let us be united and live happy all on the shore;

The bells they shall ring merrily and I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll go to sea no more.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Beautiful Star of Bethlehem
items.notes:
Beautiful Star of Bethlehem

Oh beautiful star of Bethlehem

Down by the side of Bedlam City

Once I heard a maid complain,

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôve lost my joy and my only swain.

Billy‚Äôs the lad that I do admire,

Billy‚Äôs the lad that I do adore,

versions.title: Folly Bridge sing Bedlam City

Down by the side of Bedlam City

There I heard a maid complain,

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôve lost my love, my only swain.‚ÄĚ

Chorus (after each verse):

Billy is the lad that I do admire,

Billy is the lad that I do adore.

versions.title: Arthur Knevett sings Bedlam City
versions.lyrics:
Down by the side of Bedlam City

There I heard a maid complain,

Making her moan and sad lamentation,

For her love, her only swain.

Chorus (after each verse):

Billy is the lad that I do admire,

Billy is the lad I do adore.

For his sake she lies a-crying,

If I‚Äôd wings I‚Äôd fly unto him,

Don‚Äôt you hear the cannons rattle,

items.title: Before the Daylight in the Morning
items.notes:
Before the Daylight in the Morning

[

; Ballad Index RcBTDITM

; Mudcat 40720

; trad.]

Sam Larner sang

Before Daylight in the Morning

in a recording made by Ewan MacColl, Peggy Seeger and Charles Parker in

1958-60. It was included in 2014 on his Musical Traditions anthology

Cruising Round Yarmouth .

Rod Stradling noted:

Sam Larner: He used to sing that ‚Ä¶ old man,
items.lyrics:
‚Ä¶ I wish I got killed on the banks of the Nile

Just before the daylight in the morning.

Now, when I come home from work,
it make me so wild,

There‚Äôs a mound of blue stockings
or a nasty young child,

I wish I got killed on the banks of the Nile

Just before the daylight in the morning.

Now, she‚Äôd a beard on her lip,
like a wandering Jew,

Not a tooth in her head
that was round, all but two,

A smock she had on
was neither black, white nor blue,

For it had never been wet with the washing.

Now, to kindle a fire,
it is my first job.

And (if I fail) to do it
i get a smack on the gob.

A hit, or a kick, or a smack on the gob,

I surely would get from my darling.

versions.title: Sara Cleveland sings Before the Daylight in the Morning
versions.lyrics:
Full eighteen pound pension I have in a year,

Which causes my wife to drink whisky and beer.

Her tongue like a cannon doth sound in my ear

Before the daylight in the morning.

Her praises and beauty I mean to expose;

She‚Äôs dirty and filthy with her old snuffy nose.

She‚Äôs a shame to all women wherever she goes,

With her clothes all in tatters a-hanging.

Not a shoe or a stocking I have to my feet;

My bed is without wither blanket or sheet.

I‚Äôm ashamed of myself when I walk on the street.

Pray, what do you think of my darling?

My shirt without washing it sticks to my back,

While Nell is out sporting with Paddy or Jack,

Or running in score for every knick-knack

While I must pay out my last farthing.

Not a tooth in her head with which she can chew;

God pity the poor man who‚Äôs married a shrew.

Not a shift to her back, wither white, black, or blue,

That ever was hit with the water.

Her hair without combing is matted and rough;

Her skin is like leather, all crusted and tough.

And I‚Äôm getting tired; I‚Äôve sure had enough.

O why did I wed such a darling?

And then when her cronies they come in for tay,

While I in the corner have nothing to say.

Or out in the garden a-digging away,

While Nell in her cups is a-storming.

When in for the leavings I happen to pop,

While Nell and her gossips are gone to the shop,

Back-biting their neighbours and swallowing their drops,

And I must pay out my last farthing.

To finish my ditty, I fervently pray,

Before she can ever drink whisky or tay,

That God or the devil will whip her away

Before the daylight in the morning.

items.title: Bendigo, Champion of England
items.notes:
Bendigo, Champion of England

[

; Bodleian

; Mudcat 172923

; trad.]

Dave and Toni Arthur sang

in 1967 on their Transatlantic album

Morning Stands on Tiptoe .

They noted:

Bendigo, real name William Thompson, was born in 1812. This song tells of

his third fight with Ben Caunt, also a Nottingham man. The fight was witnessed
items.lyrics:
Ye ranting lads, and sporting blades, come listen to my song,

I‚Äôm sure that it will please you well, and will not keep you long.

Concerning the great milling match that lately has been fought,

Between great Caunt and Bendigo, two lads of the right sort.

Chorus (after each verse):

So we‚Äôll drink success to Bendigo, who showed such gallant play,

For by his skill, he won the mill, and bore the prize away.

On the ninth day of September, eighteen hundred, forty five,

To Witchwood for to see the fight, the sporting coves did drive,

While some did laugh, and some did chaff, and of their man did vaunt,

Some bet their ten on Bendigo, and some on giant Caunt.

And when the ground was ready, both those champions quickly peeled,

Two braver men on England‚Äôs ground did never take the field,

The fancy swore they were top mark,‚ÄĒan honour to the ring,

Two stouter hearts had never met, since Langan and Tom Spring.

Both men shook hands, and the prize belt, it straightway was brought in,

‚ÄúThere let it hang,‚ÄĚ says Bendigo, ‚Äútill the best man does win.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThat won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt be little Bendigo,‚ÄĚ then Caunt he did reply,

‚ÄúFor I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll belt your hide till you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre satisfied, then at him he did fly.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIs that the way?‚ÄĚ says Bendigo, ‚ÄúHere, take it back again.‚ÄĚ

He made a job of poor Caunt‚Äôs nob, and hammered it amain.

This furious work soon drew the cork of Caunt‚Äôs poor claret bottle,

While Caunt returned the compliment, made Bendi‚Äôs ribs to rattle.

Twenty four rounds these heroes fought, none could tell which was the best,

But Bendigo in the next round, struck Caunt on the left breast.

Which made him stagger round the ring, and fall upon the ground,

Says Bendigo, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll have the belt, and the four hundred pound.‚ÄĚ

But Caunt did boldly come again, and showed some gallant play,

Yet Bendigo would strike a blow, and quickly get away.

Until in round the eighty fourth, he gave some ugly blows,

Which left his mark on the staring part, and fairly spoilt Caunt‚Äôs nose.

Eighty eight rounds were fought, when Caunt he could not rise,

And all declared the Bendy cock had fairly won the prize.

The Tipton Slasher now may come, but soon he‚Äôll get to know,

That he was not quite big enough to wollop Bendigo.

items.title: Big Rock Candy Mountain
items.notes:
Big Rock Candy Mountain

[

; Ballad Index LxU079

; Mudcat 4571

; Harry McClintock]

According to Wikipedia,

Big Rock Candy Mountain , first recorded by Harry McClintock in

1928, is a song about a hobo‚Äôs idea of paradise, a modern version of the

medieval concept of Cockaigne. It is a place where ‚Äúhens lay soft boiled

eggs‚ÄĚ and there are ‚Äúcigarette trees.‚ÄĚ McClintock claims to

have written the song in 1895 based on tales from his misspent youth hoboing

through the United States, but some believe the song, or at least aspects of
items.lyrics:
On a summer‚Äôs day in the month of May

A burly bum came hiking.

Down a shady lane with a sugar cane

He was lookin‚Äô for his liking

As he strolled along he sang a song

Of a land of milk and honey

Where a bum can stay for many a day

And he don‚Äôt need any money.

Chorus (after each verse):

Oh, the buzzing of the bees in the cigarette trees,

The soda water fountains

Where the lemonade springs and the bluebird sings.

In the Big Rock Candy Mountains.

In the Big Rock Candy Mountains

The cops have wooden legs,

And the bulldogs all have rubber teeth

And the hens lay soft-boiled eggs.

The farmers‚Äô trees are full of fruit,

The barns are full of hay.

Oj, I want to go where there ain‚Äôt no snow

Where the rain don‚Äôt fall and the wind don‚Äôt blow

In the Big Rock Candy Mountains.

In the Big Rock Candy Mountains

You never wash your socks

And the little streams of alcohol

Come trickling down the rocks.

There‚Äôs a lake of stew and whiskey too

And you paddle around in a big canoe,

Where they hung the turk who invented work,

In the Big Rock Candy Mountains.

items.title: Billy Reilley
items.notes:
Billy Reilley

[

; Mudcat 46593

; Bonnie Shaljean]

Harp player and singer Bonnie Shaljean wrote Billy Reilley

in 1979 and sang in with Packie Byrne on their 1981 Dingle‚Äôs album

Roundtower .

She noted:

The story of Billy Reilley takes place during the Flight of the Wild Geese,

a period in 18 th -century Ireland when poverty and hard times caused

many of the young men to leave their country and seek a new life in France.
items.lyrics:
Billy Reilley at thy rest

Your father calls you from the past

His voice is loud but you are blessed

With sleep and cannot hear him

He doesn‚Äôt talk much anymore, Billy Reilley

Long time ago you courted me

In the summer days when the world ran free

And love hung ripe on the rowan tree

You played your father‚Äôs pipes

And all my joys abounded, Billy Reilley

But time and other dreams took hold

The land grew ill and the sky turned cold

And all the things that make young men old

Must send the wild geese flying

You left me with your child, Billy Reilley

Your father raged and made me swear

I‚Äôd never tell you but I couldn‚Äôt bear

Unspoken names and whispering stares

And worst of all the nights

O curse the hour that I wrote you, Billy Reilley

You sailed for home with the next tide in

But the ship was lost and all men within

And now all Ireland‚Äôs grace can‚Äôt win you

From the sullen ocean

My heart lies dead at my feet, Billy Reilley

Your father curses the rolling sea

The stormy winds, fair France, and me

And I curse love and my own baby

And life is not the short thing

That they would have you believe, Billy Reilley

The pipes will sing you fast asleep

Where‚Äôer you lie in the ocean‚Äôs keep

Forgive me if I no longer weep

As year by year I stay here

Imprisoned by my sins, Billy Reilley

You‚Äôd never recognise me, Billy Reilley

versions.title: Martin Howley sings Blow the Candle Out
versions.lyrics:
And I came to my true love‚Äôs window to hear her mournful pain;

She arose up gently from her nap to let her true lover in.

‚ÄúI wouldn√Ę¬Ä¬ôt wish it for five guineas, love, if they would find it out,

So hold me in your arms √Ę¬Ä¬ôtil I blow the candle out.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúAnd your father and your mammy in yonder bed do lie,

Embracing one another, and why not you and I;

Embracing one another love, and that without a doubt‚ÄĚ,

And I took her in my arms and I blew the candle out.

Sure, the eighth month was over, all but one day,

My love, he wrote me a letter saying he was going away;

My love he wrote me a letter, and that without a doubt,

That he never would return again for to blow the candle out.

And come all ye pretty fair maids, a warning take by me,

Never let those false young men an inch above your knee;

For they‚Äôll kiss you and they‚Äôll court you until your time be out,

They shall leave you where my love left me When he blew the candle out.

versions.title: James McDermott sings Blow the Candle Out
versions.lyrics:
Around a young apprentice who went to meet his dear,

The moon was shining brightly and the stars were viewing clear.

She came to his love‚Äôs window and she called him by her name

She soon arose and let him inand went back to bed again.

Saying, ‚ÄúWillie, dearest Willie, tonight will be your doom.

Strip off unto your nightshirt and bear one night within,

The streets they are too lonely for you to walk about

So come roll me in your arms, love,and we‚Äôll blow the candle out.

‚ÄúMy father and my mother, next bedroom they do lie

Kissing and embracing, and why not you and I?

Kissing and embracing without a word of doubt

So come roll me in your arms, love,and we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll blow the candle out.‚ÄĚ

Was early next morning young Willie he arose

Whistling and singing and hurrying on his clothes

He never said a word within nor yet a word without

And he never said when he‚Äôd come back for to blow the candle out.

Come all my sporting young men a warning take by me

Never court a young man that ploughs the angry sea

For some day or another when walking out about

He will do to you what he done to me when he blew the candle out.

versions.title: The Furrow Collective sing Apprenticed in London
versions.lyrics:
When I was apprenticed in London I used to see my dear,

Stars were always shining, the moon shone bright and clear.

I rapped upon the window to ease her of her pain,

She rose to let me in, and she barred the door again.

‚ÄúOh my father and my mother in yonder room do lie,

A-hugging one another, so why not you and I?

A-hugging one another without a fear or doubt

So roll me in your arms, love, and blow the candles out.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNow if your prove successful, love, just name it after me,

Kiss it sweet and dress it neat and daft it on your knee.

And when my days are over and I cannot go about,

Why roll me in your arms, love, and blow the candles out,

Roll me in your arms, love, and blow the candles out.‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Craig Morgan Robson sing Blow the Wind Southerly

Blow the wind southerly, southerly, southerly,

Blow the wind southerly, southerly, southerly,

I stood by the lighthouse the last time we parted,

And no longer I saw the bright bark of my lover,

Blow the wind southerly, southerly, southerly,

Blow bonny breeze o‚Äôer the bonny blue sea;

Blow the wind southerly, southerly, southerly,

Blow bonny breeze and bring him to me.

Is it not sweet to hear the breeze singing,

As lightly it comes o‚Äôer the deep rolling sea?

But sweeter and dearer by far when ‚Äôtis bringing

(repeat first verse)

items.title: Blow ye Winds / Effects of Love
items.notes:
[

; VWML RoudFS/S156127

, SBG/1/8/18

; trad.]

Magpie Lane sang

Blow ye Winds

in 2017 on their CD

Three Quarter Time .

They noted:

Jon [Fletcher] came across Blow ye Winds in Sabine Baring-Gould‚Äôs

A Garland of Country Song (1895)

[VWML RoudFS/S156127 ]
items.lyrics:
O love is hot, and love is cold,

It is more precious that any gold,

O, it is more precious that any thing

But to my grave it will me being.

Chorus (after each verse):

Then blow, ye winds of winter, blow,

And cover me with frost and snow.

And shake the branches of the tree

And blow the dead leaves all over me.

Now the wind blows east, and the wind blows west,

He never seizes, he takes no rest.

Now he‚Äôs like a gale and then he‚Äôs like a fan,

Such is the temper of a man.

Now the leaves they grow and the flowers appear,

All in the springtime of the year.

The summer gone, they fall and fade,

Such is the beauty of a maid.

O this little circle of a life,

Is but the turning of a knife,

Where tears forever fill the eye

And every breath ‚Äôtis but a sigh.

items.title: Blow ye Winds in the Morning
items.notes:
Blow ye Winds in the Morning

[

; Ballad Index LxU044

; trad.]

Michael Cooney sang Blow, Ye Winds

in 1973 on the National Geographic Society‚Äôs album

Songs & Sounds of the Sea .

The album‚Äôs booklet commented:

‚ÄôTis advertised in Boston, New York, and Buffalo,

A hundred hearty sailors, w-whaling for to go‚Ä¶

Michael Cooney, accompanied by his sparkling five-string banjo, sings one

of the best of the whaling songs. Blow, Ye Winds comes from an old
items.lyrics:
‚ÄôTis advertised in Boston, New York and Buffalo,

Five hundred brave Americans, a-whaling for to go, singing

Chorus (after each verse):

Blow, ye winds in the morning, and blow, ye winds, high-o!

Clear away your running gear, and blow, ye winds, high-o!

They send you to New Bedord, that famous whaling port,

And give you to some land-sharks to board and fit you out.

They send you to a boarding-house, there for a time to dwell;

The thieves they there are thicker than the other side of hell!

They tell you of the clipper-ships a-going in and out,

And say you‚Äôll take five hundred sperm before you‚Äôre six months out.

It‚Äôs now we‚Äôre out to sea, my boys, the wind comes on to blow;

One half the watch is sick on deck, the other half below.

But as for the provisions, we don‚Äôt get half enough;

A little piece of stinking beef and a blamed small bag of duff.

Now comes that damned old compass, it will grieve your heart full sore.

For theirs is two-and-thirty points and we have forty-four.

Next comes the running rigging, which you‚Äôre all supposed to know;

√Ę¬Ä¬ôTis ‚ÄúLay aloft, you son-of-a-gun, or overboard you go!‚ÄĚ

The cooper‚Äôs at the vise-bench, a-making iron poles,

And the mate‚Äôs upon the main hatch a-cursing all our souls.

The Skipper‚Äôs on the quarter-deck a-squinting at the sails,

When up aloft the lookout sights a school of whales.

‚ÄúNow clear away the boats, my boys, and after him we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll travel,

But if you get too near his fluke, he√Ę¬Ä¬ôll kick you to the devil!‚ÄĚ

Now we have got him turned up, we tow him alongside;

We over with our blubber-hooks and rob him of his hide.

Now the boat-steerer overside the tackle overhauls,

The Skipper‚Äôs in the main-chains, so loudly he does bawl!

Next comes the stowing down, my boys; ‚Äôtwill take both night and day,

And you‚Äôll all have fifty cents apiece on the hundred and ninetieth lay.

Now we are bound into Tonbas, that blasted whaling port,

And if you run away, my boys, you surely will get caught.

Now we are bound into Tuckoona, full more in their power,

Where the skippers can buy the Consul up for half a barrel of flour!

But now that our old ship is full and we don‚Äôt give a damn,

We‚Äôll bend on all our stu‚Äônsails and sail for Yankee land.

When we get home, our ship made fast, and we get through our sailing,

A winding glass around we‚Äôll pass and damn this blubber whaling!

items.title: Blue Bleezin‚Äô Blind Drunk / Mickey‚Äôs Warning

items.lyrics:
Oh friends I have a sad story,

A very sad story to tell:

I married a man for his money

But he‚Äôs worse than the devil himsel‚Äô.

For when Mickey comes home I get battered,

He batters me all black and blue;

And if I say a word I get scattered

From the kitchen right ben tae the room.

Chorus (after each verse):

So I‚Äôll go and I‚Äôll get blue bleezin‚Äô blind drunk,

Just to give Mickey a warning.

And, just for to spite, I will stay out all night

And come rolling home drunk in the morning.

Oh, but whisky I ne‚Äôer was a lover

But what can a poor woman do?

I‚Äôll go and I‚Äôll drown all my sorrows;

How I wish I could drown Mickey too.

Now friends I have a sad story,

items.lyrics:

Chorus (after each verse):

Bogie‚Äôs banks far lamies play

items.title: Bold Lovell
items.notes:
Bold Lovell

[

; Laws L13B

; Ballad Index LL13

; trad.]

Roy Harris sang

in 1976 on his Topic album

A.L. Lloyd noted:

The theme of this song reminds us of the capture of Macheath in the

Beggar‚Äôs Opera. Was it suggested by it? Or is the ballad old enough to have
items.lyrics:
As Lovell was out riding out across the misty mountains,

Two merchants, two merchants, their money they was counting;

He reached for his pistol, and he never gave them warning,

He robbed them of their money and he bade them both good morning:

Chorus (after each verse):

Oh, the devil‚Äôs in the women so they say,

But how the devil can a fellow let them be?

He went to a public house and counted out his money;

He called on the landlady to bring forth pretty Polly.

But while they was talking, and thinking of no matter,

She stole away his pistol and she filled it up with water:

As Lovell and Polly were taking their sweet pleasure,

In walked the troopers, saying, ‚ÄúLovell, you must leave her!

For a long time you‚Äôve been riding on the road to the gallows,

So some along with us young man and be a decent fellow.‚ÄĚ

He reached for his pistols but they wouldn‚Äôt fire for water;

They lathered him well and gave to him no quarter.

Polly, she cried, ‚ÄúIf I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd known that they was coming,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd have fought them like a tiger, love, although I am a woman.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI have two brothers and they√Ę¬Ä¬ôre both in the Marines;

One of them‚Äôs at Chatham and the other one‚Äôs at sea.

Bold, brisk and lively lads, and champions of folly;

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôd rather they was here today than you deceitful Polly.‚ÄĚ

As Lovell was climbing up that old gallows ladder,

He called out so gaily for his highway cap and feather:

‚ÄúWell, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôve always been a lively lad, but never murdered any;

I think it bloody hard to swing for liftin√Ę¬Ä¬ô a bit of money!‚ÄĚ

items.title: Bold Nelson‚Äôs Praise
items.notes:
Bold Nelson‚Äôs Praise

[

; Ballad Index ShH88

; VWML CJS2/9/2153

, CJS2/10/2380

; Mudcat 98147

; trad.]

Maud Karpeles:

Cecil Sharp‚Äôs Collection of English Folk Songs

The Crystal Spring

Cecil J. Sharp:

One Hundred English Folk Songs .
items.lyrics:
Bold Nelson‚Äôs praise I am going to sing,

(Not forgetting our glorious King)

He always did good tidings bring,

For he was a bold commander.

There was Sydney Smith and Duncan too,

Lord Howe and all the glorious crew;

They were the men that were true blue.

Full of care, yet I swear

None with Nelson could compare,

Not even Alexander.

Bold Buonaparte he threaten‚Äôd war,

A man who fear‚Äôd not wound nor scar,

But still he lost at Trafalgar

Where Britain was victorious.

Lord Nelson‚Äôs actions made him quake,

And all French pow‚Äôrs he made to shake;

He said his king he‚Äôd ne‚Äôer forsake.

These last words thus he spake,

Stand true, my lads, like hearts of oak,

And the battle shall be glorious.

Lord Nelson bold, though threaten‚Äôd wide,

And many a time he had been tried,

He fought like a hero till he died

Amid the battle gory.

But the day was won, their line was broke,

While all around was lost in smoke,

And Nelson he got his death-stroke,

That‚Äôs the man for old England!

He faced his foe with his sword in hand

And he lived and he died in his glory.

items.notes:
Bold Robinson / Two Champions

Frank Purslow sang Bold Robinson , a song about a fictuous

items.lyrics:
Come all you young fellows that delights in a game,

Come listen to these lines, which lately have been penned.

It‚Äôs of two champions bold, fought for a sum of gold,

In fair London town lived Young Taylor, Huzza,

In fair London town lived Young Taylor, Huzza.

The stage being built, the day being come

Each party true hearted and the battle went on.

For fifty score of guineas these two champions they did play.

And the cry was, ‚ÄúRobinson that he will win the day

And Young Taylor, Huzza: Young Taylor, Huzza.

Bold Robinson forever, and Young Taylor, Huzza.‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke young Taylor, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm just in my bloom,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm willing to fight thee from morning till noon.‚ÄĚ

Young Taylor on a sudden gave Robinson a blow

Saying, ‚ÄúI will be the champion where ever I do go.‚ÄĚ

And Young Taylor, Huzza: Young Taylor, Huzza.

Bold Robinson forever, and Young Taylor, Huzza.

At the end of six rounds these two champions they did meet,

Being sadly wounded and their hearts being full of grief,

Bold Robinson he fell and expired with a groan,

The moment he died, Oh the battle it was won

And Young Taylor, Huzza: Young Taylor, Huzza.

Bold Robinson forever, and Young Taylor, Huzza.

So now to conclude, Young Taylor he has won.

A rich lady fell in love with him for what he had done.

And if he had recovered a grand wedding shall be seen.

But young Taylor he died at the end of day three.

The lady went in mourning for Taylor Huzza,

Bold Robinson forever, and Young Taylor, Huzza.

versions.title:
Two Champions in
 Down Yorkshire Lanes
versions.lyrics:
Come all you young fellows that delight in a little game,

Come listen unto me and I will tell you plain.

It‚Äôs of two champions bold who fought for a sum of gold

And their talk was that young Robinson was sure to win the day.

Chorus (after each verse):

Young Taylor, Huzzoo! Brave Taylor, Huzzoo!

Brave Robinson forever and young Taylor, Huzzoo!

Their stage it had been builded and the champions on the ground

And fifty bright guineas these champions did lay down.

For fifty bright guineas these champions they did play

And all the talk was Robinson was sure to win the day.

‚ÄôTwas in the fourteenth round these two champions they did meet,

With their bodies sorely wounded and their breasts full of grief,

When all of a sudden Taylor gave Robinson a blow,

Say, ‚ÄúI will me the champion where ever I do go.‚ÄĚ

The battle being over and young Taylor he had won,

A rich lady fell in love with him for what he had done.

And if he do recover a gay wedding shall be seen

But young Taylor he died at the end of the game.

versions.title: The Dovetail Trio sing Bold Champions
versions.lyrics:
Come, all you young men that delight in any game,

Come and listen to these few lines to you I will explain;

It‚Äôs of two champions bold fought for a sum of gold,

And it was near London Town as I have been told.

Chorus (after each verse):

‚ÄôTwas young Taylor, hurrah! ‚ÄôTwas young Taylor, hurrah!

Bold Robinson for ever, and young Taylor, hurrah!

Now the drums and the trumpets most sweetly did sound,

And the horsemen they were mounted for to guard the ground;

The fight it was most noble, and young Taylor he did say,

‚ÄúIt never shall be said that to thee I give the day.‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke bold Robinson, ‚ÄúThe game it is my own,

And I can find his heart does tremble at my every blow;

Come, all you understanding men, I‚Äôll have you for to know,

That I will be the champion, wherever I do go.‚ÄĚ

Then up spoke young Taylor, ‚ÄúI am just in my bloom,

I am willing for to fight thee from morning until noon.‚ÄĚ

Young Taylor he then sprang, and gave bold Robinson a blow,

Saying, ‚ÄúI will be the champion wherever I do go.‚ÄĚ

At the end of six rounds these champions did meet,

With their bodies sorely wounded, and their hearts so full of grief;

Bold Robinson he fell, without a sign or moan,

And from that moment when he died the battle it was won.

And now to conclude, when this young Taylor had won,

A rich lady fell in love with him for what he had done;

And if ever he recovers a grand wedding there shall be,

But young Taylor then he died at the end of days three.

But they taen him an they stole him an they‚Äôve left me tae rue

In the lover‚Äôs lanes o Udny, bonnie Udny, go through.

We‚Äôll drink an be merry, we‚Äôll drink an gyang hame

For tae bide here ony langer, we will get a bad name

An tae get a bad name, love, for that will never dee

O aa the lands o Scotland, bonnie Udny for me.

versions.title: Iona Fyfe sings Bonny Udny
versions.lyrics:
O Udny, bonny Udny, ye shine whaur ye stand

The mair I look on you, the mair my heart warms.

If I were in Udny, I‚Äôd think I‚Äôs at hame

For there I get sweethairts, for here I get nane.

It‚Äôs nae the lang road, love, that I hae tae gang,

Nor is it the lang miles that maks me think lang;

But the one thing that grieves me and maks my hairt sad,

It‚Äôs the leavin‚Äô o‚Äô you, Udny, and yon bonnie lass.

Well, the lads aboot Udny, they‚Äôre aa rovin‚Äô blades,

They tak great delight in the coortin‚Äô o maids;

They kiss them and clap them and spend money free,

Of a‚Äô the airts in Scotland, bonnie Udny‚Äôs for me.

I once loved a fair maid, she said she loved me,

An‚Äô oor parents were willing that wedded we‚Äôd be.

But for all of her promises she has forgot me,

And since she‚Äôs got another, let her go where will she.

‚ÄôTwas on a certain Sunday, that me and my love met,

Which caused me on Monday tae sigh, sore and weep;

But ti spoil my eyes weeping, such a fool I would be,

And since she‚Äôs got another, let her go where will she.

I will build my love a castle on yon piece of ground,

Where lord, duke or nobleman can ne‚Äôer pull it down;

And if anyone should ask of you, ‚ÄúO what is your name?‚ÄĚ

You can say it is Mary and from Udny ye came.

You pull the red rose, and I‚Äôll pull the thyme.

You drink tae your love and I‚Äôll drink tae mine.

For my mind will not alter or gang to and fro,

But I‚Äôll aya prove kind-hairtit tae the girl that I loe.

Let us drink and be merry, let us drink and gang hame,

For if we stay langer, we‚Äôll get a bad name;

We‚Äôll get a bad name and we‚Äôll drink ourselves fu,

And the long walks o‚Äô Udny are, aye, tae gang through.

There was a knight on a simmer‚Äôs nicht,

and where was ye cleek‚Äôd,

O bonny Portmore you shine where you stand

And the more I think on you the more I think long.

If I had you now as I had once before

All the lords in old England would not purchase Portmore.

O bonny Portmore I am sorry to see

Such woeful destruction of your ornament tree.

For it stood on your banks for many‚Äôs the long day

‚ÄôTil the longboats from Antrim came to float it away.

All the birds in the forest they bitterly weep,

Saying, where will we shelter or where will we sleep?

For the oak and the ash they are all cutten down

And the walls of bonny Portmore are all down to the ground.

(repeat first verse)

items.lyrics:
Come and gather all around me, and listen to my tale of woe.

I‚Äôve got some good advice to give you, lots of things you oughta know.

Take a tip from one who‚Äôs travelled, I never stopped rambling round

Cos once you get the roaming fever, you never want to settle down, you never want to settle down.

Well, I met a little gal up in ‚ÄôFrisco, and I asked her to be my wife

I told her I was tired of roaming, I‚Äôm gonna settle down for life.

Then I heard a whistle blowing and I knew it was a red ball train

And I left that gal beside the railroad, I never saw that gal again, I never saw that gal again.

Well, I‚Äôve travelled all over the country, I‚Äôve travelled everywhere.

I‚Äôve been on every branch line railroad, I never paid a nickel fare.

Been from Maine to California, and Canada to Mexico.

And I never tried to save no money, and now I‚Äôve got no place to go, and now I‚Äôve got no place to go.

So come on listen to my boomer‚Äôs story, pay attention to what I say.

Hear another train a-coming, I guess I‚Äôll be on my way.

If you want to do me a favour, and when I lay me down and die,

Come on and dig my grave beside the railroad so I can hear the trains go by, so I can hear the trains go by.

Come on and dig my grave beside the railroad so I can hear the trains go by, so I can hear the trains go by.

items.title: Boozing
items.notes:
Boozing

[

; DT BOOZNG

; Mudcat 52837

, 65936

; trad.]

Mike Harding sang Boozing, Bloody Well Boozing

in 1977 on his Rubber Records album

Mike Harding‚Äôs Back! .

John Roberts and Tony Barrand sang

Boozing

Live at Holsteins ,
items.lyrics:
Now what are the joys of a single young man?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

And what is he doing whenever he can?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

You may think I‚Äôm wrong or you may think I‚Äôm right,

I‚Äôm not going to argue, I know you can fight.

But what do you think we are doing tonight?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

Chorus (after each verse):

Boozing, boozing, just you and I,

Boozing, boozing, when we are dry.

Some do it openly, some on the sly,

But we all are bloody well boozing.

And what are the joys of a poor married man?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

And what is he doing whenever he can?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

He comes home at night and he gives his wife all,

He goes out a-shopping, makes many a call.

But what brings him home hanging on to the wall?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

And what does the Salvation Army run down?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

And what are they banning in every town?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

The stand on street corners, they rave and they shout,

They shout about things they know nothing about.

But what are they doing when the lights are turned out?

Why, boozing, bloody well boozing!

versions.title: Daoir√≠ Farrell sings Boozing
versions.lyrics:
What do you think I‚Äôve been doing all day?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

And how do you think I‚Äôve been spending my pay?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

Don‚Äôt say I‚Äôm not wrong for you know I‚Äôm not right,

Don‚Äôt argue the toss for you know I can‚Äôt fight.

Where would you like me to take you tonight?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

Chorus (after each verse):

Boozing, boozing, when you are dry,

Boozing, boozing, suits you and I.

Some does it open, and more on the sly,

But all of us likes to go boozing!

And what are the joys of the labouring man?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

What is he doing whenever he can?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

Comes home on a Saturday, gives his wife all,

She heads to the market, makes many a call.

But what brings her home clinging on to a wall?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

And what does the Salvation Army shoot down?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

And what are they preaching in every town?

Boozing bloody well boozing!

They preach, they don‚Äôt practice of that there‚Äôs no doubt,

And on the street comer they stand and they shout.

But what are they doing when the lights have gone out?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

What are the joys of old Jolly Jack Tar?

Boozing,bloody well boozing!

What are the joys of our bold sons of war?

Boozing, bloody well boozing!

And what makes England the land of the free?

And if another French war were to be?

Where would you find all the bold RIC?

Out boozing, bloody well boozing!

A story, a story to you I will relate:

His trial for to take, my boys, as you will understand,

items.notes:

items.lyrics:
What‚Äôs the spring-breathing jas‚Äômine and rose,

What‚Äôs the summer, with all its gay train,

Or the splendour of autumn, to those

Who‚Äôve barter‚Äôd their freedom for gain?

Chorus (after each verse):

Let the love of our land‚Äôs sacred rights,

To the love of our country succeed;

Let friendship and honour unite,

And flourish on both sides the Tweed.

No sweetness the senses can cheer,

Which corruption and bribery blind;

No brightness that gloom can e‚Äôer clear,

For honour‚Äôs the sun of the mind.

Let virtue distinguish the brave,

Place riches in lowest degree;

Think him poorest who can be a slave,

Him richest who dares to be free.

Let us think how our ancestors rose,

Let us think how our ancestors fell,

The rights they defended, and those

They bought with their blood we‚Äôll ne‚Äôer sell.

items.title: Boxing Day
items.notes:
Boxing Day

[

; Mudcat 170665

items.lyrics:
When I sit down at my table,

Clasp my hands and bow my head,

Should I thank my heavenly landlord

For my daily crust of bread?

When the hunters in his stable

And the hounds in his pack,

Get the pickings of the harvest

On which I break my back.

There‚Äôs a fence around the common land,

Put there by the law.

It‚Äôs called hunting if you‚Äôre gentry

But it‚Äôs poaching if you‚Äôre poor.

And the law forgives your trespass

Like the hounds forgive the fox,

You must number all your blessings

With the ha‚Äôpence in your box.

Chorus (after each verse):

And it feels like winter spit to eat and hell to pay,

It feels like Reynardine on Boxing Day.

It feels like winter spit to eat and hell to pay,

It feels like Reynardine on Boxing Day.

Now the forest is a shipwreck

And the field is full of stone,

It‚Äôs hard to find a blade of grass

Some bastard doesn‚Äôt own.

And they stopped the earth up for us

And they drove us into town;

Now they say there‚Äôs no work for us

And they‚Äôve closed the factory down.

They‚Äôre still meeting in the country

For the hunt and for the course,

You can join the bloody gentry

If you can afford a bleedin‚Äô horse.

And we raid along the railway

And we pray we don‚Äôt get caught,

God damn you merry gentlefolk

For your money and your sport.

When I sit down at my table,

Clasp my hands and bow my head,

Should I thank my heavenly landlord

For my daily crust of bread?

For the whip and hand that feeds us

And keeps us in our place,

One day we‚Äôll turn and wipe the smile

Clean off your bloody face.

items.lyrics:
There cam‚Äô a letter late yestreen,

That ever I was born,

To sit so late wi‚Äô my true love,

items.title: Brennan on the Moor
items.notes:
Brennan on the Moor

[

/ Song Subject MAS888

items.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of a famous highwayman, a story I maun tell,

His name was Willie Brennan, in Ireland he did dwell;

It was on the lofty mountains where he commenced his wild career,

Where many‚Äôs a noble gentleman before him shook with fear.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Brennan on the moor, Brennan on the moor

Bold and undaunted stood young Brennan on the moor.

Brennen met a packman whose name was Pedlar Brown,

They walkit on together till the day began to dawn;

Till he robbed him of his money, also his watch and chain,

But he once‚Äôt encountered Brennan when he robbed him back again.

Brennan‚Äôs wife being down to town, provisions for to buy

She saw her own dear Willie, and she began to cry.

‚ÄúCome hand to me that tenpenny, you really now forgot,‚ÄĚ

But she handed him a blunderbush from out below her cloak.

He held of the Mayor of London, till he robbed him of his gold,

And with their horse an‚Äô saddles, to the mountains they did fly;

For infantry an‚Äô cavalry to catch them they did try,

But he lay amangst the ferns that was thick upon the fields;

Nine bullet wounds he did receive before he would yield.

Here‚Äôs to my dear wife and likewise ma bairnies three

But here‚Äôs to my auld father who‚Äôs shed many‚Äôs a tear for me;

But here‚Äôs to my auld mother who tore her grey locks and cried,

‚ÄúOh, I wish young Willie Brennan, in your cradle bed had died!‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Fanny Pronger sings Brennan on the Moor
versions.lyrics:
A brace of loaded pistols he carried night and day

He never robb‚Äôd a poor man upon the King‚Äôs Highway

But what he took off from the rich, like Turpin and Black Bess

He always did divide it with the widows in distress.

He fell in with a packman, his name was Elder Bawn,

They both jogg‚Äôd on together till the day began to dawn;

The pedlar finding his money gone, likewise his watch and chain,

He at once encountered Brennan and he robb‚Äôd him back again.

Now Brennan finding the pedlar as good a man as he,

He took him on the highway, his companion for to be;

The pedlar threw away his pack, without any more delay,

And he prov‚Äôd a faithful comerade until his dying day.

One day upon the mountain high as Willie he sat down,

He saw the Mayor of Castle , a mile outside the town;

The Mayor he knew his features, ‚ÄúI think, young man,‚ÄĚ said he,

‚ÄúYour name is Willie Brennan, you must come along with me.‚ÄĚ

Now Brennan‚Äôs wife had gone to town, provisions for to buy,

And when she saw Willie she began to weep and cry;

‚ÄúWife, hand to me that tenpence,‚ÄĚ and soon as Willie spoke,

She handed him the blunderbuss from underneath her cloak.

Now with the mounted blunderbuss, the truth I will unfold:

He made the Mayor to tremble, deliver up his gold;

One hundred pounds was offer‚Äôd for his apprehension then;

On his horse and saddle to the mountains did prepare.

So they were taken prisoner and bound in iron chains,

And they were took to Clodmore Jail, strong walls did them surround

They were tried and found guilty, and the Judge made this reply,

‚ÄúFor robbing on the King√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Highway, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre both condemn√Ę¬Ä¬ôd to die.‚ÄĚ

Adieu to all my friends, my wife and children three,

Likewise my aged father who will shed tears for me,

Likewise my aged mother who‚Äôll tear her grey locks and cry,

‚ÄúO I wish that, Willie Brennan, in your cradle you had died!‚ÄĚ

versions.title: Jim ‚ÄėBrick‚Äô Harber sings Brennan on the Moor
versions.lyrics:

And many a wealthy gentleman did ‚Äôfore him shake with fear.

Chorus (repeated after each verse):

Brennan on the moor, Brennan on the moor

Bold and undaunted stood young Brennan on the moor.

Now Brennan‚Äôs wife was gone to town, provisions for to buy

And when she saw Willie she began to weep and cry.

He says, ‚ÄúHand to me that tenpence‚ÄĚ‚ÄĒno sooner had he spoke,

She handed him the blunderbuss from underneath her cloak.

Now with his loaded blunderbuss, the truth I will unfold:

He made the Mayor to tremble and he robbed him of his gold.

One hundred pounds was offered for his apprehension there,

So with his horse and saddle to the mountains did prepare.

Now Brennan being an outlaw upon the mountains high

Where infantry and cavalry, to take him, they did try.

But he laughed at them with scorn until ?? reach

Then nine wounds he did receive before that he would yield.

Now he was taken prisoner, in irons he were bound

Conveyed to Clonwood Jail, strong walls did him surround

He were tried and found guilty, the Judge made this reply,

‚ÄúFor robbing on the King√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Highway you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre both condemned to die.‚ÄĚ

Farewell unto my wife and to my children three

Likewise my ag√®d father, he may shed tears for,

And to my loving mother who tore her grey locks and cried

Saying, ‚ÄúI wish that, Willie Brennan, in your cradle you had died!‚ÄĚ

items.lyrics:
Tom Boleyn was a Scotsman born,

His shoes worn out and his stocking was torn.

The calf of his leg hung down to his shin,

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm a hell of a fella,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn.

The woolly side out and the fleecy side in,

‚ÄúCool in the summer,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn.

Tom Boleyn bought him an old grey mare,

Away he went through thick and thin,

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm going a-courting,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn.

He rode over to the Dutchman‚Äôs hall,

There he got down amongst them all.

‚ÄúCome in, come in, I bid you come in.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôve come here courting,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn

‚ÄúCome in. come in, you welcome guest,

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll take one for love and the other for kin,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll marry them both,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn.

‚ÄúAfter the wedding we must have a dinner.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs a hell of a dinner,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn.

‚ÄúAnd after the dinner we must have a bed.‚ÄĚ

The floor it was swept and the straw it was spread.

The blankets were short and the sides were thin,

‚ÄúStick close to my back,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn.

When Cecil Sharp noted the song from Mrs Pace she gave
him the following three verses which follow on from the above:

Tom Boleyn‚Äôs wife and his wife‚Äôs mother

All went over the bridge together.

The bridge broke down, they all fell in,

‚ÄúGet out, if you can,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn.

Tom Boleyn crawled in an old hollow tree,

Very well content he seemed to be.

The wind blew hard and the rain beat in,

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs hell of a lodgings,‚ÄĚ says Tom Boleyn.

Tom Boleyn‚Äôs wife being of a low squat

And out of the water she quickly got.

And away she went through thick and thin,

Enquiring for delicate Tom Boleyn.

items.title: Bright New Year
items.notes:
Bright New Year

[Alec Thompson]

Arrowsmith:Robb sang Alec Thompson‚Äôs Bright New Year

on their 2018 CD

All the Salt .

They noted:

A song of the seasons which feels traditional even though it isn‚Äôt.

Jess and Richard Arrowsmith returned to it in

2019 on Melrose Quartet‚Äôs CD

The Rudolph Variations .
items.lyrics:
Now all around is cold and chill,

But take good heart, fear no ill,

For through the frost of winter

Comes a bright new year.

A bright new year,

A bright new year

For through the frost of winter

Comes a bright new year.

Food and drink to make us grow,

Swell the seed that we would sow,

So comes the rain in springtime

And a bright new year.

A bright new year,

A bright new year

So comes the rain in springtime

And a bright new year.

Warmth and light to make us strong,

Bring the ripest fruit along

So comes the sun in summer

And a bright new year.

When harvest time has gone around

Blow out the chaff, clear the ground,

So comes the wind in autumn

And a bright new year.

So when all around is cold and chill,

Take good heart, fear no ill,

For through the frost of winter

Comes a bright new year.

items.title: Bring Hither Now the Holly Bough
items.notes:
Bring Hither Now the Holly Bough

[Stephen Fawcett, Christopher Edwin Willing]

Lady Maisery and Jimmy Aldridge & Sid Goldsmith sang

Bring Hither Now the Holly Bough

in 2019 on their winter album

Awake, Arise .

They noted:

In the winter months the evergreens are gathered and brought indoors,

conjuring a memory of summer, in defiance of winter‚Äôs scarcity.

Words by Stephen Fawcett, first published in Bradford Legends ,

1872. Tune by Christopher Edwin Willing.
items.lyrics:
Bring hither now the holly bough with berries bright and red

The ivy from the ruined tower where owls shriek o‚Äôer the dead

The mistletoe with mystic power and winter‚Äôs garland weave

And the Yule logs blaze shall shed its rays to cheer our Christmas Eve

The rack rides fast, deep howls the blast, where is the linnet now?

And where the rose of sunny June? The blossoms of the sloe?

They‚Äôre gone but they‚Äôll return again and meantime lest we grieve

We ll warm our hearts with wassail cups and cheer our Christmas Eve

Come Betty dear no voice of love in nature now we hear

But angels near us whisper it through all the varied year

In cold or heat, in gloom or shine, the heart it shall not leave

For as when sultry summer reigns it burns at Christmas Eve

We‚Äôll wake the viol‚Äôs merry strings while tempest clouds advance

And while the pane cracks with big hail we tread the careless dance

Thus shall the soul‚Äôs warm summer shine ‚Äôtil changeful earth we leave

And the Yule fire and the wassail cup shall cheer our Christmas Eve

We‚Äôll drink it up, lads, we‚Äôll drink it dry.

The poor little firkin‚Äôs nine gallons in all

Though the beer it is good, the size is too small

For lads that are drinkers like you and like I

That firkin small barrel too quickly runs dry.

And when that I‚Äôm dying and on my death bed

By my bedside leave a fine full hogshead

That if down below I mun go when I die

Me and old Nick we will both drink it dry.

The kilderkin‚Äôs next and although rather small

At least it is better than nothing at all

Its eighteen full gallons will just about do

Provided, of course, there‚Äôs another for you.

Then bring forth the puncheon and roll out the butt

Them‚Äôs the best measures before me to put

Our pots will go round and good ale it will flow

And we‚Äôll be contented for an hour or so.

items.title: Jockey / Bring Your Fiddle
items.notes:
Jockey / Bring Your Fiddle

[

; VWML CJS2/10/1575

; Bodleian

; GlosTrad

; trad.]

John Tams and Shirley Collins recited the dialogue of

‚Äúa pastoral dialogue taken from a printed broadsheet‚ÄĚ,

in 1976 on Ashley Hutchings and others‚Äô album

Son of Morris On .

Cathy Tattersfield and Jess Arrowsmith sang
items.lyrics:
As Jockey on one summers morn was walking with his Molly,

And as together they did walk, the loon was most unlucky,

He rolled her in his gentle arms, and kissed her in the bushes,

Until at length her face became a wreath of modest blushes.

‚ÄúIs this the way to win my heart by tearing of my laces?

I think you act a clownish part by these rude embraces,

The like of which I never saw so I pray young man be civil,

Or else without any more a do I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll kick you to the devil.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat kick your Jockey?‚ÄĚ he replied, ‚ÄúI think you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre very cruel,

Come sit you down with me awhile and let me kiss my jewel,

Those pretty little sparkling eyes and lips as red as rubies,‚ÄĚ

He to her said, but she replied, ‚ÄúBe gone, thou worst of boobies.

‚ÄúI can√Ę¬Ä¬ôt nor won√Ę¬Ä¬ôt come pleasure you, nor should I have to bear it,‚ÄĚ

She cried out, ‚ÄúYou have spoilt our fun, why you can wait I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll swear it.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMy dear,‚ÄĚ said he, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll wait for thee, don√Ę¬Ä¬ôt think me coarse and fickle‚ÄĚ,

And then without any more a do, young Jockey tuned his fiddle.

He played her such a merry tune he charmed all her senses,

She said, ‚ÄúBe gone thou silly loon, I pardon all offences,

My cheeks you have with blushes filled, my heart with wreaths of laughter,

I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll tell my mother, yes that I will, how you have charmed her daughter.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat kiss and tell?‚ÄĚ young Jockey said, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm sure there√Ę¬Ä¬ôs no confession.

;You told me straight, you told me true, there was no transgression.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúO no,‚ÄĚ said she, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll only say that you listened and were civil,

But the very next time you come this way, be sure and bring your fiddle.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Brockagh Brae
items.notes:
Brockagh Brae

[

; Ballad Index RcBroBra

; Mudcat 169416

; John Canavan]

George ‚ÄėGeordie‚Äô Hanna sang

Brockagh Brae

in his sister Sarah Anne O‚ÄôNeill‚Äôs home near Derrytresk, Coalisland,

Co Tyrone, to Robin Morton in 1977.

This recording was included in the following year

on his and Sarah Anne O‚ÄôNeill‚Äôs Topic album of

traditional songs of a Tyrone family,
items.lyrics:
One night as I lay slumbering in my silent bed alone,

Some rakish thoughts came in my head and it caused me for to roam,

For to leave my native country and the wee girl I adore.

So I thought it fit to take a trip strange lands to explore.

Now the night before that I went away I was walking up Brockagh Hill

When I met my darling upon the road and her eyes with tears did fill.

‚ÄúStay you at home, dear John,‚ÄĚ she said, ‚Äúand do not go away,

For I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll have none to comfort me when you are on the sea.‚ÄĚ

Well, I took her by the lily-white hands and I held her hard and fast,

Saying, ‚ÄúAdieu, my darling, I must obey for my ship lies in Belfast.

But if you prove loyal, sure, I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll prove true for you know that I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm well inclined.‚ÄĚ

So we kissed, shook hands and parted and I left my wee girl behind.

Now, when I landed in Belfast, sure, our ship she was in full sail.

Away she went down the lough with a soft and pleasant gale.

She was sailing o‚Äôer the ocean where her foaming billows roar,

So with thanks be to the heavens above that we landed safe on shore.

Now when I landed in Greendock, sure, the people all gathered around.

They said I was a rakish lad come to cut the harvest down.

They told me to return home and never no more to be seen,

So without delay, sure, I sail straightaway for Erin‚Äôs lovely green.

Now, when Mary heard that her John was home her heart it did beat with joy,

Saying, ‚ÄúCome into my arms, you√Ę¬Ä¬ôre my darling lovely boy.‚ÄĚ

Saying, ‚ÄúCome into my arms, for you I have thought a great long.

So let them all say as they will; our wedding it will go on.‚ÄĚ

Now, Brockagh Brae‚Äôs a nice wee place where nice wee girls live in.

You would swear they were the nightingales when they set down to sing.

Where the salmon trout all sport about round Lough Neagh‚Äôs verdant shore.

So let them all say as they will; you are mine for ever more.

items.title: The Broken-Down Squatter
items.notes:

items.lyrics:
Come Stumpy old man we must shift while we can

Together we‚Äôll roam from our drought stricken home

Seems hard that such things have to be

And it‚Äôs hard on a horse when he‚Äôs none for a boss

But a broken down Squatter like me

Chorus (after each verse):

And the banks are all broken they say

The merchants are all up a tree

When the big wigs are brought to the bankruptcy court

What chance for a squatter like me.

No more we will muster the river for fats

Nor spiel on the fifteen mile plain

Nor rip through the scrub by the light of the moon

Nor see the old homestead again

Leave the slip panels down they don‚Äôt matter much now

There‚Äôs none but the crows left to see

Sitting gaunt on a pine as though longing to dine

On a broken down Squatter like me.

When the country was cursed with the drought at it‚Äôs worst

The cattle were dying in scores

Though down on me luck I kept up me pluck

Thinking justice might temper the cause

But the farce has been played and the government aid

Ain‚Äôt extended to Squatters old son

When me money was spent they doubled the rent

And resumed the best half of the run.

Oh ‚Äôtwas done without reason for leaving the season

No Squatter could stand such a rub

And it‚Äôs useless to squat when the rents are so hot

That you can‚Äôt find the price of your grub

And there‚Äôs not much to choose ‚Äôtween the banks and the screws

When a feller gets put up a tree

No odds how I feel there‚Äôs no court of appeal

For a broken down Squatter like me.

items.title: Broughty Wa‚Äôs / Burd Helen
items.notes:

items.lyrics:
Burd Helen was her mother‚Äôs dear,

Her father‚Äôs heir to be;

Burd Helen she was much admired

But to Hunglen she was betrothed,

Her virgin days were oot.

Glenhazlen was a comely youth,

An virtuous his friens;

He left the schules o bonnie Dundee,

An on to Aberdeen .

It fell oot once upon a time,

Burd Helen was left alone;

All for to keep her father‚Äôs towers,

They stand two miles from toun.

Glenhazlen he came riding by,

And thinking to get in;

But the wind it blew and the rain dang on,

And weet him to the skin.

He was very well entertained,

Baith for his bed an‚Äô board,

Till a band o‚Äô men surrounded them,

They hies√®d her along wi them,

Owre mony a rock an glen,

But a‚Äô that they could say or do;

To weep she would not refrain.

‚ÄúThe highland hills are high, high hills,

The highland hills are hie,

And if you wald my favour gain,

Oh! tak me to Dundee.‚ÄĚ

It fell oot once upon a time,

They went oot to tak the air,

She threw hersel into the stream,

Between wind an‚Äô despair.

The stream was deep, he couldna wade,

Boats waurna to be found,

So he leapt in after her himsel

An‚Äô sank doun like a stone.

‚ÄúThe highland hills are high, high hills,

The highland hills are hie,

They are no like the pleasant banks o‚Äô Tay,

Nor the bonnie toun o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Dundee.‚ÄĚ

She kilted up her green cleiden,

‚ÄúI learnt this at Brochty Walls,

At Brochty near Dundee,

items.title: Brown Robin
items.notes:
Brown Robin

[

/ Song Subject MAS1065

; Child 97

; G/D 8:1932

; Ballad Index C097

; DT BRNROBIN

; trad.]

James Kinsley:

Sigrid Rieuwerts:

items.lyrics:
The king and his noble lords

Sat drinking of the wine;

And he‚Äôd have none but his own daughter

To wait on them at dine.

She‚Äôs served them and she‚Äôs served them well,

All in a gown of green,

But her eye was on Brown Robin,

Stood low under the rain.

She‚Äôs gone up to her bed chamber,

As fast as she could run,

And there she‚Äôs opened her shot-window,

And she‚Äôs harped and she sang.

‚ÄúThere sits a bird in in the apple tree,

And o but she sings sweet!

I hope to live and see the day

When with my love I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll meet.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIf this be true, my love,‚ÄĚ he said,

As your tongue tells to me,

At the hour of twelve this night,

At your bower shall I be.‚ÄĚ

For she has plied the gatekeeper

With strong beer and with wine,

And she stole of the keys to her father‚Äôs gates

And she‚Äôs let her true-love in.

When night was gone, and day was come,

And sun shone on their feet,

Then out and spoke Brown Robin,

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be discovered yet.‚ÄĚ

Then out and spoke that gay lady:

‚ÄúMy love, you need no doubt;

It was by wile I brought you in,

By wile I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll bring you out.‚ÄĚ

She‚Äôs taken up a cup of wine,

To her father in went she;

‚ÄúO drink our wine, my father,

Drink the wine with me.‚ÄĚ

And it√Ę¬Ä¬ôs, ‚ÄúWoe be to your wine, father,

That ever came o‚Äôer the sea;

It‚Äôs put my head in such a stir,

And in my chamer I cannot be.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúGo out, go out, my daughter dear,

Go out and take the air;

Go out and walk in the good green wood,

With all your maidens fair.‚ÄĚ

And she has gone to her chamber,

As fast as she could run,

And she has dressed Brown Robin

Like any fair woman.

The gown she put upon her love

Was of the dainty green,

His hose was of the soft, soft silk,

His shoes the cordwain fine.

The first young maiden she sent out

Was Brown Robin by name;

Then out it spoke the king himself,

‚ÄúThere goes is a sturdy dame.‚ÄĚ

And she went out that May morning,
