items.title: Burgundian Carol / Carol of the Beasts
items.notes:
[

; DT BURGUNDN

; Bernard de la Monnoye]

This

Burgundian Carol

was written by Bernard de la Monnoye in ca 1700.

It was translated by Oscar Brand and recorded by the Weavers

for their 1951 Decca album

We Wish You a Merry Christmas .

Pete Seeger also sang the shorter

Carol of the Beasts

in 1967 on his Folkways album
items.lyrics:
The winter season of the year,

When to this world our Lord was born,

The ox and donkey, so they say,

Did keep his holy presence warm.

How many oxen and donkeys now

If they were there when first he came,

How many oxen and donkeys you know

At such a time would do the same?

And on that night it has been told

These humble beasts so rough and rude,

Throughout the noght of holy birth

Drank no water, ate no food.

How many oxen and donkeys now

If they were there when first he came,

How many oxen and donkeys you know

At such a time would do the same?

As soon as to these humble beasts

Appeared our Lord, so mild and sweet,

With joy they knelt before his Grace

And gently kissed His tiny feet.

How many oxen and donkeys now

Dressed in ermine, silk and such,

How many oxen and donkeys you know

At such a time would do as much?

versions.title: Pete Seeger sings Carol of the Beasts
versions.lyrics:

As soon as to these humble beasts

With joy they knelt before His Grace

And gently kissed His tiny feet.

How many oxen and donkeys

Now if they were there when first He came

How many oxen and donkeys you know

And my mother she‚Äôs got lots more like I,

items.title: By Chance It Was
items.notes:
By Chance It Was

[

/ Song Subject MAS1

; Ballad Index BGFS001

; VWML RoudFS/S200746

; Folkinfo 56

; Mudcat 3960

; trad.]

By Chance It Was

is the first song in Rev. Sabine Baring-Gould‚Äôs 1913 book

Songs of the West .

Mike Bosworth sang
items.lyrics:
By chance it was I met my love,

It did me much surprise,

Down by a shady myrtle grove,

Just as the sun did rise.

The birds they sang right gloriously,

And pleasant was the air;

And there was none, save she and I,

Among the flowers fair.

In dewy grass and green we walk‚Äôd,

She timid was and was coy;

‚ÄúHow can√Ę¬Ä¬ôst thou choose but pity me,

My pretty pearl, my joy?

How comes it that thou stroll‚Äôst this way?

Sweet Maiden, tell me true,

Before bright Phoebus‚Äô glittering ray

Has supped the morning dew?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI go to tend the flocks I love

The ewes and tender lambs,

That pasture by the myrtle grove,

That gambol by their dams;

There I enjoy a pure content

At dawning of the day,‚ÄĚ

Then, hand in hand, we lovers went

To see the flock at play.

And as we wended down the road.

I said to her, ‚ÄúSweet maid,

Three years I in my place abode

And three more must be stayed.

Three times that I am bound so fast,

O fairest wait for me.

And when these weary years are passed

Then married we will be‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThree years are long, three times to long,

Too lengthy the delay‚ÄĚ.

O then I answered in my song,

‚ÄúHope wastes them quick away.

Where love is fervent, fain and fast,

And knoweth not decay.

There nimbly fleet the seasons past,

Accounted as one day.‚ÄĚ

Edith Fowke:

Penguin Book of Canadian Folk Songs .

O.J. Abbott from Hull, Quebec, sang the emigrant and Civil War song

in a 1957 field recording made by Edith Fowke.

It was included in 1961 on his Folkways album
items.lyrics:
Oh, it‚Äôs by the hush, me boys, I‚Äôm sure that‚Äôs to hold your noise,

And listen to poor Paddy‚Äôs narration.

I was by hunger pressed and in poverty distressed

So I took a thought I‚Äôd leave the Irish nation.

Chorus (after each verse):

Here‚Äôs you, boys, do take my advice,

To America I‚Äôd have youse not be coming.

There‚Äôs nothing here but war where the murdering cannons roar,

And I wish I was at home in dear old Erin.

Then I sold my horse and plough, my little pigs and cow,

And my little farm of land and I parted.,

And my sweetheart Biddy McGee I fear I‚Äôll never see,

For I left her that morning broken-hearted.

When we landed in Yankee land, shoved a gun into our hand,

Saying, ‚ÄúPaddy you must go and fight for Lincoln.‚ÄĚ

General Mahar to us said, ‚ÄúIf you get shot or lose your head,

Every murdered soul of you will get a pension.‚ÄĚ

In the war I lost my leg; all I‚Äôve now is a wooden peg,

By my soul it is the truth to you I mention.

Now I think myself in luck to be fed upon Indian buck

In old Ireland, the country I delight in,

And with the devil I do say, ‚ÄúCurse Americay‚ÄĚ,

For I‚Äôm sure I‚Äôve had enough of their hard fighting.

Donald, a ballad by Alexander Balfour of Monikie near Forfar.

just as the sun gaed doon,

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Cairn-o-Mount is bleak and bare
and cauld is Clochnaben,

And you will see the snaw lie there

I lighted aff my dapple grey

It‚Äôs war me o yer face sae fair,
yer een sae bonnie blue,

‚ÄúOh lassie, wad ye gang wi me

Wi aa ma kin ye‚Äôd bear the gree ,
there‚Äôs wealth baith but and ben;

Wi silks and satins buskit braw
and ribbons tae yer hair,

hoo can ye wish for mair?

‚ÄúOh lassie ye maun think a wee ,
my lands are far and wide.

I‚Äôve gowd in banks and ships at sea,
so come and be my bride;

a coach at my command.

I‚Äôd mak ye lady o them aa,
If ye√Ę¬Ä¬ôd gie me yer hand.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt√Ę¬Ä¬ôs Cairn-o-Mount is bleak and bare
ye‚Äôre no a match for me;

his hert is leal and true.

he soon wad mak ye rue.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh lassie think, yer Donald√Ę¬Ä¬ôs poor,
has neither horse nor coo;

‚ÄúOh Caim-o-Mount is bleak an bare
and cauld is Clochnaben;

than be fair Scotland√Ę¬Ä¬ôs queen.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúOh lassie I am loathe tae tell,
ye throw yer love awa;

‚ÄúThough ye would swear wi solemn oath

but say, ‚ÄėFause loon, ye lee .‚Äô ‚ÄĚ

It‚Äôs he‚Äôs taen aff his lowland gear,

Sayin, ‚ÄúLassie, ye√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been true tae me
and soon we‚Äôll pairt nae mair.

‚ÄúNae mair I√Ę¬Ä¬ôm shepherd o yon glen
but laird ayont the Dee;

And since ye hae been true tae me
I‚Äôll aye prove true tae thee;

It‚Äôs Caim-o-Mount is bleak and bare
and cauld is Clochnaben

And you will see the snaw lie there

items.title: Caller O‚Äôu
items.notes:

; trad.]

items.lyrics:

Chorus (after each verse):

Caller o‚Äôu! Caller o‚Äôu!

Success tae the boatmen at hame and awa

At kirk and at fair there‚Äôs nane gangs sae braw

And leal be their dames, sae blythesome and fair

Their voice in the evening is music to hear

items.title: Cameloun / Tarves Parish

; G/D 3:389

And oot o‚Äôs bed he sprang tae his feet

Tae caa the fan they set me tae

And took a look foo they wid dee

items.title: Campbell the Drover
items.notes:
Campbell the Drover

[

; Ballad Index K269

; DT DROVCAMP

; trad.]

Margaret Christl and Ian Robb sang

and Ian Robb returned to it in 2021 on his and James Stephens‚Äô album

He noted:
items.lyrics:
The first day of April, I‚Äôll never forget,

Three English lassies together they met;

They mounted their horses and swore solemnly

That they would play a trick on the first man they see.

Well, Campbell, the drover, was riding that day,

And soon he encountered those lassies so gay.

They reined in their horses and he did the same,

And in close conversation together they came.

Chorus (after each verse):

And sing fol de rol laddy,

Fol de rol laddy,

Fol de rol laddy,

Sing fol de rol day.

They asked him to show them the way to the inn,

And would he drink whiskey or would he drink gin?

Then Campbell made answer and said with a smile,

‚ÄúSure, I long for to taste the strong ale of Carlisle.‚ÄĚ

Well they called in the servants and started a dance;

They ordered the landlord to spare no expense;

They danced the next morning, ‚Äôtil ‚Äôtwixt eight and nine,

And they called for their breakfast, and afterwards wine.

They mounted their horses, alas and alack,

It dawned on the landlord they weren‚Äôt coming back.

He said, ‚ÄúMy dear Irishman, I am afraid

That those three English jokers a trick on you played.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNever mind‚ÄĚ, says old Campbell, ‚ÄúIf they√Ę¬Ä¬ôve gone away,

I‚Äôve plenty of money, the reckoning to pay.

Just sit down beside me, and before that I go,

I will show you a trick that perhaps you don‚Äôt know.

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôll show you a trick that√Ę¬Ä¬ôs contrary to law:

Two kinds of whiskey from one cask to draw.‚ÄĚ

The landlord, being eager to learn of the plan,

Straightway to the cellar, with Paddy, he ran.

He soon bore a hole in a very short space,

And he bade the landlord stick his thumb on the place.

He then bored another, ‚ÄúPlace your other thumb there,

While I for a tumbler must run up the stairs.‚ÄĚ

When Campbell was mounted, and well out of sight,

The ‚Äôostler come in in a terrible fright.

He hunted the house, high up and low down;

Half dead in the cellar, his master he found.

‚ÄúGo and find that bold Irishman!‚ÄĚ loudly he cried;

‚ÄúI fear he has vanished‚ÄĚ, the √Ę¬Ä¬ôostler replied.

He said, ‚ÄúMy dear landlord, I am afraid

That Campbell the drover a trick on you played.‚ÄĚ

items.title: Captain Grant
items.notes:
Captain Grant

[

; Master title: Captain Grant

; Ballad Index McCST091

; VWML CJS2/9/2132

, CJS2/10/2359

; Bodleian

; GlosTrad

Captain Grant

; Wiltshire

746

; DT CAPTGRNT
items.lyrics:
My name is Captain Grant I am bound for to say.

I‚Äôm one of those bold heroes all on the King‚Äôs highway

With my brace of loaded pistols and my steady broad-sword

O stand and deliver it, is always the word.

To do a duty action I always did scan

In robbing from the rich I thought it was no harm

With my gold and my jewels I always did secure

One half I kept myself and the other gave the poor.

If I meets with a traveller that is hungry and dry

I‚Äôll take him to some alehouse and his wants I will supply,

With good ale, wine and brandy, boys, till I spend all my store,

When my money is all gone, I‚Äôll boldly rob for more.

To Edinburgh gaol they marched me away

And there I did remain till my trial it did come on,

For shooting at the King I was then condemned to die

But I never had no hand in the same robbery.

Out of Edinburgh gaol then I made my way out

And those that did oppose me, I put them to the rout,

With my bars and iron bolts I knocked the sentry down

And I made my escape out of Edinburgh town.

Out of Edinburgh gaol then I made my way good,

And I took up my lodgings in the centre of a wood,

Until some wicked woman she did me betray

And she had me surrounded as sleeping I did lay.

I flew to my arms but my powder being wet,

And to my sad misfortune I found that I was beat,

And to my sad misfortune I gave myself up,

To the m‚Ä¶‚Ä¶s young hero called the man taker-up.

To Edinburgh gaol then they marched me again

And there I did remain through sorrow, grief and pain.

God bless my wife and family and may they never want

And the Lord have mercy on my soul, cries bold Captain Grant.

versions.title: Nelson Ridley sings Captain Grant
versions.lyrics:
Where I was bound apprentice was down in Northamptonshire,

Where I did take my lodgings in the centry of a wood ‚Äď

With my two metal bullets and my bright shiny sword,

And it‚Äôs quickly they did banish me to Edinborough Town.

From Edinborough Town, my boys, I thought it myself good,

Till I did take my lodgings in the centry of a wood;

It was through that wicked woman had me surrounded as I were laying,

‚ÄúMay the Lord have mercy on me!‚ÄĚ cried bold Captain Grant.

Now, here‚Äôs to my wife and my five children small,

And since I‚Äôve been drinking I have ruined them all;

God save ‚Äôem and God keep ‚Äôem from sickness and from want,

‚ÄúMay the Lord have mercy on me!‚ÄĚ said bold Captain Grant.

versions.title: The Halliard sing Captain Grant
versions.lyrics:
O my name is Captain Grant and I am bound to say

That I‚Äôm one of those bold heroes found on the highway

With a brace of pistols and my bright long sword

Stand and deliver was always my word

For doing the dirty acts I always did scorn

But taking from the rich I thought it no harm

When I went collecting I had my cash secure

One half I spent, the other I gave to the poor

When I saw poor people starve, hungry or dry

I quickly sought out their want and I did them supply

In cheerful company I spent my store

And when it‚Äôs gone, I go boldly collecting more

To Edinburgh jail then I was marched along

And there I was confined till my trial came on

I was sentenced to be hanged for sheltering M‚ÄôLay

Although I had no hand in that robbery

Ah, but before the day it came that I was to be hung

I bursted the prison doors and away I did run

And I escaped through the castle gates

And it was not safe for me along the way

Out of Edinburgh jail then I made my way good

And I took up my lodgings that night in a wood

But there was a woman who did me betray

And I was surprised as asleep there I lay

I was surrounded, away I could not get

They seized my pistols for my powder was wet

And in my ill fortune I gave myself up

To that noted hero called Natty Take-up

Back to Edinburgh jail then I was passed along

And there I was confined till my trial came on

I was sentenced to be hanged for sheltering M‚ÄôLay

Although I had no hand in that robbery

O men that I forgive, may I pardon receive

I‚Äôve took from the rich for the poor to relieve

God bless my wife and child, may they never want

Lord have mercy on the soul of poor Captain Grant

items.title: Captain Webster
items.notes:
Captain Webster

[

; Ballad Index RcCapWeb

; trad.]

Sara Cleveland of Brant Lake, New York, sang

Kenneth S. Goldstein noted:

The theme of family opposition to lovers culminating in the

death of one or both sweethearts is a common one both in the Old
items.lyrics:
Good people all, from far and near,

A dreadful story you will hear;

It is concerning a young man

Who shot himself in West Fort Ann.

He was beloved by all who knew

The life he lived from boyhood through.

He gave both parents honour due,

And now you‚Äôll see what love can do.

With a young girl he fell in love;

His mother tried his heart to change.

She said, ‚ÄúMy son, let me hear no more

About this girl for she is poor.

‚ÄúI have another girl in view

Who‚Äôll make a better wife for you.

I‚Äôd rather follow you to your grave

Than know that this poor girl you√Ę¬Ä¬ôd have.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúSo, Mother, be it as you say.‚ÄĚ

And with these words he turned away.

Straight to his love he then did go,

His mother‚Äôs words to let her know.

Said he, ‚ÄúMy dear, I can√Ę¬Ä¬ôt marry thee;

My mother‚Äôs words have ruined me.

For Mother she has laid her plans,

And I‚Äôll fulfil them, if I can.

‚ÄúFarewell, farewell, I now must leave,

Farewell, my darling, but do not grieve.

No peace on earth can I find here;

In Heaven I√Ę¬Ä¬ôll wait for you, my dear.‚ÄĚ

Alas, alas, but all too late,

We learn of Captain Webster‚Äôs fate.

They found him dead on his cabin floor,

Shot through the heart and wreathed in gone.

A pistol in his hand he held,

The dreadful story for to tell.

Without the girl he did adore,

His life was not worth living more.

Now, mothers all, a warning take,

And careful be the course you take;

Of mighty dollars was the one

That caused the death of this young man.

When I wore my apron low

items.title: Carmin Fair
items.notes:
Carmin Fair

[

; Mudcat 89979

; trad.]

Sean Doyle sang

in 2004 on his Compass album

The Light and the Half-Light .

He noted:

This is another Ulster song I came across in

Traditional Songs of the North of Ireland

by Derek Bell and Liam √ď Conchubhair [1999].
items.lyrics:
One very pleasant summer‚Äôs morning

When everything was bright and gay,

The birds sang out their notes so charming

And I myself was as glad as they.

I dressed myself in me Sunday suit,

Washed me face and combed me hair,

Greased me brogues and sallied forth

For the spend the day at Carmin fair.

When I arrived at the town of Carmin

Crowds of people thronged the street.

Some were dressed in silk and satin,

Some wore big brogues upon their feet.

Some had hankies round their heads,

With their feet and legs completely bare.

Such a comical sight as you ever did see

As I saw that day at Carmin fair.

As I was passing them by in masses

Examining them as I went along,

Looking at the fancy dresses

I spied a maid among the throng.

Her smile was sweet, her figure neat,

And grand was the dress that she did wear.

I thought there was no one there that day

To compare with her at Carmin fair.

I invited her in to have a trait

And she agreed without a frown,

So off we went with one consent

To an alehouse up in Carmin town.

I called for a pint of good strong sherry

And down in the snug we went and sat.

Us two became both light and merry

And I counted meself a lucky brat.

When in my arms I held her folded

And kissing her lips as I thought no sin,

And all of a sudden the door bursts open

And a big rough man comes thundering in.

He says, ‚ÄúYou damned infernal ruffian,

How dare you lay your hands on me wife?‚ÄĚ

He began to give me such a beating,

I feared to God he would take me life.

‚ÄúO husband dear, beat him well,‚ÄĚ says she,

‚ÄúHe kissed me squeezed me and pulled me hair.‚ÄĚ

1 was the lad paid well for the day,

I spent with his wife at Carmin fair.

Now all lads who want a bride

Of unknown females now beware,

Mind the disgrace that happened to me

In that damned deceitful Carmin fair.

items.lyrics:
The old year now away is fled, the new year it is entered,

Then let us now our sins downtread, and joyfully all appear;

Let‚Äôs merry be this holiday, and let us run with sport and play,

Hang sorrow, let√Ę¬Ä¬ôs cast care away‚ÄĒGod send us a Happy New Year.

And now with new year‚Äôs gifts, each friend unto each other they do send;

God grant we may our lives amend, so that the truth may appear.

Now like the snake cast off your skin of evil thoughts and wicked sin,

And to amend this new year begin‚ÄĒGod send us a Happy New Year.

And now let all the company in friendly manner all agree,

For we are here welcome, all may see, unto this jolly good cheer.

I thank my master and my dame, the which are founders of the same,

To eat and drink now is no shame‚ÄĒGod send us a Happy New Year.

Come lads and lasses, everyone, Jack, Tom, Dick, Beth, Mary and Joan,

We‚Äôll cut the meat unto the bone, for welcome you need not fear.

And here for good liquor we shall not lack, it will whet my brain and strengthen my back,

This jolly good cheer must not go to wrack‚ÄĒGod send us a Happy New Year.

Come, give us more liquor when I do call, I‚Äôll drink to each one in this hall,

I hope that so loud I must not bawl, but unto me lend an ear.

Good fortune to my master send, and to my dame, which is our friend,

God bless us all, and so I end‚ÄĒGod send us a Happy New Year.

items.lyrics:
It‚Äôs of a nobleman‚Äôs daughter

So comely and beautiful we‚Äôre told.

Her parents possessed a great fortune

Many thousands in gold.

She had but one only daughter;

Caroline was her name we are told.

One day from her drawing-room window

She admired a young sailor bold.

His cheeks they appeared like two roses;

His hair was black as jet.

Young Caroline watched his departure,

Walked round, and young William she met.

She said, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm a nobleman√Ę¬Ä¬ôs daughter

Possessed of thousands in gold.

I‚Äôll forsake both my father and mother

To wed with a young sailor bold.‚ÄĚ

Said William, ‚ÄúYoung lady, remember

You‚Äôre bound from your parents to roam.

On sailors there is no dependence,

When their trueloves are left far at home.

Be advised, stay at home with your parents

And do as by them you are told.

Don‚Äôt never let anyone tempt you

To wed with a young sailor bold.‚ÄĚ

She said, ‚ÄúThere√Ę¬Ä¬ôs no one shall persuade me

One moment to alter my mind.

I‚Äôll ship and proceed with my truelove;

He never shall leave me behind.‚ÄĚ

So she dressed like a gallant young sailor,

Forsook both her parents and gold,

And soon she was ploughing the salt seas

Caroline and her young sailor bold.

Three times with her love she got shipwrecked.

She always kept constant and true.

Her duty she did like a sailor;

Went aloft in her jacket so blue.

Her father long wept and lamented.

The tears from his eyes they long rolled,

Till at last she arrived safe in England

Caroline and her young sailor bold.

Caroline went straight to her father

In her jacket and trousers so blue.

He looked; he momently fainted,

When first she appeared to him in view.

She cried, ‚ÄúMy dear father, forgive me.

Deprive me for ever in gold.

Grant me my request: I‚Äôm contented

To live with my young sailor bold.‚ÄĚ

Her father admired young William.

He vowed that in sweet unity,

If life should then be spared to the morning.

They married together should be.

They were married on Caroline‚Äôs fortune

Which was many thousands in gold,

And now they live happy and contented,

Caroline and her young sailor bold.

items.lyrics:
Come all ye men and maidens, and listen to my rhyme.

‚ÄôTis of a lovely damsel just hastening in her prime.

Her cheeks were like the roses red; her hair was deep dark brown.

She was called the blooming Caroline o‚Äô Edinburgh town.

Young Henry being a roving lad a-courting to her came,

And when her parents came to know they were angry at the same.

Young Henry being offended, these words to her did say,

‚ÄúArise, my dearest Caroline, and we will run away.

‚ÄúOh, we will go to London and there we√Ę¬Ä¬ôll wed with speed,

And then, my dearest Caroline, there√Ę¬Ä¬ôll be happiness indeed.‚ÄĚ

She came tripping down the stairs; her hair was hanging down,

And away went the blooming Caroline o‚Äô Edinburgh town.

O‚Äôer hills and lofty mountains this couple they did go,

Till they arrived in London far from their native home.

She said, ‚ÄúMy dearest Henry, if you ever on me frown.

You√Ę¬Ä¬ôll break the heart of Caroline o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Edinburgh town.‚ÄĚ

They had not been in London but passing half a year,

When cruel-hearted Henry proved unto her severe.

He said, ‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôm bound for the sea, my love, since your parents on me frown,

So peg your way without delay to Edinburgh town.‚ÄĚ

O‚Äôer hills and lofty mountains this fair maid she did go,

And to the wood to eat that‚Äôs good as on the bushes grow.

Some strangers they did pity her, and some did on her frown,

And some did say, ‚ÄúWhat made you stray frae Edinburgh town?‚ÄĚ

Beneath the lofty spreading oak this maid began to cry,

And, watching all the gallant ships as they went sailing by,

She gave three shrieks for Henry, then plunged her body down,

And away went the blooming Caroline o‚Äô Edinburgh town.

A note, likewise her bonnet, she left upon the shore,

And in the note a lock of hair with the words ‚ÄúI am no more.

Full fast asleep in the ocean deep, with the fish watching all around.

I was once called the blooming Caroline o√Ę¬Ä¬ô Edinburgh town.‚ÄĚ

Now all ye tender parents ne‚Äôer try to part true love,

Or else you‚Äôll see in some degree the ruin it will prove too.

Likewise young men and maidens, ne‚Äôer on your lover frown.

Think on the fate of Caroline o‚Äô Edinburgh town.

‚ÄúI√Ę¬Ä¬ôve been to see your parents, upon me they did frown

Down by yonder spreaded oak where she sat down to cry

Watching of the gallant ship as they went passing by

She gave the ship her Henry while the fish were watching ‚Äôround

For the body of young Caroline who plunged her body down.

versions.title: Tom Lenihan sings Caroline of Edinburgh Town
versions.lyrics:
Now gentlemen and maidens,
come listen to my rhyme,

‚ÄôTis of a lovely maiden
who is scarcely in her prime.

She beat the blooming roses,
she was admired by one and all,

She being once a lovely Caroline
from Edinburgh Town.

Young Henry being a Highlander,
sure, courting her he came.

Her parents came to hear of it,
they did not like that same.

Young Henry being a Highlander,
he stole her finest gown,

So away goes lovely Caroline
from Edinburgh Town.

They being no longer in London Town,
scarcely one half year,

When Henry proved to his true love,
unkind and most severe.

He says one day, ‚ÄúYou must go and see,
for your friends, they did on me frown,

So be on your way without delay
to Edinburgh Town.‚ÄĚ

Oppressed with grief, without relief,
this fair one had to go

Into the woods to pick some fruit
that on the branches grew.

‚ÄôTis there some friends would welcome her
and more would on her frown,

And some would say, ‚ÄúWhy did you stray
from Edinburgh Town?‚ÄĚ

It was on a stone she wrote a note saying,
‚ÄúAlas, I am no more.‚ÄĚ

A lock of her hair she did leave there
to grieve him more and more.

She gave three screams for Henry
and threw her body down,

And that was the last of Caroline
from Edinburgh Town.
